2. The agent maintains a **memory of past actions** and a **planning loop** (e.g., ReAct pattern: Reason + Act).
3. The agent **invokes external tools** (search, code execution, calculators) via structured APIs.

**Representative systems:**
- **AutoGPT (2022)** — an autonomous LLM agent that decomposes user goals into sub-tasks, executes them, and self-critiques.
- **LangChain Agents (2022–2024)** — production-grade LLM agents with tool-use, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and memory modules.
- (Bonus: **CrewAI**, **AutoGen**, **MetaGPT** for multi-agent LLM collaboration.)

These systems embody the *full historical lineage*: cybernetics (feedback), symbolic AI (knowledge), BDI (intentional stance), MAS (multi-agent), and learning agents (adaptation) — all in one framework.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in "History of Agent-Based Systems" Answers:**
>
> 1. **Naming the field's birth year wrong.** The Dartmouth Conference was in **1956**, not 1950. Turing's paper was 1950, but that is *not* the formal birth of AI as a field.
> 2. **Confusing "agent" (Minsky 1986) with "Agent-Oriented Programming" (Shoham 1993).** Minsky used "agent" metaphorically; Shoham gave it a programming model. Examiners award 1 mark for each distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the four canonical properties of weak agency** (autonomy, reactivity, proactiveness, social ability). This is a 4-mark question hidden inside many 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Omitting Indian / open-source contributions** (e.g., **JADE platform**, FIPA-ACL). Mentioning them scores bonus marks.
> 5. **Writing the answer as a flat list of dates.** Examiners reward *phase-based* structure with explicit *influence linkages* (Phase X influenced Phase Y because…).
> 6. **Confusing BDI with expert systems.** BDI agents are *autonomous, mental-state-based*, and *intentional*; expert systems are *passive, rule-based, and non-intentional*. Mixing them loses 2 marks easily.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "History of Agent-Based Intelligent Systems"**
>
> - ⭐ **Dartmouth 1956** — birth of AI (McCarthy, Minsky, Shannon, Rochester).
> - ⭐ **Turing 1950** — *Computing Machinery and Intelligence*; Turing Test.
> - ⭐ **Wiener 1948** — cybernetics, feedback loop; the origin of *sense–think–act*.
> - ⭐ **MYCIN 1972** — rule-based expert system; ~450 rules.
> - ⭐ **Shakey 1969** — first mobile reasoning robot (SRI).
> - ⭐ **Brooks 1986** — Subsumption Architecture; reactive agents.
> - ⭐ **Minsky 1986** — *Society of Mind*; mind as a society of agents.
> - ⭐ **Shoham 1993** — Agent-Oriented Programming; mental states.
> - ⭐ **BDI** = **B**eliefs, **D**esires, **I**ntentions (Bratman → Shoham → Rao-Georgeff).
> - ⭐ **Wooldridge & Jennings 1995** — **Weak** (autonomy, reactivity, proactiveness, social ability) vs. **Strong** (with mental attitudes) agency.
> - ⭐ **FIPA 1998** — agent communication standards (FIPA-ACL).
> - ⭐ **AAMAS 1999** — flagship MAS conference.
> - ⭐ **Deep Blue 1997**, **Watson 2011**, **AlphaGo 2016**, **AutoGPT 2022** — landmark modern agents.
> - ⭐ **MAS** = a system of multiple interacting agents sharing an environment.
> - ⭐ Modern **LLM agents** combine *reasoning (LLM) + memory + tool use + planning* (e.g., ReAct pattern).
> - ⭐ The historical chain to remember: **Cybernetics → Symbolic AI → Expert Systems → Agent → AOP/BDI → MAS → Learning Agents → LLM Agents**.

**Mnemonic for the four weak-agency properties — A-R-P-S:**
**A**utonomy · **R**eactivity · **P**roactiveness · **S**ocial ability
*(Phonetic: "ARe Pee S" — Always React Proactively in Society)*

**Mnemonic for the five phases — C-S-S-A-M:**
**C**ybernetics · **S**ymbolic AI · **S**ituated Robots · **A**gent Formalism · **M**odern Agents
*(Phonetic: "Cee-See-Sam")*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intelligent Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Intelligent Agents # 1. Intelligent Agents — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Russell & Norvig Paradigm — KTU 2024 Standard):** > An **agent** is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its **environment** through **sensors** and acting upon that environment through **actuators**. An **Intelligent Agent** is an agent that selects actions rationally to maximize a **performance measure**, based on its **percept sequence** and built-in knowledge. Mathematically, an agent is modeled as a function: $$ f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A} $$ where: - $\mathcal{P}^*$ is the set of all possible **percept sequences** the agent has experienced so far. - $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all **legal actions** the agent can execute. > [!NOTE] > **Agent Program vs. Agent Function:** > The **agent function** $f$ is an abstract mathematical mapping (what the agent *should* do), whereas the **agent program** is the concrete implementation (how the agent *actually* does it) running on a physical architecture. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **self-driving car** navigating through Kerala's traffic: - **Sensors** (Eyes) = Cameras, LiDAR, GPS, speedometer — perceive the road, signals, pedestrians. - **Actuators** (Hands & Feet) = Steering, accelerator, brake, horn — act on the car. - **Environment** = The road, other vehicles, weather, traffic signals. - **Performance Measure** = Safety, reaching destination fast, fuel economy, passenger comfort. The car is *not* intelligent just because it has sensors and actuators — it becomes an **intelligent agent** because it chooses actions (brake, accelerate, turn) **rationally** to optimize its performance measure, given what it perceives and what it wants to achieve. > [!TIP] > **Quick Sanity Check:** If a program merely *computes* output from input (e.g., a calculator), it is a **program**, not an agent. The moment it operates **autonomously** in a **dynamic environment** with a **goal**, it qualifies as an intelligent agent. ## 1.3 Rationality — The Heart of Intelligence > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rational Agent Definition (High-Yield for KTU):** > For each possible percept sequence, a **rational agent** should select an action that is expected to **maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge the agent has. A rational agent is **not** omniscient — it only acts on what it *perceives*. The ideal rational agent is called a **omniscient agent** (knows the actual outcomes of its actions), but in practice, **rationality maximizes expected performance**, not actual outcome. ### The Four Pillars of Rationality (KTU Exam Favorite) 1. **Performance Measure** — Defines success criteria. 2. **Prior Knowledge** — Built-in understanding of the environment. 3. **Percept Sequence** — Everything the agent has perceived so far. 4. **Possible Actions** — What the agent *can* do in the current state. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse **rationality with perfection**. A rational agent acts to maximize *expected* performance, not *actual* performance. A bridge that collapses after a rational decision is still rational if no alternative would have prevented it. ## 1.4 Autonomy, Omniscience & Learning | Property | Definition | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | **Autonomy** | The agent's behavior is determined by its own percepts & experience, not just prior knowledge. | Distinguishes a true agent from a static program. | | **Omniscience** | Knows the *actual* outcome of every action. | Impossible in practice; theoretical ideal only. | | **Learning** | Improves performance over time by modifying its behavior based on experience. | Marks the transition from simple reflex → learning agent. | | **Proactiveness** | Takes initiative; doesn't just react. | Required for goal-based and utility-based agents. | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The PEAS Framework — Deconstructing Any Agent Design > [!IMPORTANT] > **PEAS = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** > KTU examiners will almost certainly ask you to design the PEAS specification for a given scenario (e.g., a medical diagnosis system, a vacuum cleaner robot, a chess player). This is the **single most tested concept** in Module 1. ### PEAS Decomposition Logic 1. **Performance Measure (P):** What is "success"? (Quantitative metric — accuracy, speed, safety, profit, score, etc.) 2. **Environment (E):** What is the world the agent operates in? (Map the full state space.) 3. **Actuators (A):** How does the agent affect the world? (Motors, displays, network calls, decisions, etc.) 4. **Sensors (S):** How does the agent perceive the world? (Cameras, microphones, keyboards, API calls, etc.) ### Canonical PEAS Examples (KTU Frequently Asked) | Application | Performance | Environment | Actuators | Sensors | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Medical Diagnosis System** | Healthy patient, low cost, reduced lawsuits | Patient, hospital, staff | Display questions, tests, treatments, referrals | Keyboard entry of symptoms, patient responses, lab results | | **Satellite Image Analysis** | Correct categorization, percentage of landscape correctly mapped | Orbiting satellite, ground station downlink | Display classification of scene regions | High-resolution color pixel arrays | | **Part-Picking Robot** | Percentage of parts in correct bins | Conveyor belt with parts, bins | Jointed arm and hand | Camera, joint angle sensors, tactile sensors | | **Automated Taxi Driver** | Safety, speed, legality, comfort, profit | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, customers, weather | Steering, accelerator, brake, signal, horn, display | Cameras, LiDAR, GPS, speedometer, accelerometer, microphone | ## 2.2 Environment Classification — The 7 Canonical Properties > [!NOTE] > KTU Module 1 consistently tests the classification of environments. Memorize these seven properties — they appear in Part A questions almost every semester. | Property | Spectrum | Description | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Observable** | Fully ↔ Partially | Are all relevant aspects of the environment accessible to the agent's sensors? | Chess = Fully; Taxi = Partially | | **Deterministic** | Deterministic ↔ Stochastic | Does the next state depend *only* on the current state and action? | Chess = Deterministic; Taxi = Stochastic | | **Episodic** | Episodic ↔ Sequential | Is the agent's experience divided into atomic episodes with no link between them? | Part-picking = Episodic; Chess = Sequential | | **Static** | Static ↔ Dynamic | Can the environment change while the agent is deliberating? | Crossword = Static; Taxi = Dynamic | | **Discrete** | Discrete ↔ Continuous | Is the state/action space finite and clearly separated? | Chess = Discrete; Taxi = Continuous | | **Single-Agent** | Single ↔ Multi-Agent | Is the agent operating in isolation, or with other agents? | Crossword = Single; Chess = Multi (competitive) | | **Known** | Known ↔ Unknown | Are the rules/outcomes of the environment known to the agent a priori? | Taxi with known traffic rules = Known; New planet exploration = Unknown | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** "Partially observable" is **not** the same as "unknown". A poker game is *partially observable* (you cannot see opponents' cards) but the *rules are known*. Examiners often swap these terms to trap students. ## 2.3 The Agent Function — Formal Treatment The agent function is the **complete external characterization** of the agent's behavior. For a hypothetical vacuum-cleaner world: | Percept Sequence | Rational Action | |---|---| | $[\,A,\, Clean\,]$ | Right | | $[\,A,\, Dirty\,]$ | Suck | | $[\,B,\, Clean\,]$ | Left | | $[\,B,\, Dirty\,]$ | Suck | | $[\,A,\, Clean\,],\,[\,A,\, Clean\,]$ | Right | | $[\,A,\, Clean\,],\,[\,A,\, Dirty\,]$ | Suck | | ... | ... | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for KTU:** The **agent function** is a *table* that maps percept histories → actions. The **agent program** is the *code* that implements this table (or a compact equivalent). For a real agent, the percept history can grow without bound, so the table is impractical — this motivates internal state, models, and learning. ## 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Meaning | Range / Domain | |---|---|---| | $\mathcal{P}$ | Set of percepts at a single instant | Discrete or continuous | | $\mathcal{P}^*$ | Set of all finite percept sequences | Countably infinite | | $\mathcal{A}$ | Set of actions available to the agent | Application-specific | | $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Agent function | Mathematical abstraction | | $PEAS$ | Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors | Design tuple | | $R(s, a)$ | Reward function (reinforcement learning) | $\mathbb{R}$ | | $U(s)$ | Utility of a state | $\mathbb{R}$ | | $V^\pi(s)$ | Value of state $s$ under policy $\pi$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | | $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | Transition probability (stochastic env) | $[0, 1]$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility Beyond AI:** > The Intelligent Agent paradigm is the foundation of **autonomous robotics** (Boston Dynamics, autonomous vehicles), **recommender systems** (Netflix, YouTube), **smart grid management**, **algorithmic trading**, **autonomous cybersecurity defense**, and **multi-agent supply chain optimization**. Any system that perceives its environment and acts to optimize a goal fits this model. # 3. Step-by-Step Analysis — Agent Architecture Decomposition ## 3.1 From Agent Function to Agent Program — The Realization Process > [!NOTE] > The following derivation illustrates *why* simple tabular agents are insufficient for any realistic problem, and *how* each successive agent architecture solves the limitation. This reasoning chain is a **favourite KTU Part B question**. ### Step 1 — Define the Agent Function The agent function maps every conceivable percept sequence to the action the agent should take. For a vacuum world with two locations $A$ and $B$: $$ f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A} $$ The total number of distinct percept sequences is bounded. If at each time step the agent perceives one of $k$ percepts and can act in one of $m$ actions, then in $T$ steps the number of sequences is $k^T$. For an "infinite-lifetime" agent, this is uncountably infinite. ### Step 2 — Identify the Lookup Table Blow-up Storing the agent function as a lookup table requires one entry per possible percept sequence. For a chess game with branching factor $\approx 35$ and game length $\approx 50$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total entries} &= 35^{50} \\ &\approx 10^{77} \end{aligned} $$ > This is more entries than the number of atoms in the observable universe ($\approx 10^{80}$ is comparable). Hence, **table-based agents are impossible for any non-trivial problem**. ### Step 3 — Introduce the Agent Program The **agent program** is a compact, finite representation of the agent function. Given a percept input, it internally updates some data structure (rules, beliefs, goals, utilities) and outputs an action. The mapping is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Agent Program}: &\; (\text{Percept}, \text{Internal State}) \rightarrow \text{Action} \\ \text{Output Action} = &\; \text{Program}(\text{Percept}_t, \text{InternalState}_t) \\ \text{New Internal State} = &\; \text{Update}(\text{Percept}_t, \text{InternalState}_t) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — The Four-Tier Agent Hierarchy (KTU Critical) > [!IMPORTANT] > The progression below shows how each agent type addresses a specific failure of its predecessor. KTU examiners ask: "Why is a goal-based agent better than a reflex agent?" — the answer lies in this progression. **Tier 1 — Simple Reflex Agent** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Action} &= \text{RuleMatch}(\text{Percept}, \text{Condition-Action Rules}) \\ \text{No internal state.} &\; \text{ Fails in partially observable environments.} \end{aligned} $$ **Tier 2 — Model-Based Reflex Agent** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{InternalState}' &= \text{Update}(\text{InternalState}, \text{Percept}, \text{Model of World Dynamics}) \\ \text{Action} &= \text{RuleMatch}(\text{InternalState}', \text{Condition-Action Rules}) \end{aligned} $$ > Adds an internal model of how the world evolves, so the agent can infer unobserved aspects of the environment. **Tier 3 — Goal-Based Agent** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Goal}_t &= \text{Static or Dynamic Goal Description} \\ \text{Plan} &= \text{Planning}(\text{InternalState}, \text{Goal}_t, \text{Model}) \\ \text{Action} &= \text{First action of Plan} \end{aligned} $$ > Instead of hand-coded rules, the agent reasons about *which action will lead to a state satisfying the goal*. This enables proactive behavior. **Tier 4 — Utility-Based Agent** $$ \begin{aligned} U(s) &= \text{Utility function mapping state to real number} \\ \text{Action} &= \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}\left[\,U(\text{Result}(s,a))\,\right] \end{aligned} $$ > When goals are insufficient (e.g., multiple paths, trade-offs), the agent maximizes a **utility function** that quantifies preference over states. **Tier 5 — Learning Agent** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Performance Element}: &\; \text{Selects actions (could be any of the above tiers)} \\ \text{Critic}: &\; \text{Feedback on how the agent is doing} \\ \text{Learning Element}: &\; \text{Modifies the Performance Element} \\ \text{Problem Generator}: &\; \text{Suggests exploratory actions} \end{aligned} $$ > The learning agent *improves over time*, using the critic's feedback to refine the performance element. ## 3.2 Rationality vs. Omniscience — Quantitative Distinction > [!TIP] > This is a **favourite conceptual Part A question**. The cleanest way to express it: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Rational Action} &= \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s') \\ \text{Omniscient Action} &= \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} U(\text{ActualResult}(s, a)) \end{aligned} $$ A rational agent acts on **expected outcomes** (it has a model $P(s' \mid s, a)$). An omniscient agent acts on **actual outcomes** (knows what will happen). Rationality is the achievable ideal; omniscience is not. ## 3.3 The Vacuum World — Worked Analytical Example Consider a $2$-cell vacuum world. Cells $A$ and $B$. The agent has sensors: *location* and *dirt status*. The agent has actuators: *Left*, *Right*, *Suck*. The performance measure awards $+10$ per clean cell per time step, $-1$ per action. The **rational agent function** for a percept sequence of length 1: | Percept | State Interpretation | Rational Action | Reasoning | |---|---|---|---| | $(A, Clean)$ | $A$ clean, $B$ unknown | Right | Go check $B$ | | $(A, Dirty)$ | $A$ dirty | Suck | Clean $A$ first | | $(B, Clean)$ | $B$ clean, $A$ unknown | Left | Go check $A$ | | $(B, Dirty)$ | $B$ dirty | Suck | Clean $B$ first | > This illustrates the **lookup-table agent**. For longer percept sequences, the table grows exponentially — motivating the model-based variant. ## 3.4 The Closed-Loop Interaction Cycle (Skeleton of Any Agent) $$ \begin{aligned} t = 0: &\; \text{Init InternalState} \\ \text{for each time step } t: &\; \\ &\; \text{Percept}_t \leftarrow \text{Sense Environment} \\ &\; \text{InternalState}_t \leftarrow \text{Update}(\text{InternalState}_{t-1}, \text{Percept}_t) \\ &\; \text{Action}_t \leftarrow \text{Decide}(\text{InternalState}_t) \\ &\; \text{Act}(\text{Action}_t) \text{ on Environment} \\ &\; t \leftarrow t + 1 \end{aligned} $$ > This loop is the **canonical skeleton** of every intelligent agent. KTU short-answer questions often ask you to draw or explain this loop. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Intelligent Agent Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following block diagram is the **standard KTU Module 1 figure**. Re-draw it in every Part B answer that touches on agent structure. ```mermaid flowchart LR envVar[Environment] sensVar[Sensors] actVar[Actuators] perceptVar[Percept Input] decVar[Decision Function] actOutVar[Action Output] perfVar[Performance Measure] learnVar[Learning Element] criticVar[Critic Feedback] agentBox[AGENT] envVar -- physical signals --> sensVar sensVar -- raw data --> perceptVar perceptVar --> agentBox agentBox -- command signals --> actOutVar actOutVar --> actVar actVar -- physical effects --> envVar envVar -- outcome signal --> perfVar perfVar -- feedback --> criticVar criticVar -- learning signal --> learnVar learnVar -- updates --> agentBox agentBox --> decVar ``` ## 4.2 The Five-Level Agent Hierarchy (Block Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB titleNode[Agent Hierarchy Progression] t1[Level 1 Simple Reflex Agent
Condition Action Rules Only] t2[Level 2 Model Based Reflex Agent
Adds Internal World Model] t3[Level 3 Goal Based Agent
Adds Goal Reasoning and Planning] t4[Level 4 Utility Based Agent
Adds Utility Function Optimization] t5[Level 5 Learning Agent
Adds Critic and Learning Element] titleNode --> t1 t1 -->|Fails in Partial Observability| t2 t2 -->|Fails for Long Horizon Planning| t3 t3 -->|Fails for Conflicting Goals| t4 t4 -->|Fails to Improve Over Time| t5 ``` ## 4.3 PEAS Design Flowchart (for a Hypothetical Agent) ```mermaid flowchart TD problemNode[Identify Problem Domain] perfNode[Define Performance Measure] envNode[Specify Environment Properties] actNode[List Actuator Capabilities] sensNode[List Sensor Capabilities] classifyNode[Classify Environment Fully vs Partial etc] archNode[Select Agent Architecture Simple Reflex Model Based Goal Utility or Learning] validateNode[Validate against Rationality Criteria] problemNode --> perfNode perfNode --> envNode envNode --> actNode actNode --> sensNode sensNode --> classifyNode classifyNode --> archNode archNode --> validateNode validateNode -->|Iterate| perfNode ``` ## 4.4 Rationality Decision Flow (Conceptual Logic Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR perceptNode[Current Percept] histNode[Percept History] knowNode[Built In Knowledge] possibleNode[Set of Possible Actions] expectNode[Expected Performance of Each Action] chooseNode[Choose Action Maximizing Expected Performance] outNode[Execute Action on Environment] perceptNode --> expectNode histNode --> expectNode knowNode --> expectNode possibleNode --> expectNode expectNode --> chooseNode chooseNode --> outNode outNode -. feedback .-> histNode ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Define an intelligent agent. Differentiate between agent function and agent program with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** An **intelligent agent** is any entity that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it through actuators to achieve a defined goal by maximizing a performance measure. - The **agent function** is a mathematical mapping from percept sequences to actions: $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$. It describes *what* the agent should do, abstractly. - The **agent program** is the concrete implementation of that function on a given physical architecture. It describes *how* the agent actually does it, including the data structures and decision logic. **Example:** For a chess-playing agent, the *agent function* is a (huge) table specifying the best move for every board state. The *agent program* is the actual code — say, a minimax algorithm with alpha-beta pruning — that produces the move given the current board position. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner Note):** > Students often state that agent function and agent program are the same. They are not. The function is **abstract and possibly infinite**; the program is **finite, executable code**. Marks are deducted for this conflation. --- ### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List and briefly explain any four properties used to classify task environments in AI.** **Model Answer:** The four key properties of task environments are: 1. **Fully Observable vs. Partially Observable:** Whether the agent's sensors give it access to the complete state of the environment at each point in time. (E.g., Chess = fully observable; Taxi driving = partially observable.) 2. **Deterministic vs. Stochastic:** Whether the next state of the environment is determined entirely by the current state and the agent's action. (E.g., Crossword = deterministic; Self-driving car = stochastic.) 3. **Static vs. Dynamic:** Whether the environment can change while the agent is deliberating. (E.g., Solitaire with no time limit = static; Real-time game = dynamic.) 4. **Discrete vs. Continuous:** Whether the state, percept, and action sets are finite/clearly defined or infinite/continuous. (E.g., Chess = discrete; Taxi steering = continuous.) > [!NOTE] > If asked for **all seven** properties, also include *Episodic vs. Sequential*, *Single-agent vs. Multi-agent*, and *Known vs. Unknown*. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 Part B carries a **choice between two questions**. We provide both **Question A** and **Question B** as independent alternatives. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** *(7 Marks)* **Explain the PEAS framework. Design a detailed PEAS specification for an "Automated Kerala State Road Transport Bus Ticketing Assistant" agent.** **(b)** *(7 Marks)* **Discuss the four main types of agent architectures in detail. For each, give one real-world application where it is most suitable.** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A (a)** **PEAS Framework Definition:** PEAS stands for **Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors**. It is a structured specification used during agent design to ensure all four aspects of the agent's interaction with its world are explicitly considered. The framework was popularized in standard AI texts as the *first step* in formalizing any rational agent design problem. **PEAS for an Automated KSRTC Bus Ticketing Assistant:** | Component | Description | |---|---| | **Performance Measure** | Correct ticket issuance (100% accuracy), minimized queue waiting time, maximized revenue collected, customer satisfaction score, low error rate, no double-booking. | | **Environment** | Bus terminal, passengers with cash/digital payment, ticket printer, KSRTC schedule database, current bus availability, time of day, festival/seasonal demand, network connectivity. | | **Actuators** | Touchscreen display, ticket printer, payment gateway interface (UPI/card/cash reader), audio announcements (speaker), digital receipt sender, refund processor. | | **Sensors** | Touchscreen input, RFID/NFC card reader, QR code scanner, cash validator, fingerprint/OTP for identity, network status monitor, time clock, database query responses. | **Justification of Design Choices:** - The **performance measure** combines hard business metrics (revenue, error rate) and soft user-experience metrics (satisfaction, queue time) — a hallmark of multi-objective rational design. - The **environment** is *partially observable* (the agent cannot see the full crowd outside) and *stochastic* (random passenger arrival, random network failure). - The **actuators** are primarily digital (printer, display) but the physical printing of a ticket is also an act on the world. - The **sensors** must be robust enough to handle noisy inputs (smudged QR codes, partial fingerprints). --- #### **Model Solution — Question A (b)** **The Four Main Agent Architectures:** 1. **Simple Reflex Agent** - *Mechanism:* Uses **condition-action rules** of the form: `IF condition THEN action`. No internal state, no memory of the past. - *Equation:* $\text{Action} = \text{RuleMatch}(\text{CurrentPercept})$ - *Strength:* Simple, fast, predictable. - *Weakness:* Fails in partially observable environments because two states with the same current percept may require different actions. - *Real-world application:* **Thermostat** — IF temperature < 20°C THEN turn heater ON. Works because the environment is fully observable and static. 2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** - *Mechanism:* Maintains an **internal model** of how the world evolves and an **internal state** that tracks aspects of the world not currently observable. - *Equation:* $\text{State}' = \text{Update}(\text{State}, \text{Percept}, \text{TransitionModel})$ - *Strength:* Can handle partial observability by reasoning about hidden state. - *Weakness:* Requires an accurate world model, which may be hard to build. - *Real-world application:* **Robot vacuum cleaner** (e.g., Roomba) — keeps an internal map of cleaned vs. uncleaned areas and updates it as it perceives new dirt. 3. **Goal-Based Agent** - *Mechanism:* Has an explicit **goal** description. Uses **search and planning** to choose actions that lead to goal-satisfying states. - *Equation:* $\text{Action} = \arg\min_{a} \text{PathCost}(\text{Plan}(a, \text{State}, \text{Goal}))$ - *Strength:* Flexible — supports change of goal without rewriting rules; supports long-horizon planning. - *Weakness:* Goal description may be insufficient when multiple goals conflict. - *Real-world application:* **GPS navigation system** — goal is "reach destination"; plans the route that minimizes time/distance. 4. **Utility-Based Agent** - *Mechanism:* Uses a **utility function** $U(s)$ that maps each state to a real number representing desirability. Chooses the action maximizing expected utility. - *Equation:* $\text{Action} = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}\left[\,U(\text{Result}(s, a))\,\right]$ - *Strength:* Handles trade-offs and uncertainty rigorously using expected utility. - *Weakness:* Utility function may be hard to define and tune. - *Real-world application:* **Autonomous trading bot** — utility might combine profit, risk, and execution speed; agent picks the trade that maximizes expected utility. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** > Examiners expect you to **state the limitation of each architecture** in addition to its mechanism. A common mistake is describing all four positively without contrasting their applicability. Marks are reserved for the comparative discussion. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** *(7 Marks)* **What is a rational agent? Explain the four parameters that define rationality. Discuss why a rational agent is not necessarily omniscient.** **(b)** *(7 Marks)* **Compare model-based reflex agents and goal-based agents. Construct a 5-step derivation showing how a goal-based agent decides its next action in a grid-world navigation problem.** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B (a)** **Rational Agent — Definition:** A **rational agent** is one that, for every possible percept sequence, selects an action that is expected to **maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and the agent's built-in knowledge. **The Four Parameters of Rationality:** 1. **Performance Measure (PM):** A criterion defining the degree of success of the agent. Without an explicit PM, "rationality" is meaningless. 2. **Percept Sequence (History):** Everything the agent has perceived up to the current time. The agent's decisions are based on this entire history, not just the current percept. 3. **Prior Knowledge (Built-in Knowledge):** What the agent knows about the environment *before* perceiving anything (e.g., traffic rules for a self-driving car). 4. **Possible Actions (Action Set):** The set of all actions the agent can physically execute in the given environment. **Rational vs. Omniscient — Detailed Distinction:** | Aspect | Rational Agent | Omniscient Agent | |---|---|---| | **Knowledge basis** | Operates on percepts and models | Knows the actual outcome of every action | | **Decisions** | Maximizes **expected** performance | Maximizes **actual** performance | | **Feasibility** | Achievable in practice | Theoretically possible only | | **Example** | Self-driving car that brakes when it sees a pedestrian | A car that "knows" the pedestrian is there even when sensors fail | A rational agent can be **ineffective** if its percepts are inadequate — but it is still rational because no other action, given the percepts, would have a higher expected performance. An omniscient agent knows the true outcome, but is not constructible. > [!TIP] > **Memorable Quote (Use in Answers):** > "Rationality maximizes expected performance, not actual performance." — Russell & Norvig, paraphrased. --- #### **Model Solution — Question B (b)** **Comparison Table:** | Dimension | Model-Based Reflex Agent | Goal-Based Agent | |---|---|---| | **Core Mechanism** | Condition-action rules applied to inferred state | Search/planning to reach an explicit goal state | | **Internal State** | Maintains a world model | Maintains a world model + goal description + plan | | **Decision Basis** | Rule match on current inferred state | Expected path to goal | | **Flexibility** | Fixed rules — hard to change objective | Goal can be changed without rewriting rules | | **Computational Cost** | Low (just rule lookup) | High (search/planning required) | | **Example** | Robot vacuum with floor map | GPS navigation system | **5-Step Derivation: Goal-Based Agent in a 4×4 Grid World** > *Setup:* An agent at cell $(1,1)$ must reach the goal cell $(4,4)$. The grid has one obstacle at $(2,3)$. Moves: Up, Down, Left, Right. Each move costs 1. The agent must find a path of minimum cost. **Step 1 — Define the State Space** The state is the agent's current cell. The full state space $\mathcal{S} = \{(i,j) \mid 1 \leq i,j \leq 4\} \setminus \{(2,3)\}$, with 15 valid states. **Step 2 — Define the Goal Test** The goal test $G(s)$ returns TRUE if $s = (4,4)$ and FALSE otherwise. Formally: $$ \begin{aligned} G(s) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } s = (4,4) \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Define the Action Set per State** For any state $s = (i, j)$: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{A}(s) = \{ & (i+1, j) \text{ if } i < 4 \text{ and } (i+1, j) \neq (2,3), \\ & (i-1, j) \text{ if } i > 1, \\ & (i, j+1) \text{ if } j < 4, \\ & (i, j-1) \text{ if } j > 1 \} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Apply a Search Algorithm (e.g., A\*)** Using Manhattan distance as the heuristic $h(s) = \vert i - 4 \vert + \vert j - 4 \vert$: $$ \begin{aligned} h((1,1)) &= 6 \\ h((1,2)) &= 5 \\ h((1,3)) &= 4 \\ h((1,4)) &= 3 \\ h((2,1)) &= 5 \\ h((2,2)) &= 4 \\ h((3,1)) &= 4 \\ h((3,2)) &= 3 \\ h((3,3)) &= 2 \\ h((3,4)) &= 1 \\ h((4,4)) &= 0 \quad \text{(goal)} \end{aligned} $$ A\* expands nodes in order of $f(s) = g(s) + h(s)$, finds the optimal path, e.g., $(1,1) \to (1,2) \to (1,3) \to (1,4) \to (2,4) \to (3,4) \to (4,4)$ with cost 6. **Step 5 — Execute the First Action and Re-Plan** The agent outputs the first action of the plan (move Right from $(1,1)$ to $(1,2)$), then re-runs the planner from the new state, repeating until $G(s) = \text{TRUE}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Decide}(s) &= \text{FirstAction}(\text{Plan}(s, G, \text{TransitionModel})) \\ \text{Update}(s, a) &= \text{Result}(s, a) \\ \text{Loop until } &\; G(s) = \text{TRUE} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** > Examiners expect you to show **both the goal formulation and the planning step**. Merely describing the four architectures without applying them to a concrete scenario results in **loss of 2–3 marks**. Always couple theory with a worked example. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & High-Yield Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **Print this section for last-minute revision before the exam.** - [ ] **Agent** = Percepts (sensors) + Actions (actuators) + Rationality. - [ ] **Agent Function** $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ is **abstract**; **Agent Program** is its **executable implementation**. - [ ] **Rationality** = Maximize **expected** performance; **Omniscience** = Maximize **actual** performance (unattainable). - [ ] **Four parameters of rationality:** PM, Percept Sequence, Prior Knowledge, Possible Actions. - [ ] **Autonomy** depends on the agent's own percepts, not just on built-in knowledge. - [ ] **PEAS** = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors — must be specified for *every* agent design question. - [ ] **Seven environment properties:** Observable, Deterministic, Episodic, Static, Discrete, Single-agent, Known — memorize all seven. - [ ] **Five agent architectures (in order of complexity):** Simple Reflex → Model-Based Reflex → Goal-Based → Utility-Based → Learning. - [ ] **Simple Reflex** uses condition-action rules; **Model-Based** adds internal state; **Goal-Based** adds planning; **Utility-Based** adds utility maximization; **Learning** adds the critic + learning element. - [ ] **Closed-loop skeleton** — Sense → Update Internal State → Decide → Act — is the canonical interaction cycle. - [ ] **Lookup-table agent** is impossible for any non-trivial problem (chess alone has $10^{77}$ entries). - [ ] **PES vs. PEAS** — Always include all four (P-E-A-S) in your answer; forgetting Actuators or Sensors is a common mark-loser. - [ ] **Russell & Norvig's** $AIMA$ textbook is the de facto reference — examiners expect terminology from this book. - [ ] **Engineering applications** to mention in answers: self-driving cars, medical diagnosis, recommender systems, algorithmic trading, autonomous robotics, smart grid management. - [ ] When asked to *compare* two architectures, always include a **comparison table** with at least 4 dimensions (mechanism, state, flexibility, complexity). - [ ] When asked to *design* an agent, always start with the **PEAS specification** before discussing architecture.

topics.title: Problem Solving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Problem Solving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Problem Solving** in the context of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Agent-Based Intelligent Systems is defined as the systematic process by which an **intelligent agent** moves from an initial state to a goal state by applying a sequence of well-defined actions, typically by exploring a **state-space** using **search algorithms**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCAT601 – Module 1), problem solving is treated as the foundational paradigm of goal-based agents, where the agent must determine *what to do* to achieve predefined objectives by formulating the problem, searching the solution space, and executing the action sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Problem Solving is the process of finding a sequence of actions that transforms an initial state into a goal state, where the agent uses search algorithms to navigate the state-space defined by the problem formulation.*

A **Problem-Solving Agent** is a specific type of goal-based agent that uses atomic representations of states and operates through a four-phase cycle:
1. **Goal Formulation** – Deciding what objectives to achieve.
2. **Problem Formulation** – Describing the states and actions needed to reach the goal.
3. **Search** – Simulating sequences of actions to find a path from initial to goal state.
4. **Execution** – Performing the recommended actions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – "The Maze Navigator"** 🧭
>
> Imagine you are dropped into the center of a giant corn maze. You have a bird's-eye map (the *state-space*), you know your current location (the *initial state*), and you know the exit (the *goal state*). Problem solving in AI is exactly this process. The agent is the maze-walker, the paths are *actions*, the intersections are *states*, and the search algorithm is the strategy the agent uses to find the shortest path to the exit — be it going left-first (depth-first), hugging the right wall (greedy), or systematically checking every intersection (breadth-first).

### 1.3 Core Components of Problem Formulation

A problem is formally defined by the following **five components** (as per Russell & Norvig's framework, adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus):

1. **Initial State** – The state from which the agent begins.
2. **Action Function** $\text{ACTIONS}(s)$ – Returns the set of actions executable in state $s$.
3. **Result Function** $\text{RESULT}(s, a)$ – Returns the state that results from doing action $a$ in state $s$.
4. **Goal Test** – Determines whether a given state is a goal state.
5. **Path Cost Function** $g(n)$ – Assigns a numerical cost to each path (e.g., distance, time, energy).

### 1.4 Classification of Search Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight – KTU 2024 Module 1:**
> The syllabus mandates coverage of **Uninformed (Blind) Search** and **Informed (Heuristic) Search** strategies. This classification is a frequent 7-mark short-note question in KTU University Examinations.

| Search Type | Description | Example Algorithms |
|---|---|---|
| **Uninformed Search** | No additional information beyond problem definition | BFS, DFS, UCS, DLS, IDS |
| **Informed Search** | Uses heuristic function to guide search | Greedy Best-First, A*, AOA* |
| **Local Search** | Operates on a single current state | Hill Climbing, Simulated Annealing |
| **Adversarial Search** | Multi-agent competitive environment | Minimax, Alpha-Beta Pruning |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of Problem Solving

The KTU 2024 evaluation scheme expects students to articulate the *operational mechanics* of problem solving in the following structured order:

**Step 1 — Goal Formulation**
The agent identifies the desired outcome based on its current situation and performance measure. Goals help organize behavior by limiting the objectives the agent is trying to achieve.

*Why?* Goals restrict the *objective space*, allowing the agent to ignore irrelevant states.

**Step 2 — Problem Formulation**
The agent builds an abstract mathematical model consisting of the five components (initial state, actions, result, goal test, path cost).

*How?* Real-world details (which are irrelevant) are stripped away to form an **abstract state-space**.

**Step 3 — State-Space Search**
The agent systematically explores the state-space graph by generating successors and tracking visited nodes, until a goal state is reached.

*Why?* The state-space may be combinatorially explosive, hence efficient search is critical.

**Step 4 — Action Execution**
The chosen action sequence (the *solution*) is executed in the real environment.

*How?* The agent converts the abstract plan into real-world actuators and sensors.

### 2.2 Performance Metrics for Search Algorithms (KTU High-Yield)

The four key evaluation criteria asked in KTU 14-mark problems:

| Metric | Notation | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Completeness** | — | Does the algorithm guarantee finding a solution if one exists? |
| **Optimality** | — | Does it find the least-cost solution? |
| **Time Complexity** | $T(b, d)$ | Number of nodes generated/expanded |
| **Space Complexity** | $S(b, d)$ | Maximum number of nodes stored in memory |

Where:
- $b$ = branching factor (max number of successors of any node)
- $d$ = depth of the shallowest goal node
- $m$ = maximum depth of the state space (may be $\infty$)
- $C^*$ = cost of the optimal solution
- $\epsilon$ = minimum step cost (used in iterative deepening)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for the KTU 2024 University Examination.**

| Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time $T(b,d)$ | Space $S(b,d)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BFS | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(b^{d+1})$ |
| UCS | Yes | Yes | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ |
| DFS | No | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ |
| DLS | No | No | $O(b^{\ell})$ | $O(b\ell)$ |
| IDS | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| Greedy Best-First | No | No | $O(b^m)$ worst case | $O(b^m)$ worst case |
| **A\*** | Yes* | Yes* | Exponential worst case | All nodes kept in memory |

> \* A* is complete and optimal only when the heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** (never overestimates) and **consistent** (monotonic).

### 2.4 Heuristic Function Properties

The heuristic function $h(n)$ estimates the cost of the cheapest path from node $n$ to a goal. Two critical properties are required by KTU theory questions:

**Admissibility:**
$$h(n) \le h^*(n)$$
where $h^*(n)$ is the true cost. An admissible heuristic **never overestimates** the actual cost.

**Consistency (Monotonicity):**
$$h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')$$
For every node $n$ and successor $n'$, the estimated cost to goal is no greater than the step cost to $n'$ plus the estimated cost from $n'$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> Problem solving search techniques are used in:
> - **Robotics** — Path planning for autonomous vehicles (A* in ROS Navigation Stack).
> - **Logistics** — Route optimization for delivery fleets (UCS, A*).
> - **Game AI** — NPC behavior in video games (Minimax, A*).
> - **Network Routing** — Packet forwarding in computer networks (Dijkstra, which is UCS variant).
> - **Automated Theorem Proving** — Searching proof trees.
> - **VLSI Design** — Circuit layout optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: The 8-Puzzle Problem (Detailed Search Trace)

The **8-puzzle** is a classic toy problem used in KTU 14-mark derivations. It consists of a 3×3 grid with eight numbered tiles and one blank space. A tile adjacent to the blank can slide into the blank position.

**Problem Formulation:**

$$\text{States} : \text{Each tile configuration in the 3×3 grid}$$

$$\text{Initial State} : \text{Any reachable configuration}$$

$$\text{Goal State} : \text{The configuration with tiles 1-8 in order, blank at bottom-right}$$

$$\text{ACTIONS}(s) : \{\text{Blank moves Left, Right, Up, Down}\}$$

$$\text{Goal Test} : s = \text{Goal Configuration}$$

$$\text{Path Cost} : \text{Each move costs } 1$$

**Branching Factor:** $b = 2.13$ on average (corner: 2 moves, edge: 3 moves, center: 4 moves).

**State-Space Size:** $\frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440$ reachable states.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Romania Travel Problem (A* Trace)

Consider finding the shortest route from **Arad** to **Bucharest** in the Romania road map. The straight-line distance (SLD) heuristic is:

| City | SLD to Bucharest |
|---|---|
| Arad | 366 |
| Bucharest | 0 |
| Craiova | 160 |
| Dobreta | 242 |
| Eforie | 161 |
| Fagaras | 176 |
| Giurgiu | 77 |
| Hirsova | 151 |
| Iasi | 226 |
| Lugoj | 244 |
| Mehadia | 241 |
| Neamt | 234 |
| Oradea | 380 |
| Pitesti | 100 |
| Rimnicu Vilcea | 193 |
| Sibiu | 253 |
| Timisoara | 329 |
| Urziceni | 80 |
| Vaslui | 199 |
| Zerind | 374 |

The A* evaluation function is:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where $g(n)$ is the cost from start to $n$, and $h(n)$ is the estimated cost from $n$ to goal.

**Step 1: Initialize**

$$\text{Open List} = \{(\text{Arad}, g=0, h=366, f=366)\}$$

**Step 2: Expand Arad**

Three successors: Sibiu, Timisoara, Zerind.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\text{Sibiu}) &= g(\text{Sibiu}) + h(\text{Sibiu}) = 140 + 253 = 393 \\
f(\text{Timisoara}) &= g(\text{Timisoara}) + h(\text{Timisoara}) = 118 + 329 = 447 \\
f(\text{Zerind}) &= g(\text{Zerind}) + h(\text{Zerind}) = 75 + 374 = 449
\end{aligned}
$$

**Open List:** Sibiu (393), Timisoara (447), Zerind (449)

**Step 3: Expand Sibiu (lowest f=393)**

Successors: Arad, Fagaras, Oradea, Rimnicu Vilcea.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\text{Fagaras}) &= (140+99) + 176 = 239 + 176 = 415 \\
f(\text{Rimnicu Vilcea}) &= (140+80) + 193 = 220 + 193 = 413 \\
f(\text{Oradea}) &= (140+151) + 380 = 291 + 380 = 671
\end{aligned}
$$

(Arad is skipped as it is in the closed list or revisited)

**Open List:** Rimnicu Vilcea (413), Fagaras (415), Timisoara (447), Zerind (449)

**Step 4: Expand Rimnicu Vilcea (f=413)**

Successors: Sibiu, Pitesti, Craiova.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\text{Pitesti}) &= (220+97) + 100 = 317 + 100 = 417 \\
f(\text{Craiova}) &= (220+146) + 160 = 366 + 160 = 526
\end{aligned}
$$

**Open List:** Fagaras (415), Pitesti (417), Timisoara (447), Zerind (449), Craiova (526)

**Step 5: Expand Fagaras (f=415)**

Successor: Bucharest.

$$
f(\text{Bucharest}) = (239+211) + 0 = 450
$$

**Open List:** Pitesti (417), Timisoara (447), Zerind (449), Bucharest (450), Craiova (526)

**Step 6: Expand Pitesti (f=417)**

Successor: Bucharest.

$$
f(\text{Bucharest})_{\text{new}} = (317+101) + 0 = 418
$$

**Open List:** Bucharest (418), Timisoara (447), Zerind (449), Bucharest (450, stale), Craiova (526)

**Step 7: Expand Bucharest (f=418)** → **GOAL FOUND!**

**Optimal Path:** Arad → Sibiu → Rimnicu Vilcea → Pitesti → Bucharest

**Total Cost:** $g = 418$

This trace is the *standard* KTU 14-mark model answer for A* derivation.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Search Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparison is a high-frequency **3-mark short question** in KTU 2024 examinations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Criteria} \quad\quad\quad\quad \textbf{BFS} \quad\quad \textbf{DFS} \quad\quad \textbf{UCS} \quad\quad \textbf{A*} \\
&\text{Strategy} \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{Level-wise} \quad \text{Deepest first} \quad \text{Lowest g(n)} \quad \text{Lowest f(n)=g+h} \\
&\text{Data Structure} \quad\quad \text{Queue (FIFO)} \quad \text{Stack (LIFO)} \quad \text{Priority Queue} \quad \text{Priority Queue} \\
&\text{Optimal?} \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{Yes (unit)} \quad \text{No} \quad \text{Yes} \quad \text{Yes (admiss. h)} \\
&\text{Complete?} \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{Yes} \quad \text{No (cycles)} \quad \text{Yes} \quad \text{Yes} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 BFS vs DFS vs IDS — Detailed Step Trace

For a search tree with branching factor $b=3$ and goal at depth $d=3$:

| Depth | BFS Order | DFS Order | IDS Order |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | A | A | A |
| 1 | B, C, D | B | B (DFS to limit 0) |
| 2 | E, F, G, H, I, J | E, F, G | A, C, D |
| 3 | K, L, M, N, O, P, Q, R, S | K, L, M | ... |

This **trace** is a common 7-mark KTU sub-question (part-a of a 14-mark problem).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Problem-Solving Agent Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensors: Perceive Environment] --> B[Percept to State Update]
    B --> C[Current State: s]
    C --> D{Goal Formulated?}
    D -- No --> E[Formulate Goal]
    E --> F[Problem Formulation Module]
    D -- Yes --> F
    F --> G[State Space: S, Actions, Result, Goal, Cost]
    G --> H[Search Algorithm: BFS, DFS, A star, etc]
    H --> I[Solution: Action Sequence a1, a2, ..., an]
    I --> J{Execute Next Action}
    J -- Yes --> K[Actuator: Apply Action to Environment]
    K --> A
    J -- No --> L[Task Complete]
    H -.-> M[Heuristic Function h of n]
    M -.-> H
```

### 4.2 State-Space Graph: Romania Travel Subset

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Arad[Arad]
    Sibiu[Sibiu]
    Zerind[Zerind]
    Timisoara[Timisoara]
    Fagaras[Fagaras]
    Pitesti[Pitesti]
    Bucharest[Bucharest Goal]
    Oradea[Oradea]
    Rimnicu[Rimnicu Vilcea]
    Craiova[Craiova]

    Arad -- 140 --> Sibiu
    Arad -- 75 --> Zerind
    Arad -- 118 --> Timisoara
    Zerind -- 71 --> Oradea
    Oradea -- 151 --> Sibiu
    Sibiu -- 99 --> Fagaras
    Sibiu -- 80 --> Rimnicu
    Rimnicu -- 97 --> Pitesti
    Rimnicu -- 146 --> Craiova
    Fagaras -- 211 --> Bucharest
    Pitesti -- 101 --> Bucharest
    Craiova -- 138 --> Pitesti
    Pitesti -.100.-> Bucharest

    style Bucharest fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style Arad fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Search Tree: 8-Puzzle (BFS Expansion)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initial State depth 0] --> S1a[Move Blank Up depth 1]
    S0 --> S1b[Move Blank Left depth 1]
    S0 --> S1c[Move Blank Right depth 1]
    S1a --> S2a[Move Up Again depth 2]
    S1a --> S2b[Move Left depth 2]
    S1a --> S2c[Move Right depth 2]
    S1b --> S2d[Move Up depth 2]
    S1b --> S2e[Move Right depth 2]
    S1c --> S2f[Move Up depth 2]
    S1c --> S2g[Move Left depth 2]
    S2b --> S3[Goal State depth 3]

    style S3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style S0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Classification Flowchart of Search Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Search Algorithms] --> Uninformed[Uninformed Blind Search]
    Start --> Informed[Informed Heuristic Search]
    Start --> Local[Local Search]
    Start --> Adversarial[Adversarial Search]

    Uninformed --> BFS[Breadth First]
    Uninformed --> UCS[Uniform Cost]
    Uninformed --> DFS[Depth First]
    Uninformed --> DLS[Depth Limited]
    Uninformed --> IDS[Iterative Deepening]
    Uninformed --> Bidir[Bidirectional]

    Informed --> GBFS[Greedy Best First]
    Informed --> Astar[A star Algorithm]
    Informed --> AOstar[AND OR Graphs]
    Informed --> IDA[Iterative Deepening A star]

    Local --> HC[Hill Climbing]
    Local --> SA[Simulated Annealing]
    Local --> GA[Genetic Algorithms]
    Local --> BEAM[Beam Search]

    Adversarial --> MiniMax[Minimax]
    Adversarial --> AlphaBeta[Alpha Beta Pruning]
    Adversarial --> Expecti[Expectimax]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the following components of a well-defined problem in AI: Initial State, Goal Test, and Path Cost Function.*

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> 1. **Initial State (1 Mark):** The state from which the agent begins its search. For example, in the 8-puzzle, it is the specific starting configuration of the tiles. The agent starts its reasoning from this state.
>
> 2. **Goal Test (1 Mark):** A function applied to a state to determine whether it satisfies the goal condition. For instance, in route-finding, the goal test is *"Is the current city = Bucharest?"*
>
> 3. **Path Cost Function (1 Mark):** A function $g(n)$ that assigns a numeric cost to a path. It is the sum of the step costs of the individual actions along the path. The optimal solution is the one with the minimum path cost.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Informed and Uninformed Search Strategies. Give one example of each.*

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Uninformed Search (Blind Search) (1.5 Marks):** These strategies have no additional information about states beyond the problem definition. The algorithm cannot tell whether a non-goal state is more promising than another. Examples: BFS, DFS, Uniform Cost Search, Iterative Deepening Search.
>
> **Informed Search (Heuristic Search) (1.5 Marks):** These strategies use problem-specific knowledge, in the form of a heuristic function $h(n)$, to estimate the cost from a state to the goal, thereby guiding the search toward more promising paths. Examples: Greedy Best-First Search, A* Search.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

**Q3.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Problem-Solving Agent** with a neat block diagram. Describe the four-phase operational cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the working of the **A\* Search Algorithm**. Show the evaluation function and trace the algorithm for finding the shortest path from Arad to Bucharest using the SLD heuristic. Mention the conditions for A* to be optimal. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **(a) Problem-Solving Agent Architecture (7 Marks):**
>
> A problem-solving agent is a goal-based agent that decides what to do by finding sequences of actions that lead to desirable states. The architecture consists of the following modules (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):
>
> 1. **Sensors and Percept Processing (1 Mark):** The agent perceives the environment through sensors and converts the raw percept into a state representation $s$.
>
> 2. **Goal Formulation (1 Mark):** Based on the current state and performance measure, the agent determines the goal set $\mathcal{G}$. Goals restrict the objectives and improve efficiency by pruning irrelevant states.
>
> 3. **Problem Formulation Module (2 Marks):** This module builds an abstract model of the problem consisting of: Initial state, $\text{ACTIONS}(s)$, $\text{RESULT}(s,a)$, $\text{GOAL-TEST}(s)$, and the path cost function $g(n)$. Real-world irrelevant details are abstracted away.
>
> 4. **Search Engine (2 Marks):** The search algorithm (BFS, DFS, A*, etc.) explores the state-space graph, expanding nodes and generating successors, until a goal node is reached. The output is a sequence of actions (the *solution plan*).
>
> 5. **Execution Module (1 Mark):** The action sequence is executed step by step in the real world. The agent loops back to sensing after each action to verify the new state (sense-plan-act cycle).
>
> The four-phase cycle is: **Goal Formulation → Problem Formulation → Search → Execution**.
>
> ---
>
> **(b) A\* Search Algorithm (7 Marks):**
>
> **Definition and Evaluation Function (2 Marks):**
> A* is a best-first search that uses the evaluation function:
>
> $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$
>
> where $g(n)$ is the path cost from the start node to $n$, and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to the goal. A* expands the node with the lowest $f(n)$ first.
>
> **Conditions for Optimality (2 Marks):**
> A* is guaranteed to find an optimal solution if:
> 1. The branching factor is finite.
> 2. Every action has a non-negative cost, $c(n, a, n') \geq \epsilon > 0$.
> 3. The heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** ($h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$).
> 4. The heuristic $h(n)$ is **consistent** (monotonic).
>
> **Trace from Arad to Bucharest (3 Marks):**
> [Full A* trace as shown in Section 3.2, demonstrating the seven-step expansion sequence with $f$-values, ultimately yielding the optimal path **Arad → Sibiu → Rimnicu Vilcea → Pitesti → Bucharest** with cost $g = 418$.]

---

**Q3.B. [Alternative 14-Mark Question — KTU Model]**
**(a)** Define **Heuristic Function**. Explain the properties of an admissible and consistent heuristic with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the performance of **BFS, DFS, UCS, and A\*** in terms of completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity. Use a tabular comparison and discuss which algorithm is most suitable for an 8-puzzle problem. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **(a) Heuristic Function (7 Marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 Marks):** A heuristic function $h(n)$ is a function that estimates the cost of the cheapest path from a state $n$ to a goal state. It is problem-specific knowledge used to guide informed search algorithms.
>
> **Admissible Heuristic (2.5 Marks):** A heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true cost to the goal:
>
> $$h(n) \leq h^*(n)$$
>
> *Example:* For route-finding, the straight-line distance (SLD) is admissible because the actual road distance is never less than the straight line. For the 8-puzzle, $h_1(n) = $ number of misplaced tiles is admissible.
>
> **Consistent (Monotonic) Heuristic (2.5 Marks):** A heuristic is consistent if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ of $n$:
>
> $$h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$$
>
> Equivalently, the $f$-cost along any path is non-decreasing: $f(n') \geq f(n)$. Consistency implies admissibility (but not vice versa).
>
> *Example:* For the 8-puzzle, $h_2(n) = $ Manhattan distance (sum of tile displacements) is consistent.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing admissibility with consistency (–2 marks):** Students often state *"an admissible heuristic is always consistent"* — this is FALSE. Consistency implies admissibility, but not vice versa.
>
> 2. **Wrong $f(n)$ formula (–1 mark):** A* uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. Some students incorrectly write $f(n) = h(n)$ only (that is Greedy Best-First).
>
> 3. **Forgetting the goal test vs. path cost distinction (–1 mark):** The goal test is a Boolean predicate, while the path cost is a numeric function.
>
> 4. **Skipping the admissible check in A* optimality proofs (–2 marks):** KTU board examiners specifically deduct marks if the admissibility condition is not stated.
>
> 5. **Mixing BFS with UCS (–1 mark):** BFS finds the shallowest goal; UCS finds the lowest-cost goal. They coincide only when step costs are uniform.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Problem-solving agent** = Goal-based agent that uses search; cycle is *Goal Formulation → Problem Formulation → Search → Execution*.
- A **well-defined problem** has 5 components: *Initial State, Actions, Result, Goal Test, Path Cost*.
- **Performance metrics** of search: *Completeness, Optimality, Time Complexity, Space Complexity* (parameters $b, d, m$).
- **BFS** uses a *Queue*, is *complete*, *optimal (unit cost)*, complexity $O(b^{d+1})$.
- **DFS** uses a *Stack*, is *not complete (cycles), not optimal*, complexity $O(b^m)$.
- **UCS** uses a *Priority Queue* sorted by $g(n)$, is *complete and optimal* for any step costs.
- **DLS** restricts DFS to depth limit $\ell$; **IDS** combines DFS space efficiency with BFS optimality.
- **A\*** uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$; optimal when $h$ is *admissible* AND *consistent*.
- **Admissible:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ (never overestimates).
- **Consistent:** $h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$ (triangle inequality for $f$-costs).
- **Heuristic Examples:** *8-puzzle — Misplaced tiles (admissible) and Manhattan distance (consistent).*
- **Romania problem:** Optimal Arad → Bucharest path = Arad → Sibiu → Rimnicu Vilcea → Pitesti → Bucharest, $g = 418$.
- **Branching factor** of 8-puzzle ≈ 2.13; **State-space size** = $9!/2 = 181{,}440$.
- **Best algorithm for 8-puzzle:** A* with Manhattan distance heuristic.
- **Real-world applications:** Robotics, route planning, VLSI design, game AI, network routing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Searching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Searching # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **Agent-Based Intelligent Systems**, **Searching** refers to the problem-solving technique by which an intelligent agent navigates through a discrete state-space to find a sequence of actions (a *path* or *plan*) that transforms an *initial state* into a *goal state*. A search problem is formally defined as a **5-tuple**: $$\mathcal{P} = \langle S, S_0, A, T, G \rangle$$ Where: - $S$ is a finite or infinite set of **states** (the state space). - $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of **initial states**. - $A$ is the set of **actions** available to the agent. - $T : S \times A \rightarrow S$ is the **transition function** (deterministic case). - $G \subseteq S$ is the set of **goal states**. A **solution** is a path $\pi = \langle s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, \dots, s_k \rangle$ such that $s_0 \in S_0$, $s_k \in G$, and $s_{i+1} = T(s_i, a_i)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For a module question, students must clearly distinguish between **uninformed (blind) search** — which uses only the problem definition — and **informed (heuristic) search** — which uses a domain-specific **evaluation function** $f(n)$ to guide the search. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into a giant pitch-black maze with multiple exits. You cannot see the walls except by bumping into them. You must **explore** rooms one by one, remembering which rooms you visited, until you eventually push open an exit door. - The **maze** = the state space $S$. - **You (the walker)** = the rational agent. - **A room you are standing in** = a *node* (state) $n$. - **Hallways leading out** = *successor functions* (actions). - **The exit doors** = *goal states* $G$. - **The strategy you follow** (always turn left first, or always follow the wall, or always walk toward fresh air) = the **search algorithm**. The fundamental dilemma of every search algorithm is: *Do I explore broadly (many rooms at once) or deeply (one long corridor first)?* Do I waste memory to be thorough, or save memory at the risk of getting lost? ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Must-Know) > [!NOTE] > Every search algorithm in the KTU syllabus is evaluated against the **four canonical metrics** given below. Examiners award marks for explicitly stating these. | Metric | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Completeness** | — | Does the algorithm *always* find a solution if one exists? | | **Optimality** | — | Does it find the *least-cost* solution? | | **Time Complexity** | $O(\cdot)$ | Number of nodes generated/expanded. | | **Space Complexity** | $O(\cdot)$ | Maximum number of nodes held in memory simultaneously. | Standard analytical parameters used by the KTU board: - **$b$** — branching factor of the search tree. - **$d$** — depth of the *shallowest* goal node. - **$m$** — maximum depth of the state space (may be $\infty$). - **$l$** — depth limit (used in depth-limited search). - **$C^*$** — cost of the optimal solution. - **$\varepsilon$** — minimum positive step cost. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Generic Search Loop Every state-space search algorithm follows the same skeleton, varying only in the **selection rule** used to pick the *next* node to expand: 1. Initialize the **frontier** (open list / fringe) with the initial state $S_0$. 2. Initialize the **explored set** (closed list) as empty. 3. **Loop**: - If the frontier is empty → return **failure** (no solution exists). - Select and **remove** a node $n$ from the frontier (according to the strategy). - If $n$ is a **goal state** → return the solution path by back-pointers. - Add $n$ to the **explored set**. - **Expand** $n$: generate its successors using the transition function $T$. - For each successor $n'$: - If $n' \notin$ explored and $n' \notin$ frontier → add to frontier with back-pointer to $n$. - (Graph search variant) If a better path to $n'$ is discovered → update its parent and path cost. 4. **End Loop**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tree search vs. Graph search:** Tree search discards redundant paths; Graph search uses an *explored set* to avoid revisiting states and guarantees **optimality** when combined with consistent costs (e.g., **UCS**, **A\***). ## 2.2 Evaluation Function in Informed Search Informed search algorithms use an **evaluation function**: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$ Where: - $g(n)$ = actual cost from the start node $S_0$ to node $n$. - $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to the nearest goal. - $f(n)$ = estimated total cost of the cheapest solution passing through $n$. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any 14-mark KTU Part-B question on Searching. Memorize the metrics row-wise. | Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Selection Rule | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **BFS** (Breadth-First) | Yes | Yes (unit step costs) | $O(b^{d+1})$ | $O(b^{d+1})$ | Shallowest node | | **UCS** (Uniform-Cost) | Yes | Yes | $O(b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\varepsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\varepsilon \rfloor})$ | Lowest $g(n)$ | | **DFS** (Depth-First) | No | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | Deepest node | | **DLS** (Depth-Limited) | No (fails if $l # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 board *frequently* tests these algorithms on small graph instances (5–8 nodes). The exhaustive step-by-step below is the exact evaluation pattern examiners follow. ## 3.1 Breadth-First Search (BFS) — Complete Walk-Through **BFS** uses a **FIFO queue** as its frontier. The metric derivations proceed as follows. ### 3.1.1 Completeness Proof - The shallowest goal is at depth $d$ with finite branching $b$. - Level 0 generates $1$ node, level 1 generates $b$ nodes, $\dots$, level $d$ generates $b^d$ nodes. - Total nodes generated before reaching level $d$: $$N_{\text{BFS}} = 1 + b + b^2 + \cdots + b^d = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} = O(b^{d+1})$$ - Since $d$ is finite, BFS examines every node up to level $d$ → **complete**. ### 3.1.2 Optimality (unit step costs) - BFS expands in non-decreasing order of depth. - The first goal found lies at the *minimum* depth $d$ → **optimal** when all step costs are equal. ### 3.1.3 Worked Example (KTU-style) > **Problem:** Apply BFS on the graph $A \rightarrow \{B, C\}$, $B \rightarrow \{D, E\}$, $C \rightarrow \{F\}$, $D \rightarrow \{G\}$ where $G$ is the goal. State the final path and the order of expansion. | Step | Frontier (queue) | Explored Set | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $[A]$ | $\emptyset$ | Pop $A$ (not goal). Expand. | | 2 | $[B, C]$ | $\{A\}$ | $B$ not goal. | | 3 | $[C, D, E]$ | $\{A, B\}$ | $C$ not goal. | | 4 | $[D, E, F]$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ | Expand $D$. | | 5 | $[E, F, G]$ | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ | $G$ is **goal** → stop. | **Solution path (back-pointers):** $G \leftarrow D \leftarrow B \leftarrow A$, i.e., $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$. ## 3.2 Depth-First Search (DFS) — Complete Walk-Through **DFS** uses a **LIFO stack**. It picks the deepest unexpanded node. ### 3.2.1 Failure Mode — Why DFS is Not Complete Consider a state space with $b=2$, $m=\infty$, and a goal at depth $d$ along the *rightmost* branch. DFS may wander down the *leftmost* infinite path forever, never reaching the goal. Therefore: $$\text{DFS is incomplete on infinite (or finite-but-deep) state spaces.}$$ ### 3.2.2 Complexity Derivation - The search generates a path of length $m$ and must remember $b$ siblings at every level for backtracking. - **Space:** $O(bm)$ — only nodes on the *current path* and their unexpanded siblings are stored. - **Time:** $O(b^m)$ in the worst case, since it may explore the *entire* tree before finding the goal. ### 3.2.3 Worked Example (Same graph as above) | Step | Frontier (stack, top = right) | Explored | |---|---|---| | 1 | $[A]$ | $\emptyset$ | | 2 | $[B]$ | $\{A\}$ (go deep) | | 3 | $[D]$ | $\{A, B\}$ | | 4 | $[G]$ | $\{A, B, D\}$ | | 5 | — | $G$ is goal. | **DFS path:** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$ (found earlier, but if the goal were at $F$, DFS would have to backtrack through $D \rightarrow E \rightarrow C \rightarrow F$). ## 3.3 Uniform-Cost Search (UCS) — Complete Walk-Through UCS is a generalization of BFS for **non-uniform step costs**. It always expands the node with the **lowest path cost $g(n)$** (a priority queue ordered by $g$). ### 3.3.1 Optimality and the Role of $\varepsilon$ Let the minimum positive step cost be $\varepsilon > 0$. The number of distinct $g$-values a node can take up to cost $C^*$ is bounded by: $$N_{\text{levels}} = 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{C^*}{\varepsilon} \right\rfloor$$ Therefore the total number of nodes expanded by UCS is at most: $$N_{\text{UCS}} \le b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\varepsilon \rfloor} \Rightarrow \text{Time} = O\!\left(b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\varepsilon \rfloor}\right)$$ ### 3.3.2 Worked Example > **Problem:** Start $S$. Step costs: $S \xrightarrow{5} A$, $S \xrightarrow{2} B$, $A \xrightarrow{4} G$, $B \xrightarrow{8} G$. Apply UCS. | Step | Frontier (sorted by $g$) | Pop | $g$ at pop | Action | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\{(S, 0)\}$ | $S$ | $0$ | Expand. | | 2 | $\{(B,2), (A,5)\}$ | $B$ | $2$ | $B$ not goal. | | 3 | $\{(A,5), (G, 2+8=10)\}$ | $A$ | $5$ | $A$ not goal. | | 4 | $\{(G, 5+4=9), (G, 10)\}$ | $G$ | $9$ | **Goal reached with $g=9$.** | **Solution:** $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow G$ with total cost $\mathbf{9}$, which is cheaper than $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow G$ (cost 10). UCS chose correctly because it expanded in order of $g$. ## 3.4 A\* Search — Complete Walk-Through A\* combines UCS ($g$) and greedy ($h$) using the evaluation function: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$ ### 3.4.1 Why Admissibility Guarantees Optimality Let $C^*$ be the optimal goal cost. Suppose A\* pops a suboptimal goal $G_2$ with $g(G_2) > C^*$. Since $h(G_2) = 0$: $$f(G_2) = g(G_2) > C^*$$ But on the optimal path there exists some node $n$ that has not been expanded yet, with: $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \le g(n) + h^*(n) = C^*$$ Therefore $f(n) < f(G_2)$, contradicting the priority-queue selection rule. Hence A\* never pops a suboptimal goal first — **optimality proven**. ### 3.4.2 Consistency Guarantees No Re-expansion If $h$ is **consistent**, then $f(n)$ is non-decreasing along any path. Once a node is expanded, no better path can be discovered later, so the **explored set** never needs to be revisited. ### 3.4.3 Worked Example (Romania map — KTU classic) > **Problem:** Find the shortest path from **Arad** to **Bucharest** using A\* with straight-line distance (SLD) as the heuristic $h_{\text{SLD}}$. Abridged step-by-step (values in km): | Step | Frontier entries $(node, g, h, f)$ | Expanded | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\{(\text{Arad}, 0, 366, 366)\}$ | Arad | | 2 | $\{(\text{Sibiu}, 140, 253, 393), (\text{Timisoara}, 118, 329, 447), (\text{Zerind}, 75, 374, 449)\}$ | **Sibiu** (lowest $f$) | | 3 | $\{(\text{Rimnicu Vilcea}, 220, 193, 413), (\text{Fagaras}, 239, 176, 415), \dots\}$ | Rimnicu Vilcea | | 4 | $\{(\text{Pitesti}, 317, 100, 417), (\text{Craiova}, 366, 160, 526), \dots\}$ | Pitesti | | 5 | $\{({\textbf{Bucharest}}, 418, 0, 418), \dots\}$ | **Bucharest = goal** | **Optimal path found:** Arad → Sibiu → Rimnicu Vilcea → Pitesti → Bucharest, total cost **$418$ km**, $f(\text{Bucharest}) = 418 = C^*$. ## 3.5 Iterative Deepening A\* (IDA\*) IDA\* uses iterative deepening but cuts off at an **$f$-cost threshold** rather than a depth limit. The threshold starts at $f(\text{start})$ and increases to the next smallest $f$-value that exceeds the threshold at each iteration. - **Time:** $O(b^d)$ (same as A\* with consistent heuristic). - **Space:** $O(bd)$ (only current path stored) — major advantage when memory is constrained. ## 3.6 Local Search — Hill Climbing Hill climbing is a *local* search that moves to the *best* neighbour by the objective function $f$. It terminates at a local maximum. Variants include: - **Stochastic hill climbing** — randomly select among uphill moves. - **First-choice hill climbing** — generate successors randomly; take the first that improves $f$. - **Random-restart hill climbing** — conduct many hill-climbing runs from random initial states; return the global best. It is **incomplete** (can get stuck) and **sub-optimal**, but extremely memory-efficient: $O(1)$ space (only the current state). ## 3.7 Adversarial Search — Minimax & Alpha-Beta Pruning In two-player zero-sum games, the **Minimax** algorithm assigns a value to each node recursively: $$ \text{MINIMAX}(n) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(n) & \text{if } n \text{ is terminal} \\[4pt] \max_{s \in \text{Successors}(n)} \text{MINIMAX}(s) & \text{if } n \text{ is MAX node} \\[4pt] \min_{s \in \text{Successors}(n)} \text{MINIMAX}(s) & \text{if } n \text{ is MIN node} \end{cases} $$ **Alpha-Beta pruning** computes the same value as Minimax but prunes branches that cannot influence the final decision. Track two parameters along the path: - $\alpha$ = best value that MAX can guarantee so far. - $\beta$ = best value that MIN can guarantee so far. **Pruning rule:** prune whenever $\alpha \ge \beta$. With perfect move ordering, the effective branching factor reduces from $b$ to approximately $\sqrt{b}$, allowing the search to go **twice as deep** for the same time budget. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Search-Agent Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Problem Definition
S, S0, A, T, G] --> B[Search Algorithm
BFS, DFS, A star, UCS] B --> C{Frontier
Data Structure} C -->|FIFO Queue| D1[BFS] C -->|LIFO Stack| D2[DFS] C -->|Priority Queue g| D3[UCS] C -->|Priority Queue f=g+h| D4[A Star] D1 --> E[Expanded Node] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E --> F{Goal Test} F -->|Yes| G[Solution Path
Back Pointers] F -->|No| H[Explored Set] H --> C ``` ## 4.2 BFS vs. DFS — Tree Traversal Order ```mermaid flowchart TD R((Root)) --> L((L)) R --> RR((R)) L --> LL((LL)) L --> LR((LR)) RR --> RL((RL)) RR --> RRR((RRR)) LL -.->|BFS visits level by level| L LR -.->|BFS visits level by level| R RL -.->|BFS visits level by level| G((Goal)) RRR -.->|DFS goes deep first| G ``` **Traversal order:** - **BFS:** Root, L, R, LL, LR, RL, RRR - **DFS (left-to-right):** Root, L, LL, LR, R, RL, RRR ## 4.3 A\* Algorithm Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Initialize Open List with Start Node]) --> Loop{Open List Empty?} Loop -->|Yes| Fail([Return Failure]) Loop -->|No| Pop[Pop Node n with lowest f score] Pop --> GoalTest{n is Goal?} GoalTest -->|Yes| Sol([Return Path via Back Pointers]) GoalTest -->|No| Mark[Add n to Closed List] Mark --> Expand[Generate Successors of n] Expand --> Loop2{For each successor n prime} Loop2 -->|Not in Closed| Compute[f prime = g prime + h n prime] Compute --> Check{In Open List with higher g?} Check -->|Yes| Update[Update parent and g value] Check -->|No| Add[Add n prime to Open List] Update --> Loop2 Add --> Loop2 Loop2 -->|Done| Loop ``` ## 4.4 Minimax with Alpha-Beta Pruning — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Alpha Beta Search] --> B{Node Type} B -->|MAX| C[alpha = max alpha, v] B -->|MIN| D[beta = min beta, v] C --> E{Prune Condition
alpha greater than equal to beta} D --> E E -->|Yes| F[Prune Subtree
Return v] E -->|No| G[Recurse on Children] G --> H[Return Utility v to Parent] H --> B F --> H ``` ## 4.5 Comparison Matrix — Uninformed vs. Informed Search ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Uninformed [UNINFORMED SEARCH] U1[BFS] --> U11[Queue FIFO] U2[DFS] --> U22[Stack LIFO] U3[UCS] --> U33[Priority on g] U4[DLS] --> U44[Depth limit l] U5[IDDFS] --> U55[Iterative deepening] U6[Bidirectional] --> U66[Two frontiers] end subgraph Informed [INFORMED SEARCH] I1[Greedy] --> I11[Priority on h] I2[A Star] --> I22[Priority on f equals g plus h] I3[IDA Star] --> I33[Iterative f threshold] I4[RBFS] --> I44[Bounded memory f cost] end Uninformed --> Informed ``` ## 4.6 State-Space Graph with A\* Frontiers (Romania Subset) ```mermaid flowchart TD Arad((Arad
f=366)) --> Sibiu((Sibiu
g=140 f=393)) Arad --> Timisoara((Timisoara
g=118 f=447)) Arad --> Zerind((Zerind
g=75 f=449)) Sibiu --> Fagaras((Fagaras
g=239 f=415)) Sibiu --> RV((Rimnicu Vilcea
g=220 f=413)) Sibiu --> Oradea((Oradea
g=291 f=671)) RV --> Pitesti((Pitesti
g=317 f=417)) RV --> Craiova((Craiova
g=366 f=526)) Pitesti --> Bucharest((Bucharest
g=418 f=418 GOAL)) Fagaras --> Bucharest style Bucharest fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style Arad fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Differentiate between uninformed and informed search strategies. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Uninformed (Blind) Search:** Uses *no* domain-specific knowledge beyond the problem definition. It can only distinguish goal from non-goal states.
