**Model and Interpretation (3 marks):** A FOL **model** `M = ⟨D, I⟩` consists of:
- A non-empty **domain** `D` — the set of objects in the world.
- An **interpretation function** `I` that maps:
  - Each constant `c` → an element `cᴵ ∈ D`
  - Each function symbol `f` → a concrete function `fᴵ : Dⁿ → D`
  - Each predicate symbol `P` → a concrete relation `Pᴵ ⊆ Dⁿ`

**Truth of Atomic Sentences (1.5 marks):** An atomic sentence `P(t₁, ..., tₙ)` is true in `M` iff the tuple of interpretations `(t₁ᴵ, ..., tₙᴵ)` belongs to the relation `Pᴵ`.

**Quantifier Truth (2.5 marks):**
- `∀x α` is **true** in `M` iff `α` is true for *every* variable assignment in `D` (i.e., for every object `d ∈ D`, the sentence obtained by setting `x := d` is true).
- `∃x α` is **true** in `M` iff there exists *at least one* `d ∈ D` such that setting `x := d` makes `α` true.

> **Example:** In model with `D = {1, 2, 3}` and `Greaterᴵ = {(2,1), (3,1), (3,2)}`, the sentence `∀x ∀y Greater(x, y) ⇒ x ≠ y` is true (no element is greater than itself).

#### Part (b) — PNF and CNF Conversion (7 marks)

**Step 1: Eliminate `⇒` (material implication):** (1 mark)

$$\forall x \, \big( \neg P(x) \;\lor\; \big( \exists y \, Q(x, y) \;\land\; \neg \exists z \, R(x, z) \big) \big)$$

> Reasoning: `A ⇒ B ≡ ¬A ∨ B`.

**Step 2: Move `¬` inward using Quantifier Negation (QN-2):** (1 mark)

`¬∃z R(x, z) ≡ ∀z ¬R(x, z)`. Substituting:

$$\forall x \, \big( \neg P(x) \;\lor\; \big( \exists y \, Q(x, y) \;\land\; \forall z \, \neg R(x, z) \big) \big)$$

**Step 3: Standardize variables apart** (rename `y` and `z` to fresh names if needed; here they are distinct):** (0.5 marks)

**Step 4: Prenex form (move all quantifiers to front):** (1 mark)

$$\forall x \, \exists y \, \forall z \, \big( \neg P(x) \;\lor\; (Q(x, y) \;\land\; \neg R(x, z)) \big)$$

> Reasoning: The quantifier order **must be preserved** when extracting — universal outside existential. The matrix (quantifier-free part) is preserved.

**Step 5: Skolemization (remove ∃):** (1.5 marks)

The `∃y` is *inside* the scope of `∀x`, so `y` depends on `x`. Replace `y` with a **Skolem function** `f(x)`:

$$\forall x \, \forall z \, \big( \neg P(x) \;\lor\; (Q(x, f(x)) \;\land\; \neg R(x, z)) \big)$$

**Step 6: Drop universal quantifiers (implicit in clause form):** (0.5 marks)

$$(\neg P(x) \lor (Q(x, f(x)) \land \neg R(x, z)))$$

**Step 7: Distribute `∨` over `∧` to get CNF (clauses):** (1.5 marks)

Applying distributivity `A ∨ (B ∧ C) ≡ (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ C)`:

- Clause 1: `¬P(x) ∨ Q(x, f(x))`
- Clause 2: `¬P(x) ∨ ¬R(x, z)`

**Final CNF (Clause Normal Form):**

$$\{\, \{\neg P(x),\; Q(x, f(x))\},\; \{\neg P(x),\; \neg R(x, z)\} \,\}$$

> **Clauses are sets of literals**, ready for input to a resolution theorem prover.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in FOL Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> 1. **Confusing `∃` with `∀` in translation:** "Someone loves John" is `∃x Loves(x, John)`, NOT `∀x Loves(x, John)`. The latter means *everyone* loves John. **Cost: up to 3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention Skolem constants/functions in EI:** When applying `∃x P(x)`, examiners expect you to (a) introduce a *new* constant `c` not in the KB, and (b) state the resulting ground fact `P(c)`. **Cost: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Confusing functions with predicates:** `FatherOf(John)` is a **function** returning a person. `Brother(John, Mary)` is a **predicate** (yes/no). Mixing these in translation loses marks. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Skipping inference step justification:** In forward-chaining proofs, you must state *which rule* (UI, EI, MP) and *on which sentence*. Writing only the final conclusion without showing steps yields **0 marks** even if the conclusion is correct.
>
> 5. **Distributing quantifiers incorrectly:** `∀x (P(x) ∨ Q(x))` is `NOT` equal to `∀x P(x) ∨ ∀x Q(x)`. The universal distributes over `∧` but NOT `∨`. Drawing this wrong loses marks in PNF/CNF questions. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Treating implication as biconditional:** `P ⇒ Q` is NOT the same as `P ⇔ Q`. The first is true when `P` is false (vacuously), but the second is false. Translating "if" as "iff" loses translation marks. **Cost: 1 mark.**
>
> 7. **Not stating the domain is non-empty:** When asked about FOL semantics, explicitly write that the domain `D` is non-empty. **Cost: 0.5–1 mark.**

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — First-Order Logic**
>
> - **Definition:** FOL extends propositional logic with variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers (`∀`, `∃`).
> - **Alphabet:** Constants, variables, function symbols, predicate symbols, connectives (`¬∧∨⇒⇔`), quantifiers, punctuation.
> - **Term:** A constant, a variable, or a function applied to terms (recursively). Represents an *object*.
> - **Atomic sentence:** A predicate applied to terms. Represents a *basic fact*.
> - **Complex sentence:** Atomic sentences combined with connectives.
> - **Quantified sentence:** `∀x α` (universal) or `∃x α` (existential).
> - **Bound vs. Free variables:** Quantified = bound; otherwise = free. KB contains only **closed** sentences.
> - **Model:** A pair `M = ⟨D, I⟩` with non-empty domain and interpretation function.
> - **Truth of `∀x α`:** True iff `α` is true for *every* element in `D`.
> - **Truth of `∃x α`:** True iff `α` is true for *at least one* element in `D`.
> - **Universal Instantiation (UI):** From `∀x α`, derive `α{v/x}` for any variable `v`.
> - **Existential Instantiation (EI):** From `∃x α`, derive `α{c/x}` where `c` is a *new* Skolem constant.
> - **Quantifier Negation:**
>   - `¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x)`
>   - `¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x)`
> - **PNF Conversion Steps:** (1) Eliminate `⇒`/`⇔`, (2) push `¬` inward (NNF), (3) standardize variables, (4) move quantifiers to front.
> - **Skolemization:** Replace each `∃x` (in PNF) with a Skolem constant; if `x` is inside a `∀`, use a Skolem function of the surrounding universals.
> - **CNF (Clausal Form):** Conjunction of disjunctions of literals — required input for resolution.
> - **Unification:** Finding substitution `θ` that makes two atoms identical; the **MGU** (Most General Unifier) is unique up to variable renaming.
> - **Resolution Rule:** From `¬P ∨ Q` and `P ∨ R`, infer `Q ∨ R`. Combined with unification, this is the basis of FOL theorem proving.
> - **KBA Link:** FOL is the *representation language* of Knowledge-Based Agents — they TELL facts and ASK queries.
> - **Decidability:** Propositional logic is decidable; FOL is **semi-decidable** (a key KTU point).
> - **Empty Domain:** Disallowed by convention in FOL.
> - **The "Propositional Catastrophe":** FOL avoids the exponential blow-up by letting one rule cover many cases.
> - **De Morgan over Quantifiers:** `¬∀x ≡ ∃x ¬` and `¬∃x ≡ ∀x ¬`.
> - **Universal Instantiation is the only way to use `∀` rules; Existential Instantiation is the only way to use `∃` rules.** Both are needed because UI is sound in both directions, but EI is only sound in one direction (you can give the witness a name, but the name doesn't generalize).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First Order Inference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** First Order Inference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **First-Order Inference (FOI)**
> *First-Order Inference* is the formal process of deriving new logical sentences (conclusions) from a given **Knowledge Base (KB)** expressed in **First-Order Logic (FOL)** by applying sound inference rules such as Modus Ponens, Universal Instantiation, Existential Instantiation, and Resolution. It enables an intelligent agent to perform **deductive reasoning** over relational, quantified knowledge and is foundational to knowledge-based agents, logic programming, and automated theorem proving.

In the KTU 2024 framework, First-Order Inference is treated as the deductive engine that empowers a rational agent to make logical entailments $\alpha \models \beta$ where both $\alpha$ (premises) and $\beta$ (conclusion) are well-formed formulas (WFFs) of FOL containing predicates, functions, variables, and quantifiers ($\forall, \exists$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Detective's Deduction

> [!NOTE] **Intuition: "Sherlock Holmes in a Library"**
> Imagine a detective (the *inference engine*) walking into a room (the *Knowledge Base*) full of case files (logical sentences). Each file contains *rules* like "All butlers are suspects" and *facts* like "Smith is a butler." The detective reads these files and **mechanically combines them** to reach new conclusions, such as "Smith is a suspect." He does not "guess" — he uses strict logical rules, much like Universal Instantiation and Modus Ponens. If the rules are valid, the conclusion is **guaranteed true** (soundness); if every true conclusion can be derived, the system is **complete**.

This analogy captures the three pillars of FOI:
1. **Knowledge Base (KB)** = the static library of facts and rules.
2. **Inference Engine** = the rule-applier (detective).
3. **Logical Entailment** = a guaranteed, mechanically derived conclusion.

---

## 1.3 Why First-Order Inference? (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Focus Point**
> Propositional logic is **declarative**, **compositional**, and has sound inference systems (truth tables, resolution). However, it **cannot represent objects or relations compactly**. First-Order Logic (FOL) overcomes this by adding:
> - **Objects** (constants like $John$, $Socrates$)
> - **Relations** (predicates like $Loves(x, y)$)
> - **Functions** (mother, father)
> - **Variables & Quantifiers** ($\forall, \exists$)

Therefore, **First-Order Inference** is the machinery that exploits this representational power to perform general-purpose reasoning.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Symbol / Notation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Constant | $A, B, John$ | Refers to a specific object |
| Variable | $x, y, z$ | Placeholder ranging over objects |
| Predicate | $P(x), Loves(x, y)$ | Denotes a relation |
| Function | $f(x), father(x)$ | Maps objects to objects |
| Universal Quantifier | $\forall$ | "For all" |
| Existential Quantifier | $\exists$ | "There exists" |
| Sentence (WFF) | $\alpha, \beta$ | A formula with no free variables |
| Knowledge Base (KB) | $\Delta$ | Set of sentences in FOL |
| Entailment | $KB \models \alpha$ | $\alpha$ logically follows from $KB$ |
| Substitution | $\theta = \lbrace x/John, y/Mary \rbrace$ | Mapping of variables to terms |

---

## 1.5 Categories of Inference in FOL

The KTU syllabus classifies First-Order Inference into the following families:

1. **Forward Chaining** — data-driven, from facts → conclusions.
2. **Backward Chaining** — goal-driven, from query → supporting facts.
3. **Resolution (Refutation)** — proof-by-contradiction using CNF.
4. **Unification** — the matching engine that binds variables to terms.

All of these are explored rigorously in the next sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: Quantifier Rules

Every FOL inference ultimately reduces quantifiers using two fundamental rules.

### A. Universal Instantiation (UI)

> [!NOTE] **Universal Instantiation Rule**
> From a universally quantified sentence, any individual may be substituted.
> $$\frac{\forall x \, P(x)}{\therefore P(c)}$$
> where $c$ is any ground term (constant) in the domain.

**Why it works:** If "ALL $x$ satisfy $P(x)$," then picking *any one specific* $c$ must also satisfy $P(c)$. This is sound.

**Why important:** It is the only way a universally quantified rule becomes a "concrete" fact usable by Modus Ponens.

### B. Existential Instantiation (EI)

> [!NOTE] **Existential Instantiation Rule**
> From an existentially quantified sentence, a *new* Skolem constant is introduced.
> $$\frac{\exists x \, P(x)}{\therefore P(c)}$$
> where $c$ is a **brand-new** constant that has not appeared before in the KB.

**Why new?** Because $\exists x \, P(x)$ only guarantees that *some* unknown element exists; we cannot reuse an existing constant as that would over-assert identity.

---

## 2.2 Modus Ponens for FOL (Generalized)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)**
> $$\frac{P_1, \; P_2, \; \dots, \; P_n, \quad (P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n \Rightarrow Q)}{\therefore Q\theta}$$
> where $\theta$ is a substitution that **unifies** the premises $P_i$ with the antecedents of the rule. GMP is the workhorse of forward and backward chaining.

**Example:**
- Rule: $\forall x \; (King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x))$
- Facts: $King(John)$, $Greedy(John)$
- Substitution: $\theta = \lbrace x/John \rbrace$
- Conclusion: $Evil(John)$

---

## 2.3 Unification — The Matching Engine

> [!IMPORTANT] **Unification Definition**
> *Unification* is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions identical. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest such substitution with no redundant bindings.

### Unification Algorithm (KTU Board Favorite)

The standard recursive unification algorithm $\text{UNIFY}(x, y)$ proceeds as follows:

| Step | Condition | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Both $x$ and $y$ are constants | Succeed if $x = y$, else fail |
| 2 | $x$ is a variable | Substitute $x$ with $y$ (return $\lbrace x/y \rbrace$) |
| 3 | $y$ is a variable | Substitute $y$ with $x$ (return $\lbrace y/x \rbrace$) |
| 4 | Both are compound: $P(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ and $Q(y_1,\dots,y_n)$ | Succeed if $P = Q$ and $n = m$, then recurse on $x_i, y_i$ |
| 5 | Otherwise | Apply **Occurs Check**: fail if $x$ occurs inside $y$ |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall**
> Forgetting the **Occurs Check** leads to infinite loops. Example: $UNIFY(x, f(x))$ — variable $x$ occurs in $f(x)$, so it must FAIL.

---

## 2.4 Forward Chaining (Data-Driven Reasoning)

> [!NOTE] **Forward Chaining Algorithm**
> A complete inference procedure for definite clauses (Horn subset of FOL). It works by:
> 1. **Instantiate** all rules whose antecedents are satisfied.
> 2. **Trigger** the rule using GMP and add the consequent to the KB.
> 3. **Repeat** until the query is found or no new facts can be added.

**Properties (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Sound**: only derives entailed facts.
- **Complete**: terminates for definite clauses (Datalog).
- **Data-driven**: useful for monitoring/agent reactions.
- **Inefficient** when the goal is known (use backward chaining instead).

---

## 2.5 Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven Reasoning)

> [!NOTE] **Backward Chaining Algorithm**
> Starts from the goal and works *backwards*, trying to find substitutions that make the goal unify with a rule's head. Each antecedent becomes a sub-goal. Recursive process; the engine of **Prolog**.

**Properties:**
- **Goal-driven** (efficient for known queries).
- **Depth-first** by default in Prolog.
- **Incomplete** for infinite loops without memoization.

---

## 2.6 Resolution — The Universal Inference Engine

> [!IMPORTANT] **Resolution Rule (FOL Version)**
> $$\frac{\ell_1 \lor \dots \lor \ell_k, \quad m_1 \lor \dots \lor m_n}{(l_1 \lor \dots \lor l_{i-1} \lor l_{i+1} \lor \dots \lor l_k \lor m_1 \lor \dots \lor m_{j-1} \lor m_{j+1} \lor \dots \lor m_n)\theta}$$
> where $\ell_i$ and $m_j$ are complementary literals ($\ell_i = \neg m_j$ or vice versa) under a unifying substitution $\theta$.

**Resolution is:**
- **Sound** ✓
- **Refutation-complete** ✓ (for FOL: if $KB \models \alpha$, then $KB \land \neg \alpha$ is unsatisfiable — resolution will find the contradiction)

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula / Rule Cheat Sheet

| Rule / Concept | Form | Key Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Universal Instantiation | $\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow P(c)$ | Sound; reduces to ground facts |
| Existential Instantiation | $\exists x \, P(x) \Rightarrow P(k)$ where $k$ = Skolem constant | Sound; introduces new symbol |
| Generalized Modus Ponens | Premises $\land$ Rule $\Rightarrow$ Conclude $Q\theta$ | Workhorse of chaining |
| Unification | Find $\theta$ such that $\alpha\theta = \beta\theta$ | MGU is unique up to renaming |
| Modus Ponens Special Case | $\frac{P \Rightarrow Q, \; P}{\therefore Q}$ | Foundation of logic programming |
| Resolution | $\frac{C_1, \; C_2}{C\theta}$ | Refutation-complete |
| Skolemization | Replace $\exists x$ with Skolem function/constant | Eliminates $\exists$ for CNF |
| Standardize Apart | Rename variables to be unique | Avoids variable capture |
| Herbrand Universe | All ground terms from KB symbols | Foundation of completeness proofs |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Expert Systems (MYCIN, DENDRAL):** Use backward chaining over rule-based medical/legal KBs.
- **Automated Theorem Proving (ACL2, Coq, Lean):** Use FOL resolution/refinement.
- **Semantic Web (RDF, OWL):** Inference engines (Pellet, HermiT) perform FOL-style reasoning over ontologies.
- **Robotics & Multi-Agent Systems:** Agents use FOL KBs to reason about world states.
- **Natural Language Understanding:** Compositional semantics are mapped to FOL and queried via inference.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Modus Ponens + UI (Classic Socrates)

> [!NOTE] **Problem Statement**
> Given the KB:
> 1. $\forall x \; (Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$
> 2. $Man(Socrates)$
>
> **Prove:** $Mortal(Socrates)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Universal Instantiation (UI) to sentence 1.**
We instantiate $x$ with the constant $Socrates$. This is valid because the rule holds for *all* $x$.

$$UI(1): \quad \forall x \, (Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x)) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Man(Socrates) \Rightarrow Mortal(Socrates)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Modus Ponens (MP) to the result of Step 1 and sentence 2.**

We now have:
- Premise A: $Man(Socrates) \Rightarrow Mortal(Socrates)$
- Premise B: $Man(Socrates)$ (the KB's atomic fact)
- Conclusion: $Mortal(Socrates)$

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

$$\therefore Mortal(Socrates) \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Why this is valid:** UI is sound; MP is sound. Composition of sound rules is sound. The conclusion is *guaranteed true* in every model of the KB.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Forward Chaining (King / Greedy / Evil)

### The Knowledge Base

| # | Sentence | English |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\forall x \; (King(x) \Rightarrow Person(x))$ | All kings are persons |
| 2 | $\forall x \; (Person(x) \Rightarrow Human(x))$ | All persons are humans |
| 3 | $\forall x \; (Human(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$ | All humans are mortal |
| 4 | $King(John)$ | John is a king |
| 5 | $\forall x \; (King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x))$ | Greedy kings are evil |
| 6 | $Greedy(John)$ | John is greedy |

### Goal
Prove $Mortal(John)$ and $Evil(John)$.

### Trace of Forward Chaining

**Iteration 1:**
- UI on (1) with $x/John$: $King(John) \Rightarrow Person(John)$.
- Since $King(John)$ is in KB, MP fires → **New Fact: $Person(John)$**.

**Iteration 2:**
- UI on (2) with $x/John$: $Person(John) \Rightarrow Human(John)$.
- MP with new fact → **New Fact: $Human(John)$**.

**Iteration 3:**
- UI on (3) with $x/John$: $Human(John) \Rightarrow Mortal(John)$.
- MP → **New Fact: $Mortal(John)$**. ✓ Goal 1 reached.

**Iteration 4:**
- UI on (5) with $x/John$: $King(John) \land Greedy(John) \Rightarrow Evil(John)$.
- Both antecedents $King(John)$ and $Greedy(John)$ are true.
- MP → **New Fact: $Evil(John)$**. ✓ Goal 2 reached.

> [!NOTE] **Observations for KTU**
> - Forward chaining is **monotonic**: once derived, facts are never retracted.
> - Order of rule triggering does not affect the final KB (commutativity).
> - Each iteration increases the set of "known facts."

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Unification (Board Examination Style)

### Problem
Find the MGU of: $p(f(a), g(y))$ and $p(x, g(b))$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compare function symbols.**
- Both expressions have predicate $p$ with arity 2. Good.
- First argument of LHS: $f(a)$; First argument of RHS: $x$. (Variable)
- Second argument of LHS: $g(y)$; Second argument of RHS: $g(b)$. (Both $g$ — match)

**Step 2 — Unify first arguments.**
- $\text{UNIFY}(f(a), x)$: $x$ is a variable that does not occur in $f(a)$ (occurs-check passes).
- Add binding: $\theta_1 = \lbrace x / f(a) \rbrace$.

**Step 3 — Apply $\theta_1$ to second arguments and unify.**
- After $\theta_1$: second args are $g(y)$ and $g(b)$.
- Unify the inner terms: $\text{UNIFY}(y, b)$.
- Add binding: $\theta_2 = \lbrace y / b \rbrace$.

**Step 4 — Compose the substitutions.**

$$\theta = \theta_1 \circ \theta_2 = \lbrace x / f(a), \; y / b \rbrace$$

**Step 5 — Verify.**
- LHS$\theta = p(f(a), g(b))$
- RHS$\theta = p(f(a), g(b))$
- ✓ Both expressions are identical. Unification succeeded.

**Most General Unifier (MGU):**
$$\theta_{\text{MGU}} = \lbrace x / f(a), \; y / b \rbrace$$

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistake:** The substitution $\lbrace x / f(a), \; y / b, \; x / f(a) \rbrace$ is the same MGU — multiple syntactic representations are equivalent. KTU expects the *simplest* form.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Resolution Refutation (Full KTU Pattern)

### The Knowledge Base
1. $\forall x \; (Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$
2. $Man(Socrates)$
3. $\forall x \; (Man(x) \Rightarrow \neg Immortal(x))$

### Goal
Prove $Mortal(Socrates)$ by refutation.

### Step 1 — Negate the Goal

$$\neg Mortal(Socrates)$$

This is the *goal sentence* added to the KB for refutation.

### Step 2 — Convert KB + Negated Goal to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)

| # | Original | CNF Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\forall x \; (Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$ | $\neg Man(x) \lor Mortal(x)$ |
| 2 | $Man(Socrates)$ | $Man(Socrates)$ |
| 3 | $\forall x \; (Man(x) \Rightarrow \neg Immortal(x))$ | $\neg Man(x) \lor \neg Immortal(x)$ |
| 4 | $\neg Mortal(Socrates)$ (negated goal) | $\neg Mortal(Socrates)$ |

### Step 3 — Apply Resolution Iterations

**Resolve 1 and 4** on $Mortal(x)$ / $Mortal(Socrates)$:
- $\theta = \lbrace x / Socrates \rbrace$
- Result: $\neg Man(Socrates)$

**Resolve the result with 2** on $Man(Socrates)$:
- $\neg Man(Socrates)$ and $Man(Socrates)$ are complementary.
- Result: **EMPTY CLAUSE** $\square$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

The derivation reached the **empty clause** (denoted $\square$), which represents a contradiction. Therefore the original negated goal is unsatisfiable, and:

$$KB \models Mortal(Socrates) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.5 Skolemization — The Substitution Function in Action

> [!NOTE] **Definition**
> *Skolemization* eliminates existential quantifiers by replacing each $\exists$-bound variable with a **Skolem function** of the surrounding universally quantified variables.

### Example Derivation

Starting sentence:
$$\forall x \exists y \; (Loves(x, y))$$

**Step 1:** Replace $\exists y$ with Skolem function $f(x)$ (function of $x$, the only enclosing universal):

$$\forall x \; (Loves(x, f(x)))$$

> **English:** "For every $x$, there exists some $y$ that $x$ loves." The new symbol $f(x)$ is an *anonymous* function — its name is irrelevant; what matters is the dependency on $x$.

If instead the sentence were $\exists y \, (Loves(John, y))$, the Skolem replacement would be a **constant** $C$ (no enclosing universals):

$$Loves(John, C)$$

> [!WARNING] **Skolem Pitfall:** $f(x)$ is dependent on $x$ because $x$ is *outside* the existential. If the existential is **outside** the universal, $f$ takes no arguments.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Inference Method

| Scenario | Best Inference Method | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Real-time agent reactions (sensors fire) | **Forward Chaining** | Data flows naturally; fast reactive rules |
| Single query: "Is X true?" | **Backward Chaining** | Goal-directed; avoids irrelevant rule firing |
| Prove mathematical theorem | **Resolution Refutation** | Complete; CNF uniform |
| Very large KB, unknown goal | **Forward Chaining** | Eventually derives all entailments |
| Logic programming (Prolog) | **Backward Chaining** | Native execution model |
| Ontology reasoning (OWL) | **Resolution + Tableau** | Refutation-complete over large KBs |
| KB is propositional (no objects) | **DPLL / CDCL** | SAT-solver style |
| KB has functions, infinite domain | **Resolution with Herbrand** | Refutation still complete |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Architecture of a First-Order Inference Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Knowledge Base KB in FOL] --> B[KB Preprocessor]
    B --> C[Convert to CNF]
    C --> D[Skolemize and Standardize Apart]
    D --> E[Clause Store]
    E --> F[Inference Engine Core]
    F --> G[Unification Module]
    G --> H[Substitution theta]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Resolution or Chaining Logic]
    I --> J{Derived New Sentence}
    J -- Yes --> K[Add to Clause Store]
    K --> F
    J -- No --> L[Empty Clause Detected]
    L --> M[Goal Proved: KB entails query]
    F --> N[Max Iterations Reached]
    N --> O[Failure: Not entailed or Timeout]
```

## 4.2 Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fact P1 in KB] --> C[Unification with Rule Antecedent]
    B[Fact P2 in KB] --> C
    X[Fact Pn in KB] --> C
    C --> D[Compute MGU theta]
    D --> E[Substitute theta into Consequent Q]
    E --> F[New Fact Q theta in KB]
```

## 4.3 Forward Chaining Iteration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Initialize KB with Facts] --> B[Select Applicable Rule]
    B --> C{All Antecedents Unified and Present}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply GMP with theta]
    D --> E[Add Consequent to KB]
    E --> F{Goal Reached}
    F -- Yes --> G[Success Exit]
    F -- No --> H{New Facts Added}
    H -- Yes --> B
    H -- No --> I[Failure Exit No New Inferences]
    C -- No --> J[Try Next Rule]
    J --> B
```

## 4.4 Backward Chaining Goal Reduction Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Goal Q] --> B[Find Rule whose Head Unifies with Q]
    B --> C[Sub-goal 1: Antecedent 1]
    B --> D[Sub-goal 2: Antecedent 2]
    B --> E[Sub-goal n: Antecedent n]
    C --> F[Recursively Expand or Match with Fact]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{All Sub-goals Satisfied}
    G -- Yes --> H[Goal Proved]
    G -- No --> I[Backtrack and Try Alternative Rule]
    I --> B
```

## 4.5 Resolution Refutation Pipeline (Full KTU Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[KB Sentences] --> B[Step 1: Eliminate Implications]
    B --> C[Step 2: Move Negations Inward]
    C --> D[Step 3: Standardize Variables Apart]
    D --> E[Step 4: Skolemize]
    E --> F[Step 5: Drop Universal Quantifiers]
    F --> G[Step 6: Distribute OR over AND to get CNF]
    G --> H[Add Negated Goal as Clause]
    H --> I[Resolution Loop with Unification]
    I --> J{Empty Clause Derived}
    J -- Yes --> K[REFUTATION SUCCESS: Goal Proved]
    J -- No --> I
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams**
> The above Mermaid diagrams are equivalent to the block-level architecture an examiner expects when the question reads "Draw the inference engine architecture" or "Show the resolution pipeline." Always include all six CNF conversion steps in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
**Define First-Order Logic (FOL) inference. With a suitable example, explain Universal Instantiation and Existential Instantiation.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

First-Order Logic (FOL) inference is the process of deriving new logical sentences (conclusions) from a Knowledge Base (KB) expressed in FOL by applying sound rules of inference such as Universal Instantiation, Existential Instantiation, and Modus Ponens.

- **Universal Instantiation (UI):** From $\forall x \, P(x)$ we may infer $P(c)$ for any constant $c$. *Example:* From $\forall x \, Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x)$ and $x/Socrates$ we get $Man(Socrates) \Rightarrow Mortal(Socrates)$.
- **Existential Instantiation (EI):** From $\exists x \, P(x)$ we may infer $P(k)$ where $k$ is a **new** Skolem constant. *Example:* From $\exists x \, Crown(x)$ we infer $Crown(C_1)$ where $C_1$ is a fresh symbol.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**What is unification? Explain the Most General Unifier (MGU) with an example.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

Unification is the algorithmic process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two FOL expressions syntactically identical. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest such substitution that commits the least — i.e., does not bind variables unnecessarily.

*Example:* Unify $Knows(John, x)$ with $Knows(y, Mary)$.
- $\theta_1 = \lbrace y/John \rbrace$
- $\theta_2 = \lbrace x/Mary \rbrace$
- MGU $\theta = \lbrace y/John, \; x/Mary \rbrace$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Rule**
> Part B questions carry **internal choice**. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Forward & Backward Chaining

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain Generalized Modus Ponens. Describe the **forward chaining** algorithm and apply it to the following KB to prove $Evil(John)$:

| Clause | Sentence |
| :--- | :--- |
| R1 | $\forall x \; (King(x) \Rightarrow Person(x))$ |
| R2 | $\forall x \; (Person(x) \Rightarrow Human(x))$ |
| R3 | $\forall x \; (Human(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$ |
| R4 | $King(John)$ |
| R5 | $\forall x \; (King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x))$ |
| R6 | $Greedy(John)$ |

**[(a) carries 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Generalized Modus Ponens (3 marks of the 7):**
GMP extends propositional Modus Ponens to FOL by allowing quantified premises and unifying substitutions:

$$\frac{P_1(a), \; P_2(b), \quad (P_1(x) \land P_2(y) \Rightarrow Q(x,y))}{Q(a,b)\theta} \quad \text{where } \theta = \lbrace x/a, \; y/b \rbrace$$

**Forward Chaining Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Initialize KB with known facts.
2. Repeat: pick a rule whose antecedents are all satisfied under some MGU $\theta$, add the instantiated consequent to the KB.
3. Stop when goal is found or no new facts emerge.

**Proof Trace (2 marks):**

| Iteration | Rule Fired | MGU | New Fact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | R1 | $\theta = \lbrace x/John \rbrace$ | $Person(John)$ |
| 2 | R2 | $\theta = \lbrace x/John \rbrace$ | $Human(John)$ |
| 3 | R3 | $\theta = \lbrace x/John \rbrace$ | $Mortal(John)$ |
| 4 | R5 | $\theta = \lbrace x/John \rbrace$ | $Evil(John)$ ✓ |

Hence $Evil(John)$ is proved by forward chaining.

---

**(b)** Describe **backward chaining** with a suitable example. Compare it with forward chaining in terms of efficiency, completeness, and typical applications.

**[(b) carries 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Backward Chaining (3 marks):**
A goal-driven strategy that starts with the query and works backwards through rules to find supporting facts. The algorithm:

1. Given goal $G$, find a rule whose **head** unifies with $G$.
2. Replace the goal with the rule's **antecedents** (with MGU applied).
3. Recursively prove each new sub-goal.
4. If a sub-goal matches a fact in the KB, succeed. Otherwise, backtrack and try a different rule.

**Example (2 marks):** To prove $Evil(John)$ backward:
- Goal: $Evil(John)$. Unifies with R5's head $Evil(x)$ via $\theta = \lbrace x/John \rbrace$.
- New sub-goals: $King(John)$ (✓ fact) and $Greedy(John)$ (✓ fact).
- Both succeed → $Evil(John)$ proved.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direction | Data → Goal | Goal → Data |
| Trigger | New facts in KB | User query |
| Efficient when | Many goals / reactive agent | Single / few specific goals |
| Application | Monitoring, production systems | Logic programming (Prolog), Q&A |
| Completeness (Horn) | Complete | Complete |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Resolution Refutation

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is the **resolution rule** in FOL? Outline the **complete algorithm** to convert any FOL sentence into Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) suitable for resolution. State **Skolemization** with an example.

**[(a) carries 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Resolution Rule (2 marks):**
For two clauses $C_1$ and $C_2$ containing complementary literals $\ell_1$ and $\neg \ell_2$ that unify with MGU $\theta$:

$$\frac{C_1, \; C_2}{\text{Res}(C_1, C_2, \theta)}$$

The result is the disjunction of both clauses with $\ell_1$ and $\neg \ell_2$ removed and $\theta$ applied.

**CNF Conversion Algorithm (3 marks):**

1. Eliminate $\Rightarrow$ using $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$.
2. Move $\neg$ inward using De Morgan and quantifier duality.
3. Standardize variables apart (rename bound variables uniquely).
4. **Skolemize** to remove $\exists$.
5. Drop universal quantifiers (implicit in CNF).
6. Convert to CNF using distributive laws: $P \lor (Q \land R) \equiv (P \lor Q) \land (P \lor R)$.

**Skolemization (2 marks):** Replace each $\exists$-bound variable with a Skolem function of the enclosing $\forall$-bound variables.
- *Example:* $\forall x \exists y \; Loves(x, y) \to \forall x \; Loves(x, f(x))$.
- *Example:* $\exists y \; Loves(John, y) \to Loves(John, C_1)$.

---

**(b)** Using **resolution refutation**, prove that $Mortal(Socrates)$ from the following KB:

1. $\forall x \; (Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$
2. $Man(Socrates)$

Show all CNF steps and the resolution derivation.

**[(b) carries 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Negate the goal (1 mark):**

$$\neg Mortal(Socrates)$$

**Step 2 — Convert KB + negated goal to CNF (3 marks):**

| # | Original | CNF |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\forall x \; (Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$ | $\neg Man(x) \lor Mortal(x)$ |
| 2 | $Man(Socrates)$ | $Man(Socrates)$ |
| 3 | $\neg Mortal(Socrates)$ (negated goal) | $\neg Mortal(Socrates)$ |

**Step 3 — Apply resolution (3 marks):**

*Resolve 1 and 3* on $Mortal(x)$ vs $Mortal(Socrates)$ with MGU $\theta = \lbrace x/Socrates \rbrace$:
$$\frac{\neg Man(x) \lor Mortal(x), \quad \neg Mortal(Socrates)}{\neg Man(Socrates)}$$

*Resolve result with 2* on $Man(Socrates)$:
$$\frac{\neg Man(Socrates), \quad Man(Socrates)}{\square}$$

**Conclusion:** Empty clause derived → contradiction found → the negated goal is unsatisfiable. Hence $KB \models Mortal(Socrates)$. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Forgetting Skolemization of $f(x)$ vs $C$**: Examiners deduct **1 mark** if you don't identify whether the existential is inside or outside a universal.
> 2. **Skipping the occurs-check**: A unification answer that says "unifies with $\theta = \lbrace x / f(x) \rbrace$" loses **1 mark** — the correct answer is **FAIL**.
> 3. **Not negating the goal before resolution**: Refutation requires $\neg Goal$ in the clause set. Forgetting this is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 4. **Variable capture in CNF**: When moving quantifiers, **always** standardize variables apart first. Skipping this step loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing $\theta$ application order**: Substitution applies *after* combining clauses, not before. Wrong order costs **1 mark**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [x] **First-Order Logic (FOL)** extends propositional logic with objects, predicates, functions, and quantifiers.
- [x] **Universal Instantiation (UI):** $\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow P(c)$ for any constant $c$.
- [x] **Existential Instantiation (EI):** $\exists x \, P(x) \Rightarrow P(k)$ where $k$ is a **new** Skolem constant.
- [x] **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** is the workhorse of FOL chaining — always applied with an MGU $\theta$.
- [x] **Unification** finds substitution $\theta$ such that $\alpha\theta = \beta\theta$; the **MGU** is unique up to variable renaming.
- [x] **Occurs Check** is mandatory in any unification algorithm; without it, infinite loops occur.
- [x] **Forward Chaining** is data-driven, sound, and complete for Horn (definite) clauses. Used in monitoring agents.
- [x] **Backward Chaining** is goal-driven, depth-first (in Prolog), and efficient for known queries.
- [x] **Resolution** is the canonical refutation-complete FOL inference — works on CNF.
- [x] **CNF Conversion has 6 steps:** $\Rightarrow$ elimination → $\neg$ inward → standardize → Skolemize → drop $\forall$ → distribute.
- [x] **Skolemization Rule:** $\exists$ inside $\forall$ → Skolem **function**; $\exists$ outside all $\forall$ → Skolem **constant**.
- [x] **Empty clause** $\square$ in resolution = contradiction = goal proved (refutation complete).
- [x] **Soundness:** derived sentences are entailed. **Completeness:** all entailed sentences can be derived.
- [x] **Standard exam example (Socrates):** UI on $\forall x \, (Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))$ + MP with $Man(Socrates)$ → $Mortal(Socrates)$.
- [x] **Resolution refutation template:** Negate goal → Add to KB → Convert all to CNF → Resolve until $\square$.
- [x] **Common Notation in KTU Answers:** $\theta$ = substitution, $\models$ = entails, $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = sentences, $\Delta$ = KB.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unification** is the algorithmic process of finding a **substitution** that makes two (or more) logical expressions (typically first-order predicate logic terms, atoms, or clauses) syntactically identical. The substitution that achieves this identity, while remaining as general as possible, is termed the **Most General Unifier (MGU)**.

In the context of **Agent-Based Intelligent Systems**, unification is the foundational engine that powers:
- **Logic Programming** (Prolog, Datalog)
- **Automated Theorem Proving** (Resolution, SLD-Resolution)
- **Knowledge Representation & Reasoning** (forward/backward chaining agents)
- **Natural Language Processing** (semantic parsing)
- **Rule-Based Expert Systems** (pattern matching against a knowledge base)

A **substitution** $\sigma$ is a finite mapping from variables to terms, formally written as:
$$\sigma = \{x_1/t_1, x_2/t_2, \ldots, x_n/t_n\}$$

where each $x_i$ is a variable and each $t_i$ is a term, with the condition that $x_i \neq t_i$ (no self-mapping) and all $x_i$ are distinct.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Jigsaw Puzzle Solver"** 🧩

Imagine you have two jigsaw puzzles side by side. You are told that the blank pieces in **Puzzle A** can be filled with the same shapes used in **Puzzle B**. Your job is to figure out *which shape* goes into *which blank space* so that, after filling, the two puzzles look **exactly identical**.

- The **blank spaces** in the puzzles are like **variables** ($X, Y, Z$).
- The **shapes you insert** are like the **terms** (`John`, `father(bob)`, `42`).
- The **filled-in pieces** form a **substitution** $\sigma$.
- The **rule "no shape can contain a copy of its own blank space"** is the **Occur Check** (a critical safety rule).

**Engineering Utility**: In an intelligent agent, unification allows the agent to decide whether a perceived event *matches* a rule in its knowledge base, enabling deductive reasoning under uncertainty.

### 1.3 The Occur Check

> [!WARNING]
> **Occur Check Failure:** A substitution $\{X/f(X)\}$ is **illegal** because it creates an infinite term $f(f(f(\ldots)))$. The unification algorithm **must reject** such substitutions to preserve termination.

$$\text{Occur}(X, t) = \text{true} \iff X \text{ appears as a sub-term inside } t$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of Unification

Unification operates on **First-Order Logic (FOL) terms**. A term is recursively defined as:
- A **variable** (e.g., $X$, `Person`)
- A **constant** (e.g., `john`, `42`, `Kerala`)
- A **function/predicate symbol** applied to terms: $f(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$

The unification process has three fundamental outcomes:

| Outcome | Condition | Implication for Agent |
|---|---|---|
| **Success (MGU found)** | A consistent substitution exists | Rule matches → trigger inference |
| **Failure** | Conflicting constants/functors | No match → try next rule |
| **Infinite Loop** | Occur check violation | Algorithm hangs → invalid KB |

### 2.2 The Unification Algorithm — Stepwise Logic

The **standard recursive unification algorithm** (Robinson, 1965) works on a set of **disagreement pairs**:

1. **Initialize:** Build a set of equation pairs $E = \{s_1 \doteq t_1, \ldots, s_n \doteq t_n\}$.
2. **Base Cases (Terminate):**
   - If $E$ is empty → **Success** (current substitution is the MGU).
   - If both terms are identical constants/variables → delete the pair.
3. **Variable Elimination:**
   - If $s$ is a variable and $t$ is a term not containing $s$ → apply $\{s/t\}$ to the rest of $E$.
4. **Structural Decomposition:**
   - If both $s$ and $t$ are compound terms $f(s_1, \ldots, s_n)$ and $g(t_1, \ldots, t_m)$:
     - If $f = g$ and $n = m$ → replace with $\{s_1 \doteq t_1, \ldots, s_n \doteq t_n\}$.
     - Otherwise → **Failure** (clash).
5. **Occur Check (Safety):**
   - Reject any substitution where the variable appears inside the term being substituted.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the high-yield formulas you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Concept | Formal Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Substitution Application | $s\sigma$ | Result of applying $\sigma$ to term $s$ |
| 2 | Composition | $\sigma \circ \theta$ | Apply $\theta$ first, then $\sigma$ to variables of $\sigma$ |
| 3 | MGU | $\text{mgu}(s, t)$ | The most general substitution unifying $s$ and $t$ |
| 4 | Occur Check | $\text{Occur}(X, t) = \top$ | True iff variable $X$ occurs in $t$ |
| 5 | Disagreement Set | $D(E)$ | Pair of subterms at first differing position |
| 6 | Idempotence of MGU | $\sigma \circ \sigma = \sigma$ | An MGU is idempotent |
| 7 | Variance | $\sigma \leq \theta$ | $\theta$ is more specific than $\sigma$ |
| 8 | Clash Condition | $f(\ldots) \doteq g(\ldots)$ when $f \neq g$ | Unification failure |

### 2.4 Composability of Substitutions (Critical for KTU)

$$\sigma \circ \theta = \{x / t\theta \mid (x/s) \in \sigma \text{ and } x\theta = x\}$$

> **Why this matters:** When chaining multiple inference steps in a logical agent, you must compose substitutions to keep the bindings consistent. KTU 2024 frequently tests this with two-step unification problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Expert Systems (MYCIN, CLIPS):** Rule antecedent unification to fire production rules.
- **Automated Planning Agents:** Unify action preconditions with world state.
- **Semantic Web / RDF:** Matching triples in ontological reasoning (e.g., OWL reasoners).
- **Compiler Design:** Type inference in Hindley–Milner systems uses unification.
- **Prolog-based BDI Agents:** Goal-driven reasoning engines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Unification (KTU Favourite)

**Problem:** Unify $p(X, f(Y), Z)$ with $p(a, f(g(b)), W)$.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1: Construct the initial equation set.**
$$E_0 = \{p(X, f(Y), Z) \doteq p(a, f(g(b)), W)\}$$

**Step 2: Apply structural decomposition (predicate symbol $p$ matches, arity 3 = 3).**
$$E_1 = \{X \doteq a,\ f(Y) \doteq f(g(b)),\ Z \doteq W\}$$

**Step 3: Solve $X \doteq a$.** Variable $X$ does not occur in $a$. Apply substitution $\sigma_1 = \{X/a\}$.
$$E_2 = \{f(Y) \doteq f(g(b)),\ Z \doteq W\}$$
$$\sigma_{\text{current}} = \{X/a\}$$

**Step 4: Solve $f(Y) \doteq f(g(b))$.** Function symbols match, decompose.
$$E_3 = \{Y \doteq g(b),\ Z \doteq W\}$$

**Step 5: Solve $Y \doteq g(b)$.** Variable $Y$ does not occur in $g(b)$. Apply $\sigma_2 = \{Y/g(b)\}$.
$$E_4 = \{Z \doteq W\}$$
$$\sigma_{\text{current}} = \{X/a,\ Y/g(b)\}$$

**Step 6: Solve $Z \doteq W$.** Both are variables. Conventionally, substitute the **left** with the **right** (or vice-versa). Apply $\sigma_3 = \{Z/W\}$.
$$E_5 = \{\}$$

**Step 7: Termination.** $E_5$ is empty → **Success!**

**Final MGU:**
$$\sigma_{\text{MGU}} = \{X/a,\ Y/g(b),\ Z/W\}$$

**Verification:** Apply $\sigma_{\text{MGU}}$ to the first term:
$$p(X, f(Y), Z)\sigma_{\text{MGU}} = p(a, f(g(b)), W) \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Occur Check Failure

**Problem:** Unify $f(X, g(X))$ with $f(g(Y), Y)$.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1: Decompose the outer $f$.**
$$\{X \doteq g(Y),\ g(X) \doteq Y\}$$

**Step 2: Solve $X \doteq g(Y)$.** Apply $\{X/g(Y)\}$.
$$\{g(g(Y)) \doteq Y\}$$
$$\sigma_1 = \{X/g(Y)\}$$

**Step 3: Attempt to solve $g(g(Y)) \doteq Y$.** Now check:
- $Y$ is a variable.
- $g(g(Y))$ is a compound term.
- **Occur Check:** Does $Y$ occur inside $g(g(Y))$? **YES.**

Therefore, applying $\{Y/g(g(Y))\}$ would create an infinite term. **Algorithm halts with FAILURE.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Standard Prolog (e.g., SWI-Prolog) historically *skipped* the occur check for performance. In an intelligent agent built for **safety-critical reasoning** (medical, aerospace), you must enable the occur check using `?- unify_with_occurs_check/2`.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Clash Failure

**Problem:** Unify $h(a, b, c)$ with $h(a, b, d)$.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1: Predicate $h$ matches, arity 3 = 3. Decompose.**
$$\{a \doteq a,\ b \doteq b,\ c \doteq d\}$$

**Step 2: First two pairs delete trivially.**

**Step 3: $c \doteq d$ — both are distinct constants → CLASH.**

$$\boxed{\text{UNIFICATION FAILS — No substitution exists.}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Composability of Substitutions

**Given:** $\sigma = \{X/f(Y), Z/W\}$ and $\theta = \{Y/a, W/b\}$. Compute $\sigma \circ \theta$.

**Step 1: Definition.**
$$\sigma \circ \theta = \{x / t\theta \mid (x/t) \in \sigma \text{ and } x\theta = x\}$$

**Step 2: Examine each pair in $\sigma$.**

For $(X/f(Y))$:
- $X\theta = X$ ✓ (since $X \notin \text{dom}(\theta)$)
- Apply $\theta$ to $f(Y)$: $f(Y)\theta = f(a)$
- New pair: $X/f(a)$

For $(Z/W)$:
- $Z\theta = Z$ ✓
- Apply $\theta$ to $W$: $W\theta = b$
- New pair: $Z/b$

**Step 3: Combine with unchanged pairs from $\theta$ applied to free variables** (those not in $\sigma$).

$$\sigma \circ \theta = \{X/f(a),\ Z/b\}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Standard Unification | E-Unification | Higher-Order Unification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variables** | First-order | Existential quantifiers | Function variables |
| **Decidability** | Decidable | Undecidable in general | Undecidable |
| **Unifier Form** | Unique MGU | May have multiple | Complex (spine form) |
| **Agent Use Case** | Prolog BDI agents | Constraint satisfaction | Symbolic math agents |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Unification Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two terms s and t] --> B[Build equation set E]
    B --> C{Is E empty?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Return accumulated substitution as MGU]
    C -- No --> E[Select first equation lhs = rhs from E]
    E --> F{both identical constants or variables?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Delete pair from E] --> C
    F -- No --> H{Is lhs a variable X and rhs is a term not containing X?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply substitution X/rhs to remaining E] --> C
    H -- No --> J{Is rhs a variable Y and lhs is a term not containing Y?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Apply substitution Y/lhs to remaining E] --> C
    J -- No --> L{Both are compound terms with same functor and arity?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Decompose into sub-term equations] --> C
    L -- No --> N[Return FAILURE - Clash]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Unification in an Agent Reasoning Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KBase[Knowledge Base]
        R1[Rule 1: If P x Y then Q x]
        R2[Rule 2: If P a Z then R Z]
        R3[Rule 3: If Q b then S]
    end
    subgraph AgentCycle[Agent Reasoning Engine]
        P[Percept / Goal Input] --> U[Unification Engine]
        U --> M{Match Found?}
        M -- Yes MGU sigma --> I[Infer New Facts]
        M -- No MGU --> F[Backtrack / Try Next Rule]
        I --> U
    end
    KBase --> U
    F --> KBase
    I --> A[Action Selection]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Substitution Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> Partial : Apply single binding
    Partial --> Extended : Compose with new substitution
    Extended --> MGU : Verify most general property
    Extended --> Failure : Occur check violation
    Extended --> Failure : Constant clash
    MGU --> Applied : Substitute into target term
    Applied --> [*]
    Failure --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — Relationships in Unification Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T[Terms] --> S[Substitutions]
    S --> C[Composition sigma compose theta]
    C --> MGU[Most General Unifier]
    MGU --> A[Algorithm: Robinson 1965]
    A --> OC[Occur Check]
    A --> DS[Disagreement Set]
    OC --> S
    A --> APP[Applications]
    APP --> AG[BDI Agents]
    APP --> PR[Prolog]
    APP --> ATP[Automated Theorem Proving]
    APP --> SW[Semantic Web]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define unification in the context of First-Order Logic. What is a Most General Unifier (MGU)?*

**Model Answer:**
Unification is the process of finding a substitution $\sigma$ that, when applied to two first-order logic terms, makes them syntactically identical. A substitution is a mapping $\sigma = \{x_1/t_1, x_2/t_2, \ldots, x_n/t_n\}$ from variables to terms. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is a unifier $\sigma_{\text{MGU}}$ such that any other unifier $\theta$ of the same pair of terms can be expressed as $\theta = \sigma_{\text{MGU}} \circ \gamma$ for some substitution $\gamma$. In other words, the MGU is the least-committed (most general) way to make two terms equal.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the Occur Check condition. Why is it important in intelligent agent design?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Occur Check** requires that when binding a variable $X$ to a term $t$, the variable $X$ must **not** appear as a sub-term inside $t$. Formally, we reject the substitution $\{X/t\}$ if $\text{Occur}(X, t) = \text{true}$. This condition is essential to prevent the construction of infinite/circular terms (e.g., $X = f(X)$), which would cause the unification algorithm—and consequently the agent's reasoning engine—to loop indefinitely. In safety-critical agent systems (aerospace, medical), skipping the occur check can lead to non-termination and system hang.

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice 1

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the unification algorithm in detail. List all possible failure conditions. (7 marks)
**(b)** Unify the following two terms, showing every step:  
`loves(X, mother(john))` and `loves(father(Y), mother(Z))`. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**
The unification algorithm (Robinson, 1965) is a recursive procedure operating on a set of term equations $E = \{s_1 \doteq t_1, \ldots\}$. The steps are:
1. If $E$ is empty, return the accumulated substitution as the MGU.
2. If $s$ and $t$ are identical constants or variables, delete the equation.
3. If $s$ is a variable not occurring in $t$, apply $\{s/t\}$ to remaining equations.
4. If $t$ is a variable not occurring in $s$, apply $\{t/s\}$ to remaining equations.
5. If $s = f(s_1, \ldots, s_n)$ and $t = g(t_1, \ldots, t_m)$ with $f = g$ and $n = m$, decompose into $\{s_i \doteq t_i\}$.
6. **Failure conditions:** (i) Clash — different functors, $f \neq g$. (ii) Arity mismatch — $n \neq m$. (iii) Constant conflict — $c_1 \neq c_2$. (iv) Occur check violation.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Decompose outer predicate `loves`.**
$$E_0 = \{X \doteq \text{father}(Y),\ \text{mother}(\text{john}) \doteq \text{mother}(Z)\}$$
$$\sigma = \{\}$$

**Step 2: Solve $X \doteq \text{father}(Y)$.** $X$ is a variable not occurring in $\text{father}(Y)$. Apply $\sigma_1 = \{X/\text{father}(Y)\}$.
$$E_1 = \{\text{mother}(\text{john}) \doteq \text{mother}(Z)\}$$
$$\sigma = \{X/\text{father}(Y)\}$$

**Step 3: Decompose `mother`.**
$$E_2 = \{\text{john} \doteq Z\}$$

**Step 4: Solve $\text{john} \doteq Z$.** $Z$ is a variable. Apply $\sigma_2 = \{Z/\text{john}\}$.
$$E_3 = \{\}$$
$$\sigma = \{X/\text{father}(Y),\ Z/\text{john}\}$$

**Step 5: Success!**
$$\boxed{\text{MGU} = \{X/\text{father}(Y),\ Z/\text{john}\}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **propagate the substitution** through the remaining equations. If you apply $\sigma_1 = \{X/\text{father}(Y)\}$ but fail to substitute $X$ elsewhere, the final MGU will be incorrect. The examiner will deduct **2 marks** for this in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice 2

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Define substitution, composition of substitutions, and the MGU. Illustrate composition with an example. (7 marks)
**(b)** Attempt to unify `p(f(X), g(Y, a))` with `p(f(g(b)), g(c, Z))`. If unification fails, state the reason. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**
A **substitution** $\sigma$ is a finite set of mappings $\{x_1/t_1, \ldots, x_n/t_n\}$ where each $x_i$ is a variable and $t_i$ is a term. The **composition** $\sigma \circ \theta$ produces a new substitution by first applying $\theta$ to the terms of $\sigma$, then merging with bindings from $\theta$ for variables not bound in $\sigma$. The **MGU** is the most general substitution that unifies two terms; it is unique up to variable renaming and is idempotent.

**Example:** Let $\sigma = \{X/f(Y), Z/a\}$ and $\theta = \{Y/b, W/c\}$.
- Apply $\theta$ to $\sigma$'s terms: $f(Y)\theta = f(b)$.
- $\sigma \circ \theta = \{X/f(b), Z/a, W/c\}$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Decompose outer $p$.**
$$E_0 = \{f(X) \doteq f(g(b)),\ g(Y, a) \doteq g(c, Z)\}$$
$$\sigma = \{\}$$

**Step 2: Decompose $f$.**
$$E_1 = \{X \doteq g(b),\ g(Y, a) \doteq g(c, Z)\}$$

**Step 3: Solve $X \doteq g(b)$.** Apply $\sigma_1 = \{X/g(b)\}$.
$$E_2 = \{g(Y, a) \doteq g(c, Z)\}$$
$$\sigma = \{X/g(b)\}$$

**Step 4: Decompose $g$.**
$$E_3 = \{Y \doteq c,\ a \doteq Z\}$$

**Step 5: Solve $Y \doteq c$.** Apply $\sigma_2 = \{Y/c\}$.
$$E_4 = \{a \doteq Z\}$$
$$\sigma = \{X/g(b),\ Y/c\}$$

**Step 6: Solve $a \doteq Z$.** $Z$ is a variable. Apply $\sigma_3 = \{Z/a\}$.
$$E_5 = \{\}$$
$$\sigma = \{X/g(b),\ Y/c,\ Z/a\}$$

**Step 7: Success.**
$$\boxed{\text{MGU} = \{X/g(b),\ Y/c,\ Z/a\}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfall:** In sub-part (a), students often confuse **composition** ($\sigma \circ \theta$) with **union** ($\sigma \cup \theta$). Union is **not** valid substitution composition because it can lead to inconsistent bindings. KTU 2024 examiners explicitly check the application of $\theta$ to $\sigma$'s term side. Up to **3 marks** are deducted for this confusion.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ⭐ **Unification** = finding a substitution $\sigma$ that makes two terms syntactically identical.
- ⭐ **MGU (Most General Unifier)** = the least-committed unifying substitution; **unique up to variable renaming**.
- ⭐ **Four Failure Conditions:** clash of functors, arity mismatch, constant conflict, **occur check violation**.
- ⭐ **Occur Check** is mandatory for terminating unification; reject $\{X/t\}$ if $X \in \text{vars}(t)$.
- ⭐ **Composition Formula:** $\sigma \circ \theta = \{x / t\theta \mid (x/t) \in \sigma, x\theta = x\}$.
- ⭐ **MGU is idempotent:** $\sigma \circ \sigma = \sigma$.
- ⭐ **Algorithm complexity:** $O(n \cdot \alpha(n))$ with union-find; $O(n^2)$ with naive lists (Corbin–Bidoit).
- ⭐ **Always propagate** a newly applied substitution to all remaining equations in $E$.
- ⭐ **Application in BDI Agents:** unification of perceived events against plan preconditions drives goal-driven behavior.
- ⭐ **Resolution Theorem Proving** in automated agents fundamentally relies on unification (SLD-resolution).
- ⭐ **Prolog quirk:** Standard Prolog omits the occur check — agents requiring soundness must use `unify_with_occurs_check`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chaining Resolution Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chaining Resolution Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Chaining — The Academic Definition

In Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KRR) for intelligent agents, **Chaining** is a class of *rule-based inference mechanisms* that derive new facts (or prove goals) by repeatedly applying logical rules of the form:

$$ \text{IF } P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n \text{ THEN } Q $$

The two canonical chaining strategies are:

- **Forward Chaining (Data-Driven Reasoning):** Start from known facts in the *Knowledge Base (KB)* and derive new conclusions until the goal is reached (or no more rules are applicable).
- **Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven Reasoning):** Start from a *goal/query* and work backward, decomposing it into sub-goals until the sub-goals match known facts in the KB.

**Resolution**, on the other hand, is a *refutation-based inference rule* that proves a theorem by showing that the negation of the goal is *unsatisfiable* when combined with the KB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every agent that performs logical reasoning — diagnosis agents, planning agents, expert systems, semantic web agents — internally uses one or a combination of these chaining/resolution strategies. Understanding **when** to use which is a high-weightage exam topic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making it Click

Imagine you are a **detective in a crime scene** 🕵️:

| Reasoning Style | Detective Analogy | Direction of Search |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Chaining** | You see a wet floor, an open window, muddy footprints → conclude "a person entered recently." | Facts → Conclusions |
| **Backward Chaining** | You *suspect* a person entered. You check: was the window open? Are there footprints? Was the floor wet? | Goal → Sub-goals → Facts |
| **Resolution** | You assume "no one entered." If this assumption causes a contradiction with the evidence, the opposite must be true. | Proof by Contradiction |

Another analogy — **medical diagnosis**:
- **Forward**: Patient has fever + cough + sore throat → conclude "likely flu."
- **Backward**: Doctor *suspects* flu → checks for fever → checks for cough → confirms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Forward chaining is best when you have *lots of data* but no specific question. Backward chaining is best when you have *a specific question* but the KB is large. Resolution is best for *automated theorem proving* and FOL (First-Order Logic) agents.

---

## 1.3 Resolution — The Formal Definition

**Resolution** is a *sound* and *complete* inference rule operating on clauses (disjunctions of literals). Given two clauses containing complementary literals, resolution produces a new clause called the **resolvent**.

For literals $L_1$ and $L_2$ such that $L_1$ and $\neg L_2$ unify with substitution $\theta$:

$$ (L_1 \lor \alpha) \quad \text{and} \quad (\neg L_2 \lor \beta) \;\Longrightarrow\; (\alpha \lor \beta)\,\theta $$

The empty clause $\square$ is derived if the resolvent is empty — this signals a **contradiction**, meaning the original goal is proved.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forward Chaining — Operational Breakdown

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize** the working memory (WM) with known facts from the KB.
2. **Match** the antecedents (IF-parts) of all rules against current WM.
3. **Fire** (execute) a rule whose antecedents are satisfied.
4. **Add** the rule's consequent (THEN-part) as a new fact to WM.
5. **Repeat** steps 2–4 until:
   - The goal fact is derived, **OR**
   - No new rule can be fired (fixed point reached).

> [!NOTE]
> Forward chaining is **data-driven** and naturally corresponds to the **RETE algorithm** used in production systems like CLIPS, Drools, and OPS5.

**Properties:**
- **Sound** ✅ — every derived fact is logically entailed by the KB.
- **Complete for Horn clauses** ✅ — if the goal is entailed, it will eventually be derived.
- **Inefficient** ⚠️ — may fire irrelevant rules, wasting computation.

---

## 2.2 Backward Chaining — Operational Breakdown

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Start** with the goal/query $G$.
2. **Find** a rule whose consequent unifies with $G$ (i.e., a rule that *could* prove $G$).
3. **Recursively prove** the antecedents of that rule as sub-goals.
4. **Terminate** when all sub-goals are unified with facts in the KB (success) or no rule applies (failure).
5. **Backtrack** if a chosen rule path fails.

> [!NOTE]
> Backward chaining is **goal-driven**, mirrors **Depth-First Search (DFS)** with unification, and is the engine behind **Prolog**.

**Properties:**
- **Sound** ✅
- **Complete for Horn clauses** ✅
- **Efficient for focused queries** ✅ — only relevant sub-goals are explored.
- Can suffer from **infinite recursion** in cyclic KBs ⚠️.

---

## 2.3 Resolution — Operational Breakdown

**Steps for Resolution Refutation:**

1. Convert the KB to **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**.
2. **Negate the goal/query** (to prove by contradiction).
3. Add the negated goal to the KB clause set.
4. Repeatedly apply the **resolution rule** to pairs of clauses.
5. If the **empty clause $\square$** is derived → **goal proved (refutation successful)**.
6. If no new resolvents can be generated → **goal cannot be proved**.

**Key Sub-Concepts:**

- **Unification** $\theta$ — the most general unifier (MGU) that makes two literals identical.
- **Standardization Apart** — renaming variables to avoid clashes before resolution.
- **Refutation Completeness** — resolution can prove *any* FOL theorem (Gödel, Herbrand basis).

---

## 2.4 Resolution Strategies (Optimization Variants)

To avoid the exponential blowup of blind resolution, several **strategies** constrain the search:

| Strategy | Rule of Selection | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit Resolution** | At least one parent clause is a unit (single literal) | Produces shorter resolvents; complete for Horn clauses |
| **Input Resolution** | At least one parent is an input clause (from KB/negated goal) | Linear chain; efficient |
| **Linear Resolution** | One parent is the previous resolvent | Bounded branching; complete |
| **Set of Support (SOS)** | At least one parent is in SOS (initially the negated goal) | Focuses search; complete |
| **Subsumption** | Discard clauses subsumed by existing ones | Reduces clause space |
| **Purity / Tautology Elimination** | Drop pure literals or tautologies $(P \lor \neg P)$ | Prunes redundancy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Students are frequently asked to compare strategies, especially **Unit vs. Set of Support** and to demonstrate **resolution refutation** on a small Horn-clause KB.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Chaining Trigger | $\text{IF } (P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n) \in \text{WM}$ | $\longrightarrow$ | Fire rule, add consequent |
| 2 | Backward Chaining Trigger | $\text{Goal } G \Leftarrow \text{Rule with head } G$ | $\text{unify}(G, \text{head})$ | Recursive sub-goaling |
| 3 | Resolution Rule | $(L_1 \lor \alpha),\;(\neg L_2 \lor \beta) \Rightarrow (\alpha \lor \beta)\theta$ | $\theta = \text{MGU}(L_1, L_2)$ | Only on complementary literals |
| 4 | Refutation End | Derive $\square$ (empty clause) | $\square$ | Goal $\phi$ is a theorem of KB |
| 5 | CNF Conversion Steps | Eliminate $\Rightarrow,\;\Leftrightarrow \;\to\;$ Move $\neg$ inward (De Morgan) $\;\to\;$ Distribute $\lor$ over $\land$ | 5 mechanical steps | Standard preprocessing |
| 6 | Unification Failure | $\text{unify}(f(a),\,g(a)) = \text{FAIL}$ |  | Different function symbols |
| 7 | Occurs Check | $X \in \text{terms}(t) \Rightarrow \text{unify}(X, t) = \text{FAIL}$ | Prevents infinite structures | Implemented in Prolog with `occurs_check` |
| 8 | Horn Clause | At most **one** positive literal | $( \neg P_1 \lor \neg P_2 \lor \dots \lor Q)$ | Efficient for forward/backward |

> **Units / Symbols Convention:**
> - Literals are dimensionless propositional/first-order atoms.
> - $\theta$ is a substitution set: $\{X_1/t_1, X_2/t_2, \dots\}$
> - $\square$ denotes the empty clause (contradiction marker).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Strategy Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis Systems (MYCIN)** | Backward Chaining | Doctor starts with hypothesis, asks targeted questions |
| **Business Rule Engines (Drools)** | Forward Chaining (RETE) | Continuous data stream triggers business rules |
| **Automated Theorem Provers (Vampire, E, SPASS)** | Resolution + SOS | Prove software/hardware correctness |
| **Prolog / Datalog Programs** | Backward Chaining (SLD) | Database-style logical queries |
| **Semantic Web / OWL Reasoners (Pellet, HermiT)** | Tableau + Resolution | Ontology consistency checking |
| **Robotic Planning Agents (STRIPS, PDDL)** | Forward Chaining / Regression | Plan from initial state to goal state |
| **SAT Solvers (z3, MiniSAT)** | DPLL + CDCL (resolution-derived) | Hardware verification, crypto, AI planning |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Forward Chaining (Data-Driven)

**Knowledge Base (KB):**

1. $A \land B \rightarrow C$
2. $C \land D \rightarrow E$
3. $A$
4. $B$
5. $D$

**Goal:** Prove $E$.

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Step | Working Memory (WM) | Rule Fired | New Fact Added | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\{A,\,B,\,D\}$ | — | — | Initial facts from KB |
| 1 | $\{A,\,B,\,D,\,C\}$ | Rule 1 | $C$ | $A$ and $B$ are in WM → fire Rule 1 |
| 2 | $\{A,\,B,\,D,\,C,\,E\}$ | Rule 2 | $E$ | $C$ and $D$ are in WM → fire Rule 2 |
| 3 | $\{A,\,B,\,D,\,C,\,E\}$ | — | — | **Fixed point** — no new rule applicable |

**Conclusion:** $E$ is **derived** → goal achieved. ✅

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven)

**Same KB as above. Goal: Prove $E$.**

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1: Initialize goal stack with $\{E\}$.**

**Step 2:** Look for a rule whose **head** unifies with $E$.
- Rule 2: $C \land D \rightarrow E$ → head $E$ matches! ✅
- Push antecedents $C$ and $D$ as sub-goals.
- Goal stack: $\{C,\,D\}$.

**Step 3:** Sub-goal $C$. Look for rule with head $C$.
- Rule 1: $A \land B \rightarrow C$ → matches! ✅
- Push $A$ and $B$. Goal stack: $\{A,\,B,\,D\}$.

**Step 4:** Sub-goal $A$. Check KB facts. $A$ exists in KB → ✅.

**Step 5:** Sub-goal $B$. Check KB facts. $B$ exists in KB → ✅.

**Step 6:** Sub-goal $D$. Check KB facts. $D$ exists in KB → ✅.

**Step 7:** All sub-goals satisfied → **Backtrack success** → $E$ is proved. ✅

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Resolution Refutation (Full CNF Pipeline)

**Given KB:**

1. $\forall x\,[\text{Man}(x) \rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x)]$
2. $\text{Man}(\text{Socrates})$

**Goal:** Prove $\text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$.

### Step 1: Eliminate Implications

Rule 1 becomes:

$$ \neg \text{Man}(x) \lor \text{Mortal}(x) $$

### Step 2: Skolemize & Standardize

No Skolem functions needed. Variables renamed if required. KB in CNF (two clauses):

$$ C_1: \neg \text{Man}(x) \lor \text{Mortal}(x) $$

$$ C_2: \text{Man}(\text{Socrates}) $$

### Step 3: Negate the Goal

$$ C_3: \neg \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates}) $$

### Step 4: Apply Resolution

**Resolve $C_1$ and $C_3$** on complementary literals $\text{Mortal}(x)$ and $\neg \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$.

Find MGU $\theta = \{x / \text{Socrates}\}$.

Apply $\theta$ to $C_1$:

$$ C_1\theta = \neg \text{Man}(\text{Socrates}) \lor \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates}) $$

Resolve with $C_3 = \neg \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$:

$$ C_4: \neg \text{Man}(\text{Socrates}) $$

### Step 5: Second Resolution

**Resolve $C_2$ and $C_4$** on $\text{Man}(\text{Socrates})$ and $\neg \text{Man}(\text{Socrates})$.

MGU $\theta = \{\}$ (empty substitution).

$$ C_5: \square \quad \text{(Empty Clause)} $$

### Step 6: Conclusion

The empty clause $\square$ represents a **contradiction** → the assumption $\neg \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$ is **false** → $\text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$ is **proved**. ✅

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Dimension | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data $\rightarrow$ Goal | Goal $\rightarrow$ Data | Goal + KB $\rightarrow$ Contradiction |
| **Trigger** | New fact arrives | Query submitted | Refutation step on clauses |
| **Search Strategy** | Breadth-First (rule firing) | Depth-First (sub-goaling) | SAT-style search over clause space |
| **Best For** | Monitoring, control systems | Query answering, expert systems | Theorem proving, FOL agents |
| **Completeness** | Horn clauses | Horn clauses | Full FOL (refutation complete) |
| **Complexity** | Polynomial (Horn) | Linear (Horn) | Exponential worst-case |
| **KB Size Suitability** | Small to medium | Large, sparse | Medium with good strategy |
| **Representative Tool** | RETE engine (Drools, CLIPS) | Prolog, Datalog | Vampire, E prover, Otter |
| **Output Style** | All derivable facts | Single answer (with backtracking) | Proof trace / refutation tree |

---

## 3.5 Resolution Strategy Application — Set of Support

Using the **Set of Support (SOS)** strategy on the Socrates example:

- **Input clauses**: $C_1, C_2$
- **SOS (negated goal)**: $C_3$
- Rule: At least one parent of every resolution must be in SOS (or derived from SOS).

**First resolution:** $\{C_1, C_3\} \rightarrow C_4$. Since $C_3 \in$ SOS → allowed. $C_4$ added to SOS.

**Second resolution:** $\{C_2, C_4\} \rightarrow C_5 = \square$. Since $C_4 \in$ SOS → allowed. ✅

**Empty clause derived in 2 steps** — SOS focused the search perfectly, avoiding wasted resolutions on irrelevant input pairs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forward Chaining — Iterative Rule-Firing Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load KB into Working Memory] --> B[Initialize WM with all known facts]
    B --> C{Match: Any rule antecedent satisfied by WM?}
    C -- No --> Z[Fixed Point Reached: Stop and report facts]
    C -- Yes --> D[Select a fired rule via conflict resolution]
    D --> E[Add rule consequent to WM as new fact]
    E --> F[Mark rule as fired in this cycle]
    F --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cycle terminates either when the **goal fact** appears in WM or when no rule antecedent can be matched (fixed point).

---

## 4.2 Backward Chaining — Recursive Sub-Goal Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive query Q from user] --> B[Push Q onto goal stack]
    B --> C{Does Q unify with a fact in KB?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Goal Q is PROVED: Return SUCCESS]
    C -- No --> E[Find rule R with head H such that unify Q with H = theta]
    E --> F{Any such rule exists?}
    F -- No --> G[Backtrack to previous choice point or FAIL]
    F -- Yes --> H[Apply theta to rule body to get sub-goals]
    H --> I[Push sub-goals onto goal stack in order]
    I --> J[Pop top sub-goal and recurse]
    J --> C
```

---

## 4.3 Resolution Refutation — Top-Level Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: KB in FOL + Goal to prove] --> B[Step 1: Convert all KB formulas to CNF]
    B --> C[Step 2: Skolemize existential quantifiers]
    C --> D[Step 3: Standardize variables apart]
    D --> E[Step 4: Negate the goal and add as new clause]
    E --> F[Step 5: Apply resolution rule on complementary literals]
    F --> G{Empty clause square derived?}
    G -- Yes --> H[REFUTATION SUCCESS: Goal is a theorem]
    G -- No --> I{New resolvents generated?}
    I -- No --> J[FAIL: Goal cannot be proved]
    I -- Yes --> F
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture of the Three Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FC [Forward Chaining]
        FC1[KB Facts] --> FC2[Pattern Match] --> FC3[Conflict Resolution] --> FC4[Rule Execution] --> FC5[New Fact in WM] --> FC2
    end
    subgraph BC [Backward Chaining]
        BC1[Query Q] --> BC2[Unify with Rule Head] --> BC3[Generate Sub-Goals] --> BC4{All sub-goals provable?}
        BC4 -- Yes --> BC5[Return Q TRUE]
        BC4 -- No --> BC6[Backtrack or FAIL]
    end
    subgraph RES [Resolution]
        RES1[KB CNF] --> RES2[Negate Goal] --> RES3[Unify Complementary Literals] --> RES4[Build Resolvent Clause] --> RES5{Derived empty clause?}
        RES5 -- Yes --> RES6[Refutation Success]
        RES5 -- No --> RES3
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Resolution-Based Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    USER[User / Environment Query] --> PARSE[Query Parser]
    PARSE --> KBMOD[Knowledge Base Module]
    KBMOD --> CNF[CNF Conversion Engine]
    CNF --> SKOL[Skolemization and Variable Standardization]
    SKOL --> NEGGOAL[Goal Negation Unit]
    NEGGOAL --> CLAUSESET[Clause Set Buffer]
    CLAUSESET --> UNIFY[Unification and MGU Engine]
    UNIFY --> RESRULE[Resolution Rule Engine]
    RESRULE --> STRAT[Strategy Selector - Unit, SOS, Subsumption]
    STRAT --> CLAUSESET
    RESRULE --> EMPTYCHK{Empty Clause Derived?}
    EMPTYCHK -- Yes --> SUCCESS[Agent Returns PROVED with Proof Trace]
    EMPTYCHK -- No --> TIMEOUT{Resource Limit Reached?}
    TIMEOUT -- No --> UNIFY
    TIMEOUT -- Yes --> FAIL[Agent Returns UNPROVABLE within limits]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Strategy Selector** block is the heart of any modern theorem prover. It uses heuristics like SOS + Subsumption to avoid combinatorial explosion — a direct application of the resolution strategy theory you have just studied.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Set]
Distinguish between **Forward Chaining** and **Backward Chaining** in rule-based reasoning systems. State one real-world application where each is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data-driven (facts → conclusions) | Goal-driven (goal → sub-goals → facts) |
| **Trigger** | New fact enters KB | User query is submitted |
| **Search Pattern** | Bottom-up, breadth-like | Top-down, depth-first |
| **Output** | All derivable consequences | Single (or few) answer(s) to query |
| **Application** | Real-time monitoring, business rule engines (Drools, CLIPS) | Medical diagnosis (MYCIN), Prolog interpreters |

**Preferred application:**
- *Forward*: **Fire-alarm monitoring agent** — continuously derives alarm state from sensor data.
- *Backward*: **Medical diagnostic agent** — doctor asks "Does patient have flu?" and system reasons backward.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
What is meant by **Resolution Refutation**? Why is the empty clause $\square$ considered a proof of the original goal?

**Model Answer:**

**Resolution Refutation** is a proof technique that shows a goal $G$ follows from a KB by demonstrating that the **negation of $G$**, when added to the KB, leads to a **logical contradiction** via repeated application of the resolution rule.

**Why $\square$ proves the goal:**

If from the set of clauses $\{KB \cup \{\neg G\}\}$ we derive the **empty clause $\square$**, this means we have produced a contradiction (since $\square$ is unsatisfiable under every interpretation). By the principle of *proof by contradiction* (Reductio ad Absurdum):

$$ KB \cup \{\neg G\} \models \square \;\Longrightarrow\; KB \models G $$

Hence, $G$ is a **logical consequence** of the KB. The refutation tree serves as an explicit, machine-checkable proof trace.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Model Pattern]

**(a)** Consider the following Knowledge Base expressed as Horn clauses:
- $P \rightarrow Q$
- $L \land M \rightarrow P$
- $B \land L \rightarrow M$
- $A \land P \rightarrow L$
- $A \land B \rightarrow L$
- $A$
- $B$

**Using Forward Chaining, prove that $Q$ is true. Show every step of the Working Memory update.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Convert the following FOL sentence into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** and apply **Resolution Refutation** to prove the goal $\exists x\, \text{Loves}(x, \text{John})$ from the KB clauses. Show the full resolution trace. *(7 marks)*

KB:
- $\forall x\,[\text{Man}(x) \rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x)]$
- $\forall x\,[\text{Mortal}(x) \rightarrow \text{Loves}(x, \text{John})]$
- $\text{Man}(\text{Plato})$

---

### **Model Solution to Q-A(a): Forward Chaining Trace**

**Initial Working Memory (WM₀):** $\{A,\;B\}$

| Iteration | Fired Rule | Antecedents Check | New Fact | Updated WM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rule 5 ($A \land B \rightarrow L$) | $A \in$ WM, $B \in$ WM ✅ | $L$ | $\{A,\;B,\;L\}$ |
| 2 | Rule 3 ($B \land L \rightarrow M$) | $B \in$ WM, $L \in$ WM ✅ | $M$ | $\{A,\;B,\;L,\;M\}$ |
| 3 | Rule 2 ($L \land M \rightarrow P$) | $L \in$ WM, $M \in$ WM ✅ | $P$ | $\{A,\;B,\;L,\;M,\;P\}$ |
| 4 | Rule 1 ($P \rightarrow Q$) | $P \in$ WM ✅ | $Q$ | $\{A,\;B,\;L,\;M,\;P,\;Q\}$ |

**Conclusion:** $Q$ has been derived. ✅ Forward chaining terminates successfully in 4 iterations.

---

### **Model Solution to Q-A(b): Resolution Refutation**

**Step 1: Eliminate Implications and Move to CNF**

Rule 1: $\neg \text{Man}(x) \lor \text{Mortal}(x)$ → **Clause C₁**

Rule 2: $\neg \text{Mortal}(y) \lor \text{Loves}(y, \text{John})$ → **Clause C₂**

Rule 3: $\text{Man}(\text{Plato})$ → **Clause C₃**

**Step 2: Negate the Goal**

$\neg(\exists x\, \text{Loves}(x, \text{John})) \equiv \forall x\, \neg \text{Loves}(x, \text{John})$

→ **Clause C₄:** $\neg \text{Loves}(z, \text{John})$

**Step 3: Resolution Trace**

**Resolution 1:** Resolve **C₂** and **C₄** on $\text{Loves}(y, \text{John})$ and $\neg \text{Loves}(z, \text{John})$.

MGU $\theta = \{y / z\}$

Result: **C₅:** $\neg \text{Mortal}(z)$

**Resolution 2:** Resolve **C₁** and **C₅** on $\text{Mortal}(x)$ and $\neg \text{Mortal}(z)$.

MGU $\theta = \{x / z\}$

Result: **C₆:** $\neg \text{Man}(z)$

**Resolution 3:** Resolve **C₃** and **C₆** on $\text{Man}(\text{Plato})$ and $\neg \text{Man}(z)$.
