MGU $\theta = \{z / \text{Plato}\}$

Result: **C₇:** $\square$ (**Empty Clause**)

**Conclusion:** Empty clause derived → refutation successful → $\exists x\,\text{Loves}(x, \text{John})$ is **proved**, with witness $x = \text{Plato}$. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain **Unification** in First-Order Logic. Apply the unification algorithm on the literals $\text{Knows}(\text{John}, x)$ and $\text{Knows}(y, \text{Mary})$ and derive the MGU. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss any **three resolution strategies** used to optimize resolution refutation. For each, state its selection rule and one advantage. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution to Q-B(a): Unification**

**Definition:** Unification is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions *syntactically identical*. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest such substitution, containing no redundant bindings.

**Given Literals:**
$$ L_1 = \text{Knows}(\text{John}, x) \qquad L_2 = \text{Knows}(y, \text{Mary}) $$

**Step-by-step Unification Algorithm Trace:**

| Step | Sub-expressions Being Unified | Substitution Update | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Knows}(a_1, a_2)$ vs $\text{Knows}(b_1, b_2)$ | — | Recurse into args |
| 2 | $\text{John}$ vs $y$ | $\{y / \text{John}\}$ | $y$ bound to John |
| 3 | $x$ vs $\text{Mary}$ | $\{y / \text{John},\; x / \text{Mary}\}$ | $x$ bound to Mary |
| 4 | Apply occurs check — no $x$ in $\text{Mary}$ and no $y$ in $\text{John}$ | — | ✅ Safe |

**MGU:** $\theta = \{y / \text{John},\; x / \text{Mary}\}$

**Verification:** $L_1 \theta = \text{Knows}(\text{John}, \text{Mary}) = L_2 \theta$ ✅

---

### **Model Solution to Q-B(b): Three Resolution Strategies**

**1. Unit Resolution**
- **Rule:** At least one of the two parent clauses must be a **unit clause** (a clause containing a single literal).
- **Advantage:** Produces shorter resolvents and terminates in linear time for Horn clauses.

**2. Set of Support (SOS)**
- **Rule:** At least one parent clause must belong to the *Set of Support* (initially the negated goal and all clauses derived from it).
- **Advantage:** Focuses the search on goal-relevant clauses, drastically reducing the number of resolutions attempted.

**3. Subsumption**
- **Rule:** Discard any clause that is *subsumed* by another (i.e., a more general clause exists in the set).
- **Advantage:** Eliminates redundant clauses from the search space, reducing memory and improving efficiency.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping CNF steps** — Examiners expect the *full* 5-step CNF conversion (eliminate $\Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$; push $\neg$ inward; standardize variables; skolemize; drop universal quantifiers). Skipping even one costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to negate the goal** — In resolution refutation, the goal **must be negated** before adding to the KB. A common slip is to add the goal positively, breaking the refutation logic entirely.
> 3. **Wrong MGU computation** — Always apply the **occurs check**; binding $X$ to a term containing $X$ creates infinite structures and is invalid.
> 4. **Confusing direction of chaining** — Forward = facts → goal; Backward = goal → facts. Writing the trace in the wrong direction loses the conceptual mark.
> 5. **No empty clause interpretation** — You must explicitly state that $\square$ means *contradiction* and that refutation succeeds.
> 6. **Variable clashes in resolution** — Always **standardize variables apart** before resolving. Forgetting this is a classic 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Chaining Resolution Strategies**

- **Forward Chaining** = **data-driven**, **bottom-up**, used in monitoring & control agents, RETE algorithm, complete for Horn clauses.
- **Backward Chaining** = **goal-driven**, **top-down**, used in Prolog, expert systems, complete for Horn clauses.
- **Resolution** = **refutation-based**, operates on **CNF clauses**, complete for full FOL.
- **CNF Pipeline** = eliminate $\Rightarrow$ → eliminate $\Leftrightarrow$ → push $\neg$ inward (De Morgan) → standardize variables → eliminate existential quantifiers (Skolemize) → drop universal quantifiers → distribute $\lor$ over $\land$.
- **Resolution Rule**: combine complementary literals with MGU $\theta$ to produce a **resolvent** clause.
- **Empty Clause $\square$** = contradiction = goal proved ✅
- **MGU** = most general unifier, computed via recursive unification with **occurs check**.
- **Unit Resolution** = uses a unit parent clause, fast on Horn.
- **Input Resolution** = at least one parent from KB or negated goal.
- **Linear Resolution** = one parent is previous resolvent (chain-shaped proof).
- **Set of Support** = focus on clauses tied to the negated goal, complete strategy.
- **Subsumption** = drop more-specific clauses already covered by general ones.
- **Horn Clauses** = at most one positive literal — the only FOL fragment where forward/backward chaining is tractable.
- **Applications**: medical diagnosis, business rule engines, theorem provers, semantic web, robotic planners, SAT solvers, model checkers.
- **Key Symbols to Know**: $\theta$ (substitution), $\square$ (empty clause), $\lor$ (OR), $\land$ (AND), $\neg$ (NOT), $\forall$ (forall), $\exists$ (exists), $\models$ (entails).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Knowledge Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Knowledge Representation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Knowledge Representation (KR)** is the sub-field of Artificial Intelligence dedicated to formally encoding information about the world in a structured format that an intelligent agent can utilize for reasoning, decision-making, planning, and communication. In the context of KTU 2024 Agent-Based Intelligent Systems, KR is the *bridge* between raw data and intelligent behavior, providing the symbolic vocabulary and inference machinery that an agent's Belief Base (B), Commitment Store (C), and Intentions (I) rely upon. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Knowledge Representation is the methodology by which an intelligent agent encodes facts, rules, heuristics, and relationships about its environment into a formal symbolic structure (such as predicate logic, semantic networks, frames, or ontologies) so that automated reasoning algorithms can manipulate, infer, and derive new conclusions.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Card Catalogue" Model Imagine a massive library containing millions of books. Without a **card catalogue** (subject headings, cross-references, an indexing system), even the smartest librarian cannot find or reason about the books efficiently. Knowledge Representation is exactly that **catalogue** for an AI agent: - **Books on shelves** = Raw sensory data / facts - **Card Catalogue** = The formal symbolic representation (frames, rules, predicates) - **Librarian** = The inference engine - **Answering a patron's question** = Drawing logical conclusions (reasoning) Just as a good catalogue groups books by subject, links related topics, and enforces a consistent format, a good KR system groups facts, links related entities, and uses a formal syntax so the agent can "ask questions" and get intelligent answers. ### 1.3 Why Knowledge Representation is Foundational in Agent Design An **intelligent agent** (Russell & Norvig's architecture) perceives its environment via sensors and acts upon it via actuators. Between these lies a critical layer: 1. **Sensors** → receive Percepts 2. **Agent Program** → updates its Knowledge Base (KB) 3. **Inference Engine** → reasons over the KB 4. **Actuators** → execute Actions If the KB is poorly represented, the agent cannot reason — it becomes a "blind librarian." Hence KR is rated as one of the **core pillars** of the BDI (Belief-Desire-Intention) agent architecture. > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Insight:** > An agent is only as *intelligent* as the *quality* of its knowledge representation. The famous maxim in classical AI states: *"The medium is the message"* — the choice of KR formalism directly determines the kind of reasoning an agent can perform. ### 1.4 The Four Fundamental Types of Knowledge Every KR system must be capable of representing these four categories: | Knowledge Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Declarative (Descriptive)** | Facts about the world — "what is" | `Bird(Tweety)`, `Mammal(Elephant)` | | **Procedural (Imperative)** | How to do things — step-by-step know-how | Production rules, algorithms | | **Heuristic** | Rule-of-thumb, experiential knowledge | "If traffic is heavy, take the metro" | | **Meta-knowledge** | Knowledge *about* knowledge | "Rule 7 is more reliable than Rule 3" | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Core Requirements of a Good Knowledge Representation System A formal KR system must satisfy the following properties (frequently asked in KTU exams): - **Representational Adequacy:** Ability to represent all kinds of knowledge required in the domain. - **Inferential Adequacy:** Ability to manipulate the represented knowledge to derive new structures (inferences). - **Inferential Efficiency:** Ability to incorporate additional heuristics to guide the inference process. - **Acquisitional Efficiency:** Ability to acquire new knowledge easily, often through automatic learning. ### 2.2 The Five Major Knowledge Representation Schemes KTU Module 1 explicitly emphasizes the following KR techniques: #### 2.2.1 Predicate Logic (First-Order Logic — FOL) The most rigorous, mathematically grounded scheme. **Syntax Building Blocks:** $$\text{Constants: } a, b, c \quad \text{Variables: } x, y, z$$ $$\text{Predicate Symbols: } P, Q, R \quad \text{Function Symbols: } f, g$$ $$\text{Logical Connectives: } \neg, \wedge, \vee, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$$ $$\text{Quantifiers: } \forall \text{ (Universal)}, \quad \exists \text{ (Existential)}$$ **Example Encoding:** - English: "All humans are mortal." - FOL: $\forall x \, (Human(x) \rightarrow Mortal(x))$ #### 2.2.2 Semantic Networks A **graph-based** representation where nodes represent objects/concepts and edges represent relationships. Pioneered by **Quillian (1968)**. - Nodes → Concepts (e.g., "Bird", "Tweety") - Edges → Relations (e.g., "is-a", "has-part", "can") #### 2.2.3 Frames Proposed by **Minsky (1975)**, a frame is a data structure representing a stereotyped situation or object. Contains **slots** (attributes) and **fillers** (values). - Slots can be of type: `range`, `default`, `if-needed` (procedural attachment) #### 2.2.4 Production Rules The most widely used representation in **Expert Systems** and **rule-based agents**. $$\text{IF } <\text{condition}> \text{ THEN } <\text{action}>$$ - The **condition** matches against the Working Memory. - The **action** either modifies Working Memory or triggers an external actuator. #### 2.2.5 Ontologies (Description Logics) Formal specifications of shared conceptualizations. The modern foundation of the **Semantic Web (OWL, RDF)**. Uses concepts (classes), roles (properties), and individuals. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Symbol Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Usage in KR | |---|---|---| | $\forall x$ | "For all x" | Universal quantification in FOL | | $\exists x$ | "There exists an x" | Existential quantification in FOL | | $\land$ | Logical AND (Conjunction) | Joining multiple facts | | $\lor$ | Logical OR (Disjunction) | Alternative possibilities | | $\neg$ | Negation (NOT) | Negating a fact | | $\rightarrow$ | Implication | Encoding IF-THEN rules | | $\leftrightarrow$ | Biconditional (iff) | Equivalence between facts | | $\text{is-a}(x, y)$ | Inheritance relation | $x$ is a sub-class of $y$ | | $\text{instance}(x, y)$ | Instantiation | Object $x$ belongs to class $y$ | | $\text{has-part}(x, y)$ | Part-whole relation | $x$ contains $y$ | | $KB \vdash \alpha$ | Knowledge Base entails $\alpha$ | Logical entailment | | $KB \models \alpha$ | Semantic entailment | $\alpha$ is true in all models of KB | | $\models$ | Satisfiability / model-theoretic consequence | Soundness check | | $\theta$ | Substitution (Unification) | $\{x/Tweety, y/Bird\}$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In FOL conversion questions, students often confuse the *universal* ($\forall$) with *existential* ($\exists$) quantifiers. The rule: use $\forall$ for "All/Every" and $\exists$ for "Some/At least one". ### 2.4 The Knowledge Representation Pipeline in an Agent The end-to-end flow inside an intelligent agent is: 1. **Perception** → raw data from environment 2. **Lexical Analysis** → tokenizing the input 3. **Parsing** → mapping to a formal syntax tree 4. **Semantic Interpretation** → assigning meaning via ontology 5. **Knowledge Base Update** → storing new facts 6. **Inference / Reasoning** → forward-chaining or backward-chaining 7. **Action Selection** → output decision This pipeline directly maps to the **BDI Agent Loop**: Beliefs are updated in the KB, Desires are derived via reasoning, and Intentions trigger actions. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Healthcare:** Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS) use frames + production rules to diagnose diseases (e.g., MYCIN). - **Autonomous Vehicles:** Ontologies describe road entities (cars, pedestrians, signs); rules drive braking decisions. - **Smart Manufacturing:** Semantic networks model machine hierarchies for predictive maintenance. - **Natural Language Processing:** FrameNet uses frame semantics to represent sentence meaning. - **Robotics:** SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) uses spatial KR schemes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Step-by-Step Conversion: English Sentence to First-Order Logic (FOL) This is the **highest-weightage derivation type** in KTU exams. The exhaustive step-by-step method is shown below. **Problem (KTU-Style):** Convert the following to First-Order Logic: *"Every student who reads a book in the library is intelligent. Some books in the library are novels. There exists a student who reads every novel in the library."* **Step 1: Identify the Predicates and Quantifiers** We need: - $S(x)$ → x is a student - $I(x)$ → x is intelligent - $B(x)$ → x is a book - $L(x,y)$ → x is in y - $R(x,y)$ → x reads y - $N(x)$ → x is a novel **Step 2: Translate Sentence 1** *English:* "Every student who reads a book in the library is intelligent." The structure is: $\forall x \, [\text{Student}(x) \land \exists y \, (\text{Book}(y) \land \text{In}(y, \text{Library}) \land \text{Reads}(x, y))] \rightarrow \text{Intelligent}(x)$ $$\forall x \, \Big[ \big( S(x) \land \exists y \, (B(y) \land L(y, \text{Lib}) \land R(x, y)) \big) \rightarrow I(x) \Big]$$ This sentence is universally quantified (every student), so we use $\forall x$. The antecedent is the conjunction of (being a student) and (reading some book in the library), and the consequent is (being intelligent). **Step 3: Translate Sentence 2** *English:* "Some books in the library are novels." Since the statement is existential (some books), we use $\exists$. $$\exists x \, (B(x) \land L(x, \text{Lib}) \land N(x))$$ **Step 4: Translate Sentence 3** *English:* "There exists a student who reads every novel in the library." This is an existential statement about a specific student, but the inner action covers *every* novel. The proper structure nests $\exists$ outside and $\forall$ inside. $$\exists x \, \big( S(x) \land \forall y \, ((B(y) \land N(y) \land L(y, \text{Lib})) \rightarrow R(x, y)) \big)$$ **Step 5: Combine into the Final Knowledge Base** $$KB = \Big\{ \forall x \, [ ( S(x) \land \exists y \, (B(y) \land L(y, \text{Lib}) \land R(x, y)) ) \rightarrow I(x) ],$$ $$\exists x \, (B(x) \land L(x, \text{Lib}) \land N(x)),$$ $$\exists x \, ( S(x) \land \forall y \, ((B(y) \land N(y) \land L(y, \text{Lib})) \rightarrow R(x, y)) ) \Big\}$$ **Step 6: Verify Scope and Binding** The variable $x$ in Sentence 1 and Sentence 3 are *different* variables, but since FOL allows variable renaming, the final KB is syntactically correct. No free variables remain. --- ### 3.2 Unification Algorithm (Exhaustive Derivation) **Unification** is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions identical. The KTU board expects a *complete* algorithmic walkthrough. **Problem:** Unify the following two predicates: - $P(x, f(y), B)$ - $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$ **Step 1: Initialize the substitution set** Start with the empty substitution $\theta_0 = \{\}$. **Step 2: Compare first arguments** Left: $x$, Right: $A$. Since $x$ is a variable and $A$ is a constant, substitute $x \leftarrow A$. $$\theta_1 = \{x/A\}$$ **Step 3: Apply $\theta_1$ to both expressions** - $P(A, f(y), B)$ - $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$ **Step 4: Compare second arguments** Left: $f(y)$, Right: $f(g(z))$. Both have the same function symbol $f$, so unify the inner terms: $y$ and $g(z)$. Substitute $y \leftarrow g(z)$. $$\theta_2 = \{x/A,\, y/g(z)\}$$ **Step 5: Apply $\theta_2$ to both expressions** - $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$ - $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$ **Step 6: Compare third arguments** Left: $B$, Right: $B$. Both identical, no substitution needed. **Step 7: Final Unifier** $$\theta_{\text{final}} = \{x/A, \, y/g(z)\}$$ Both expressions become $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$. **Unification is successful.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Unification Rule (Occur Check):** > A variable $x$ can NEVER be unified with a term that contains $x$. For example, $x$ cannot be bound to $f(x)$, because this creates an infinite term. Always perform the **occur check** before applying a substitution. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right KR Scheme KTU frequently poses the question: *"Which KR scheme is best for a given problem?"* The following decision matrix is the standard answer: | Property | Predicate Logic | Semantic Networks | Frames | Production Rules | Ontologies | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Formal Rigor** | Very High | Low | Low | Medium | Very High | | **Expressiveness** | High | Medium | High | Medium | Very High | | **Ease of Implementation** | Low | High | High | High | Medium | | **Handles Uncertainty** | No (Propositional) | No | No | Yes (with factors) | No | | **Inheritance Support** | Manual | Yes (is-a links) | Yes (slot inheritance) | No | Yes (sub-class) | | **Best Suited For** | Mathematical proofs, AI theorem provers | Taxonomies, conceptual models | Object-oriented domains, visual scenes | Expert systems, diagnostics | Semantic web, knowledge graphs | | **Limitation** | Combinatorial explosion | No standard semantics | Frame exception problem | Rule conflicts | Computational complexity | ### 3.4 Resolution Refutation (KTU-Style Proof) **Problem:** Prove that "Socrates is mortal" given: - Premise 1: $\forall x \, (Man(x) \rightarrow Mortal(x))$ - Premise 2: $Man(Socrates)$ **Step 1: Convert to Clause Form (CNF)** From Premise 1: Eliminate $\rightarrow$: $$\forall x \, (\neg Man(x) \lor Mortal(x))$$ Since no quantifiers restrict variables, the clause is: $$C_1 = \{\neg Man(x), \, Mortal(x)\}$$ From Premise 2: $$C_2 = \{Man(Socrates)\}$$ **Step 2: Negate the Goal** We want to prove $Mortal(Socrates)$, so add the negation: $$C_3 = \{\neg Mortal(Socrates)\}$$ **Step 3: Resolve $C_2$ and $C_3$** - $C_2$: $Man(Socrates)$ - $C_3$: $\neg Mortal(Socrates)$ These are **NOT** complementary (they don't cancel). So we must first resolve $C_1$ with $C_2$. **Step 4: Resolve $C_1$ and $C_2$** Apply substitution $\theta = \{x/Socrates\}$: - $C_1$ becomes: $\{\neg Man(Socrates), \, Mortal(Socrates)\}$ - $C_2$: $\{Man(Socrates)\}$ Complementary literals: $\neg Man(Socrates)$ and $Man(Socrates)$ cancel. Result: $C_4 = \{Mortal(Socrates)\}$ **Step 5: Resolve $C_4$ and $C_3$** - $C_4$: $Mortal(Socrates)$ - $C_3$: $\neg Mortal(Socrates)$ These are complementary, so resolution yields the **empty clause** $\{\}$. **Step 6: Conclusion** The empty clause is a contradiction. Therefore, the negation of the goal is false, meaning: $$KB \models Mortal(Socrates)$$ This proves that **Socrates is mortal**. The proof is sound and complete. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Knowledge Representation in an Intelligent Agent ```mermaid flowchart TB sensor[Sensor Input Layer] --> percept[Percept Module] percept --> parser[Lexical and Syntax Parser] parser --> sem[Semantic Interpreter] sem --> kb[(Knowledge Base
Facts plus Rules)] kb --> inf[Inference Engine
Forward or Backward Chaining] inf --> reason[Reasoning Module
Resolution and Unification] reason --> decide[Decision and Action Selector] decide --> act[Actuator Output Layer] act --> env[Environment] kb -.updates.-> kb inf -.queries.-> kb reason -.queries.-> inf sem -.stores.-> kb style sensor fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1 style act fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309 style env fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D style kb fill:#FAE8FF,stroke:#7E22CE style inf fill:#FFE4E6,stroke:#BE123C style reason fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#4338CA ``` ### 4.2 Semantic Network Representation: A Bird Taxonomy ```mermaid graph TD animal[Animal] bird[Bird] fish[Fish] mammal[Mammal] robin[Robin] tweety[Tweety] sparrow[Sparrow] canary[Canary] eagle[Eagle] animal -- is-a parent of --> bird animal -- is-a parent of --> fish animal -- is-a parent of --> mammal bird -- is-a parent of --> robin bird -- is-a parent of --> sparrow bird -- is-a parent of --> canary bird -- is-a parent of --> eagle robin -- instance of --> tweety bird -- has property --> can_fly[Can Fly] bird -- has property --> has_feathers[Has Feathers] fish -- has property --> can_swim[Can Swim] mammal -- has property --> warm_blooded[Warm Blooded] eagle -- has property --> sharp_vision[Sharp Vision] canary -- has property --> can_sing[Can Sing] style animal fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#B45309 style bird fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#15803D style fish fill:#BAE6FD,stroke:#0369A1 style mammal fill:#FECDD3,stroke:#BE123C style tweety fill:#F5D0FE,stroke:#A21CAF ``` ### 4.3 Frame Structure: A "University Student" Frame ```mermaid flowchart LR title[Frame Name: UNIVERSITY STUDENT]:::frame slots[Slots and Fillers]:::slots s1[Slot: name
Filler: string]:::slot s2[Slot: age
Filler: integer 18 to 60]:::slot s3[Slot: GPA
Filler: float 0.0 to 10.0]:::slot s4[Slot: enrolled_courses
Filler: list of Course objects]:::slot s5[Slot: advisor
Filler: Professor instance]:::slot s6[Slot: if_needed calculate_fees
Filler: procedure]:::slot s7[Slot: default hostel
Filler: Yes]:::slot s8[Slot: isa
Filler: Person]:::inherit title --> slots slots --> s1 slots --> s2 slots --> s3 slots --> s4 slots --> s5 slots --> s6 slots --> s7 slots --> s8 classDef frame fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef slots fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,color:#000 classDef slot fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151,color:#000 classDef inherit fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Production System Cycle (Rule-Based Agent) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Match Match: Match Phase
Find applicable rules Match --> Conflict Conflict: Conflict Resolution
Select one rule from matched set Conflict --> Act Act: Act Phase
Execute the selected rule Act --> Match Act --> [*] ``` ### 4.5 Taxonomy of Knowledge Representation Techniques ```mermaid mindmap root((Knowledge Representation Techniques)) Logical Propositional Logic First Order Logic Higher Order Logic Modal Logic Network Based Semantic Networks Conceptual Graphs Conceptual Dependency Structured Frames Scripts Schemata Rule Based Production Rules IF THEN Rules Horn Clauses Ontological RDF and RDFS OWL Description Logics Knowledge Graphs ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define Knowledge Representation. List any four requirements of a good knowledge representation system.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Knowledge Representation is the formal encoding of facts, rules, concepts, and relationships about a domain into a structured symbolic form that an intelligent agent can store, retrieve, and reason over using inference algorithms. **Four Requirements (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Representational Adequacy:** The system should be able to represent all necessary kinds of knowledge within the domain. 2. **Inferential Adequacy:** The system should support inference — the ability to derive new knowledge from existing knowledge. 3. **Inferential Efficiency:** The system should be able to incorporate extra heuristic knowledge to improve inference speed. 4. **Acquisitional Efficiency:** The system should allow easy addition of new knowledge, preferably through automated learning. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q2. Differentiate between Procedural Knowledge and Declarative Knowledge with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Declarative Knowledge | Procedural Knowledge | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Facts about the world, expressed as propositions | Step-by-step "how-to" knowledge | | **Form** | Statements (sentences in FOL) | Algorithms, rules, scripts | | **Example** | "The capital of France is Paris." | "To sort a list, compare adjacent elements and swap if out of order." | | **Storage** | Stored as static data | Stored as executable code | | **Reasoning Type** | Deductive, uses inference engine | Reactive, executes procedures directly | **(2 Marks for the table, 1 Mark for examples.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Important:** The KTU ESE follows Module Internal Choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adapted]** **Q (a)** Explain in detail the various **Knowledge Representation techniques** used in intelligent systems. Compare their strengths and weaknesses. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Predicate (First-Order) Logic (2 Marks)** - Most rigorous, mathematically grounded KR scheme. - Uses constants, variables, predicates, functions, connectives, and quantifiers. - Supports sound and complete inference via resolution. - **Strength:** High expressiveness, formal semantics. - **Weakness:** Slow inference, cannot handle uncertainty. **2. Semantic Networks (1.5 Marks)** - Graph-based: nodes = concepts, edges = relationships. - Supports inheritance automatically via *is-a* links. - **Strength:** Visually intuitive, easy to build. - **Weakness:** No standard semantics, ambiguity in edge labels. **3. Frames (1.5 Marks)** - Data structure representing a stereotyped object or situation. - Uses slots (attributes) and fillers (values). - Supports default values, procedural attachments (`if-needed`, `if-added`). - **Strength:** Excellent for object-oriented domains, supports inheritance. - **Weakness:** The "frame exception problem" — exceptions to defaults are hard to handle. **4. Production Rules (1 Mark)** - `IF condition THEN action` format. - Foundation of expert systems (e.g., MYCIN, CLIPS). - **Strength:** Modular, easy to modify, supports heuristic reasoning. - **Weakness:** Conflict resolution between rules; can be slow with thousands of rules. **5. Ontologies (1 Mark)** - Formal specification of a shared conceptualization (Gruber, 1993). - Foundation of the modern Semantic Web (RDF, OWL). - **Strength:** Standardized, machine-readable, supports reasoning over web-scale data. - **Weakness:** Computationally expensive; description logics can be undecidable in the worst case. --- **Q (b)** Convert the following English statements into **First-Order Logic (FOL)**. Show every step. **(7 Marks)** > *i. All professors are researchers.* > *ii. Some professors publish in conferences.* > *iii. No researcher is ignorant.* > *iv. Every conference has at least one chair.* **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Identify Predicates** - $P(x)$: $x$ is a professor - $R(x)$: $x$ is a researcher - $C(x)$: $x$ is a conference - $Pub(x, y)$: $x$ publishes in $y$ - $Ch(x, y)$: $x$ chairs $y$ **Step 2: Translate Each Statement** **(i) All professors are researchers.** This is a universal conditional. For every entity $x$, if it is a professor, it is a researcher. $$\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow R(x))$$ **(ii) Some professors publish in conferences.** This is an existential statement. There exists at least one $x$ and one $y$ such that $x$ is a professor and $x$ publishes in $y$ and $y$ is a conference. $$\exists x \, \exists y \, (P(x) \land C(y) \land Pub(x, y))$$ **(iii) No researcher is ignorant.** "No A is B" is logically equivalent to "All A are not-B" — i.e., $\forall x (A(x) \rightarrow \neg B(x))$. Let $I(x)$: $x$ is ignorant. $$\forall x \, (R(x) \rightarrow \neg I(x))$$ **(iv) Every conference has at least one chair.** This is universally quantified over conferences and existentially quantified over chairs. Let $Chair(x)$: $x$ is a chair (person). $$\forall y \, \big( C(y) \rightarrow \exists x \, (Chair(x) \land Ch(x, y)) \big)$$ **Step 3: Final KB** $$KB = \Big\{ \forall x (P(x) \rightarrow R(x)), \; \exists x \exists y (P(x) \land C(y) \land Pub(x, y)),$$ $$\forall x (R(x) \rightarrow \neg I(x)), \; \forall y (C(y) \rightarrow \exists x (Chair(x) \land Ch(x, y))) \Big\}$$ --- ### ➤ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]** **Q (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a **Semantic Network**. Demonstrate how inheritance works in a semantic net using an example. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 Mark):** A semantic network is a graph-based knowledge representation where nodes represent objects/concepts and labeled edges represent binary relationships between them. It was proposed by M. Ross Quillian in 1968 as a model of human associative memory. **Structure (2 Marks):** The basic structure consists of two elements: - **Nodes** (vertices): Represent concepts, objects, events, or categories. For example, "Bird", "Tweety", "Mammal". - **Links** (edges): Represent binary relations between concepts. Labeled with the relation name. Common labels include: `is-a`, `instance`, `has-part`, `can`, `property`. **Example Diagram (2 Marks):** ``` [Animal] | is-a | [Bird]——————has————→[Wings] | is-a | [Robin] | instance | [Tweety] ``` **Inheritance Demonstration (2 Marks):** Inheritance is the property by which a node automatically acquires the properties of its parent nodes via the `is-a` link. In the above network: - "Bird" has the property "has wings". - "Robin" is-a "Bird" → by inheritance, "Robin" automatically has the property "has wings". - "Tweety" is an *instance* of "Robin" → therefore "Tweety" also has wings. This is called **property inheritance** and saves explicit storage of repeated facts. If we ask the system "Can Tweety fly?", it follows the chain: `Tweety → instance of → Robin → is-a → Bird → has property → Can Fly` and returns "Yes" without an explicit fact about Tweety. **Limitation:** Semantic networks lack standard semantics — the same edge label can mean different things in different networks, making automatic reasoning difficult. --- **Q (b)** Using the **Resolution Refutation method**, prove that "Ramu likes food" from the following KB. **(7 Marks)** > *P1: $\forall x \, (Dog(x) \rightarrow Animal(x))$* > *P2: $\forall x \, (Animal(x) \rightarrow Likes(Ramu, x))$* > *P3: $Dog(Puppy)$* > *Goal: $Likes(Ramu, Food)$* (assume $Food$ is a constant and we are given $Animal(Food)$ as P4) **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Convert Premises to Clause Form (CNF) (2 Marks)** P1: $\forall x (Dog(x) \rightarrow Animal(x))$ becomes: $$C_1 = \{\neg Dog(x), \, Animal(x)\}$$ P2: $\forall x (Animal(x) \rightarrow Likes(Ramu, x))$ becomes: $$C_2 = \{\neg Animal(x), \, Likes(Ramu, x)\}$$ P3: $Dog(Puppy)$ becomes: $$C_3 = \{Dog(Puppy)\}$$ P4: $Animal(Food)$ becomes: $$C_4 = \{Animal(Food)\}$$ **Step 2: Negate the Goal (0.5 Marks)** Goal: $Likes(Ramu, Food)$ Negation: $\neg Likes(Ramu, Food)$ $$C_5 = \{\neg Likes(Ramu, Food)\}$$ **Step 3: Resolve $C_1$ and $C_3$ (1 Mark)** Apply $\theta_1 = \{x/Puppy\}$: - $C_1$: $\{\neg Dog(Puppy), \, Animal(Puppy)\}$ - $C_3$: $\{Dog(Puppy)\}$ Complementary literals cancel. Result: $$C_6 = \{Animal(Puppy)\}$$ **Step 4: Resolve $C_2$ and $C_6$ (1 Mark)** Apply $\theta_2 = \{x/Puppy\}$: - $C_2$: $\{\neg Animal(Puppy), \, Likes(Ramu, Puppy)\}$ - $C_6$: $\{Animal(Puppy)\}$ Complementary literals cancel. Result: $$C_7 = \{Likes(Ramu, Puppy)\}$$ **Step 5: Resolve $C_4$ and $C_2$ (1 Mark)** Apply $\theta_3 = \{x/Food\}$: - $C_4$: $\{Animal(Food)\}$ - $C_2$: $\{\neg Animal(Food), \, Likes(Ramu, Food)\}$ Complementary literals cancel. Result: $$C_8 = \{Likes(Ramu, Food)\}$$ **Step 6: Resolve $C_8$ and $C_5$ (1 Mark)** - $C_8$: $Likes(Ramu, Food)$ - $C_5$: $\neg Likes(Ramu, Food)$ Complementary literals cancel. Result: $$C_9 = \{\} \quad \text{(Empty Clause)}$$ **Step 7: Conclusion (0.5 Marks)** The empty clause $\{\}$ indicates a contradiction. Therefore, the negation of the goal is false, and: $$KB \models Likes(Ramu, Food)$$ Hence, **Ramu likes food** is proven. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Quantifier Scope Error (–2 Marks):** When converting "Every student reads some book" — students often wrongly write $\exists y \forall x$ instead of the correct $\forall x \exists y$. The quantifier order is critical. > 2. **Skipping CNF Conversion (–1 Mark):** In resolution problems, students jump directly to resolution without showing the conversion to Clause Form. Always show the CNF steps explicitly. > 3. **Occur Check Omission (–1 Mark):** In unification, students bind a variable to a term containing itself. Always verify the occur check. > 4. **Confusing Inheritance with Instantiation:** "Robin is-a Bird" is class-level; "Tweety instance Robin" is object-level. Mixing them loses marks. > 5. **Using `|` in Markdown Tables:** Many students use the absolute value symbol `|x|` inside KTU answer tables, which breaks the table syntax. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - [x] **Definition:** KR = formal encoding of knowledge for AI agent reasoning. - [x] **Four Knowledge Types:** Declarative, Procedural, Heuristic, Meta-knowledge. - [x] **Five KR Schemes:** Predicate Logic, Semantic Networks, Frames, Production Rules, Ontologies. - [x] **First-Order Logic Symbols:** $\forall, \exists, \land, \lor, \neg, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$ — memorize all. - [x] **Quantifier Rules:** "All/Every" → $\forall$; "Some/At least one" → $\exists$. - [x] **FOL Conversion Rule:** "No A is B" ≡ $\forall x (A(x) \rightarrow \neg B(x))$. - [x] **Unification:** Always perform **occur check** before binding a variable. - [x] **Resolution:** Convert KB to CNF → negate goal → resolve until empty clause is derived. - [x] **Inheritance:** Travels along `is-a` edges in semantic networks; saves redundancy. - [x] **Frame Slots:** `default`, `if-needed`, `if-added` are the three procedural attachment types. - [x] **Production Rule Cycle:** Match → Conflict Resolution → Act → repeat. - [x] **Ontology Standard:** RDF + RDFS + OWL (Semantic Web stack). - [x] **Key Formula:** $KB \models \alpha$ means $\alpha$ is true in **every** model of KB. - [x] **Inference Types:** Deductive (sound), Inductive (not sound), Abductive (plausible). - [x] **BDI Link:** Beliefs ↔ KB facts; Desires ↔ goal states; Intentions ↔ committed action sequences. - [x] **Common Examples:** MYCIN (medical KR), Cyc (common-sense KR), WordNet (lexical KR). - [x] **Don't forget:** Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ for absolute value, NEVER `|`, in markdown tables.

topics.title: Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Agent Based Intelligent Systems (ABIS)**, an **Object** is a discrete, identifiable, passive entity that resides within the agent's operating environment. Unlike an agent, an object does not possess autonomous behavior, does not initiate actions, and does not have its own goals. Objects are characterized entirely by their **state** (attribute values), **identity**, **spatial/temporal location**, and their **capabilities for interaction** (e.g., being inspected, picked up, consumed, modified, or used as tools by agents).

Formally, an object $O$ in a multi-agent system can be represented as a tuple:

$$O = \langle id, Attr, Loc, Cap, \rho \rangle$$

Where:
- $id$ → Unique identifier of the object
- $Attr = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$ → Set of attributes (e.g., color, weight, size, material)
- $Loc$ → Location in the environment (coordinates, region, container reference)
- $Cap$ → Set of capabilities or affordances (what can be done with the object)
- $\rho: Attr \rightarrow Val$ → Attribute-to-value mapping function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In ABIS, the environment is modelled as a **collection of objects plus agents**. The strict separation of *active* (agents) and *passive* (objects) entities is a foundational concept tested frequently in the ESE. Do **not** confuse objects with agents in descriptive answers.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **smart warehouse** managed by robots:
- The **robots** are the **agents** — they move, decide, plan, pick items, and respond to orders on their own.
- The **boxes, parcels, shelves, conveyor belts, and barcode labels** are the **objects** — they sit there, have properties (weight, destination, fragile/non-fragile), and can be acted upon.

A box doesn't *decide* to be moved. It is *moved*. A barcode doesn't *announce* itself. It is *scanned*. That is the essence of an object in ABIS: it is the **"stuff of the world"** that agents perceive, reason about, and manipulate.

## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Properties of Objects

> [!NOTE]
> The five canonical properties examiners expect:

1. **Identity** — Every object is uniquely identifiable (via `id`).
2. **State** — Object state changes only as a side-effect of agent (or environment) action.
3. **Passivity** — Objects do not exhibit autonomous goal-directed behavior.
4. **Persistence** — Objects exist independently of any single agent's perception of them.
5. **Affordance** — Objects present *action possibilities* to agents (Gibson's ecological affordance).

## 1.4 Objects vs. Agents — The Core Contrast

| Property | Object | Agent |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Autonomy | ❌ Passive | ✅ Autonomous |
| Goals | None | Has explicit/implicit goals |
| Initiates actions | Never | Yes |
| Senses environment | No | Yes (via sensors) |
| Acts on environment | Cannot | Yes (via actuators) |
| Reactivity | None | Reactive / Proactive |
| Has Beliefs/Desires | No | Yes (in BDI model) |

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU trick: *"A thermostat is an agent."* — Examine whether it **senses, decides, and acts autonomously** over time. A plain rock is an object; a robot vacuum is an agent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Objects in Agent Environments

Objects in an ABIS environment can be classified along several orthogonal dimensions. Mastering these classifications is essential for the **ESE Module 1** and for higher-level modules (communication, coordination, planning).

### 2.1.1 By Mutability of State

- **Static (Immovable) Objects** — Walls, landmarks, obstacles. Their location is fixed.
- **Mobile (Movable) Objects** — Boxes, vehicles, parcels. An agent can change their location.
- **Dynamic-State Objects** — Objects whose internal attributes change over time (e.g., a fuel tank's level).
- **Static-State Objects** — Objects with constant attribute values (e.g., a labelled container).

### 2.1.2 By Composability (Containment Hierarchy)

Objects can contain other objects, forming a **containment tree** (or DAG).

$$Parent(O_p) \rightarrow \{O_{c1}, O_{c2}, \ldots, O_{ck}\}$$

A *container* object is one with a non-empty contained set. Examples: a *room* contains *furniture*; a *box* contains *items*.

### 2.1.3 By Affordance Type (Functional Taxonomy)

| Affordance Class | Example Object | Action Enabled |
|------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Pickup-able | Stone, key | `grasp(object)` |
| Open-able | Door, drawer | `open(object)` |
| Read-able | Sign, label | `read(object)` |
| Consume-able | Battery, food | `use(object)` |
| Container | Box, room | `put-in(obj, container)` |
| Tool | Hammer, key | `apply-tool(tool, target)` |
| Obstacle | Wall, tree | `avoid(object)` |

### 2.1.4 By Observability

- **Fully Observable Objects** — All attributes accessible to the agent.
- **Partially Observable Objects** — Only some attributes visible (e.g., contents of a closed box).
- **Hidden Objects** — Existence unknown until revealed (e.g., treasure in a maze).

## 2.2 The Environment–Object–Agent Triangle

In Russell & Norvig's PEAS framework, the environment is **populated by objects**, and the agent's perception function returns features of those objects.

$$Percept: E \rightarrow P, \quad P = \{f(O_1), f(O_2), \ldots\}$$

Where $f$ is a feature-extraction function on each object $O_i$ visible to the agent.

The state transition model of the environment can be written as:

$$S_{t+1} = \delta(S_t, \text{Action}_t)$$

Where $S_t$ is a snapshot of the environment at time $t$, including the state of **all objects**.

## 2.3 Why Objects Matter in ABIS

- **Perceptual Grounding** — Agents build their belief base ($B$) from objects they perceive:
$$B = \{believe(Obj_{id}, Attr, Val)\}$$
- **Planning** — Action preconditions and effects are defined over objects (STRIPS-style: `on(block, table)`, `clear(block)`).
- **Communication** — Agents refer to objects using speech-act content (`FIPA-SL`: `((agent i) (inform j (object-id o1)))`).
- **Coordination** — Task allocation often hinges on object location and ownership.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Description | Exam Frequency |
|---|-------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $O = \langle id, Attr, Loc, Cap, \rho \rangle$ | Formal object tuple | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $B = \{believe(Obj, Attr, Val)\}$ | Belief base over objects | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $S_{t+1} = \delta(S_t, A_t)$ | Environment state transition | ⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $Percept: E \rightarrow P$ | Perception mapping | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $\text{Affordance}(O) = \{a \mid a \text{ is a valid action on } O\}$ | Action possibility set | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Static vs. Mobile vs. Dynamic | Object classification | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Containment hierarchy | Parent-child object relation | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | PEAS framework | Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Objects form the substrate of *Digital Twins*, *IoT systems*, *Robotic Process Automation (RPA)*, and *Autonomous Vehicle perception stacks*. In each, the agent (AI controller) reasons over a digital or physical inventory of objects.

## 2.5 Worked Mini-Example (Conceptual)

**Scenario:** A warehouse robot (agent) operates among **50 boxes** (objects) and **3 doors** (objects).

- 50 boxes → `Mobile, Pickup-able, Container, Partially-Observable` (contents hidden).
- 3 doors → `Static, Open-able, Affords-passage`.
- Belief base sample:
$$B = \{b_1: \text{box}_{17}.\text{location} = (x\!=\!5, y\!=\!3),$$
$$b_2: \text{door}_2.\text{state} = \text{closed}\}$$

The agent's plan involves: `move-to(box17) → pickup(box17) → move-to(door2) → open(door2) → move-through(door2) → drop(box17)`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw World State to Agent Belief About Objects

We will derive, step by step, how an agent's belief about a single object $O$ is constructed from the raw environment state $S$.

### Step 1 — Define the World State

The world state at time $t$ is the snapshot of all entities (agents + objects) in the environment.

$$S_t = \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_m\} \cup \{O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_n\}$$

For every object $O_k$:

$$O_k = \langle id_k, Attr_k, Loc_k, Cap_k, \rho_k \rangle$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Sensor Model

The agent has a (possibly noisy) sensor that produces a percept $p_{k,t}$ for object $O_k$:

$$p_{k,t} = Sensor(S_t, O_k)$$

The sensor extracts a subset of attributes (e.g., location, color) into a feature vector.

### Step 3 — Apply the Perception Function

The agent's perception function $\text{Percept}$ converts the raw sensor reading into an internal symbolic representation:

$$b_{k,t} = Percept(p_{k,t})$$

This yields a *belief token* about $O_k$:

$$b_{k,t} = believe(Agent_i, Obj_k, Attr, Val)$$

### Step 4 — Update the Belief Base

The agent's belief base $B_t$ is updated by *adding* the new belief token (and *retracting* any conflicting older one):

$$B_{t+1} = (B_t \setminus \{believe(\cdot, Obj_k, Attr, Val_{old})\}) \cup \{b_{k,t}\}$$

### Step 5 — Decide Action via Affordance Reasoning

The agent selects actions from the *affordance set* of the perceived object:

$$\text{Affordance}(O_k) = \{a \mid \text{precond}(a, O_k, B_t) = \text{true}\}$$

The chosen action $a^*$ maximizes expected utility:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \text{Affordance}(O_k)} \; EU(a \mid B_t)$$

### Step 6 — Execute and Transition

The action modifies the world state:

$$S_{t+1} = \delta(S_t, a^*)$$

This in turn may change an object's location or attribute:

$$\rho_{k}(attr_j) \leftarrow Val_{new}$$

The loop continues.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Object Properties to Agent Behavior

The following matrix maps *object characteristics* to *required agent capabilities*. This is a **favourite KTU question** in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) sub-questions.

| Object Property | Sample Object | Required Agent Sensor | Required Agent Actuator | KTU-Relevant Concept |
|-----------------|---------------|-----------------------|-------------------------|----------------------|
| Mobile | Box in warehouse | Camera / RFID | Gripper / Wheels | Navigation & Manipulation |
| Container | Drawer | Proximity sensor | Robotic arm | Containment reasoning |
| Hidden | Treasure in maze | Sonar / X-ray | Drill | Exploration / Search |
| Dynamic-State | Fuel tank | Fuel gauge | None directly | Monitoring / Time-decay belief |
| Static | Wall | Lidar | Brake | Obstacle avoidance |
| Consume-able | Battery | Voltage sensor | Connector | Resource-bounded planning |
| Open-able | Door | Vision / Door sensor | Handle-grabber | Affordance-based action selection |
| Tool | Hammer | Vision | Hand | Tool-use planning |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Conceptual Scoring)

**Question (KTU-style):** An agent perceives 8 objects. 3 are static, 5 are mobile. Of the 5 mobile, 2 are containers. How many distinct *pickup-action* affordances exist, assuming each mobile object (whether container or not) affords exactly one pickup action?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mobile objects} &= 5 \\
\text{Pickup affordances} &= 1 \text{ per mobile object} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Total pickup affordances} &= 5 \times 1 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

For *container-related* affordances (e.g., `put-in`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Containers} &= 2 \\
\text{Items that can be put-in (mobile non-containers)} &= 5 - 2 = 3 \\
\text{put-in affordances} &= 2 \times 3 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final aggregate action space from objects:**

$$\text{Affordance Space} = 5 \;(\text{pickup}) + 6 \;(\text{put-in}) = 11 \text{ actions}$$

## 3.4 Common Pitfalls in Derivation-Type Questions

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **Forgetting to retract** outdated beliefs when updating $B_t$.
> 2. **Conflating observation with truth** — sensor noise means $Percept$ is not always equal to the true state.
> 3. **Ignoring object identity persistence** — object $O_k$ keeps the same $id$ even when its state changes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Object–Agent–Environment Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Env["Environment E"]
    subgraph ObjSet["Object Population"]
        O1["Object O1: id=box17, mobile, container"]
        O2["Object O2: id=door2, static, openable"]
        O3["Object O3: id=wall, static, obstacle"]
        O4["Object O4: id=battery, mobile, consumable"]
    end
    subgraph AgentSet["Agent Population"]
        A1["Agent A1: robot arm"]
        A2["Agent A2: forklift"]
    end
    S["Sensor Module"]
    R["Reasoner / Belief Base B"]
    P["Planner / Action Selector"]
    E["Effector / Actuator Module"]

    Env --> S
    S --> R
    R --> P
    P --> E
    E --> Env

    O1 -. perceived by .-> S
    O2 -. perceived by .-> S
    O3 -. perceived by .-> S
    O4 -. perceived by .-> S

    A1 -->|manipulates| O1
    A2 -->|moves| O1
    A1 -->|opens| O2
    A1 -->|avoids| O3
    A1 -->|consumes| O4
```

## 4.2 Containment Hierarchy of Objects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WH["Warehouse"]
    R1["Room A"]
    R2["Room B"]
    SH1["Shelf 1"]
    SH2["Shelf 2"]
    B1["Box X"]
    B2["Box Y"]
    IT1["Item: Bolt"]
    IT2["Item: Nut"]

    WH --> R1
    WH --> R2
    R1 --> SH1
    R1 --> SH2
    SH1 --> B1
    SH1 --> B2
    B1 --> IT1
    B1 --> IT2
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow: From Object to Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Perception Stage"] --> P2["Feature Extraction"]
    P2 --> P3["Object Identification id"]
    P3 --> P4["Attribute Mapping rho"]
    P4 --> P5["Affordance Computation Cap"]
    P5 --> P6["Belief Update B"]
    P6 --> P7["Action Selection a*"]
    P7 --> P8["Actuator Execution"]
    P8 --> P9["World State Transition S"]
    P9 --> P1
```

## 4.4 Classification Decision Tree for Objects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{"Can object move under agent action?"}
    Q -->|Yes| M["Mobile Object"]
    Q -->|No| S["Static Object"]
    M --> Q2{"Does it have internal state changes?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| DM["Dynamic-State Mobile"]
    Q2 -->|No| SM["Static-State Mobile"]
    S --> Q3{"Does it have affordances?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| SA["Static Affordance Object"]
    Q3 -->|No| SI["Static Inert Object e.g. plain wall"]
    M --> Q4{"Can it hold other objects?"}
    Q4 -->|Yes| MC["Mobile Container"]
    Q4 -->|No| MS["Mobile Standalone"]
```

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE, drawing such a **classification tree** or **containment diagram** in your answer script for a 7-mark sub-question fetches **bonus presentation marks** (typical: +1 to +2 marks for clear visual structure).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**Define an object in the context of an agent-based intelligent system. List any four key properties that distinguish an object from an agent.**

**Model Answer:**

An *object* in an agent-based intelligent system is a **discrete, passive entity** residing in the environment, characterized by a unique identity, a set of attributes (state), a location, and a set of affordances (capabilities for interaction). Unlike an agent, an object does not exhibit autonomous, goal-directed behavior.

Four distinguishing properties:

1. **Passivity** — Objects do not initiate actions; they are acted upon.
2. **State only** — Objects have attribute values but no goals, beliefs, or intentions.
3. **No autonomy** — They cannot sense the environment on their own.
4. **Affordance-driven use** — Their interaction is mediated entirely through the actions an agent can perform on them.

**Marks Allocation (3):** Definition (1) + Any 4 properties (2 — 0.5 each).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`
**Explain the concept of "affordance" of an object with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The *affordance* of an object is the set of actions that an agent can legitimately perform on or with that object, given the object's current state and the agent's capabilities. The term (popularized by J. J. Gibson) refers to the action possibilities an object *offers* to an agent.

**Example:** A *closed door* affords the action `open` to an agent with a free hand. Once opened, the same door affords the action `pass-through`. The affordance set is therefore *dynamic* and depends on both the object's state and the agent's situation.

**Formalization:**

$$\text{Affordance}(O, Ag) = \{a \mid \text{precond}(a, O, B_{Ag}) = \text{true}\}$$

**Marks Allocation (3):** Definition (1) + Example (1) + Formalization (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]`

**Q. (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **classification of objects** in an agent environment based on mobility, state dynamics, and affordance. Give an example for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A warehouse robot operates in an environment with **6 mobile boxes**, **2 static shelves**, and **1 door (openable)**. Construct the **object set** using the formal tuple notation. Compute the total action affordance space for a robot that can `pickup`, `place-on-shelf`, and `open-door`. Assume each mobile box affords 1 `pickup` action, each shelf affords `place` for any box, and the door affords 1 `open` action. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

Objects in an agent environment are classified along three primary axes:

**1. By Mobility**
- **Static Objects** — Cannot be relocated (e.g., walls, shelves, doors).
- **Mobile Objects** — Can be relocated by an agent (e.g., boxes, parcels, items).

**2. By State Dynamics**
- **Dynamic-State Objects** — Internal attributes change over time (e.g., a fuel gauge reading).
- **Static-State Objects** — Attribute values remain constant (e.g., a labelled crate).

**3. By Affordance**
- **Pickup-able** — Affords `pickup` (e.g., box, stone).
- **Container** — Affords `put-in` / `take-out` (e.g., shelf, drawer).
- **Open-able** — Affords `open` / `close` (e.g., door).
- **Tool** — Affords `apply-to-target` (e.g., hammer).
- **Obstacle** — Affords `avoid` (e.g., wall).

**Diagram (textual, in answer script):**

```
                  Object
                    |
      ----------------------------
      |              |            |
   Mobility     StateDyn     Affordance
      |              |            |
  Static/Mobile  Dyn/Static  Pick/Container/...
```

**Marks:** 3 sub-classes (3) + examples (2) + diagram (2).

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Build the object set:**

- 6 mobile boxes: $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_6$
- 2 static shelves: $S_1, S_2$
- 1 openable door: $D_1$

Each object as a tuple:

$$O_{B_i} = \langle B_i,\; \{weight, fragility, contents\},\; (x_i, y_i),\; \{\text{pickup}\},\; \rho_{B_i} \rangle$$

$$O_{S_j} = \langle S_j,\; \{capacity, material\},\; (x_j, y_j),\; \{\text{place-on}\},\; \rho_{S_j} \rangle$$

$$O_{D_1} = \langle D_1,\; \{state=\text{closed}\},\; (x_d, y_d),\; \{\text{open}\},\; \rho_{D_1} \rangle$$

**Step 2 — Compute affordance space:**

- `pickup` affordances = 1 per box × 6 boxes = **6**
- `place-on-shelf` affordances = (number of boxes) × (number of shelves) = $6 \times 2 = \mathbf{12}$
- `open-door` affordances = 1 × 1 door = **1**

**Step 3 — Total action affordance space:**

$$|\mathcal{A}_{afford}| = 6 + 12 + 1 = 19 \text{ actions}$$

**Marks:** Tuple construction (3) + Affordance counts (2) + Final aggregation (2).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1]` (Alternative Choice)

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **role of objects in agent perception, planning, and communication**. Use a real-world example (such as a smart warehouse or autonomous vehicle) to illustrate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **static, mobile, container, and consumable objects** in a multi-agent system. For each, give one example and state the corresponding action affordance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

Objects play three pivotal roles in any multi-agent system:

**1. Role in Perception**
Agents build their internal belief base by *sensing* objects. The percept function extracts object features:

$$B_t = \{(Obj_k, Attr_j, Val) \mid Obj_k \text{ is sensed at time } t\}$$

**2. Role in Planning**
STRIPS-style planners define preconditions and effects in terms of object states:
- Precondition: `holding(agent, key)`
- Effect: `door(door2).state = open`

**3. Role in Communication**
FIPA-ACL messages refer to objects explicitly, e.g.:

`(inform :sender A1 :receiver A2 :content (object-id box17 location (5 3)))`

**Real-world Example — Autonomous Vehicle:**
The car (agent) perceives **pedestrians, vehicles, traffic signs, lane markings** (objects). It plans routes (avoid obstacles), and communicates with other vehicles (V2V) by referring to object IDs.

**Marks:** 3 roles × (1.5 each) + Example (1.5).

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

| Class | Definition | Example | Affordance |
|-------|------------|---------|------------|
| **Static** | Cannot be moved by agents | Wall, pillar | `avoid` |
| **Mobile** | Can be relocated | Box, parcel | `pickup`, `move` |
| **Container** | Can hold other objects | Shelf, drawer, box | `put-in`, `take-out` |
| **Consumable** | Depletes on use | Battery, fuel, medicine | `use`, `consume` |

**Marks:** 4 classes × (1.5 each, split as: definition 0.5 + example 0.5 + affordance 0.5).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on "Objects" questions:**
> 1. **Conflating Object with Agent** — A 14-mark question that asks you to *classify* will fail instantly if you list the thermostat as an object without justification.
> 2. **Forgetting the formal tuple** — KTU examiners *expect* the tuple $O = \langle id, Attr, Loc, Cap, \rho \rangle$ for any 7-mark definition. Omit it, lose 2 marks.
> 3. **No diagram in classification answers** — Always include a tree or a table. Bare bullet points get 1–2 marks less than a structured diagram-based answer.
> 4. **Ignoring partial observability** — Many objects are only *partially observable*. Mentioning this adds a high-yield concept token.
> 5. **Wrong affordance linkage** — An affordance is *not* an inherent property of the object alone; it is a **relation** between the object and the agent. Write it as `Affordance(O, Ag)`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **object** in ABIS is a **passive, state-bearing, identifiable entity** in the environment.
- ✅ Formal tuple: $O = \langle id, Attr, Loc, Cap, \rho \rangle$.
- ✅ Objects are classified by **mobility, state dynamics, affordance, observability, and composability**.
- ✅ **Affordance** is a relation between *object* and *agent*, not a property of the object alone.
- ✅ **Containment** creates a hierarchy: warehouse → room → shelf → box → item.
- ✅ Objects contribute to **perception, belief formation, planning preconditions/effects, and inter-agent communication**.
- ✅ The **environment state** $S_t$ is the union of *all agents* and *all objects* at time $t$.
- ✅ **Distinction from agents**: no autonomy, no goals, no reactive loop of its own.
- ✅ Real-world instantiations: **digital twins, IoT, warehouse robots, autonomous vehicles, smart homes**.
- ✅ Frequently tested via: definition (3m), classification with diagram (7m), affordance computation (7m).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Agent-Based Intelligent Systems**, an **Action** is formally defined as a discrete, executable operation that an intelligent agent can perform within its environment to deliberately transition the world from its current state to a subsequent state. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework (drawn from canonical AI literature such as Russell & Norvig and Wooldridge), an action is the fundamental unit of agency — it is the *only* mechanism through which an agent exerts causal influence over its environment.

Mathematically, an action $a$ from an agent's repertoire $Ac$ (Action Set) applied to a state $s$ yields a successor state:

$$ \text{Result}(s, a) = s' $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The "Actions" topic forms the bedrock of the *Agent Function* and *Agent Program*. Without a well-defined action space, no agent — be it reactive, deliberative, or hybrid — can exhibit goal-directed behavior.

## 1.2 Intuition — Real-World Analogy

Think of a **human waiter in a restaurant**:
- The **state** of the world is the current table layout, customer orders, and kitchen status.
- The waiter's **actions** include: `take_order()`, `serve_food()`, `bring_bill()`, `refill_water()`.
- Each action **changes the world** (state transition), and each action has **preconditions** (you cannot `serve_food()` before `take_order()` is completed).

For an agent (the waiter), choosing **which action to execute next** is the essence of intelligence. The action set $Ac$ is like the waiter's *menu of possible moves*, and the agent must intelligently select one based on its perception and goals.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Relevant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Action Vocabulary You MUST Know for KTU Boards:**

| Term | Definition | Standard Symbol |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Action** | A basic operation an agent can execute | $a$ |
| **Action Set / Repertoire** | The complete list of actions available to an agent | $Ac$ |
| **Precondition** | A logical condition that must hold before an action can be executed | $Pre(a)$ |
| **Effect** | The resulting change in the world state after action execution | $Eff(a)$ |
| **State Transition** | The mapping from a state–action pair to a new state | $\text{Result}(s, a)$ |
| **Action Schema** | A generalized variable-based specification of an action (STRIPS-style) | — |

## 1.4 Constants & Quantitative Boundaries

- In deterministic environments, $\text{Result}(s, a)$ is a **single** next state.
- In stochastic environments, $\text{Result}(s, a)$ returns a **probability distribution** over possible next states: $P(s' \mid s, a)$.
- The size of the action space $|Ac|$ is **finite** for almost all KTU board problems unless explicitly stated as continuous.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an Action — Structural Breakdown

An action in an intelligent agent system is not just "something the agent does." It is a structured entity with **three mandatory logical components**:

1. **Precondition ($Pre(a)$)** — The logical guard. It answers: *"Under what conditions is this action legal/allowed?"*
2. **Effect ($Eff(a)$)** — The outcome. It answers: *"What does the world look like after this action?"*
3. **Cost/Utility (optional)** — A real-valued measure of how "expensive" or "desirable" the action is. It answers: *"Is this action worth taking?"*

### Why are preconditions important?
Without preconditions, the agent might attempt to open a door that does not exist, or drive a car with no fuel. The precondition filter prevents **illegal actions** from corrupting the agent's world model.

### Why are effects important?
Effects allow the agent to **simulate** the future. This is the foundation of deliberative planning (e.g., STRIPS, PDDL, and BDI architectures).

## 2.2 Classification of Actions (High-Yield for KTU)

Agent-based systems classify actions along several axes. The following is a board-favorite taxonomy:

### A) By Determinism
- **Deterministic Action:** Given state $s$ and action $a$, the result is guaranteed. Example: A robot arm moving from coordinate $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$ always succeeds.
- **Stochastic Action:** Given state $s$ and action $a$, multiple outcomes are possible, each with a probability. Example: Rolling a die — $P(\text{result} = 6 \mid \text{roll}) = 1/6$.

### B) By Observability of Effects
- **Episodic Action:** Each action is independent; the result does not depend on prior actions. Example: Classifying an email as spam.
- **Sequential Action:** The result depends on the full history of prior actions. Example: Playing chess — every move alters future possibilities.

### C) By Cognitive Depth
- **Primitive Action:** A single, atomic, indivisible operation. Example: `pickup(blockA)`.
- **Composite / Macro Action:** A sequence of primitive actions bundled together. Example: `make_breakfast()` = `get_cereal()` + `pour_milk()` + `get_spoon()`.

## 2.3 Action Representation — The STRIPS Formalism

The **STanford Research Institute Problem Solver (STRIPS)** is the canonical action representation that KTU frequently tests. Each action is expressed as a schema with three lists:

$$ \text{Action: } \texttt{[NAME]}(\text{parameters}) \\ \texttt{PRECONDITION: } \text{Pre}(a) \\ \texttt{EFFECT: } \text{Eff}(a) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example — STRIPS Action Schema:**
> `Fly(p, from, to)`
> **Precondition:** `At(p, from) ∧ Plane(p) ∧ Airport(from) ∧ Airport(to)`
> **Effect:** `¬At(p, from) ∧ At(p, to)`

## 2.4 Action Selection — The Rational Action Principle

The cornerstone of agent intelligence is choosing the *right* action. KTU tests this via the **Performance Measure ($PM$)** and the **Utility Function ($U$)**:

$$ a^* = \arg\max_{a \in Ac} \; U(\text{Result}(s, a)) $$

Where:
- $a^*$ is the **optimal action**.
- $U(\cdot)$ is the utility (desirability) of the resulting state.
- The agent selects the action that **maximizes expected utility** in the current state $s$.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** If a question says *"What is the rational action of the agent?"* — your answer should always mention **maximizing the performance measure**, not just "doing something useful."

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Action Application |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Actions: `accelerate`, `brake`, `turn_left`, `turn_right` — preconditions include lane-clearance checks. |
| **Robotic Process Automation (RPA)** | Actions: `click`, `type`, `extract_text` — preconditions check UI element availability. |
| **Game AI (Chess, Go)** | Actions: legal piece moves — effects alter board state. |
| **Smart Grid Agents** | Actions: `dispatch_power`, `shed_load` — preconditions include demand thresholds. |
| **IoT Home Automation** | Actions: `turn_on_AC`, `lock_door` — preconditions include sensor readings. |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it appears in ~70% of action-related KTU questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | State Transition | $\text{Result}(s, a) = s'$ | Deterministic case |
| 2 | Stochastic Transition | $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | Probability distribution |
| 3 | Optimal Action | $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in Ac} U(\text{Result}(s, a))$ | Rational agent principle |
| 4 | Expected Utility | $EU(a \mid s) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$ | Sum over all next states |
| 5 | Action Constraint | $a \text{ is legal iff } Pre(a) \subseteq s$ | Precondition must hold in current state |
| 6 | Action Schema (STRIPS) | $\texttt{[Act]}(\vec{x}) \; : \; \text{Pre}, \; \text{Eff}$ | Standard notation |
| 7 | Conditional Effect | $a : \text{when } C \text{ then } E$ | Effect triggered only if $C$ holds |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Optimal Action Selection in a Stochastic World

### Problem Setup (KTU-style)
A vacuum-cleaner agent can perform two actions:
- **Action $a_1$ = `Suck`**: Cleans the current square with probability **0.8**; otherwise leaves dirt unchanged.
- **Action $a_2$ = `MoveRight`**: Moves to the next square with probability **0.9**; otherwise stays put.

The utility of a clean square is **+10**, and the utility of a dirty square is **−5**. The current state $s_0$ is *dirty*. Compute the expected utility of each action and determine the rational action.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1: Define the state space.**
The relevant world after either action is whether the square is clean or dirty.

**Step 2: Compute the state transition probabilities for $a_1 = \texttt{Suck}$.**

$$ P(s' = \text{Clean} \mid s_0 = \text{Dirty}, a_1) = 0.8 $$
$$ P(s' = \text{Dirty} \mid s_0 = \text{Dirty}, a_1) = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 $$

**Step 3: Apply the Expected Utility formula.**

$$ EU(a_1 \mid s_0) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s_0, a_1) \cdot U(s') $$

Substituting the values:

$$ EU(a_1 \mid s_0) = (0.8 \cdot U(\text{Clean})) + (0.2 \cdot U(\text{Dirty})) $$

$$ EU(a_1 \mid s_0) = (0.8 \cdot 10) + (0.2 \cdot (-5)) $$

$$ EU(a_1 \mid s_0) = 8.0 - 1.0 = 7.0 $$

**Step 4: Compute the Expected Utility for $a_2 = \texttt{MoveRight}$.**

The `MoveRight` action does not clean. Whether dirty or clean, the square's status doesn't change. The utility is therefore that of a dirty square (since current state is dirty and `MoveRight` does not modify dirtiness):

$$ EU(a_2 \mid s_0) = 1.0 \cdot U(\text{Dirty}) = 1.0 \cdot (-5) = -5.0 $$

**Step 5: Apply the Rational Action Selection Rule.**

$$ a^* = \arg\max_{a \in Ac} EU(a \mid s_0) = \arg\max \{ 7.0, \; -5.0 \} = a_1 \; (\texttt{Suck}) $$

### Result Box
> ✅ **The rational action is $a_1$ = `Suck`, with Expected Utility $EU = 7.0$ units.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: STRIPS Action Schema for a Blocks-World Scenario

### Problem Setup
In the classic blocks world, derive the STRIPS schema for the action `Stack(x, y)` — placing block $x$ on top of block $y$.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Construction

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**

The action depends on two blocks: $x$ (the block to be moved) and $y$ (the destination block).

**Step 2: Identify the preconditions.**
For `Stack(x, y)` to be legal, the following must hold:
- Block $x$ must currently be held by the robot (i.e., the arm has $x$).
- Block $y$ must be clear (no other block on top of $y$).

Therefore:

$$ Pre(\texttt{Stack}(x, y)) = \{ \texttt{Holding}(x), \; \texttt{Clear}(y) \} $$

**Step 3: Identify the effects.**
After stacking, the robot arm is no longer holding $x$, and $x$ is now on top of $y$:

$$ Eff(\texttt{Stack}(x, y)) = \{ \neg \texttt{Holding}(x), \; \neg \texttt{Clear}(y), \; \texttt{On}(x, y) \} $$

**Step 4: Final Schema.**

$$ \boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{Action: Stack}(x, y) \\
&\texttt{Precondition: Holding}(x) \wedge \texttt{Clear}(y) \\
&\texttt{Effect: } \neg \texttt{Holding}(x) \wedge \neg \texttt{Clear}(y) \wedge \texttt{On}(x, y)
\end{aligned}
} $$

### Analytical Insight
> [!NOTE]
> The **negation of preconditions that are "consumed"** (like `Clear(y)` and `Holding(x)`) is what makes STRIPS a **delete-list + add-list** formalism. This is a *favorite 7-mark board question* — practice writing schemas for `Unstack`, `Pickup`, and `Putdown` as well.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Action Type for a Domain

| Agent Domain | Recommended Action Class | Rationale |
|--------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| Spam Email Classifier | Episodic + Deterministic | Each email is independent; outcome is binary. |
| Autonomous Car | Sequential + Stochastic | History matters; outcomes are probabilistic. |
| Chess Engine | Sequential + Deterministic (within rules) | History matters; rules fix outcomes. |
| Stock Trading Bot | Sequential + Stochastic | History + market uncertainty. |
| Robotic Arm Pick-and-Place | Sequential + Stochastic | Sensor noise and slip possible. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agent–Action–Environment Loop

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical interaction between an **Agent's perception**, its **action selection**, and the **environment's state transition**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    env1["ENVIRONMENT"] -->|Percept| sens1["SENSORS"]
    sens1 --> perc1["Percept Sequence P"]
    perc1 --> agent1["AGENT FUNCTION"]
    agent1 -->|Action Selected| act1["ACTION a in Ac"]
    act1 --> eff1["EFFECT on Environment"]
    eff1 -->|New State s prime| env1
    agent1 --> perf1["PERFORMANCE MEASURE PM"]
    perf1 --> eval1["Evaluates Utility U"]
    eval1 --> agent1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
1. The **Environment** emits percepts to the agent's sensors.
2. The **Agent Function** maps the percept history to an action $a \in Ac$.
3. The **Action** is executed and produces an **Effect** on the environment.
4. The new state $s'$ feeds back into the environment, closing the loop.
5. The **Performance Measure** evaluates utility and guides the agent function's choices.

---

## 4.2 Action Selection Decision Pipeline

This diagram shows the internal decision-making process an agent uses to pick an action:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateA["Current State s"] --> condA{"Precondition Pre a Satisfied?"}
    condA -->|No| skipA["Action REJECTED"]
    condA -->|Yes| utilA["Compute EU a given s"]
    utilA --> compA["Compare EU across all a in Ac"]
    compA --> maxA{"argmax EU"}
    maxA -->|Selected| actA["Execute Optimal Action a star"]
    actA --> transA["Environment Transitions to s prime"]
```

---

## 4.3 STRIPS Action Schema Structural View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    actNode["ACTION SCHEMA: Stack x, y"] --> preNode["PRECONDITIONS"]
    actNode --> effNode["EFFECTS"]
    preNode --> pre1["Holding x"]
    preNode --> pre2["Clear y"]
    effNode --> eff1["Not Holding x"]
    effNode --> eff2["Not Clear y"]
    effNode --> eff3["On x, y"]
```

---

## 4.4 Hierarchical Action Decomposition (Primitive vs. Macro)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    macroA["MACRO ACTION: Make Breakfast"] --> prim1["Primitive: Get Cereal"]
    macroA --> prim2["Primitive: Pour Milk"]
    macroA --> prim3["Primitive: Get Spoon"]
    macroA --> prim4["Primitive: Place Bowl on Table"]
    prim1 --> effP1["Effect: CerealInBowl"]
    prim2 --> effP2["Effect: MilkInBowl"]
    prim3 --> effP3["Effect: SpoonAvailable"]
    prim4 --> effP4["Effect: SetupComplete"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: Define an "action" in the context of an intelligent agent. Differentiate between a primitive action and a composite (macro) action with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** An action is a discrete, executable operation performed by an intelligent agent that causes a transition from the current state $s$ to a new state $s'$ in the environment. Formally, $\text{Result}(s, a) = s'$.
- **Primitive Action (1 Mark):** A primitive action is atomic and cannot be decomposed further. *Example:* `Pickup(blockA)` in a robotic arm.
- **Composite Action (1 Mark):** A composite (macro) action is a sequence of primitive actions bundled into a single high-level operation. *Example:* `MakeBreakfast()` = `GetCereal()` + `PourMilk()` + `GetSpoon()`.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]
**Q: What is the role of a precondition in agent action execution? Illustrate with a STRIPS-style example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Role (1 Mark):** A precondition specifies the logical conditions that must hold in the current state for an action to be legally executable. It acts as a guard clause preventing illegal state transitions.
- **Why Needed (1 Mark):** Without preconditions, an agent could attempt infeasible actions (e.g., opening a non-existent door), corrupting its world model and leading to plan failures.
- **STRIPS Example (1 Mark):** For the action `Fly(plane, A, B)`, the precondition is `At(plane, A) ∧ Airport(A) ∧ Airport(B)`. The plane can only take off if it is currently at airport A and both airports exist.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Marks are deducted when students write only the *definition* and skip the **formula/example**.
> - Always include the **STRIPS schema** OR the **utility calculation** in any 14-mark action question.
> - Vague phrases like *"the agent does something useful"* fetch **zero marks**. Use **precise terminology**: *precondition, effect, state transition, expected utility*.

---

### 📝 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the agent–environment interaction loop with emphasis on action execution. Discuss the different categories of actions (deterministic vs. stochastic, episodic vs. sequential) with suitable examples. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

**Model Solution:**

The agent–environment interaction loop is the foundational model for any intelligent agent. It consists of the following steps:

1. **Perception:** The agent observes the environment through its sensors, receiving a percept.
2. **Interpretation:** The percept is combined with prior history to form a percept sequence.
3. **Action Selection:** The agent function $f: P^* \rightarrow Ac$ maps the percept history to a specific action $a \in Ac$.
4. **Action Execution:** The action is dispatched to the environment via actuators.
5. **State Transition:** The environment transitions from state $s$ to $s' = \text{Result}(s, a)$.
6. **Evaluation:** The performance measure evaluates the utility of the resulting state.

> *(Examiner expects the loop diagram — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for 3 marks reserved for the diagram.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Categories of Actions

**Deterministic Actions (3 Marks):**
A deterministic action yields a guaranteed next state for any given state–action pair. There is no randomness.
- *Example:* A calculator agent computing $2 + 3$ always yields 5.

**Stochastic Actions (2 Marks):**
A stochastic action yields a probability distribution over possible next states, i.e., $P(s' \mid s, a)$.
- *Example:* A weather-predicting agent calling an API has a 70% chance of receiving the correct forecast.

**Episodic vs. Sequential (2 Marks):**
- *Episodic:* Each action is independent; previous actions do not affect the current decision. *Example:* Image classification.
- *Sequential:* The current decision depends on all prior actions. *Example:* A chess move depends on the entire game history.

---

### 📝 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: Explain the STRIPS representation for actions. Construct the STRIPS schema for the actions `Pickup(x)`, `Putdown(x)`, and `Stack(x, y)` in a blocks world. Also, illustrate how an agent selects an action using the principle of maximum expected utility. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: STRIPS Schema Construction

**Model Solution:**

The STRIPS (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver) formalism represents each action as a schema with **three components**: Name, Precondition, and Effect.
