**Schema for `Pickup(x)`:**
- **Precondition:** `OnTable(x) ∧ Clear(x) ∧ ArmEmpty`
- **Effect:** `¬OnTable(x) ∧ ¬ArmEmpty ∧ Holding(x)`

**Schema for `Putdown(x)`:**
- **Precondition:** `Holding(x)`
- **Effect:** `¬Holding(x) ∧ OnTable(x) ∧ ArmEmpty ∧ Clear(x)`

**Schema for `Stack(x, y)`:**
- **Precondition:** `Holding(x) ∧ Clear(y)`
- **Effect:** `¬Holding(x) ∧ ¬Clear(y) ∧ On(x, y)`

> *(Full marks require all three schemas; each is worth ~2 marks, with 1 mark reserved for explaining the precondition-effect structure.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Action Selection via Maximum Expected Utility

**Model Solution:**

In a stochastic environment, the agent cannot be sure which next state will result from an action. Therefore, the rational agent chooses the action that **maximizes the expected utility** $EU$ across all possible next states.

**Formula:**

$$ a^* = \arg\max_{a \in Ac} \; EU(a \mid s) = \arg\max_{a \in Ac} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s') $$

**Selection Procedure:**
1. Identify the current state $s$.
2. List all legal actions in $Ac$ (those whose preconditions are satisfied).
3. For each legal action $a$, compute $EU(a \mid s)$ by summing $P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$ over all reachable $s'$.
4. Pick the action $a^*$ that yields the highest expected utility.
5. Execute $a^*$ and observe the new state $s'$.

**Illustrative Example (3 Marks):**
If `Pickup(A)` yields Expected Utility $= 5$ and `Stack(A, B)` yields Expected Utility $= 8$, the rational agent selects `Stack(A, B)`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These 10 Bullets:**

- ⭐ An **action** is a discrete operation an agent performs to transition the environment from state $s$ to $s'$.
- ⭐ The formal state transition is: $\text{Result}(s, a) = s'$.
- ⭐ In **stochastic** environments, transitions are probability distributions: $P(s' \mid s, a)$.
- ⭐ Every action has **three components**: Precondition, Effect, and optional Cost.
- ⭐ **STRIPS** is the canonical action representation (Name, Precondition, Effect).
- ⭐ The **rational action principle** is: $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in Ac} U(\text{Result}(s, a))$.
- ⭐ **Expected utility** is used when outcomes are uncertain: $EU(a \mid s) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$.
- ⭐ **Primitive actions** are atomic; **macro/composite actions** are sequences of primitives.
- ⭐ **Episodic actions** are independent of history; **Sequential actions** depend on history.
- ⭐ KTU Boards love: STRIPS schema construction (7 marks) + Maximum Expected Utility calculation (7 marks) — be ready for this combination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Events

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal architecture of an **Intelligent Agent**, an **Event** is defined as any *temporal occurrence* in the agent's operating environment, internal state, or inter-agent communication channel that is significant enough to alter the agent's belief base, trigger an action, or initiate a deliberation cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (PCCAT601):** An event is a *discrete, instantaneous, semantically meaningful change* in the state of the world, the agent, or the multi-agent system (MAS) that is detectable, classifiable, and capable of being communicated across agents via a well-defined event-bus or message-passing protocol.

Mathematically, an event can be expressed as a tuple:

$$E = \langle t, \sigma, \kappa, \delta \rangle$$

Where:
- $t$ = timestamp or temporal index at which the event occurs
- $\sigma$ = the source (environment sensor, peer agent, internal state monitor)
- $\kappa$ = the event class/type (e.g., `BUTTON_CLICKED`, `RESOURCE_DEPLETED`)
- $\delta$ = the event payload (a set of key-value attributes or a structured data object)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Doorbell and the House Occupant**
> Imagine you (the **Agent**) are inside a house working on something. You cannot constantly look at the front door. The **Doorbell** is the *event generator*. The **Sound of the Bell Ringing** is the *event itself*. The **Bell Wire** is the *event channel*. The moment the bell rings, you *stop your current action*, *interpret* why it rang (someone at the door), and *react* appropriately (open the door). In agent terms: **Event Detection → Event Interpretation → Action Selection**.

This analogy captures the three core properties of events in intelligent systems:
1. **Asynchronicity** — The agent is not polling; the event *pushes* itself onto the agent's attention.
2. **Atomicity** — A doorbell ring is a single, indivisible occurrence.
3. **Reactivity Enablement** — Events are the *primary trigger* for reactive and BDI (Belief-Desire-Intention) agents.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classification of Events

| Metric | Standard Value / Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Event Processing Latency | measured in **milliseconds (ms)** | Time from event occurrence to agent response |
| Event Throughput | measured in **EPS (Events Per Second)** | Capacity of the event-processing engine |
| Event Window Size | $\vert W \vert$ | Number of events buffered in temporal aggregation |

> [!TIP]
> Events are classified in KTU syllabus under three primary headings: **(i) Simple (Primitive) Events, (ii) Composite (Complex) Events, and (iii) Temporal Events**. Master all three — examiners frequently mix them in Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Lifecycle of an Event (Step-by-Step Logic)

Every event in a multi-agent intelligent system traverses a deterministic 5-stage pipeline:

1. **Generation (Emission)** — A producer (sensor, another agent, or an internal monitor) emits an event tuple $E$.
2. **Propagation (Transport)** — The event is transmitted across the **event bus**, **blackboard**, or **FIPA-ACL message channel** to all subscribed consumers.
3. **Detection (Reception)** — The agent's *event listener* or *sensor module* captures the event and places it in the **event queue**.
4. **Filtering & Pattern Matching** — The agent's inference engine checks if $E$ matches a registered pattern $P_i$ in its rule base.
5. **Trigger (Activation)** — If matched, the associated **Event-Condition-Action (ECA)** rule is fired: $E \land C \rightarrow A$.

## 2.2 Event-Condition-Action (ECA) Rule — The Core Operational Paradigm

The ECA paradigm is the *heart* of event-driven agent reasoning. It is the standard formalism taught under PCCAT601.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECA Rule Format:** $ON\ event\ IF\ condition\ DO\ action$
>
> Formally: $\forall E \in \mathcal{E}, \text{ if } \text{cond}(E) = \text{true} \Rightarrow \text{exec}(act_E)$

Where:
- $\mathcal{E}$ = the set of all observable events
- $\text{cond}(E)$ = a Boolean predicate evaluated over the event payload and agent beliefs
- $\text{act}_E$ = a procedural action that mutates the agent's belief base or environment

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Notation | Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \langle t, \sigma, \kappa, \delta \rangle$ | Event tuple representation | Defining an event formally |
| 2 | $ON\ E\ IF\ C\ DO\ A$ | ECA rule syntax | Reactive agent rule base |
| 3 | $E_c = E_1 \oplus E_2$ | Composite event (OR-disjunction) | Combining two event types |
| 4 | $E_c = E_1 \otimes E_2$ | Composite event (AND-conjunction) | Sequencing two events |
| 5 | $\Delta t_{proc} = t_{react} - t_{emit}$ | Event processing latency | Real-time agent performance |
| 6 | $\mathcal{E}_w = \{E_i \mid t_i \in [t-w, t]\}$ | Sliding window of events | Temporal event aggregation |
| 7 | $\mathcal{P}(E) = \text{match}(E, P_i)$ | Event-pattern match function | Filtering and dispatch |
| 8 | $\text{cond}(E) = \bigwedge_{j=1}^{n} c_j(E)$ | Composite condition as conjunction | Multi-condition triggering |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **Composite Events** (multiple event instances combined) with **Complex Events** (events with complex internal structure/payload). In KTU answer scripts, examiners expect the distinction: *Composite* = assembled from primitives; *Complex* = inherently hierarchical/structured.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Events are foundational in:
- **Autonomous Vehicles** — detecting obstacles, traffic signal changes
- **IoT Smart Systems** — sensor event streams
- **Trading Agents** — market tick events
- **Robotics** — bumper sensors, vision-based events
- **Smart Grids** — load-balancing event triggers
- **Healthcare Monitoring** — patient vital-sign threshold breach events

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Formal Transition from Raw Observation to Event

An intelligent agent does not directly perceive an event — it perceives a **raw signal** $s(t)$ from a sensor and *interprets* it into an event. This derivation walks through that transformation.

### Step 1: Sensor Signal Acquisition

The agent's sensor module samples an environmental signal continuously (or at a sampling rate $f_s$ Hz). The raw observation at time $t$ is:

$$s(t) = s_0 + \eta(t)$$

Here, $s_0$ is the true underlying physical quantity (e.g., temperature, light intensity) and $\eta(t)$ represents additive stochastic noise.

### Step 2: Threshold-Based Event Detection

To convert a continuous signal into a discrete event, the agent applies a **threshold function** $\theta(\cdot)$:

$$E_{\text{fired}} = 
\begin{cases} 
\text{true} & \text{if } s(t) \geq \theta_{\text{upper}} \;\lor\; s(t) \leq \theta_{\text{lower}} \\
\text{false} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

This is a *hysteresis-based* detection — a *true* event is fired only when the signal crosses a boundary.

### Step 3: Event Tuple Construction

Once a trigger is detected, the event tuple is constructed by the **event factory** component of the agent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \langle t, \sigma, \kappa, \delta \rangle \\
  &= \langle t_{\text{now}},\ \text{Sensor}_{i},\ \text{THRESHOLD\_BREACH},\ \{\text{value} = s(t),\ \text{threshold} = \theta\} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: ECA Rule Evaluation

The event is dispatched to the rule engine, which evaluates a candidate rule $\mathcal{R}_k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cond}(\mathcal{R}_k, E) &= \bigwedge_{j=1}^{n} c_j(E) \\
&= c_1(E) \land c_2(E) \land \dots \land c_n(E)
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\text{cond}(\mathcal{R}_k, E) = \text{true}$, the associated action $\text{act}_{\mathcal{R}_k}$ is enqueued for execution.

### Step 5: Event Processing Latency

The total time from event occurrence to action initiation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{proc} &= t_{\text{react}} - t_{\text{emit}} \\
                &= (t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{emit}}) + (t_{\text{dispatch}} - t_{\text{detect}}) + (t_{\text{react}} - t_{\text{dispatch}}) \\
                &= \Delta t_{\text{sens}} + \Delta t_{\text{comm}} + \Delta t_{\text{reason}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **real-time agents**, KTU examiners expect: $\Delta t_{proc} \leq \Delta t_{\text{Deadline}}$ (a hard real-time constraint).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event Types with KTU Exam Relevance

| Event Type | Definition | Operator | Example | Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primitive** | Atomic, single occurrence | — | `TEMP > 100` | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Composite (Disjunction)** | $E_1 \oplus E_2$ | OR | `TEMP_HIGH or HUMIDITY_HIGH` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Composite (Sequence)** | $E_1 \rightarrow E_2$ | Then | `MOTION then DOOR_OPEN` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Temporal** | Within time window $\Delta t$ | Within | `TEMP_HIGH within 5s` | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Complex** | Hierarchically structured | Tree | Full BDI belief update | ⭐⭐ |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Composite Event Algebra (KTU-style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Construct the formal composite event expression for the rule: *"Trigger an alarm if motion is detected AND the door opens within 10 seconds."*

**Solution using Event Algebra:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{composite}} &= E_{\text{motion}} \otimes_{\Delta t=10s} E_{\text{door}} \\
\text{where } E_{\text{motion}} &= \langle t_1,\ \text{PirSensor},\ \text{MOTION\_DETECTED},\ \emptyset \rangle \\
\text{and } E_{\text{door}} &= \langle t_2,\ \text{MagneticSwitch},\ \text{DOOR\_OPENED},\ \{\text{zone} = \text{front}\} \rangle \\
\text{Constraint: } t_2 - t_1 &\leq 10\text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

The ECA rule is therefore:

$$ON\ (E_{\text{motion}} \otimes_{\Delta t=10s} E_{\text{door}})\ \ IF\ (\text{alarm\_enabled} = \text{true})\ \ DO\ \text{actuate\_alarm()}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Event Lifecycle in an Intelligent Agent — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment / Peer Agent] -->|Emits raw signal| B[Sensor Module]
    B -->|Continuous observation s of t| C[Threshold Detector]
    C -->|Crossing detected| D[Event Factory]
    D -->|Constructs E = t, sigma, kappa, delta| E[Event Bus / Blackboard]
    E -->|Dispatches| F[Event Listener in Agent]
    F -->|Enqueues in Event Queue| G[Pattern Matcher]
    G -->|Compares E with rule patterns| H{Rule Match Found?}
    H -->|Yes, cond E is true| I[Activate ECA Action]
    H -->|No match| J[Discard or Log Event]
    I --> K[Update Belief Base / Mutate Environment]
    K --> L[Agent Action Effectuatore]
    J --> M[Event Audit Log]
```

## 4.2 ECA Rule Processing Architecture — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EventSource
        ES1[Sensor Event]
        ES2[Message Event]
        ES3[Internal Event]
    end
    subgraph EventDispatcher
        ED1[Event Classifier]
        ED2[Priority Queue]
    end
    subgraph AgentReasoningCore
        AR1[Belief Base]
        AR2[ECA Rule Engine]
        AR3[Action Scheduler]
    end
    ES1 --> ED1
    ES2 --> ED1
    ES3 --> ED1
    ED1 --> ED2
    ED2 --> AR2
    AR1 --> AR2
    AR2 -->|Matched Rule with cond true| AR3
    AR3 -->|exec act E| OUT[External Action / Actuator]
```

## 4.3 Composite Event Composition — Mermaid Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[E1: Motion Detected] --> C1{AND within 10s}
    P2[E2: Door Opened] --> C1
    P3[E3: Window Broken] --> C2{OR operator}
    P4[E4: Glass Vibration] --> C2
    C1 --> R1[Composite: Motion-then-Door Event]
    C2 --> R2[Composite: Window-or-Glass Event]
    R1 --> F[Final Composite Event E_final]
    R2 --> F
    F --> ALARM[Trigger Alarm Action]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Textual Fallback Diagram)

For a deeper architectural view of an **Event-Driven Agent**, the data flow is:

```
[External Environment] 
        │
        ▼
[Layer 1: Perception] ── Raw signal s(t)
        │
        ▼
[Layer 2: Event Abstraction] ── Converts to E = ⟨t, σ, κ, δ⟩
        │
        ▼
[Layer 3: Event Bus / Blackboard] ── Publishes & subscribes
        │
        ▼
[Layer 4: Deliberation Engine] ── BDI / Rule-based reasoning
        │
        ▼
[Layer 5: Action Subsystem] ── Executes act_E
        │
        ▼
[Feedback to Environment] ── Mutates world state
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an event in the context of an intelligent agent. List any four event types recognized in multi-agent systems.**

**Model Answer:**
An event in an intelligent agent system is a discrete, timestamped occurrence that signifies a meaningful change in the agent's environment, internal state, or inter-agent communication. Formally, it is represented as a tuple $E = \langle t, \sigma, \kappa, \delta \rangle$.

Four event types:
1. **Primitive (Simple) Events** — atomic, indivisible occurrences (e.g., a sensor threshold breach).
2. **Composite Events** — assembled from multiple primitive events using operators (AND, OR, sequence).
3. **Temporal Events** — events constrained by time windows $\Delta t$.
4. **Internal (Cognitive) Events** — generated by the agent's own deliberation (e.g., goal generated, plan failed).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is an ECA rule? Write its general form and explain each component in one line.**

**Model Answer:**
An **ECA (Event-Condition-Action) rule** is the standard reactive reasoning formalism in agent systems.

General form:

$$ON\ event\ IF\ condition\ DO\ action$$

- **ON event** — specifies the event pattern $P(E)$ that triggers the rule.
- **IF condition** — a Boolean predicate $\text{cond}(E)$ evaluated against the agent's current belief base.
- **DO action** — the procedure $\text{act}_E$ executed when both event and condition are satisfied.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Composite & Temporal Event Analysis

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain in detail the classification of events in an intelligent agent system. Differentiate clearly between **composite events** and **complex events** with suitable examples.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A smart home agent must fire an **intrusion alert** if *any one* of the following occurs:
- (i) Motion is detected AND door opens within 5 seconds
- (ii) Window-break sound is detected between 22:00 and 06:00

Formulate the **ECA rule(s)** and express the composite events using event algebra.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Event Lifecycle & ECA Rule Engine

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With a neat block diagram, describe the complete **lifecycle of an event** in an intelligent agent — from emission in the environment to action execution. Explain the role of the **event bus** and **pattern matcher**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Consider a temperature monitoring agent in a chemical plant with the following rule:
*"If temperature exceeds 90°C AND pressure exceeds 5 bar, then activate the emergency cooling system within 2 seconds."*
Translate this into a formal ECA rule and compute the maximum allowed $\Delta t_{proc}$ for real-time compliance.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

**(a) Classification of Events:**

Events are classified as follows in PCCAT601:

- **Primitive Events:** Atomic, single-source events. Cannot be decomposed further. Example: `TEMP > 90°C`.
- **Composite Events:** Composed using event algebra operators ($\oplus, \otimes, \rightarrow$) from two or more primitives. Example: $E_1 \otimes E_2$ (motion AND door).
- **Temporal Events:** Time-constrained events, evaluated within a window $\Delta t$. Example: `E within 10s`.
- **Complex Events:** Hierarchically structured events with nested semantic content, often used in CEP (Complex Event Processing). Example: a multi-step attack pattern recognized across logs.

| Aspect | Composite Event | Complex Event |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Flat combination of primitives | Hierarchical, nested structure |
| Operator | Event algebra ($\oplus, \otimes$) | Tree/graph pattern matching |
| Example | Motion AND Door | Multi-stage cyber-attack signature |

**(b) ECA Formulation for Smart Home Intrusion:**

Event primitives defined:
- $E_1$ = Motion Detected
- $E_2$ = Door Opened
- $E_3$ = Window-Break Sound

Composite event construction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{composite1}} &= E_1 \otimes_{\Delta t=5s} E_2 \quad \text{(i: motion-then-door within 5s)} \\
E_{\text{composite2}} &= E_3 \otimes_{t \in [22:00, 06:00]} \quad \text{(ii: window-break in night window)} \\
E_{\text{final}} &= E_{\text{composite1}} \oplus E_{\text{composite2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

ECA rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{R}_1 &: ON\ (E_1 \otimes_{\Delta t=5s} E_2)\ \ IF\ (\text{home\_mode} = \text{armed})\ \ DO\ \text{raise\_intrusion\_alert()} \\
\mathcal{R}_2 &: ON\ (E_3 \otimes_{t \in [22:00, 06:00]})\ \ IF\ (\text{home\_mode} = \text{armed})\ \ DO\ \text{raise\_intrusion\_alert()}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Solution to Question B

**(a) Event Lifecycle:**

(Refer to **Section 4.1** Mermaid diagram for the full lifecycle.)

Five stages:
1. **Emission** — sensor/agent emits raw signal
2. **Abstraction** — raw signal → event tuple $E$
3. **Propagation** — event travels via bus/blackboard
4. **Detection & Filtering** — pattern matcher checks against rule base
5. **Activation** — matched ECA rule fires its action

**Role of Event Bus:** Decouples producers from consumers; supports publish-subscribe semantics.

**Role of Pattern Matcher:** Compares incoming $E$ against registered patterns $P_i$; computes $\mathcal{P}(E) = \text{match}(E, P_i)$.

**(b) ECA Formulation for Chemical Plant:**

Event primitives:
- $E_T$ = `TEMP > 90°C` (timestamp $t_T$)
- $E_P$ = `PRESSURE > 5 bar` (timestamp $t_P$)

Composite event:

$$E_{\text{critical}} = E_T \otimes E_P$$

ECA rule:

$$
\mathcal{R}_{\text{cool}}:\ ON\ (E_T \otimes E_P)\ \ IF\ (\text{cooling\_system\_online} = \text{true})\ \ DO\ \text{activate\_emergency\_cooling()}
$$

Real-time constraint calculation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{\text{proc}} &= t_{\text{react}} - t_{\text{emit}} \\
\Delta t_{\text{Deadline}} &= 2 \text{ seconds (specified)} \\
\text{Hard real-time requirement: }\ \Delta t_{proc} &\leq 2 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Decomposition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{proc} &= \Delta t_{\text{sens}} + \Delta t_{\text{comm}} + \Delta t_{\text{reason}} \\
\text{If each stage budgeted: } &\Delta t_{\text{sens}} \leq 0.5\text{s},\ \Delta t_{\text{comm}} \leq 0.3\text{s},\ \Delta t_{\text{reason}} \leq 1.2\text{s} \\
\text{Total: } &0.5 + 0.3 + 1.2 = 2.0\text{ s} \quad \checkmark \text{(meets deadline)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in 'Events' Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating composite with complex events** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the distinction is missing.
> 2. **Forgetting the timestamp $t$** in the event tuple — many students write $E = \langle \sigma, \kappa, \delta \rangle$ only. Always include the temporal component.
> 3. **Skipping the time constraint** in temporal composite events — when writing $E_1 \otimes_{\Delta t} E_2$, always state the $\Delta t$ explicitly.
> 4. **Not showing ECA rule syntax correctly** — must be $ON\ event\ IF\ condition\ DO\ action$, not a free-form sentence.
> 5. **Omitting the event bus from the lifecycle diagram** — this is a 2-mark loss in diagram questions.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Event tuple:** $E = \langle t, \sigma, \kappa, \delta \rangle$ — must include **timestamp, source, class, payload**.
- 📌 **ECA Rule:** $ON\ event\ IF\ condition\ DO\ action$ — the **gold standard** reactive formalism in PCCAT601.
- 📌 **Composite Operators:** $\oplus$ (OR), $\otimes$ (AND/sequence), $\rightarrow$ (temporal sequence).
- 📌 **Event Lifecycle:** 5 stages — Emission → Abstraction → Propagation → Detection → Activation.
- 📌 **Latency Formula:** $\Delta t_{proc} = \Delta t_{\text{sens}} + \Delta t_{\text{comm}} + \Delta t_{\text{reason}}$.
- 📌 **Real-time Constraint:** $\Delta t_{proc} \leq \Delta t_{\text{Deadline}}$.
- 📌 **Composite vs Complex:** Composite = flat combination; Complex = hierarchical structure.
- 📌 **Event Bus Role:** Decouples producers from consumers via publish-subscribe.
- 📌 **Pattern Matcher:** Computes $\mathcal{P}(E) = \text{match}(E, P_i)$ against rule base.
- 📌 **High-yield exam topics:** ECA rule syntax, composite event algebra, lifecycle diagram, latency computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Planning Problem

In the context of **Agent Based Intelligent Systems**, a **Planning Problem** is the computational problem of synthesising a **sequence of actions** (a *plan*) that an intelligent agent, situated in an initial world state and acting through a known repertoire of actions, can execute to transform the environment from the initial state into a state in which the agent's **goal condition** is satisfied.

Formally, a classical (STRIPS-style) planning problem is defined as the tuple:

$$\Pi = (S, A, \gamma, s_0, G)$$

where:
- $S$ = finite (or computable) set of world states,
- $A$ = finite set of ground actions (or action schemas),
- $\gamma : S \times A \rightarrow S$ = deterministic state-transition function,
- $s_0 \in S$ = the **initial state**,
- $G \subseteq S$ = the **goal condition** (set of goal states, or a logical formula).

A **solution (plan)** is a finite sequence of actions $\pi = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$ such that the state sequence produced by deterministic application satisfies $s_n \in G$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A planning problem is fundamentally a *search* problem, but the state-space is **exponentially large** (factored representation) and the **actions have structure** (preconditions + effects). This is what distinguishes *planning* from generic state-space search.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Recipe"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you are in a messy kitchen, and you want to bake a cake. The kitchen is your **initial state** (flour, eggs, sugar scattered around, oven cold). A **recipe** is a **plan**. Each step in the recipe ("preheat oven", "mix flour and eggs") is an **action**, but each step has **preconditions** (you cannot bake until the oven is hot) and **effects** (mixing produces batter). Planning is the act of sequencing these steps to reach the **goal state** (cake on the table). The agent is the chef.

Similarly, an AI planning system asks: *"Given what the world looks like now, what series of actions will get me to a state where my goal is achieved?"*

---

## 1.3 Components of a Planning Problem (KTU High-Yield)

A planning problem is specified by **four core components** that the KTU examiner loves to test:

| Component | Meaning | Example (Blocks World) |
|-----------|---------|------------------------|
| **Initial State** | The current situation, fully observable | `On(A,B) ∧ On(B,Table) ∧ On(C,Table) ∧ Clear(A) ∧ Clear(C)` |
| **Actions (Schemas)** | What the agent can do; each with preconditions & effects | `Stack(x,y)`, `Unstack(x,y)` |
| **Goal State / Test** | The desired condition to be reached | `On(A,B) ∧ On(B,C)` |
| **Plan** | The sequence of actions that achieves the goal | `Unstack(A,Table) → Stack(A,B)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed-World Assumption (CWA):** Any atom (fact) not stated in the state description is assumed **false**. This is the bedrock of classical planning and is highly tested in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 The "Why" of Planning — Beyond Search

Classical state-space search (BFS, DFS, A*) treats a problem as a graph. Planning goes a step further by exploiting:

1. **Factored representation** — a state is a *set* of variables/atoms, not a single opaque node.
2. **Action structure** — actions are described declaratively via preconditions/effects.
3. **Goal structure** — only a subset of facts matter; the agent can ignore irrelevant ones.

This allows **subgoal independence**, **heuristic extraction**, and **decomposition** — features that generic search cannot exploit.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked "Why planning is more efficient than search?", always mention: *factored representation, declarative action description, and the ability to use domain-independent heuristics.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Planning Problem (Expanded)

### 2.1.1 State Representation
A state is a conjunction of **ground atomic literals** (positive fluents). In first-order STRIPS:
$$s = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k\}$$
where each $p_i$ is a positive ground atom (no variables, no negation).

### 2.1.2 Action Representation (STRIPS Schema)
Each action is a triple:

$$\langle \text{Name}(x_1, \ldots, x_k),\ \text{Precond}(a),\ \text{Effect}(a) \rangle$$

The **Effect** is split into the **Add-list** $ADD(a)$ and the **Delete-list** $DEL(a)$:
$$\text{Effect}(a) = ADD(a) \cup \neg DEL(a)$$

The action is **applicable in state $s$** iff:
$$\text{Precond}^+(a) \subseteq s \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Precond}^-(a) \cap s = \emptyset$$

The **result of applying $a$ in $s$** is:
$$\gamma(s, a) = (s - DEL(a)) \cup ADD(a)$$

### 2.1.3 Goal
A goal is a conjunction of positive (and sometimes negative) literals. A state $s$ **satisfies** goal $g$ iff:
$$g^+ \subseteq s \quad \text{and} \quad g^- \cap s = \emptyset$$
where $g^+$ and $g^-$ are the positive and negative literal sets.

### 2.1.4 Plan
A plan $\pi = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$ is **valid** if each $a_i$ is applicable in the state produced by the previous actions, and the final state satisfies $G$.

---

## 2.2 Categories of Planning Problems (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Description | KTU Cue |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Classical / STRIPS Planning** | Deterministic, fully observable, static, discrete | "Most basic form" |
| **Conformant Planning** | Uncertain initial state, no sensing | "No observations" |
| **Conditional Planning** | Actions depend on percepts (if-then-else plans) | "Contingency plans" |
| **Hierarchical Task Network (HTN)** | High-level tasks decomposed into sub-tasks | "Task networks" |
| **Temporal Planning** | Actions have durations, parallelism allowed | "Time-bounded plans" |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Definition Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use \vert instead of \vert symbol to avoid table-breaking pipes.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | Planning problem tuple | $\Pi = (S, A, \gamma, s_0, G)$ | 5-tuple |
| 2 | State (set of atoms) | $s = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k\}$ | Factored |
| 3 | Action schema | $\langle \text{Name},\ \text{Precond},\ \text{Effect} \rangle$ | Triple |
| 4 | Effect partition | $\text{Effect}(a) = ADD(a) \cup \neg DEL(a)$ | Add/Delete |
| 5 | Action applicability | $\text{Precond}^+(a) \subseteq s \;\wedge\; \text{Precond}^-(a) \cap s = \emptyset$ | Precondition test |
| 6 | State transition | $\gamma(s, a) = (s - DEL(a)) \cup ADD(a)$ | Result function |
| 7 | Goal satisfaction | $g^+ \subseteq s \;\wedge\; g^- \cap s = \emptyset$ | Satisfaction |
| 8 | Closed-World Assumption | Any atom not in $s$ is $\textit{false}$ | CWA |
| 9 | Plan length | $\vert \pi \vert = n$ (number of actions) | Plan metric |
| 10 | Ign-Delete-list heuristic | $h(s) = $ number of unsatisfied positive subgoals | Domain-independent |
| 11 | Plan-space search | Search over partial plans using flaws/constraints | POP |
| 12 | State-space search | Search over world states (forward/backward) | Progression/Regression |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** The *ignoring delete lists* heuristic is the cheapest admissible heuristic. It assumes we never need to *undo* a fact, so plan cost = sum of unsatisfied subgoals.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Robotics**: Sequencing manipulator actions to assemble a product.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Planning lane-change, merge, and turn sequences.
- **Logistics**: Warehouse pick-pack-ship scheduling.
- **Manufacturing**: CNC machining operation sequencing.
- **Cybersecurity**: Attack-tree planning for red-teaming.
- **Smart Grids**: Load-shedding and restoration plan synthesis.

---

## 2.5 Planning as Search — Two Paradigms

### 2.2.1 State-Space Search (Progression / Regression)
- **Forward (Progression)**: Start from $s_0$, apply applicable actions, search forward until $G$ is reached. *Breadth-first forward search* is complete and optimal.
- **Backward (Regression)**: Start from $G$, regress the goal through actions, search backward to reach $s_0$. Reduces branching factor because only *relevant* actions are considered.

### 2.2.2 Plan-Space Search
- Search operates over *partial plans* (sets of actions with ordering constraints, causal links, and open preconditions).
- A plan is **complete** when all preconditions are supported by a causal link or persist over no action.
- The "flaw-removal" loop identifies **open preconditions** (unsupported preconditions) and **threats** (actions that may undo a causal link), then resolves them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Partial-Order Planning (POP)** is the canonical plan-space planner. KTU frequently asks: *"What is a causal link in POP?"* — answer: a triple $\langle a_i, p, a_j \rangle$ meaning action $a_i$ achieves precondition $p$ for $a_j$, and no action in between may delete $p$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The Blocks World

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

**Initial State:**
$$s_0 = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{On}(B,C),\ \text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{Clear}(A),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$$

**Goal:**
$$G = \{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{On}(A,B)\}$$

**Action Schemas (STRIPS):**

1. **Stack$(x, y)$** — place $x$ on $y$.
   - Pre: $\text{Holding}(x) \wedge \text{Clear}(y)$
   - Add: $\text{ArmEmpty},\ \text{On}(x,y)$
   - Del: $\text{Holding}(x),\ \text{Clear}(y)$

2. **Unstack$(x, y)$** — pick up $x$ from $y$.
   - Pre: $\text{On}(x,y) \wedge \text{Clear}(x) \wedge \text{ArmEmpty}$
   - Add: $\text{Holding}(x),\ \text{Clear}(y)$
   - Del: $\text{On}(x,y),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$

3. **Pickup$(x)$** — pick $x$ from the table.
   - Pre: $\text{On}(x,Table) \wedge \text{Clear}(x) \wedge \text{ArmEmpty}$
   - Add: $\text{Holding}(x)$
   - Del: $\text{On}(x,Table),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$

4. **Putdown$(x)$** — place $x$ on the table.
   - Pre: $\text{Holding}(x)$
   - Add: $\text{ArmEmpty},\ \text{On}(x,Table)$
   - Del: $\text{Holding}(x)$

---

### 3.1.2 Forward Search (Progression) Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Examine Initial State $s_0$:**
- $G$ requires $\text{On}(C,A)$. In $s_0$, $\text{On}(C,Table)$ and $\text{On}(A,B)$. We need $C$ on $A$, but $A$ is *not clear* (B is not on A; in this state, A is clear). Actually, $\text{Clear}(A)$ holds in $s_0$. We can pick up $C$ from $B$, but $B$ is *not clear* (B has C on top? — wait, $\text{On}(B,C)$ means B is on C, so C is on the table and B is on C. Re-examine:

Let me restate the initial state visually:
- Table: $C$
- On $C$: $B$
- On $B$: $A$

**Visual:**
```
    [A]
    [B]
    [C]
  [Table]
```

So $A$ is clear (nothing on $A$). $B$ is *not* clear ($A$ is on it). $C$ is *not* clear ($B$ is on it). Arm is empty.

**Goal:** $C$ on $A$, and $A$ on $B$.
- $A$ on $B$ is *already true*. 
- $C$ on $A$ is the missing goal.

**Step 2 — Choose a relevant action:**
We need $C$ free and on $A$. To put $C$ on $A$, we must first **Unstack(A,B)** so we can place it back. But to put $C$ on $A$, $C$ must be held or $A$ must be clear with $C$ somewhere we can move. Let's plan:

**Step 3 — Plan Construction:**

1. **Unstack(A,B)**: 
   - Pre: $\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{Clear}(A),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$ — *all true* ✓
   - Result: $s_1 = \{\text{On}(B,C),\ \text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{Holding}(A),\ \text{Clear}(B)\}$

2. **Putdown(A)**:
   - Pre: $\text{Holding}(A)$ ✓
   - Result: $s_2 = \{\text{On}(B,C),\ \text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{Clear}(A),\ \text{Clear}(B),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$

3. **Unstack(B,C)**:
   - Pre: $\text{On}(B,C),\ \text{Clear}(B),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$ ✓
   - Result: $s_3 = \{\text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C)\}$

4. **Stack(B,A)**:
   - Pre: $\text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(A)$ ✓
   - Result: $s_4 = \{\text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{On}(B,A),\ \text{Clear}(C),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$

5. **Pickup(C)**:
   - Pre: $\text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{Clear}(C),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$ ✓
   - Result: $s_5 = \{\text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{On}(B,A),\ \text{Holding}(C)\}$

6. **Stack(C,B)**:
   - Pre: $\text{Holding}(C),\ \text{Clear}(B)$ ✓
   - Result: $s_6 = \{\text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{On}(B,A),\ \text{On}(C,B),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$

**Step 4 — Verify Goal:**
- Goal $G = \{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{On}(A,B)\}$
- In $s_6$: $\text{On}(C,B)$ and $\text{On}(B,A)$ — **Not satisfied.**

**Re-plan with correct goal interpretation:** The goal is C on A, A on B. So we need $A$ on $B$ and $C$ on $A$. Building from the start:

1. **Unstack(A,B)** → $s_1$ (A is held, B is clear)
2. **Stack(A,Table)** — or place $A$ somewhere useful. Actually, $A$ needs to be on $B$ *in the end*, and $C$ on $A$. So $A$ on $B$, $C$ on $A$, $B$ on table. **Target stack:** Table → $B$ → $A$ → $C$.

**Correct plan:**

1. **Unstack(A,B)** — Pick up A.
   - $s_1 = \{\text{On}(B,C),\ \text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{Holding}(A),\ \text{Clear}(B)\}$

2. **Stack(A,Table)** — Put A on table to clear B.
   - Actually we need A on B *eventually*. Let A go to table first, then we can rebuild.

3. **Unstack(B,C)** — Pick up B (B is now clear).
   - $s_3 = \{\text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C)\}$

4. **Stack(B,Table)** — Put B on table.
   - $s_4 = \{\text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{On}(B,Table),\ \text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{Clear}(A),\ \text{Clear}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C)\}$

5. **Stack(A,B)** — Put A on B.
   - $s_5 = \{\text{On}(B,Table),\ \text{On}(A,B),\ \text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{Clear}(A),\ \text{Clear}(C)\}$

6. **Stack(C,A)** — Put C on A.
   - $s_6 = \{\text{On}(B,Table),\ \text{On}(A,B),\ \text{On}(C,A),\ \text{Clear}(C)\}$

**Step 5 — Goal Check:**
- $G = \{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{On}(A,B)\}$ ✓ **Both satisfied!**

**Final Plan:** $\pi = \langle \text{Unstack}(A,B),\ \text{Stack}(A,Table),\ \text{Unstack}(B,C),\ \text{Stack}(B,Table),\ \text{Stack}(A,B),\ \text{Stack}(C,A) \rangle$

**Plan length** $= 6$ actions.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Forward vs Backward Search

| Property | Forward (Progression) | Backward (Regression) | Plan-Space (POP) |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| **Starts at** | Initial state $s_0$ | Goal $G$ | Empty plan |
| **Search node** | World state | Subgoal set | Partial plan |
| **Branching factor** | High (all applicable actions) | Lower (only relevant actions) | Medium (flaw resolution) |
| **Strong in** | Domain-independent heuristics | Goal relevance | Least-commitment |
| **Weakness** | Irrelevant states explored | Regression can be complex | Threat detection overhead |
| **Example planner** | FF, Fast-Downward | HSP (heuristic search planner) | UCPOP, Graphplan's partial order |

---

## 3.3 Heuristic Computation — Worked Example

Consider state $s = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{On}(B,C),\ \text{On}(C,Table),\ \text{Clear}(A)\}$ and goal $G = \{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{On}(A,B)\}$.

**Unsatisfied subgoals:** $\{\text{On}(C,A)\}$ → $h_{count}(s) = 1$.

**Ignore-delete-list heuristic** assumes we never need to re-achieve an atom. So the cost is the **number of actions needed to add the missing atom**: 1 (Stack C on A) — but A must be clear (it is). Hence $h_{igndel}(s) = 1$.

**Set-level heuristic (h_max):** If multiple subgoals, take the **maximum** cost of any single subgoal = 1.

**h_add:** **Sum** of costs of each subgoal = 1 (for this example).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** $h_{add} \geq h_{max}$ always, and $h_{max}$ is more informative than $h_{add}$ for *admissible* heuristics but is *inadmissible* in general. KTU questions often ask which heuristic is *admissible*.

---

## 3.4 Algorithm: Breadth-First Forward Planner (Pseudocode Outline)

> [!TIP]
> Below is a structured pseudocode outline — written in **structured natural language** (no programming language syntax) to remain language-agnostic per KTU convention.

```
PROCEDURE Forward-Search-Planner(initial_state, actions, goal)
    frontier ← [initial_state]
    reached   ← {initial_state}
    LOOP:
        IF frontier is empty THEN RETURN failure
        pop node s from frontier
        IF s satisfies goal THEN RETURN extract_plan(s)
        FOR each action a applicable in s:
            s' ← apply(a, s)
            IF s' not in reached:
                add s' to reached
                record parent(s') ← s, action(s') ← a
                push s' into frontier
```

**Properties:**
- **Complete?** Yes (if branching finite).
- **Optimal?** Yes (in number of actions).
- **Space:** $O(b^d)$ — exponential.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation — Plan-Space Causal Link

A **causal link** in POP is formally:
$$\langle a_i, q, a_j \rangle$$
meaning: action $a_i$ provides precondition $q$ for action $a_j$, and no action scheduled between $a_i$ and $a_j$ deletes $q$.

**Threat:** An action $a_k$ is a threat to link $\langle a_i, q, a_j \rangle$ if $a_k$ has $\neg q$ in its effects and $a_k$ is unordered relative to either $a_i$ or $a_j$ but ordered between them.

**Resolution:** Add ordering constraint $a_k \prec a_i$ (demotion) or $a_j \prec a_k$ (promotion) to remove the threat.

> [!NOTE]
> A **partial-order plan** is *complete* and *consistent* when:
> 1. Every precondition of every action is supported by a causal link.
> 2. Every threat is resolved by promotion or demotion.
> 3. The partial order is acyclic.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Generic Classical Planner

> Below is a **block-level functional architecture** of a generic planning system, showing how the planner's components interact to produce a plan.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Domain Description PDDL] --> C[Planner Engine]
    B[Problem Description] --> C
    C --> D[State Space Search\nForward or Backward]
    D --> E[Heuristic Estimator\nh-function]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Plan Validator]
    F --> G{Plan Valid?}
    G -- No --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Final Plan\nSequence of Actions]
    C --> I[Action Library\nSTRIPS Schemas]
    I --> D
    H --> J[Agent Executor]
    J --> K[Environment]
    K --> L[New Percepts]
    L --> J
```

**Explanation:**
- The **Domain Description** declares predicates, action schemas, and types (PDDL).
- The **Problem Description** specifies the initial state and the goal.
- The **Planner Engine** invokes a **State-Space Search** routine (forward/backward/POP).
- A **Heuristic Estimator** provides the $h$ value for A*-style informed search.
- The **Plan Validator** checks that the synthesised plan is correct.
- The **Agent Executor** runs the plan in the real world and ingests new percepts for replanning.

---

## 4.2 Forward vs Backward Search — Side-by-Side Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Forward_Search
        F1[Initial State s0] --> F2[Applicable Actions]
        F2 --> F3[Successor States s1 s2 s3]
        F3 --> F4{Goal Reached?}
        F4 -- No --> F2
        F4 -- Yes --> F5[Extract Plan]
    end
    subgraph Backward_Search
        B1[Goal G] --> B2[Relevant Actions\nthat achieve subgoals]
        B2 --> B3[Regressed Subgoal Sets]
        B3 --> B4{Initial State Reached?}
        B4 -- No --> B2
        B4 -- Yes --> B5[Extract Plan]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 STRIPS Action Schema — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Action Schema Name args] --> B[Preconditions\nPRECOND Plus Minus]
    A --> C[Effects\nEFFECT Add Delete]
    B --> D{Applicability Test}
    C --> D
    D -- Pass --> E[State Transition Function\ngamma s a]
    E --> F[New State s prime]
    D -- Fail --> G[Action Not Applicable\nin State s]
```

---

## 4.4 Plan-Space (POP) Search — Flaw-Removal Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Partial Plan] --> S1{All Preconditions\nSupported?}
    S1 -- No --> S2[Select Open\nPrecondition p]
    S2 --> S3[Choose Action a that\nachieves p]
    S3 --> S4[Add Causal Link\na_i p a_j]
    S4 --> S5{Any Threats\non New Link?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Resolve via Promotion\nor Demotion]
    S6 --> S7[Add Ordering Constraint]
    S7 --> P
    S5 -- No --> P
    S1 -- Yes --> T{Plan Complete and\nConsistent?}
    T -- Yes --> OUT[Final Plan]
    T -- No --> P
```

---

## 4.5 Planning Graph (Graphplan-Style Layered Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Fact_Level_0
        F0A[Initial State Facts]
    end
    subgraph Action_Level_1
        A1A[Action a1]
        A1B[Action a2]
        A1C[NoOp a1]
        A1D[NoOp a2]
    end
    subgraph Fact_Level_1
        F1A[New Fact p]
        F1B[New Fact q]
    end
    subgraph Action_Level_2
        A2A[Action a3]
        A2B[NoOp a1]
        A2C[NoOp a2]
    end
    subgraph Fact_Level_2
        F2A[Goal Fact r]
    end
    F0A --> A1A
    F0A --> A1B
    F0A --> A1C
    F0A --> A1D
    A1A --> F1A
    A1B --> F1B
    A1C --> F1A
    A1D --> F1B
    F1A --> A2A
    F1B --> A2A
    F1A --> A2B
    F1B --> A2C
    A2A --> F2A
    A2B --> F1A
    A2C --> F1B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **planning graph** alternates *fact levels* (states) and *action levels* (operators + no-ops). Mutual exclusion (mutex) links are also recorded. The level at which all goal facts appear non-mutex gives the **graphplan heuristic** $h_{level}$.

---

## 4.6 Heuristic Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Current State s] --> R[Relaxed Solver\nIgnore Deletes]
    R --> C[Cost of Each Subgoal]
    C --> SUM[h_add sum of costs]
    C --> MAX[h_max maximum cost]
    C --> LVL[h_level from Planning Graph]
    SUM --> P[Planner Chooses Best Heuristic]
    MAX --> P
    LVL --> P
```
