# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a planning problem. List and explain the four basic components of a planning system.**

**Model Answer:**

A **planning problem** is the problem of finding a sequence of actions that an agent can execute, starting from a given initial state, to reach a state in which the goal condition is satisfied.

The four basic components of a planning system are:

1. **Initial State** — The complete description of the world at the time the agent begins planning. It is a set of ground atomic facts.
2. **Actions (Action Schemas)** — A set of operators the agent can perform. Each action has a *name*, a set of *preconditions* (what must be true to apply it), and a set of *effects* (what becomes true/false after execution). In STRIPS, effects are divided into an *add list* and a *delete list*.
3. **Goal (Goal Test)** — A logical condition (conjunction of literals) the agent wants to achieve. The planner succeeds when a state satisfying this condition is reached.
4. **Plan** — The output of the planner: an ordered sequence of action instances $\pi = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$ whose application transforms the initial state into a goal state.

*(Examiner Note: Any 4 valid components with explanation → 3 marks. Definition alone is 1 mark.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between forward (progression) state-space search and backward (regression) state-space search in planning.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Forward (Progression) | Backward (Regression) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Starting point** | Begins at the initial state $s_0$ | Begins at the goal $G$ |
| **Direction** | Applies actions forward, generating successors | Regresses goal through actions, generating pre-images |
| **Branching factor** | Can be very large (all applicable actions) | Smaller (only relevant actions that achieve a subgoal) |
| **Redundancy** | May explore many irrelevant states | Avoids irrelevant states |
| **Difficulty** | Simple to implement | Requires computing the regression of a state through an action |
| **When to prefer** | When good heuristic is available | When goals are much fewer than states |

Forward search is complete but can be slow; backward search is more focused but can be complex when the goal has many literals.

*(Examiner Note: 3 distinct differences → 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Represent the following Blocks World problem using STRIPS notation. Initial state has A on B, B on C, C on table. Goal is to have C on A and A on B. Define the required action schemas and show the step-by-step plan generation using forward search.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Formal STRIPS Representation of Initial State:**
$$s_0 = \{\text{On}(A,B),\ \text{On}(B,C),\ \text{On}(C,\text{Table}),\ \text{Clear}(A),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$$

**Step 2 — Formal Goal:**
$$G = \{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{On}(A,B)\}$$
Note: $\text{On}(A,B)$ is already satisfied in $s_0$; the unsatisfied subgoal is $\text{On}(C,A)$.

**Step 3 — Action Schemas Required:**

- **Unstack$(x,y)$** — pick $x$ off $y$:
  - Pre: $\text{On}(x,y),\ \text{Clear}(x),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$
  - Add: $\text{Holding}(x),\ \text{Clear}(y)$
  - Del: $\text{On}(x,y),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$

- **Stack$(x,y)$** — place $x$ on $y$:
  - Pre: $\text{Holding}(x),\ \text{Clear}(y)$
  - Add: $\text{ArmEmpty},\ \text{On}(x,y)$
  - Del: $\text{Holding}(x),\ \text{Clear}(y)$

- **Pickup$(x)$** — pick $x$ off table:
  - Pre: $\text{On}(x,\text{Table}),\ \text{Clear}(x),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$
  - Add: $\text{Holding}(x)$
  - Del: $\text{On}(x,\text{Table}),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$

- **Putdown$(x)$** — place $x$ on table:
  - Pre: $\text{Holding}(x)$
  - Add: $\text{ArmEmpty},\ \text{On}(x,\text{Table})$
  - Del: $\text{Holding}(x)$

**Step 4 — Forward Search Plan Generation:**

| Step | Action | Preconditions Met? | New State After Action |
|------|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Unstack(A,B) | On(A,B)✓, Clear(A)✓, ArmEmpty✓ | Holding(A), On(B,C), On(C,Table), Clear(B) |
| 2 | Stack(A,Table) — use Putdown(A) | Holding(A)✓ | On(A,Table), On(B,C), On(C,Table), Clear(A), Clear(B), ArmEmpty |
| 3 | Unstack(B,C) | On(B,C)✓, Clear(B)✓, ArmEmpty✓ | On(A,Table), On(C,Table), Holding(B), Clear(C) |
| 4 | Putdown(B) | Holding(B)✓ | On(A,Table), On(B,Table), On(C,Table), Clear(A), Clear(B), Clear(C), ArmEmpty |
| 5 | Stack(A,B) | Holding? — A is on table. We need A held. **This action not applicable** — so we need to Pickup(A) first. |

**Corrected Step 5:**
| Step | Action | Preconditions Met? | New State |
|------|--------|--------------------|----------|
| 5 | Pickup(A) | On(A,Table)✓, Clear(A)✓, ArmEmpty✓ | On(B,Table), On(C,Table), Holding(A), Clear(B), Clear(C) |
| 6 | Stack(A,B) | Holding(A)✓, Clear(B)✓ | On(B,Table), On(C,Table), On(A,B), Clear(A), Clear(C), ArmEmpty |
| 7 | Pickup(C) | On(C,Table)✓, Clear(C)✓, ArmEmpty✓ | On(B,Table), On(A,B), Holding(C), Clear(A) |
| 8 | Stack(C,A) | Holding(C)✓, Clear(A)✓ | On(B,Table), On(A,B), On(C,A), ArmEmpty |

**Step 5 — Goal Verification:**
- $G = \{\text{On}(C,A),\ \text{On}(A,B)\}$
- Final state contains: $\text{On}(C,A)$ ✓, $\text{On}(A,B)$ ✓
- **Goal achieved.**

**Final Plan (8 actions):**
$$\pi = \langle \text{Unstack}(A,B),\ \text{Putdown}(A),\ \text{Unstack}(B,C),\ \text{Putdown}(B),\ \text{Pickup}(A),\ \text{Stack}(A,B),\ \text{Pickup}(C),\ \text{Stack}(C,A) \rangle$$

*(7 marks: 1 for STRIPS formalism, 2 for action schemas, 3 for plan steps, 1 for verification.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the partial-order planning (POP) algorithm. What is a causal link? How is a "threat" detected and resolved?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A **Partial-Order Plan (POP)** is a planning algorithm that builds a plan by *incrementally adding actions* and constraints to a partial plan, without committing to a fixed ordering of all actions. Actions are ordered only when necessary to resolve threats or to support preconditions.

**Structure of a Partial Plan:**
A partial plan is a 4-tuple:
$$\text{Plan} = \langle A,\ O,\ L,\ B \rangle$$
where:
- $A$ = set of action instances (Start, Finish, plus ground actions),
- $O$ = set of ordering constraints (e.g., $a \prec b$),
- $L$ = set of causal links (e.g., $\langle a, p, b \rangle$),
- $B$ = set of binding constraints (variable equalities).

**Algorithm Outline (Flaw-Removal Loop):**
1. Initialise partial plan with **Start** action (no preconditions, effect = $s_0$) and **Finish** action (precondition = $G$, no effects).
2. **Loop** while the plan is not complete:
   - **Select a flaw** — either an *open precondition* (a precondition of some action not yet supported) or a *threat* (a possible causal link violation).
   - **Resolve the flaw**:
     - For an *open precondition* $p$ of action $b$: choose an action $a$ (existing or new) that has $p$ in its add-list, add causal link $\langle a, p, b \rangle$ and ordering $a \prec b$.
     - For a *threat* from action $k$ on link $\langle a, p, b \rangle$: apply *demotion* (add $k \prec a$) or *promotion* (add $b \prec k$).
3. Terminate when the plan is **complete and consistent** (all preconditions supported, all threats resolved, no cycles).

**Causal Link:**
A causal link $\langle a_i, p, a_j \rangle$ records that action $a_i$ achieves precondition $p$ for $a_j$, and **no action between them deletes $p$**. It is the planning analogue of an "established commitment".

**Threat Detection:**
An action $a_k$ threatens causal link $\langle a_i, p, a_j \rangle$ if:
- $a_k$ has $\neg p$ in its effects,
- $a_k$ is *unordered* with respect to $a_i$ or $a_j$,
- But $a_k$ is constrained to lie *between* $a_i$ and $a_j$.

**Threat Resolution:**
1. **Promotion:** Add ordering $a_j \prec a_k$ so $a_k$ happens after the consumer.
2. **Demotion:** Add ordering $a_k \prec a_i$ so $a_k$ happens before the provider.
3. If neither resolves it, backtrack.

**Properties of POP:**
- **Sound:** Every linearisation of the final partial plan is a valid total-order plan.
- **Complete:** A solution exists ⇒ POP will find one.
- **Flexible:** Independent subgoals can be planned in parallel and merged.

*(7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for plan structure + algorithm, 2 for causal links, 2 for threats and resolution.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the STRIPS planning representation in detail. How is a STRIPS action applied to a state? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
STRIPS (**Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver**) is the foundational language for representing classical planning problems. It is *declarative* — actions are described by their preconditions and effects, not by procedural code.

**State Representation:**
A state in STRIPS is a **conjunction of function-free, ground, positive literals**:
$$s = p_1 \wedge p_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge p_k$$
By the **Closed-World Assumption (CWA)**, any atom not mentioned is assumed false.

**Goal Representation:**
A goal is a conjunction of **positive and negative literals**:
$$G = g_1 \wedge g_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge g_m$$
A state satisfies $G$ if it contains all positive goal literals and excludes all negative goal literals.

**Action Representation (Schema):**
An action schema has three parts:
1. **Action name with parameters:** $\text{Name}(x_1, \ldots, x_k)$
2. **Precondition:** $\text{Precond}(a)$ — a conjunction of literals that must hold.
3. **Effect:** $\text{Effect}(a)$ — split into:
   - **Add list** $ADD(a)$: literals that become true.
   - **Delete list** $DEL(a)$: literals that become false.
   
   So: $\text{Effect}(a) = ADD(a) \cup \neg DEL(a)$.

**Action Application Rule:**

An action $a$ is **applicable** in state $s$ if and only if:
$$\text{Precond}^+(a) \subseteq s \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Precond}^-(a) \cap s = \emptyset$$

The **resulting state** after applying $a$ to $s$ is:
$$\gamma(s, a) = (s - DEL(a)) \cup ADD(a)$$

**Worked Example:**

Consider the action **Stack(B,C)** in Blocks World:
- Pre: $\text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C)$
- Add: $\text{On}(B,C),\ \text{ArmEmpty}$
- Del: $\text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C)$

Current state:
$$s = \{\text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C),\ \text{Clear}(A)\}$$

**Step 1 — Check Applicability:**
- $\text{Precond}^+ = \{\text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C)\}$
- Is $\{\text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C)\} \subseteq s$? Yes. ✓
- $\text{Precond}^- = \emptyset$, so trivially satisfied. ✓
- **Action is applicable.**

**Step 2 — Compute Result State:**
$$s' = (s - DEL(a)) \cup ADD(a)$$
$$s' = (\{\text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C),\ \text{Clear}(A)\} - \{\text{Holding}(B),\ \text{Clear}(C)\}) \cup \{\text{On}(B,C),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$$
$$s' = \{\text{On}(A,Table),\ \text{Clear}(A),\ \text{On}(B,C),\ \text{ArmEmpty}\}$$

This new state now satisfies: $B$ on $C$, $A$ is clear, and the arm is empty.

**Properties of STRIPS:**
- **Expressiveness:** Lacks conditional effects, quantifiers, negation (in preconditions of some variants), and numeric resources — extensions are handled by **PDDL**.
- **Frame problem (partial):** STRIPS handles the frame problem by *assuming* unchanged atoms remain true (this is itself an assumption — the *STRIPS assumption*).

*(7 marks: 2 for state/goal/action representation, 2 for applicability rule, 2 for result rule with worked example, 1 for properties.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Describe the Graphplan algorithm. Explain how a planning graph is constructed and how the goal is extracted from it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
**Graphplan** (Blum & Furst, 1995) is a planning algorithm that constructs a compact structure called a **planning graph** and uses it both as a heuristic estimator and as a direct plan extraction structure.

**Planning Graph Structure:**

A planning graph is a **layered directed graph** with alternating levels:
- **Fact Levels** $F_0, F_1, F_2, \ldots$ — sets of propositions (facts).
- **Action Levels** $A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots$ — sets of operator instances and **persistence actions** (no-ops) that carry forward unchanged facts.

Edges:
- **Precondition edges** from fact level $F_{i-1}$ to action level $A_i$.
- **Add edges** from action level $A_i$ to fact level $F_i$.
- **Delete edges** also from $A_i$ to $F_i$ (for facts that get removed).
- **No-op edges** for persistence: a fact $p \in F_{i-1}$ is carried to $F_i$ via a no-op.

**Mutex Links:**
At each level, pairs of facts (or actions) are marked as **mutually exclusive (mutex)** if they *cannot* both hold (for facts) or be simultaneously executed (for actions). Mutex conditions include:
- *Inconsistent effects* (one deletes the other's preconditions/adds).
- *Interference* (one deletes a precondition of the other).
- *Competing needs* (preconditions are mutex).
- *Inconsistent support* (every way to achieve $p$ is mutex with every way to achieve $q$).

**Construction Algorithm:**

1. Start with $F_0$ = initial state facts.
2. **For** $i = 1, 2, \ldots$ until fixed-point:
   - **Action level $A_i$:** Include any action whose preconditions are present and *non-mutex* in $F_{i-1}$. Include a no-op for each fact in $F_{i-1}$.
   - **Fact level $F_i$:** Include all add-effects of $A_i$ (union).
   - **Compute mutexes** at $F_i$ and $A_{i+1}$.
3. Stop when $F_i = F_{i-1}$ and mutex sets stabilise (*fixed point*).

**Goal Extraction (Backward Search):**

1. Let $k$ be the first level where all goal facts appear **non-mutex** in $F_k$.
2. **For** $j = k, k-1, \ldots, 1$:
   - Choose, for each goal fact $g$, an action $a$ at level $A_j$ that adds $g$ and is *non-mutex* with all other chosen actions.
   - Add the preconditions of all chosen $a$'s to the new subgoal set for level $F_{j-1}$.
3. If a level cannot be satisfied, **backtrack** and try different action choices.
4. If we reach $F_0$ with all preconditions present, **extract the plan** by collecting all chosen actions.

**Heuristic Value (h_level):**
The level index $k$ at which all goal facts first appear non-mutex is used as the **Graphplan heuristic**:
$$h_{\text{level}}(s) = k$$
This is an **admissible** estimate of plan length (under the relaxation that ignores deletes).

**Properties:**
- **Polynomial time per level** (construction is polynomial).
- **Sound and complete** (when goal extraction succeeds).
- **Provides excellent heuristic** $h_{\text{level}}$ used by many modern planners.

*(7 marks: 1 for structure, 2 for construction, 2 for mutex rules, 1 for goal extraction, 1 for heuristic.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Planning Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Closed-World Assumption (CWA):** Students often list "negative" facts explicitly in the state. *Do not* — negative facts are inferred.
> 2. **Mixing up Add and Delete lists:** A literal in the *Add* list must be removed from the *Delete* list — never both. Verify by checking the action is reversible logically.
> 3. **Forgetting the ArmEmpty precondition:** In Blocks World, only one block can be held at a time. Always include ArmEmpty / Holding fluents.
> 4. **Conflating total-order and partial-order plans:** POP plans are *partial* until linearised; don't claim POP returns a sequence.
> 5. **Causal-link and threat conflation:** A threat is a *possible* violation, not a definite one — resolution is preventive.
> 6. **Heuristic admissibility mistakes:** $h_{add}$ is **not** admissible in general; $h_{max}$ is also inadmissible. Only $h_{\text{level}}$ from the planning graph is admissible under the relaxation. KTU often asks this.
> 7. **Skipping the applicability check:** When asked "apply this action to this state", explicitly verify preconditions first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Planning Problem**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Planning problem** = find a sequence of actions from $s_0$ to a state satisfying $G$.
- ☐ **Tuple form:** $\Pi = (S, A, \gamma, s_0, G)$.
- ☐ **State** = set of positive ground atoms (CWA).
- ☐ **Action schema** = $\langle \text{Name}, \text{Precond}, \text{Effect} \rangle$ with $\text{Effect} = ADD \cup \neg DEL$.
- ☐ **Plan** = action sequence whose application reaches $G$.

### Key Equations
- ☐ **Applicability:** $\text{Precond}^+(a) \subseteq s$ and $\text{Precond}^-(a) \cap s = \emptyset$.
- ☐ **Result:** $\gamma(s, a) = (s - DEL(a)) \cup ADD(a)$.
- ☐ **Goal satisfaction:** $g^+ \subseteq s$ and $g^- \cap s = \emptyset$.

### Search Paradigms
- ☐ **Forward (progression):** From $s_0$; complete, optimal, but state explosion.
- ☐ **Backward (regression):** From $G$; relevant-action filtering, but regression is complex.
- ☐ **Plan-space (POP):** Search over partial plans using flaws (open preconditions, threats) and constraints (causal links, orderings).
- ☐ **Planning graph (Graphplan):** Layered fact-action structure with mutexes, enables fast heuristic $h_{\text{level}}$.

### Heuristics
- ☐ **$h_{unsat}$:** count of unsatisfied positive subgoals.
- ☐ **$h_{\text{igndel}}$:** cost under no-delete relaxation.
- ☐ **$h_{add}$:** sum of relaxed-regression costs (inadmissible in general).
- ☐ **$h_{max}$:** maximum relaxed cost (inadmissible in general).
- ☐ **$h_{\text{level}}$:** Graphplan level (admissible).

### POP Essentials
- ☐ **Causal link:** $\langle a_i, p, a_j \rangle$ — commitment that $a_i$ achieves $p$ for $a_j$.
- ☐ **Threat:** action $a_k$ with $\neg p$ in effects, between $a_i$ and $a_j$.
- ☐ **Resolution:** **promotion** ($a_j \prec a_k$) or **demotion** ($a_k \prec a_i$).

### Exam Triggers (Keywords → Concepts)
- ☐ "Sequence of actions" → **Plan**
- ☐ "Preconditions and effects" → **STRIPS**
- ☐ "Open precondition" → **POP flaw**
- ☐ "Mutex" → **Planning graph**
- ☐ "Domain-independent heuristic" → **Relaxed planning graph**
- ☐ "Causal link" → **POP commitment**
- ☐ "Frame problem" → **STRIPS assumption / successor-state axioms**

### Mnemonics
- ☐ **AD3R** for action application: **A**pplicability test → **D**elete removed → **A**dd added → **R**esult state.
- ☐ **C-L-T** for POP flaw handling: **C**hoose → resolve via **L**ink or **T**hreat resolution.
- ☐ **CMF** for mutex causes: **C**ompeting needs, **M**utual effects (inconsistent), **F**or interference.

### Common Examples
- ☐ **Blocks World:** Stack / Unstack / Pickup / Putdown.
- ☐ **Rocket problem:** Load, Unload, Move.
- ☐ **Dinner Date problem:** Have meal, pay bill, drive.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Mantra for this module:** *If a question mentions "factored state", "preconditions/effects", or "sequence of actions", you are in the **Planning Problem** domain. Anchor your answer to the four components: Initial State, Actions, Goal, and Plan — and mention the search paradigm (forward/backward/POP/graph).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Space Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** State Space Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Space Search** is a computational paradigm in Artificial Intelligence (AI) where a problem is formally represented as a directed graph of **states** (nodes) connected by **actions/operators** (edges), and a solution is found by systematically exploring this graph from an **initial state** to one or more **goal states** using a defined **search strategy**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) framework, state space search forms the **mathematical backbone** of rational agent design. Every goal-based agent (as defined in Module 1) operates by:

1. **Formulating** a real-world problem as a state space.
2. **Searching** through that space using a systematic algorithm.
3. **Executing** the sequence of actions (plan) that leads to the goal.

The **state space** $\mathcal{S}$ is formally defined as the tuple:
$$\mathcal{S} = \langle S, A, s_0, G, C \rangle$$

Where:
- $S$ = Finite (or infinite) set of all possible world configurations (states).
- $A$ = Set of allowable actions/operators available to the agent.
- $s_0 \in S$ = The initial state from which the agent begins.
- $G \subseteq S$ = The set of goal states.
- $C: S \times A \rightarrow S$ = The transition (successor) function.
- (Optional) $c: S \times A \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ = Step-cost function.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *path* in the state space is a sequence of states $\langle s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n \rangle$ where each $s_{i+1} = C(s_i, a_i)$ for some $a_i \in A$. A *solution* is a path from $s_0$ to any $g \in G$, and an *optimal solution* is the one with the minimum total path cost $\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c(s_i, a_i)$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Maze Solving with a Torch"**
>
> Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into an unknown maze with a single goal: reach the **exit door**. You cannot see the full maze layout (the **state space graph**). You have a **torch** (your **search algorithm**) that lets you explore one room (state) at a time.
>
> - Each **room** = a state.
> - Each **doorway** = an action/operator.
> - The **room you wake up in** = initial state.
> - The **exit** = goal state.
> - The **distance between rooms** = step cost.
> - Your **torch strategy** (e.g., always turn left, always go straight, follow the smell of fresh air) = search strategy.
>
> Some strategies (like BFS) ensure you find the *shortest* path but explore many rooms. Others (like DFS) explore deeply but may get lost. Some (like A*) use a "hunch" (heuristic) — e.g., always move toward the door you *feel* is the exit. State space search is exactly this: formalizing the torch strategy into a rigorous algorithm.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Favorite Definitions)

| Term | Formal Meaning | Standard Metric |
|------|----------------|-----------------|
| **State** | A complete description of the world at one instant | Represented as a data structure (e.g., $(x,y)$ coordinates, board configuration) |
| **Operator / Action** | A function that maps one state to a successor state | Often denoted $a \in A$ |
| **Search Tree** | A tree generated by expanding states, where each node represents a state and each branch represents an action | Root = $s_0$; leaves = unexpanded states |
| **Branching Factor $b$** | The maximum (or average) number of successors of any state | Typical values: $b \approx 3$ to $35$ |
| **Depth $d$** | The length of the shortest path from $s_0$ to the shallowest goal | Often the *depth of the optimal solution* |
| **Path Cost** | Sum of step costs along a path | $g(n) = \sum c(s_i, a_i)$ |
| **Open List / Frontier** | The set of unexpanded (leaf) nodes available for expansion | Also called *fringe* |
| **Closed List / Explored Set** | The set of already-expanded states, used to avoid loops | Critical for graph search |
| **Admissible Heuristic** | A function $h(n)$ that **never overestimates** the true cost to goal | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ where $h^*$ is the true cost |
| **Consistent (Monotone) Heuristic** | $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ for all successors $n'$ | Stronger condition than admissibility |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Search Metrics in AI:** Algorithms are evaluated on four axes — **(i) Completeness** (does it find a solution if one exists?), **(ii) Optimality** (does it find the *best* solution?), **(iii) Time Complexity**, and **(iv) Space Complexity**. These four appear in nearly every KTU question on search.

---

### 1.4 Problem Classification in State Space Search

Problems are characterized along several dimensions:

- **Fully vs. Partially Observable** — Are all state variables known?
- **Deterministic vs. Stochastic** — Do actions have guaranteed outcomes?
- **Discrete vs. Continuous** — Is the state/action space countable?
- **Static vs. Dynamic** — Does the world change while the agent deliberates?
- **Known vs. Unknown** — Are the transition and cost models known?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> State space search (as covered in KTU Module 2) is the canonical framework for **fully observable, deterministic, discrete, static, known** environments — the simplest class, also called *classical planning* problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Search Algorithm

Every state space search algorithm — whether BFS, DFS, or A* — shares the same **operational skeleton**. The differences lie only in the *order* in which nodes are expanded and the *data structure* used for the frontier.

**Step-by-Step Logic (Universal Search Loop):**

1. **Initialize** the frontier (open list) with the node containing the initial state $s_0$.
2. **Initialize** the explored set (closed list) as empty.
3. **Loop**:
   - **a.** If the frontier is empty → return **FAILURE** (no solution exists).
   - **b.** *Select* a node $n$ from the frontier (the selection rule defines the algorithm).
   - **c.** If $n$ contains a goal state → return the path from root to $n$ (the **solution**).
   - **d.** Remove $n$ from the frontier and add it to the explored set.
   - **e.** *Expand* $n$: for each action $a$ applicable to $n$'s state, generate the successor $n'$.
   - **f.** For each successor $n'$:
     - If $n'$ is **not** in the explored set and **not** in the frontier, add it to the frontier (and store its parent pointer to $n$ for path reconstruction).
     - If $n'$ is already in the frontier with a *higher* path cost, replace it with the new cheaper one (graph search optimization).
4. **End Loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **selection rule** in step (b) is what differentiates BFS (FIFO queue), DFS (LIFO stack), Uniform-Cost (priority queue ordered by $g$), Greedy Best-First (priority by $h$), and A* (priority by $f = g + h$).

---

### 2.2 Search Strategy Taxonomy (KTU Mandatory Coverage)

#### A. Uninformed (Blind) Search Strategies

These strategies use **no domain knowledge** beyond the problem definition. They differ only in node ordering.

**1. Breadth-First Search (BFS)**
- **Selection Rule:** Expand the shallowest unexpanded node.
- **Data Structure:** FIFO queue.
- **Behavior:** Explores all nodes at depth $k$ before any node at depth $k+1$.
- **Why it works:** Guarantees finding the shallowest goal first.

**2. Depth-First Search (DFS)**
- **Selection Rule:** Expand the deepest unexpanded node.
- **Data Structure:** LIFO stack.
- **Behavior:** Drills down one path until it hits a dead-end, then backtracks.

**3. Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)**
- **Selection Rule:** Expand the unexpanded node with the **lowest path cost** $g(n)$.
- **Data Structure:** Priority queue (min-heap) keyed on $g$.
- **Behavior:** Equivalent to Dijkstra's algorithm on the state graph.

**4. Depth-Limited Search (DLS)**
- **Selection Rule:** Like DFS, but with a fixed depth limit $\ell$.
- **Why it exists:** Cures DFS's infinite-path problem.

**5. Iterative Deepening Search (IDS / IDDFS)**
- **Selection Rule:** Run DLS with $\ell = 0, 1, 2, 3, \dots$ until goal found.
- **Why it works:** Combines BFS's completeness/optimality with DFS's space efficiency.

#### B. Informed (Heuristic) Search Strategies

These use an **evaluation function** $f(n)$ that incorporates a heuristic estimate $h(n)$ of the cost from $n$ to the nearest goal.

**1. Greedy Best-First Search**
- **Selection Rule:** $f(n) = h(n)$ — expand the node that *appears* closest to the goal.
- **Behavior:** Fast, but **not optimal** and **not complete** (can get trapped in loops).

**2. A\* Search**
- **Selection Rule:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — estimated total cost via $n$.
- **Behavior:** Optimal *if* $h$ is **admissible** (and *consistent* for graph search).
- **Why it works:** Balances "cost so far" with "estimated cost to go."

**3. Hill Climbing (Local Search)**
- **Selection Rule:** Move to the neighbor with the **highest** $h$ (or lowest $c$).
- **Behavior:** Greedy local optimization; no backtracking, no path.
- **Variants:** Steepest-ascent, stochastic, random-restart, simulated annealing.

#### C. Adversarial Search (Game Playing)

**1. Minimax**
- Two players (MAX and MIN) alternate. MAX picks the move that maximizes the worst-case outcome; MIN picks the move that minimizes MAX's outcome. Recurses to a fixed depth or terminal state, then applies a heuristic **evaluation function**.

**2. Alpha-Beta Pruning**
- An optimization of Minimax that prunes branches provably unable to influence the final decision. Uses two parameters: $\alpha$ (best value MAX can guarantee) and $\beta$ (best value MIN can guarantee). Prunes when $\alpha \geq \beta$.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU BOARD TABLE — Memorize These Complexity Results.**
> All formulas below use: $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal, $m$ = maximum depth of search tree, $C^*$ = cost of optimal solution, $\epsilon$ = minimum step cost (positive).

| **Algorithm** | **Complete?** | **Optimal?** | **Time Complexity** | **Space Complexity** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Breadth-First Search (BFS) | Yes | Yes (unit costs) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| Uniform-Cost Search (UCS) | Yes (if $\epsilon > 0$) | Yes | $O\left(b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor}\right)$ | $O\left(b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor}\right)$ |
| Depth-First Search (DFS) | No (fails on infinite spaces, cycles) | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ |
| Depth-Limited Search (DLS) | Yes (if $\ell \geq d$) | No | $O(b^\ell)$ | $O(b\ell)$ |
| Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS) | Yes | Yes (unit costs) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| Greedy Best-First | No | No | $O(b^m)$ worst | $O(b^m)$ worst |
| A\* Search | Yes (if $h$ admissible) | Yes (if $h$ admissible) | $O(b^d)$ exponential worst | $O(b^d)$ — keeps all nodes in memory |
| Iterative Deepening A\* (IDA\*) | Yes | Yes (if $h$ admissible) | depends on thresholds | $O(bd)$ |
| Minimax (depth $m$, branching $b$) | — | Yes (vs. optimal play) | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ |
| Alpha-Beta Pruning | — | Yes (vs. optimal play) | $O(b^{m/2})$ best case | $O(bm)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **For Alpha-Beta Pruning**, the best-case complexity is $O(b^{m/2})$ when successors are ordered perfectly (best moves explored first). The average case is roughly $O(b^{3m/4})$. This is a high-yield KTU 14-marker.

| **Key Heuristic Relationships** | **Formula / Definition** |
|---|---|
| A* evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Admissibility condition | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$ |
| Consistency (monotonicity) | $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ |
| Dominance of heuristics | $h_2$ dominates $h_1$ if $\forall n: h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ and both admissible |
| Effective branching factor $b^*$ | $N + 1 = 1 + b^* + (b^*)^2 + \cdots + (b^*)^d$ where $N$ = nodes expanded |
| Minimax value at depth | $V(n) = \max_{a \in \text{Actions}(n)} \min_{n' \in \text{Succ}(n,a)} V(n')$ |
| Alpha-Beta cutoff condition | Prune when $\alpha \geq \beta$ |
| Effective depth with Alpha-Beta | $m' \approx m/2$ (best), $m' \approx 2m/3$ (random ordering) |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of State Space Search

- **Robotics & Path Planning:** Mobile robot navigation in warehouses (e.g., Amazon Kiva robots) uses A* on a grid state space.
- **Game AI:** Chess engines (Stockfish) use Iterative Deepening Alpha-Beta on a tree of up to $10^{40}$ states.
- **Network Routing:** UCS-style shortest-path algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford) are the foundation of OSPF, BGP, and modern SDN routing.
- **Automated Planning & Scheduling:** NASA's Deep Space 1 mission planner, industrial job-shop scheduling, and logistics at DHL all use state space search backbones.
- **VLSI & Circuit Design:** Routing wires on a chip is formulated as a state space search.
- **Bioinformatics:** DNA sequence alignment is solved via dynamic-programming-style state space search.

> [!TIP]
> KTU Board Tip: In 14-mark questions, students are often asked to "evaluate the suitability of a search strategy for a given problem." Always justify your choice by listing the four evaluation criteria (completeness, optimality, time, space) **and** linking them to the *branching factor* and *solution depth* of the specific problem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why IDDFS Achieves $O(b^d)$ Time Complexity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU 14-marker** ("Justify the time complexity of Iterative Deepening Search").

**Setup:** IDDFS runs DLS with depth limit $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \dots, d$. Let $N(\ell)$ be the number of nodes generated at iteration $\ell$.

$$N(0) = 1$$

$$N(1) = b + 1 = b^1 + b^0$$

$$N(2) = b^2 + b^1 + b^0$$

In general, the number of nodes at depth $k \leq d$ (generated during the $\ell$-th iteration, $\ell \geq k$) is $b^k$. Summing over all iterations:

$$T_{\text{IDDFS}} = \sum_{\ell=0}^{d} \sum_{k=0}^{\ell} b^k$$

We reverse the order of summation. The term $b^k$ appears once for each $\ell$ such that $k \leq \ell \leq d$, i.e., $(d - k + 1)$ times:

$$T_{\text{IDDFS}} = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k \cdot (d - k + 1)$$

$$= (d+1) \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k - \sum_{k=0}^{d} k \cdot b^k$$

Use the geometric series identity $\sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ and the weighted sum $\sum_{k=0}^{d} k b^k = \dfrac{b - (d+1) b^{d+1} + d \cdot b^{d+2}}{(b-1)^2}$:

$$T_{\text{IDDFS}} = (d+1) \cdot \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} - \frac{b - (d+1) b^{d+1} + d \cdot b^{d+2}}{(b-1)^2}$$

To leading order, both terms are dominated by the highest power $b^d$:

$$T_{\text{IDDFS}} = \Theta(b^d)$$

Specifically, the total node count is:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{IDDFS}} = O(b^d) \cdot \left(\frac{b}{b-1}\right) \approx O(b^d) \text{ for } b \geq 2}$$

The constant factor over BFS is at most $b/(b-1)$, e.g., for $b=2$ the overhead is $2/1 = 2$ (i.e., 100% more nodes than BFS), and for $b=10$ only $\approx 11\%$. This shows IDDFS is **asymptotically equivalent to BFS in time** while retaining DFS's $O(bd)$ space.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: A* Optimality Proof (Sketch — High-Yield KTU 14-Marker)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** A* search is optimal (i.e., finds a minimum-cost path) if $h(n)$ is admissible.

**Proof by contradiction:**

Assume A* terminates at a non-optimal goal node $n_g$ with $f(n_g) = g(n_g) > C^*$ (where $C^*$ is the optimal cost). At the moment of termination, there exists at least one optimal node $n^*$ on the frontier — a node on an optimal solution path such that $n^*$ is not yet expanded.

For $n^*$: $f(n^*) = g(n^*) + h(n^*) = C^* + h(n^*)$ (since $n^*$ lies on the optimal path, $g(n^*)$ is the cost to reach it along that path, but not necessarily the full $C^*$ — let $g(n^*) = C^* - h^*(n^*)$ where $h^*(n^*)$ is the true cost from $n^*$ to goal).

By admissibility, $h(n^*) \leq h^*(n^*)$, so:

$$f(n^*) = g(n^*) + h(n^*) \leq g(n^*) + h^*(n^*) = C^*$$

Since A* picks the node with the lowest $f$ for expansion, and at the moment of termination $n_g$ was selected over $n^*$, we must have $f(n_g) \leq f(n^*)$. Thus:

$$f(n_g) \leq f(n^*) \leq C^*$$

But we assumed $f(n_g) = g(n_g) > C^*$ — **contradiction**. Hence $n_g$ must be optimal. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Effective Branching Factor $b^*$

The **effective branching factor** is the empirical gold standard for measuring the "hardness" of a search problem instance. It is defined as the unique $b^*$ satisfying:

$$N + 1 = \sum_{i=0}^{d} (b^*)^i = \frac{(b^*)^{d+1} - 1}{b^* - 1}$$

where $N$ is the total number of nodes expanded and $d$ is the depth of the solution. A good heuristic yields $b^* \approx 1$.

**Worked example** (KTU typical 3-marker):

A* is run on a problem with $d = 5$ and expands $N = 52$ nodes (excluding the root). Find $b^*$.

We need to solve $52 + 1 = 53 = 1 + b^* + (b^*)^2 + (b^*)^3 + (b^*)^4 + (b^*)^5$.

Try $b^* = 2$: $\sum_{i=0}^{5} 2^i = 63$. Too high.

Try $b^* = 1.8$: $\sum = 1 + 1.8 + 3.24 + 5.832 + 10.4976 + 18.8957 \approx 41.27$. Too low.

Try $b^* = 1.9$: $\sum = 1 + 1.9 + 3.61 + 6.859 + 13.0321 + 24.761 \approx 51.16$. Close.

Try $b^* = 1.92$: $\sum \approx 53.2$. So $b^* \approx 1.92$.

This is a healthy branching factor — the heuristic is informative.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Search Strategy (KTU Viva/Design Question)

| **Problem Characteristic** | **Best-Fit Strategy** | **Rationale** |
|---|---|---|
| Shallow solution, unit step costs, $b$ small | **BFS** | Optimal + complete; space is tolerable |
| Deep solution, very large $b$, memory is tight | **IDDFS** | Combines BFS completeness with DFS space |
| Non-uniform step costs, optimality required | **UCS** (or A* with $h \equiv 0$) | Handles arbitrary positive costs |
| Strong admissible heuristic available | **A*** | Best informed-search trade-off |
| Infinite state space, need *any* solution fast | **Greedy Best-First** | Speed over optimality |
| Local optimization (e.g., circuit layout) | **Hill Climbing with Random Restarts** | Escapes local optima |
| Two-player zero-sum game, finite horizon | **Alpha-Beta Pruning** | Prunes suboptimal branches |
| Real-time, no time for full search | **Beam Search / Greedy** | Approximates with limited frontier |
| Adversarial, time-critical (e.g., mobile games) | **Minimax to fixed depth + heuristic eval** | Tractable approximation |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: BFS on the 8-Puzzle

**Problem (KTU board favourite):** Solve the 8-puzzle via BFS, with initial state:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 4 \\ 8 & 6 & 2 \\ 7 & \text{_} & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$
and goal state:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 8 & \text{_} & 4 \\ 7 & 6 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**State space size:** The 8-puzzle has $9! / 2 = 181{,}440$ reachable states — half of all permutations are unreachable (parity argument). Branching factor $b \approx 2.13$ on average.

**BFS expansion order** (depth-first optimal solution length is $d = 4$ moves: e.g., 5 → _ → 2 → 3 → 4 — multiple solutions exist; BFS finds the *shortest* in 4 moves).

**Total nodes generated up to depth 4:**
$$N_{\text{BFS}} = 1 + b + b^2 + b^3 + b^4 \approx 1 + 2.13 + 4.54 + 9.66 + 20.58 \approx 37.91 \approx 38 \text{ nodes}$$

**Space:** $O(b^d) \approx O(38)$ nodes kept in memory — manageable for 8-puzzle.

**Time:** $O(38)$ expansions — trivial for modern hardware. BFS is *the* right choice for the 8-puzzle.

**Contrast with 15-puzzle** ($b \approx 2.13$, $d \approx 52$): BFS would require $O(b^{52}) \approx O(10^{16})$ nodes — completely infeasible. This is where A* with the **Manhattan distance heuristic** becomes essential.

**Manhattan distance** for 8-puzzle:
$$h(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert x_i^{\text{current}} - x_i^{\text{goal}} \vert + \vert y_i^{\text{current}} - y_i^{\text{goal}} \vert$$

This heuristic is **admissible** because each tile must move at least its Manhattan distance to reach its goal, and is **consistent** (the triangle inequality holds for Manhattan distance).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal State Space Search Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef coreNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storageNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef algoNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    InitState["Initial State s0"]:::startNode
    GoalState["Goal State(s) G"]:::startNode
    ActionSet["Action Set A"]:::storageNode
    TransFn["Transition Function C"]:::storageNode
    CostFn["Cost Function c"]:::storageNode

    OpenList["Frontier Open List"]:::storageNode
    ClosedList["Explored Closed List"]:::storageNode
    HeuristicFn["Heuristic Function h"]:::storageNode

    Formulate["PROBLEM FORMULATION"]:::coreNode
    SearchEngine["SEARCH ALGORITHM CORE"]:::coreNode
    Strategy["Strategy Selector"]:::algoNode

    BFS["BFS - Queue"]:::algoNode
    DFS["DFS - Stack"]:::algoNode
    UCS["UCS - PQ by g"]:::algoNode
    AStar["A STAR - PQ by f g plus h"]:::algoNode
    Minimax["Minimax / Alpha Beta"]:::algoNode

    Expand["EXPANSION MODULE"]:::coreNode
    Eval["EVALUATION MODULE"]:::coreNode
    GoalTest["GOAL TEST MODULE"]:::coreNode
    PathRecon["PATH RECONSTRUCTION"]:::coreNode

    SolutionPath["SOLUTION PATH"]:::outputNode
    NoSolution["FAILURE SIGNAL"]:::outputNode

    InitState --> Formulate
    GoalState --> Formulate
    ActionSet --> Formulate
    TransFn --> Formulate
    CostFn --> Formulate

    Formulate --> OpenList
    Formulate --> SearchEngine
    Strategy --> BFS
    Strategy --> DFS
    Strategy --> UCS
    Strategy --> AStar
    Strategy --> Minimax

    BFS --> SearchEngine
    DFS --> SearchEngine
    UCS --> SearchEngine
    AStar --> SearchEngine
    Minimax --> SearchEngine

    HeuristicFn --> AStar
    HeuristicFn --> Minimax

    SearchEngine --> Expand
    SearchEngine --> GoalTest
    SearchEngine --> Eval

    Expand --> OpenList
    Expand --> ClosedList

    GoalTest -->|goal reached| PathRecon
    GoalTest -->|not goal| Expand

    OpenList --> PathRecon
    ClosedList --> PathRecon

    PathRecon --> SolutionPath
    SearchEngine -->|frontier empty| NoSolution
```

**Reading the diagram (for the board examiner):**
- The **yellow nodes** are the problem inputs (initial/goal state and operators).
- The **blue nodes** are the procedural core (problem formulation → search → expansion/goal test).
- The **purple nodes** are the five canonical search strategies.
- The **green nodes** are the outputs.
- The **pink nodes** are the data structures (frontier, explored set, heuristic).

---

### 4.2 State Space Graph: Romania Travel Problem (Partial)

The following is a *small excerpt* of the classical Romania route map used in Russell & Norvig (a standard KTU reference for state space search).

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef cityNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef goalNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef startNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff

    Arad((Arad)):::startNode
    Sibiu((Sibiu)):::cityNode
    Timisoara((Timisoara)):::cityNode
    Zerind((Zerind)):::cityNode
    Lugoj((Lugoj)):::cityNode
    Fagaras((Fagaras)):::cityNode
    RimnicuVilcea((Rimnicu Vilcea)):::cityNode
    Pitesti((Pitesti)):::cityNode
    Bucharest((Bucharest)):::goalNode
    Oradea((Oradea)):::cityNode
    Craiova((Craiova)):::cityNode
    Giurgiu((Giurgiu)):::cityNode

    Arad ---|118| Timisoara
    Arad ---|140| Sibiu
    Arad ---|75| Zerind
    Zerind ---|71| Oradea
    Oradea ---|151| Sibiu
    Sibiu ---|99| Fagaras
    Sibiu ---|80| RimnicuVilcea
    Timisoara ---|111| Lugoj
    Lugoj ---|70| Mehadia((Mehadia))
    Fagaras ---|211| Bucharest
    RimnicuVilcea ---|97| Pitesti
    RimnicuVilcea ---|146| Craiova
    Pitesti ---|101| Bucharest
    Craiova ---|138| Pitesti
    Bucharest ---|90| Giurgiu
```

**KTU Application:** A\* on this graph with **straight-line distance (SLD) to Bucharest** as the heuristic $h$ yields the optimal path Arad → Sibiu → Rimnicu Vilcea → Pitesti → Bucharest (cost 418). This is the canonical A* worked example.

**Heuristic values (SLD to Bucharest):**
- Arad: 366
- Sibiu: 253
- Fagaras: 176
- Pitesti: 100
- Bucharest: 0 (goal)

---

### 4.3 A* Search Tree Expansion Sequence (Arad → Bucharest)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef startNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef optNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef expandNode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prunNode fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef goalNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    A["Arad g=0 h=366 f=366"]:::startNode
    S["Sibiu g=140 h=253 f=393"]:::expandNode
    T["Timisoara g=118 h=329 f=447"]:::expandNode
    Z["Zerind g=75 h=374 f=449"]:::expandNode

    A --> S
    A --> T
    A --> Z

    RV["RimnicuVilcea g=220 h=193 f=413"]:::optNode
    F["Fagaras g=239 h=176 f=415"]:::expandNode
    O["Oradea g=146 h=380 f=526"]:::prunNode

    S --> RV
    S --> F
    S --> O

    P["Pitesti g=317 h=100 f=417"]:::optNode
    Bg["Bucharest via Fagaras g=450 h=0 f=450"]:::expandNode
    C["Craiova g=366 h=160 f=526"]:::prunNode

    RV --> P
    RV --> C
    F --> Bg

    BgGoal["Bucharest via Pitesti g=418 h=0 f=418"]:::goalNode
    P --> BgGoal
```

> [!TIP]
> **Read the diagram:** A* expands nodes in increasing order of $f$. The path Arad → Sibiu → Rimnicu Vilcea → Pitesti → Bucharest (total cost 418) is opened and selected. Bucharest via Fagaras (cost 450) is never selected because $f = 450 > 418$ is reached. The "pruned" node (Oradea) shows that A* can avoid expanding suboptimal branches.

---

### 4.4 Alpha-Beta Pruning Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef maxNode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef minNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leafNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pruneNode fill:#696969,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef rootNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    Root["MAX ROOT alpha = -inf beta = inf"]:::rootNode
    L1["MIN L1 alpha = 3 beta = inf"]:::minNode
    L2["MIN L2 alpha = 3 beta = inf"]:::minNode

    Leaf1["Leaf value 3"]:::leafNode
    Leaf2["Leaf value 12 - PRUNED"]:::pruneNode
    Leaf3["Leaf value 2"]:::leafNode
    Leaf4["Leaf value value x - not computed"]:::prunNode

    Root --> L1
    Root --> L2
    L1 --> Leaf1
    L1 --> Leaf2
    L2 --> Leaf3
    L2 --> Leaf4
```

**Reading the diagram:** After MAX evaluates the first child of L1 (leaf = 3), $\alpha = 3$. The second child (leaf = 12) is examined; since $12 \geq \beta = +\infty$, no pruning yet, but L1's value updates to $\min(3, 12) = 3$. Now at L2, $\alpha = 3$. The first child of L2 evaluates to 2, so $L2 \leq 2 \leq \alpha = 3$. **Beta cutoff** triggers: remaining children of L2 are pruned because MIN cannot raise L2's value above 2, which is already $\leq \alpha$. This is the classical alpha-cutoff case.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Q1.** Differentiate between **tree search** and **graph search**. Why is graph search preferred for problems with repeated states?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tree Search | Graph Search |
|---|---|---|
| States revisited | Yes — each path is independent | No — uses an **explored set** |
| Memory | Less bookkeeping | More memory (closed list) |
| Optimality (with $h$) | Not always optimal | Optimal if $h$ is consistent |
| When to use | No cycles / no repeated states | Real problems with loops |

**Graph search is preferred** for problems like the 8-puzzle, route-finding, and any problem where actions are reversible, because it eliminates redundant work — without it, the same state can be reached via exponentially many paths.

**Marks Breakdown (KTU scheme):** Definition of tree search (1), definition of graph search (1), one valid reason for preference (1).

---

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
**Q2.** Define an **admissible heuristic**. Why is the **Manhattan distance** heuristic admissible for the 8-puzzle but the **Euclidean distance** is not generally used?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **admissible heuristic** $h(n)$ is one that **never overestimates** the true cost to reach a goal from $n$: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$.

**Manhattan distance is admissible** for the 8-puzzle because each tile must move at least its Manhattan distance to reach its goal square, and Manhattan distance can be exactly achieved (e.g., by a sequence of single-axis moves), so it is a *lower bound* on the true cost.

**Why not Euclidean:** Euclidean distance is *also* a lower bound, but for grid-moving puzzles the actual cost is measured in *grid steps*, not straight-line distance. Manhattan is the **tightest** admissible grid-distance heuristic, giving stronger guidance. (2 of 3 marks go to admissibility, 1 to the comparison.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Module Convention:** "Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each full question carries **14 marks**, typically split into (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**."

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q4]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the working of the **A\* search algorithm** with a suitable example. Prove that A* is optimal if the heuristic used is **admissible**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider the following state space graph where node **S** is the start and **G** is the goal. Step costs are shown on edges. Trace the execution of **A\*** using $h$ values given below. Show the open and closed lists at each step, and report the final optimal path and its cost.

```
         S ----1----> A ----3----> G
         |            ^
         5            |
         |            1
         v            |
         B ----1----> C
                       |
                       1
                       v
                       G (also reachable from C, cost 1)
```

Heuristic values: $h(S) = 4$, $h(A) = 2$, $h(B) = 4$, $h(C) = 1$, $h(G) = 0$.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Working of A\* (5 marks):**

A\* maintains two lists: **OPEN** (frontier of generated but unexpanded nodes) and **CLOSED** (already expanded). It selects for expansion the node $n$ in OPEN with the **lowest $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$**. $g(n)$ is the path cost from start to $n$, and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to the goal.

**Pseudocode outline (Board expects algorithmic style, not Python):**

```
1. Put start node S in OPEN (with f(S) = h(S)).
2. Repeat:
    a. If OPEN is empty → return FAILURE.
    b. Remove n with lowest f from OPEN → put in CLOSED.
    c. If n is goal → return path by backtracking pointers.
    d. Expand n: for each successor n':
         - compute g(n') = g(n) + cost(n → n')
         - compute f(n') = g(n') + h(n')
         - if n' not in OPEN or CLOSED, add to OPEN.
         - if n' already in OPEN with higher g, replace.
3. End repeat.
```

**Example — Romania map (Arad → Bucharest):** A* with straight-line-distance heuristic expands Arad ($f=366$), then Sibiu ($f=253+140=393$), then Rimnicu Vilcea ($f=220+193=413$), then Pitesti ($f=317+100=417$), then Bucharest via Pitesti ($f=418+0=418$). The path Arad → Sibiu → Rimnicu Vilcea → Pitesti → Bucharest of cost 418 is returned.

**Optimality Proof (2 marks):**

[Refer to the proof in Section 3.2 of these notes. Key steps:]

- Suppose A* terminates at non-optimal goal $n_g$ with $f(n_g) = g(n_g) > C^*$.
- There exists an optimal node $n^*$ on OPEN, lying on an optimal solution path, with $f(n^*) \leq C^*$ (by admissibility).
- A* chose $n_g$ over $n^*$, so $f(n_g) \leq f(n^*) \leq C^*$, contradicting $f(n_g) > C^*$.
- Therefore $n_g$ is optimal. $\blacksquare$

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step-by-step A* execution table (7 marks):**

| Step | Node Expanded | OPEN (with $f$-values) | CLOSED |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | $\{(S, 0+4=4)\}$ | $\{\}$ |
| 1 | **S** | $\{(A, 1+2=3), (B, 5+4=9)\}$ | $\{S\}$ |
| 2 | **A** | $\{(C, 1+1+1=3 \text{ via A→C}), (B, 5+4=9)\}$ | $\{S, A\}$ |
| 3 | **C** | $\{(G, 1+1+1+0=3 \text{ via C→G}), (B, 5+4=9)\}$ | $\{S, A, C\}$ |
| 4 | **G** | **GOAL — return path** | $\{S, A, C, G\}$ |

Wait — let me recompute carefully. Path S→A→C→G has cost $1 + 1 + 1 = 3$, with $f = 3 + 0 = 3$ at G. Direct path via C: $g = 3$.

**Optimal path: S → A → C → G with total cost = 3.**

(Each step in the table gets ~1.4 marks; the final answer = 1.4 marks; explanation of A* selection rule = 1.4 marks.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Q5]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **Breadth-First Search (BFS)**, **Depth-First Search (DFS)**, and **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)** with respect to completeness, optimality, time, and space complexities. Justify the time complexity of IDDFS.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Apply the **Minimax algorithm with Alpha-Beta pruning** to the following game tree (MAX plays at the root). Show the final values at each node, the cutoffs (if any), and the move chosen by MAX at the root.

```
                          MAX
                       /      \
                     MIN        MIN
                    /   \      /   \
                   3     12  2     ?
                  /     /     \     \
                 15    2      8     x (prune candidate)
                                  (deeper: 14, 5)
```

Use the additional terminal values given (assume the "?" branch has leaf values 14 and 5, in that order, from left to right).

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Criterion | BFS | DFS | IDDFS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Complete? | Yes | No (infinite paths/loops) | Yes |
| Optimal? | Yes (unit costs) | No | Yes (unit costs) |
| Time | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| Space | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bm)$ | $O(bd)$ |

(4 marks for the table; 2 marks for IDDFS time complexity derivation; 1 mark for the right conclusion.)

**IDDFS Time Complexity Derivation (1.5–2 marks):**

IDDFS runs DLS with $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \dots, d$.

Total nodes expanded:
$$N = \sum_{\ell=0}^{d} \sum_{k=0}^{\ell} b^k = (d+1) \cdot 1 + d \cdot b + (d-1) \cdot b^2 + \cdots + 1 \cdot b^d$$

Reordering the sum (term $b^k$ appears in iterations $\ell = k, k+1, \dots, d$, giving $d - k + 1$ occurrences):

$$N = \sum_{k=0}^{d} (d - k + 1) b^k$$

Using the geometric series identities, the dominant term is $b^d$, and the total is at most $\frac{b}{b-1} \cdot b^d = O(b^d)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $(b/(b-1))$ constant** — Students often claim IDDFS expands *exactly* the same nodes as BFS. This is wrong; it expands up to **2×** as many for $b=2$, and the constant $\frac{b}{b-1}$ should appear.
> 2. **Confusing optimality of DFS with IDDFS** — DFS is **not** optimal; IDDFS **is** (for unit costs). This is a common conflation.
> 3. **Confusing space complexities** — A common mistake is claiming BFS uses $O(bm)$ or IDDFS uses $O(b^d)$. The correct space for BFS is $O(b^d)$ (it stores all frontier nodes), DFS is $O(bm)$, and IDDFS is $O(bd)$.
> 4. **Not specifying "for unit costs"** when claiming BFS/IDDFS are optimal. If step costs vary, BFS is **not** optimal — UCS is.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

Working through the tree with Alpha-Beta, with $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$:

1. **Left MIN node:**
   - First child: leaf = 3. MIN updates value = 3. Send back to MAX: $\alpha = \max(\alpha, 3) = 3$. Now $\alpha = 3$, $\beta = \infty$.
   - Second child: leaf = 12. MIN updates value = $\min(3, 12) = 3$. Send back: $\alpha = 3$. (No cutoff yet because this is the last child.)

2. **Right MIN node:**
   - First child: leaf = 2. MIN updates value = 2. Send back to MAX: $\alpha = \max(3, 2) = 3$, $\beta = \infty$.
   - Second child: leaf = 14. MIN updates value = $\min(2, 14) = 2$. No cutoff — but now $2 \leq \alpha = 3$, so when the third child (x = 5) is examined:
   - Third child: leaf = 5. Before evaluating, $\alpha = 3$, $\beta = 2$. Since $\alpha \geq \beta$ → **BETA CUTOFF** at the right child of MIN. (However, in this particular tree we already evaluated all children; if there were a fourth child, it would be pruned.)

**Final annotated values:**

```
                          MAX (3)
                       /      \
                     MIN(3)    MIN(2)
                    /   \      / | \
                   3    12    2  14  5
```

**MAX's choice at root: LEFT subtree** (value 3 > 2). MAX selects the first MIN child.

**Marks Distribution (7 marks for part b):**
- Tracing algorithm step-by-step (3 marks)
- Identifying cutoffs / annotations (2 marks)
- Final answer (path chosen) (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Alpha-Beta Common Errors:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$** — Remember: $\alpha$ = best value MAX can guarantee (so far); $\beta$ = best value MIN can guarantee (so far). Pruning happens when $\alpha \geq \beta$.
> 2. **Forgetting that the order of evaluation matters** — Alpha-Beta gives $O(b^{m/2})$ *only* with perfect move ordering. The examiner may deduct marks if you state this complexity without mentioning the ordering assumption.
> 3. **Not annotating both pruned branches and final values** — many students compute the answer but forget to mark which branches are cut.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Summary for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 2: State Space Search**

**Core Definitions (must memorize verbatim):**
- ☐ **State Space** = $\langle S, A, s_0, G, C, c \rangle$ — 6-tuple of states, actions, initial, goal, transition, cost.
- ☐ **Search Tree** = explicit enumeration of reachable states from $s_0$.
- ☐ **Admissible Heuristic** = $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — never overestimates true cost.
- ☐ **Consistent Heuristic** = $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ — triangle inequality on costs.

**Algorithm Complexity Cheat Sheet (the four pillars):**
- ☐ **BFS:** Complete ✓, Optimal ✓ (unit costs), Time $O(b^d)$, Space $O(b^d)$.
- ☐ **UCS:** Complete ✓, Optimal ✓, Time $O(b^{1+C^*/\epsilon})$, Space same.
- ☐ **DFS:** Complete ✗, Optimal ✗, Time $O(b^m)$, Space $O(bm)$.
- ☐ **DLS:** Complete ✓ (if $\ell \geq d$), Optimal ✗, Time $O(b^\ell)$, Space $O(b\ell)$.
- ☐ **IDDFS:** Complete ✓, Optimal ✓, Time $O(b^d)$, Space $O(bd)$.
- ☐ **A\*:** Complete ✓, Optimal ✓ (if $h$ admissible), Time $O(b^d)$ worst, Space $O(b^d)$.
- ☐ **Minimax:** Time $O(b^m)$, Space $O(bm)$.
- ☐ **Alpha-Beta Best Case:** Time $O(b^{m/2})$, Space $O(bm)$.

**Key Heuristics to Know:**
- ☐ **8-puzzle/15-puzzle:** Manhattan distance $h_1$.
- ☐ **8-puzzle advanced:** $h_2$ = Manhattan + (number of linear conflicts × 2).
- ☐ **Map routing:** Straight-line distance (SLD) to goal.
- ☐ **Sliding-tile admissibility proof:** Manhattan ≤ true cost because any sequence achieving the goal must move each tile at least its Manhattan distance.

**Alpha-Beta Pruning Rules (high-yield):**
- ☐ Prune when $\alpha \geq \beta$.
- ☐ Best case = $O(b^{m/2})$ with perfect move ordering.
- ☐ Average (random ordering) = $O(b^{3m/4})$.
- ☐ Worst case = $O(b^m)$ (same as plain Minimax).

**Frequently-Asked KTU Pitfalls (avoid these):**
- ☐ Don't claim BFS is optimal for **non-uniform** step costs (it isn't — UCS is).
- ☐ Don't claim A* finds an optimal solution if $h$ is **not** admissible.
- ☐ Don't confuse **graph search** (uses closed list) with **tree search** (no closed list).
- ☐ Don't forget IDDFS wastes some node-expansions on earlier depth limits — but only by a constant factor of $\frac{b}{b-1}$.
- ☐ For Minimax, remember **MAX picks max of children's min-values**, alternating layers.
- ☐ When asked "is X complete?", qualify with the conditions (e.g., "yes, if step costs $\geq \epsilon > 0$").

**One-Sentence Summary:**
> *State space search is the process of systematically exploring the graph of all reachable world configurations — using a strategy that trades off completeness, optimality, time, and space — to find a sequence of actions that transforms the agent's initial state into a goal state.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partial Order Planning Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Partial Order Planning Graphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Partial Order Planning Graphs — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Partial Order Planning (POP) Graph** is a bipartite, leveled graph data structure used in classical AI planning to represent a planning problem's state space, action applicability, and mutual exclusion (mutex) relations in a compact, layered form. It extends the concept of a **planning graph** (introduced by Blum & Furst, 1995 in Graphplan) by superimposing *partial-order* semantics: nodes within and across levels are not rigidly sequenced, but are bound by a set of **partial ordering constraints**, **causal links**, and **threat relations**.

Formally, a POP Graph is defined as the 4-tuple:

$$\mathcal{PG} = \langle P, A, E, M \rangle$$

where:
- $P$ = set of proposition (fact) levels $\{P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}$
- $A$ = set of action levels $\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$ interleaved between proposition levels
- $E \subseteq (P_i \times A_{i+1}) \cup (A_i \times P_{i+1})$ = set of precondition and effect edges
- $M$ = set of binary **mutex** (mutual exclusion) relations between nodes within the same level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCCAT601 — Module 2):**
> A *Partial Order Plan* is a plan in which steps are partially ordered; only those orderings required to respect causal dependencies are specified. The associated *plan graph* compactly encodes reachability and conflict information, allowing the planner to detect dead-ends early and extract **heuristics** for guiding the search.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

Imagine you are organizing a **wedding reception** in a hall.

- The **tasks** (book caterer, order flowers, arrange chairs, bake cake, send invitations) do not all have to happen in a strict sequence.
- *Booking the caterer* **must** happen before *ordering food* (causal link).
- *Arranging chairs* and *baking the cake* can be done in **any order** (partial order) — neither depends on the other.
- However, you cannot "book the caterer" and "cancel the caterer" at the same time (mutex / threat).

A **Partial Order Plan** records *only the necessary dependencies* and leaves everything else flexible. The **Planning Graph** is your *checklist board* on the wall, where each column is a time-slice showing what facts are true and what actions are happening, with red strings (mutex) tying down tasks that cannot co-occur. This visual board is what a POP solver uses to plan efficiently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Total Order Planning (TOP):**
> - **TOP** → strict linear sequence: $S_0 \to a_1 \to S_1 \to a_2 \to S_2 \to \ldots$
> - **POP** → flexible ordering: only the *causally essential* sequencing is enforced, giving more freedom and often shorter, more robust plans.

## 1.3 Physical / Conceptual Constants & Metrics

- **Graph Expansion Rule:** Each proposition level $P_i$ contains all facts that could possibly be true at step $i$.
- **Persistence Actions (No-Ops):** A virtual action $\epsilon_p$ that propagates proposition $p$ from $P_i$ to $P_{i+1}$ if no real action produces it. The set of no-ops is denoted $A_{\epsilon}$.
- **Mutex Pruning Threshold:** A planning graph *levels off* when $P_i = P_{i+1}$ and $A_i = A_{i+1}$ (fixed point reached). If goals are not all present in a non-mutex state at that level, the problem is **unsolvable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Mapping — Course Outcomes (CO):**
> This topic directly supports **CO2:** *Apply partial-order planning techniques to construct causal-link plans and reason about threats, enabling the design of intelligent agent planners.* (Cognitive Level: Apply / Analyze — Bloom's Level 3–4)

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Structure of a POP Graph

A POP Graph is built **level by level**, alternating between *proposition levels* and *action levels*:

| Level Index | Type | Contents |
|:-----------:|:----:|:---------|
| $P_0$ | Proposition | Initial state facts (ground literals) |
| $A_1$ | Action | All actions whose preconditions ⊆ $P_0$ + no-op actions |
| $P_1$ | Proposition | Add-effects of $A_1$ actions ∪ persistent $P_0$ facts |
| $A_2$ | Action | Actions whose preconditions ⊆ $P_1$ + no-ops |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $P_k$ | Proposition | $k$-step reachable facts |
| $A_k$ | Action | $k$-step executable actions |

## 2.2 Construction Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Initialize Proposition Level $P_0$**
Insert every ground literal true in the initial state $s_0$.

**Step 2 — Build Action Level $A_i$**
For every ground action $a$ in the planning domain, instantiate $a$ in $A_i$ if:
- $\text{pre}(a) \subseteq P_{i-1}$
- Also include the *no-op* action $\epsilon_p$ for every $p \in P_{i-1}$.

**Step 3 — Build Proposition Level $P_i$**
$P_i = \{ p \mid \exists a \in A_i \text{ with } p \in \text{eff}^+(a) \} \cup \{ p \in P_{i-1} \mid \epsilon_p \in A_i \text{ and no deleter of } p \text{ in } A_i \}$

**Step 4 — Compute Mutex Relations**
Two actions $a, b$ at the same level are **mutex** if any of:
1. *Inconsistent effects:* $\text{eff}^+(a) \cap \text{eff}^-(b) \neq \emptyset$ or vice-versa
2. *Interference:* $\text{eff}^-(a) \cap \text{eff}^+(b) \neq \emptyset$
3. *Competing needs:* $\exists p \in \text{pre}(a) \cap \text{pre}(b)$ such that $p$ is mutex in $P_{i-1}$ with the corresponding pre of the other action

Two propositions $p, q$ at the same level are **mutex** if:
- Every action producing $p$ is mutex with every action producing $q$.

**Step 5 — Termination / Fixed-Point**
Stop when $P_i = P_{i-1}$ **and** $A_i = A_{i-1}$.

## 2.3 Partial-Order Plan Elements

A Partial-Order Plan is itself a 4-tuple:

$$\Pi = \langle S, O, B, L \rangle$$

| Element | Symbol | Meaning |
|:-------:|:------:|:--------|
| Steps | $S$ | Set of plan steps (actions $a_1, a_2, \ldots$ + dummy $a_0$ (start) and $a_\infty$ (finish)) |
| Ordering Constraints | $O$ | Partial order: $a_i \prec a_j$ ("$a_i$ before $a_j$") |
| Binding Constraints | $B$ | Equality/inequality constraints on variable bindings |
| Causal Links | $L$ | $a_i \xrightarrow{p} a_j$: $a_i$ achieves precondition $p$ for $a_j$ |

## 2.4 Threats and Protection

A **threat** arises when an action $a_k$ has an effect $\neg p$ (deletes $p$) and is *unconstrained* with respect to a causal link $a_i \xrightarrow{p} a_j$, i.e., $a_i \prec a_k \prec a_j$ is not enforced. The planner resolves threats by:

1. **Promotion:** Add ordering $a_k \prec a_i$ (do threatener before producer)
2. **Demotion:** Add ordering $a_j \prec a_k$ (do threatener after consumer)
3. **Separation:** Add binding constraint making threatener's effect inapplicable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A POP graph's *mutex relations* are the static, pre-computed equivalent of the dynamic *threat detection* in POP. Recognizing a mutex in the graph immediately reveals a potential threat without re-running threat analysis.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation / Unit |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------------|:----------------|
| 1 | Plan Graph Levels | $P_0, A_1, P_1, A_2, \ldots, P_n$ | Integer levels $i \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| 2 | Add-Effects of Level | $P_i = \bigcup_{a \in A_i} \text{eff}^+(a)$ | Set union |
| 3 | Action Mutex — Inconsistent Effects | $\text{eff}^+(a) \cap \text{eff}^-(b) \neq \emptyset$ | Boolean |
| 4 | Action Mutex — Interference | $\text{eff}^-(a) \cap \text{eff}^+(b) \neq \emptyset$ | Boolean |
| 5 | Prop Mutex Condition | $\forall a_p \in \text{prod}(p), \forall a_q \in \text{prod}(q): a_p \, \text{mutex}\, a_q$ | Universal quantifier |
| 6 | Fixed-Point Termination | $P_i = P_{i+1} \land A_i = A_{i+1}$ | Set equality |
| 7 | Causal Link | $a_i \xrightarrow{p} a_j$ | Tuple $\langle a_i, p, a_j \rangle$ |
| 8 | Threat Condition | $\exists a_k : p \in \text{eff}^+(a_i) \land \neg p \in \text{eff}(a_k) \land \text{not } (a_k \prec a_i \lor a_j \prec a_k)$ | Existential |
| 9 | No-Op Count | $\vert A_\epsilon \vert = \vert P_{i-1} \vert$ | Cardinality |
| 10 | Graphplan Heuristic $h_{\text{level}}$ | $h_{\text{level}}(s) = \min_i \, [s \subseteq P_i \text{ and no two goals mutex in } P_i]$ | Layer index $i$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall — Mutex Distinction:**
> Students often confuse *Proposition Mutex* with *Action Mutex*. Remember:
> - **Action Mutex** = two actions cannot execute at the *same time step*.
> - **Proposition Mutex** = two facts *cannot both be true* at the same time step.
> These are *related but not identical*: a proposition mutex *is the consequence of* action mutexes on its producers.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Utility of POP Graphs |
|:-------|:----------------------|
| **Robotics / Autonomous Agents** | Partial-order task planning where actions are concurrent or parallelizable (e.g., warehouse robot fleets). |
| **Manufacturing Workflows** | Flexible assembly lines where some operations can be reordered without violating constraints. |
| **Spacecraft Mission Planning** | NASA EUROPA / MAPGEN use partial-order causal-link planning for downlink/uplink scheduling. |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Resolving inter-dependencies in delivery, packaging, and inventory replenishment. |
| **Cybersecurity / IDS** | Modeling attacker-defender action sequences with non-deterministic ordering. |

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — The "Dinner Domain"

We will use the classical AI textbook domain (Russell & Norvig) to construct a POP graph.

### Domain Definition

**Initial State ($S_0$):**
- $\text{garbage}, \text{cleanHands}, \text{quiet}$

**Goal State ($G$):**
- $\text{dinner}, \text{presented}, \text{noGarbage}$

**Operators:**

| Action | Precondition | Add-Effect | Delete-Effect |
|:-------|:-------------|:-----------|:--------------|
| **Cook** | cleanHands | dinner | — |
| **Wrap** | quiet | presented | — |
| **Carry** | — | garbage, cleanHands | garbage, cleanHands |
| **Dolly** | — | garbage | — |
| **TakeOut** | — | noGarbage, cleanHands | garbage |
| **Hand-wash** | — | cleanHands | — |

### Construction of the Planning Graph

**Level $P_0$ (Initial):**

$$
P_0 = \{ \text{garbage},\; \text{cleanHands},\; \text{quiet} \}
$$

**Level $A_1$ (Applicable actions + no-ops):**

Check preconditions against $P_0$:
- $\text{Cook}$: needs cleanHands ✓ → applicable
- $\text{Wrap}$: needs quiet ✓ → applicable
- $\text{Carry}$: no preconditions ✓ → applicable
- $\text{Dolly}$: no preconditions ✓ → applicable
- $\text{TakeOut}$: no preconditions ✓ → applicable
- $\text{Hand-wash}$: no preconditions ✓ → applicable
- No-ops $\epsilon_{\text{garbage}}, \epsilon_{\text{cleanHands}}, \epsilon_{\text{quiet}}$

$$
A_1 = \{ \text{Cook}, \text{Wrap}, \text{Carry}, \text{Dolly}, \text{TakeOut}, \text{Hand-wash}, \epsilon_{\text{garbage}}, \epsilon_{\text{cleanHands}}, \epsilon_{\text{quiet}} \}
$$

**Compute Action Mutexes at $A_1$:**

- $\text{Carry}$ vs $\text{TakeOut}$: TakeOut deletes garbage, Carry adds garbage → **Inconsistent effects** → **MUTEX**
- $\text{Carry}$ vs $\text{Cook}$: Carry deletes cleanHands, Cook needs cleanHands at the next level (via precondition, but no immediate interference) → no direct mutex here.
- $\text{TakeOut}$ vs $\text{Cook}$: TakeOut adds cleanHands (no conflict with Cook's pre). No mutex.
- $\text{TakeOut}$ vs $\text{Dolly}$: Both produce garbage-related effects inconsistently (Dolly adds garbage, TakeOut deletes garbage) → **MUTEX**

**Level $P_1$:**

Add-effects of all actions:

$$
P_1 = \{ \text{garbage},\; \text{cleanHands},\; \text{quiet},\; \text{dinner},\; \text{presented},\; \text{noGarbage} \}
$$

**Proposition Mutexes at $P_1$:**

- $\text{garbage}$ vs $\text{noGarbage}$: Producers garbage is from (Carry, Dolly, $\epsilon_{\text{garbage}}$) and noGarbage is from (TakeOut). Carry is mutex with TakeOut, Dolly is mutex with TakeOut → **all producers of garbage are mutex with all producers of noGarbage** → **garbage MUTEX noGarbage**.
- $\text{garbage}$ vs $\text{cleanHands}$: TakeOut produces both. → NOT mutex.

**Level $A_2$:**

New applicable actions: Now Cook's precondition cleanHands is preserved (or re-established), Wrap is preserved.

$$
A_2 = A_1 \cup \text{repeat} = \{ \text{Cook}, \text{Wrap}, \text{Carry}, \text{Dolly}, \text{TakeOut}, \text{Hand-wash}, \ldots \}
$$

**Level $P_2$:**

Goals $\{$dinner, presented, noGarbage$\}$ all present, with no two being mutex (dinner and noGarbage have no mutex producers, presented and noGarbage have no mutex producers). 

**Therefore, the goals are *non-mutex* in $P_2$ → the plan exists!**

### Plan Extraction (with Partial-Order Semantics)

We can now extract a valid **Partial-Order Plan**:

$$
\Pi = \langle S, O, B, L \rangle
$$

**Steps $S$:**

$$
S = \{ a_0, \text{Carry}, \text{TakeOut}, \text{Cook}, \text{Wrap}, a_\infty \}
$$

**Causal Links $L$ (which action achieves which precondition):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Carry} &\xrightarrow{\text{garbage}} \text{TakeOut} \\
\text{TakeOut} &\xrightarrow{\text{cleanHands}} \text{Cook} \\
\text{Cook} &\xrightarrow{\text{dinner}} a_\infty \\
\text{Wrap} &\xrightarrow{\text{presented}} a_\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ordering Constraints $O$ (the partial order):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &\prec \text{Carry} \\
\text{Carry} &\prec \text{TakeOut} \\
a_0 &\prec \text{Wrap} \\
\text{TakeOut} &\prec \text{Cook} \\
\text{Cook} &\prec a_\infty \\
\text{Wrap} &\prec a_\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: **$\text{Wrap}$ and $\text{Carry}$ are NOT ordered** — they can be done in any order (or even in parallel). This is the *partial* nature of the plan.

## 3.2 Threat Resolution Walkthrough

Suppose we add a hypothetical action $\text{DirtyHands}$ with effect $\neg\text{cleanHands}$ applicable at $A_1$.

**Threat detected:**

$$
\text{TakeOut} \xrightarrow{\text{cleanHands}} \text{Cook} \quad \text{is threatened by} \quad \text{DirtyHands}
$$

**Resolution choices:**

| Strategy | New Ordering Added | Interpretation |
|:---------|:-------------------|:---------------|
| **Promotion** | DirtyHands $\prec$ TakeOut | DirtyHands must run *before* TakeOut |
| **Demotion** | Cook $\prec$ DirtyHands | DirtyHands must run *after* Cook (i.e., not before Cook consumes cleanHands) |
| **Separation** | (binding constraint) | Force parameter disequality so effect is inapplicable |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mutex Inference Rules Summary

| Rule Name | Type | Condition | Conclusion |
|:----------|:----:|:----------|:-----------|
| **Inconsistent Effects (IE)** | Action | $\text{eff}^+(a) \cap \text{eff}^-(b) \neq \emptyset$ | $a \, \text{mutex} \, b$ |
| **Interference (INT)** | Action | $\text{eff}^-(a) \cap \text{eff}^+(b) \neq \emptyset$ | $a \, \text{mutex} \, b$ |
| **Competing Needs (CN)** | Action | $\exists p \in \text{pre}(a) \cap \text{pre}(b) \text{ with } p \, \text{mutex} \, p'$ in $P_{i-1}$ | $a \, \text{mutex} \, b$ |
| **Inconsistent Support (IS)** | Proposition | $\forall a_p, a_q: a_p \, \text{mutex} \, a_q$ (producers) | $p \, \text{mutex} \, q$ |
| **Persistence is Default** | Proposition | $\epsilon_p$ exists, no deleter | $p \in P_{i+1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Insight — Why the Graph Levels Off:**
> Because $P_i \subseteq P_{i+1}$ (monotonic growth) and the action set is finite, by the **monotone sequence theorem** on finite sets, the graph must reach a fixed point in $O(2^N)$ levels where $N$ = number of propositions. In practice, the level-off occurs within $N$ to $2N$ levels for most domains.

## 3.4 Heuristic Extraction from POP Graphs

Once the graph is built, we can derive **admissible heuristics** for a forward planner:

**Level Heuristic $h_{\text{level}}(s)$:** smallest $i$ such that all propositions in $s$ are present in $P_i$ with no two being mutex.

**Set-Level Heuristic $h_{\text{set-level}}$:** Sum (or max) of individual level costs:

$$
h_{\text{set-level}}(s) = \sum_{p \in s} h_{\text{level}}(\{p\})
$$

$$
h_{\text{max-level}}(s) = \max_{p \in s} h_{\text{level}}(\{p\})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $h_{\text{set-level}}$ is **inadmissible** (overestimates) but very informative in practice. $h_{\text{max-level}}$ is **admissible** if the propositions are independent.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Architecture of a POP Graph Solver

The following block diagram illustrates how the POP graph and partial-order plan interact within a planner agent:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Domain Description] --> parserA
    inputB[Initial State] --> parserA
    inputC[Goal Description] --> parserA
    parserA[Problem Parser] --> graphBuilder
    graphBuilder[Plan Graph Constructor] --> mutexEngine
    mutexEngine[Mutex Inference Engine] --> leveledGraph
    leveledGraph[Leveled Plan Graph PG] --> heuristicExtractor
    leveledGraph --> planExtractor
    heuristicExtractor[Heuristic Extractor] --> searchController
    planExtractor[Plan Extractor Backward Search] --> causalLinkChecker
    causalLinkChecker[Causal Link + Threat Analyzer] --> popPlan
    searchController[Search Controller] --> popPlan
    popPlan[Partial Order Plan] --> validator
    validator[Plan Validator Simulator] --> outputA
    outputA[Output Executable Plan or Failure]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block Architecture — Internal Function Mapping

This is a more granular functional block diagram of the POP graph planner's internal data flow:

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        A1[Load Operators] --> A2[Load Initial Facts]
        A2 --> A3[Load Goal Facts]
    end

    subgraph B[Graph Builder]
        B1[Prop Level 0 Builder] --> B2[Action Level i Builder]
        B2 --> B3[Prop Level i Builder]
        B3 --> B4[Fixed Point Detector]
    end

    subgraph C[Mutex Engine]
        C1[Action Mutex Rule IE] --> C4[Mutex Set A_mutex]
        C2[Action Mutex Rule INT] --> C4
        C3[Action Mutex Rule CN] --> C4
        C4 --> C5[Prop Mutex Rule IS]
        C5 --> C6[Mutex Set P_mutex]
    end

    subgraph D[Plan Synthesizer]
        D1[Backward Goal Search] --> D2[Action Selection]
        D2 --> D3[Causal Link Creation]
        D3 --> D4[Threat Detection]
        D4 --> D5{Threat Found?}
        D5 -- Yes --> D6[Promotion or Demotion or Separation]
        D6 --> D4
        D5 -- No --> D7[Add Ordering Constraint]
        D7 --> D8{All Goals Achieved?}
        D8 -- No --> D1
        D8 -- Yes --> D9[Output POP Plan]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    NoThreat --> GoalCheck

    ThreatDetected --> Demotion: Resolve by demoting threatener
    ThreatDetected --> Separation: Resolve by binding constraint
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Partial Order Plan. How does it differ from a Total Order Plan?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Partial Order Plan (POP)** is a plan representation in which the ordering between steps is *not fully specified*; only the orderings required to satisfy causal dependencies (precondition-effect links) are enforced.
