> **KTU Board Diagram Rule:** Always include the **feedback arrow from MONITOR back to PLAN**. Many students lose 2 marks for drawing a one-way pipeline instead of a closed loop. The closed loop *is* the essence of "continuous."

### 4.2 Component-Level Block Diagram

This block diagram shows the **internal functional modules** of a continuous planning agent and their information flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph World[ENVIRONMENT]
        W[World State s]
    end
    subgraph Agent[INTELLIGENT AGENT]
        S[Sensor Module] --> P[Planner Module P]
        P --> E[Executor Module E]
        E --> A[Actuator Module]
        M[Monitor Module M] --> P
        M --> R[Replanner Module R]
        R --> P
        KB[Belief / Knowledge Base T R] --> P
        KB --> M
    end
    A --> W
    W --> S
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Repair vs. Replan

The following diagram clarifies the **decision logic** the agent uses to choose between local repair and full replanning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Failure Detected] --> Q1{Is root cause localised?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Cost of repair less than replan?}
    Q2 -- YES --> Rep[Perform LOCAL REPAIR]
    Q2 -- NO --> ReP[Perform FULL REPLAN]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Goal g changed?}
    Q3 -- YES --> ReG[Replan with NEW GOAL]
    Q3 -- NO --> ReM[Replan with UPDATED WORLD MODEL]
    Rep --> Resume[Resume Execution]
    ReP --> Resume
    ReG --> Resume
    ReM --> Resume
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) View of Continuous Planning

For complex agents (e.g., autonomous drones), continuous planning is often organized hierarchically. The top level monitors mission goals; the middle level manages sub-tasks; the bottom level executes primitive actions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Mission Goal Level] --> S1[Subtask 1 Navigate to Waypoint A]
    M --> S2[Subtask 2 Survey Region R]
    M --> S3[Subtask 3 Return to Base]
    S1 --> P1[Primitive Move North 5 km]
    S1 --> P2[Primitive Avoid No Fly Zone]
    S2 --> P3[Primitive Capture Image]
    S2 --> P4[Primitive Hover at Altitude 100 m]
    S3 --> P5[Primitive Land Safely]
    P1 --> MON[Continuous Monitor]
    P2 --> MON
    P3 --> MON
    P4 --> MON
    P5 --> MON
    MON --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HTN is central:** HTN planning allows the agent to **decompose goals** into smaller tasks. When a low-level task fails, only that sub-tree needs repair — the higher-level plan remains valid. This is precisely the *efficiency* advantage continuous planning has over flat replanning.

### 4.5 Timeline of a Continuous Plan vs. a Static Plan

```mermaid
gantt
    title Plan Adaptation Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Static Plan
    Step 1 (Fixed)      :a1, 0, 2
    Step 2 (Fixed)      :a2, 2, 2
    Step 3 (Fixed)      :a3, 4, 2
    section Continuous Plan
    Step 1              :b1, 0, 1
    Step 2              :b2, 1, 1
    Replan after Sensor Update :crit, b3, 2, 1
    Step 2 Revised      :b4, 3, 2
    Step 3              :b5, 5, 1
    Step 4 New          :b6, 6, 1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading this diagram:** The continuous plan branch contains a *critical milestone* (the replan event) at $t=2$, after which the plan is regenerated. The static plan cannot adapt, so it would either fail or perform suboptimally at the same moment.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define Continuous Planning. How does it differ from classical (one-shot) planning?*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **(1 mark)** Continuous Planning is the iterative process in which an agent perpetually interleaves plan generation, execution, monitoring, and replanning to adapt to dynamic environments and evolving goals.
> - **(1 mark)** Classical planning produces a single complete plan offline assuming a static world and fixed goal; continuous planning regenerates/refines the plan online as the world changes.
> - **(1 mark)** Classical plans halt on failure, while continuous plans self-repair or replan to maintain goal-directed behavior.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*List and briefly explain the four major components of a continuous planning agent.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **(0.75)** **Planner (P):** synthesizes the initial or revised plan from the current state and goal.
> 2. **(0.75)** **Executor (E):** applies plan actions to the environment via actuators.
> 3. **(0.75)** **Monitor (M):** observes world state, compares it to predicted state, and detects failures/invariants.
> 4. **(0.75)** **Replanner (R):** performs plan repair (local fix) or full replanning when the monitor signals a deviation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Slot]**
*(a)* Define Continuous Planning. With a neat block diagram, explain the Sense–Plan–Act–Monitor–Replan cycle in a continuous planning agent. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Differentiate between plan repair and full replanning. Under what conditions is repair preferred over replanning? Provide a numerical example to justify. *(7 marks)*

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**
Continuous Planning is a paradigm of agent-based planning in which the agent does not commit to a single static plan, but continuously interleaves perception, deliberation, and action. It dynamically refines, repairs, or regenerates its plan in response to environmental changes, execution failures, and evolving goals.

**The Sense–Plan–Act–Monitor–Replan Cycle (5 marks):**

| Stage | Function | Output |
|-------|----------|--------|
| Sense | Perceive current world state $s_t$ via sensors | Belief update |
| Plan | Generate or refine plan $\pi_t$ using planner $\mathcal{P}$ | Plan structure |
| Act | Execute next action $a_t = \pi_t(s_t)$ | Action effect |
| Monitor | Verify $s_{t+1}^{\text{obs}} \approx T(s_t, a_t)$ and check preconditions | Validity flag |
| Replan | Repair locally or regenerate fully via $\mathcal{R}$ | Updated plan |

```
        +-----------------------------+
        |         AGENT CORE          |
        |                             |
   Sensors --> SENSE --> PLAN --+    |
                                |    |
                                v    |
                              ACT ---+---> Actuators --> Environment
                                ^    |
                                |    |
                            MONITOR |
                                ^    |
                                |    |
                            REPLAN -+
                                |
        +-----------------------+
        |  Feedback to PLAN closes the loop  |
        +-----------------------------------+
```

The arrows from MONITOR and REPLAN back to PLAN **close the loop**, transforming a one-shot planner into a *continuous* planner. The cycle repeats until the goal is achieved or abandoned.

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Plan Repair:** Localized modification of an *existing* plan to fix a failure. Only the steps affected by the failure (and their dependents) are replaced; the rest of the plan is preserved.
- **Full Replanning:** The current plan (or its invalid portion) is discarded and a *new* plan is synthesized from the current state, world model, and goal.

**Comparative Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Plan Repair | Full Replanning |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| Scope | Local | Global |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Preserves intent | Yes | No |
| Speed | Fast | Slow |
| Used when | Root cause is localized | Goal changed, model drastically wrong |

**Conditions favoring repair (1 mark):**
1. The failure is confined to a small sub-plan.
2. Repair cost $C_r$ is less than replan cost $C_p$.
3. The goal and most of the world model remain valid.

**Numerical Example (1 mark):**
A delivery drone has $C_r = 3$ s (rerouting around a tree), $C_p = 12$ s (recomputing the full mission), and $C_f = 8$ s (forcing through the tree with reduced safety). Since $C_r < C_p < C_f$, the optimal decision is **repair**.

---

#### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Slot]**
*(a)* Explain how the Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework supports continuous planning. Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation step by step. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss two real-world applications where continuous planning is essential. For each, explain (i) why classical planning fails, (ii) which component of the continuous loop is most critical, and (iii) one limitation of the deployed system. *(7 marks)*

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**MDP as the foundation (2 marks):**
An MDP is a tuple $\langle S, A, T, R, \gamma \rangle$. Because the MDP framework models the environment as *stochastic* and *state-dependent*, the optimal solution is a **policy** $\pi: S \to A$ rather than a single action sequence. A policy is naturally **anytime** — at every state, the agent can re-query the policy. This is mathematically equivalent to *continuous replanning*.

**Step-by-step derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation (5 marks):**

**Step 1.** Define the discounted return:
$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}, \quad 0 \le \gamma \le 1$$

**Step 2.** Define the state-value function:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 3.** Unroll the expectation:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} T(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

**Step 4.** Take the optimal action:
$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} T(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^{*}(s') \right]$$

**Step 5.** Express via action-value:
$$Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s'} T(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \right]$$

**Step 6.** Recover the optimal policy:
$$\pi^{*}(a \mid s) = \mathbb{1}\{a = \arg\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s, a')\}$$

**Conclusion:** Because this recursion is re-evaluable at every time step with fresh observations of $T$ and $R$, MDPs are the **canonical mathematical engine of continuous planning**.

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Application 1: Autonomous Mars Rovers (NASA's MER / Curiosity) — 3.5 marks**

(i) **Why classical planning fails:** The Martian environment has unpredictable dust storms, wheel slippage, and communication blackouts. A pre-computed Earth-uploaded plan becomes invalid within hours.

(ii) **Critical loop component:** The **Monitor** — the rover continuously compares expected vs. actual traversability, energy usage, and science return, and triggers **on-board replanning** using the CASPER system.

(iii) **Limitation:** On-board CPU is severely constrained (RAD750 ~100 MIPS), so the replanning horizon is limited to ~200 steps, occasionally producing suboptimal long-term strategies.

**Application 2: Amazon Warehouse Robots (Kiva / Hercules systems) — 3.5 marks**

(i) **Why classical planning fails:** Thousands of mobile shelf units operate simultaneously; collisions, order cancellations, and inventory shifts make the world state extremely dynamic.

(ii) **Critical loop component:** The **Replanner** — a centralized traffic manager reroutes robots every 100 ms to avoid congestion, balancing throughput and collision avoidance.

(iii) **Limitation:** The system depends on a reliable centralized network; if Wi-Fi fails, robots default to *idle* states, halting fulfillment.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points on Continuous Planning Questions**
> 1. **Drawing a one-way pipeline instead of a closed loop** — you lose 2 marks. The *loop* is the entire definition of "continuous."
> 2. **Conflating Plan Repair with Replanning** — these are *not* synonyms. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for explicit distinction.
> 3. **Writing the Bellman equation without defining $\gamma$ and $T$** — the KTU answer-key scheme deducts 0.5 marks for missing symbol definitions.
> 4. **Omitting the discount factor** in long derivations — $\gamma$ is non-negotiable in any MDP-based planning question.
> 5. **Listing applications without explaining *why* classical planning fails** — applications must be justified, not just named.
> 6. **Confusing STRIPS with HTN** — STRIPS is a flat, one-shot planner; HTN supports hierarchical, continuous decomposition. Mixing them is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuous Planning**

- ✅ **Core definition:** Continuous Planning = perpetual Sense–Plan–Act–Monitor–Replan loop.
- ✅ **Five mandatory components:** Sensor, Planner, Executor, Monitor, Replanner.
- ✅ **Feedback loop is non-negotiable** — diagram must show a closed loop.
- ✅ **Plan Repair vs. Replanning:** repair is local + cheap; replanning is global + expensive.
- ✅ **Mathematical foundation:** MDP and the Bellman Optimality Equation with discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1]$.
- ✅ **Policy vs. Plan:** policies are *anytime* and naturally support continuous replanning.
- ✅ **HTN** allows hierarchical decomposition, limiting the scope of each replan.
- ✅ **Real-world users:** Mars rovers, autonomous vehicles, warehouse robots, spacecraft, UAVs.
- ✅ **Key trade-off:** global optimality (one-shot) ↔ robustness & adaptability (continuous).
- ✅ **Decision rule:** choose *Repair* if $C_r < C_p$ and $C_r < C_f$; otherwise *Replan*.
- ✅ **KEEP IN MIND:** The agent *never finishes planning* — it only finishes *acting* on the current plan.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiagent Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiagent Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Multiagent Planning (MAP)** is the process of generating, coordinating, and executing plans by a set of autonomous agents — each possessing its own beliefs, goals, capabilities, and knowledge — such that the resulting joint plan is consistent, conflict-free, and successfully achieves either individual or collective objectives in a shared environment.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MAP is a sub-field of **Distributed Planning** situated between classical AI planning and Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), where the central challenge is to integrate *local reasoning* (what should *I* do?) with *global coherence* (does *our* combined behavior achieve the goal without conflicts?).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> In MAP, two foundational paradigms are distinguished:
> 1. **Cooperative Multiagent Planning** – agents share a common goal (e.g., disaster-response robots).
> 2. **Competitive / Self-Interested Planning** – agents have private, possibly conflicting goals (e.g., automated trading bots).

A formal planning problem is defined as the tuple:

$$
\Pi = \langle \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{S}, s_0, G, \mathcal{A}ct, T, C \rangle
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{A}$ = set of agents $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$
- $\mathcal{S}$ = set of world states
- $s_0$ = initial shared state
- $G$ = (possibly distributed) goal condition
- $\mathcal{A}ct$ = union of all individual action sets $\bigcup_i Act_i$
- $T$ = transition function $T: \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A}ct \rightarrow \mathcal{S}$
- $C$ = cost or utility function

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **hospital emergency department with multiple specialist doctors**:
- A **cardiologist** needs to interpret ECG readings.
- A **radiologist** needs to scan and report on X-rays.
- A **nurse** needs to administer medication and monitor vitals.

Each doctor independently *plans* their actions — but the patient's recovery requires their plans to be **temporally aligned** (no surgery before anesthesia), **resource-compatible** (only one CT scanner exists), and **information-coupled** (radiologist needs cardiologist's preliminary findings). The hospital's MAP system is what coordinates their individual plans into one coherent treatment protocol.

> [!NOTE]
> The "multi" in Multiagent Planning refers to **multiple decision-makers**, not multiple goals. Even when agents share one common goal, planning still requires coordination because each agent has only a *partial* view of the world.

## 1.3 Key Metrics in Multiagent Planning

- **Makespan** – the total wall-clock time from plan start to goal achievement.
- **Plan Length** – number of total actions summed across all agents.
- **Coordination Overhead** – communication/computation spent resolving conflicts.
- **Plan Quality** – measured against the optimal centralized solution.

## 1.4 Why Multiagent Planning is Hard

| Difficulty | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Partial Observability** | Each agent has a local view, not full state knowledge. |
| **Decentralized Control** | No single agent has authority to dictate the joint plan. |
| **Communication Cost** | Sharing all information is infeasible; selective communication is required. |
| **Dynamic Environments** | Other agents' actions are non-deterministic from one agent's perspective. |
| **Coordination Constraints** | Mutual exclusion on resources, temporal ordering, etc. must be respected. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Centralized vs Decentralized Multiagent Planning

### 2.1.1 Centralized Planning
A single planner has full knowledge of **all** agents' states, goals, and actions. It produces a **joint plan** and then dispatches sub-plans to individual agents.

**Advantages:** Optimality, no conflicts.
**Disadvantages:** Single point of failure, not scalable, violates autonomy.

### 2.1.2 Decentralized Planning
Each agent plans *locally* using its own knowledge. Coordination happens through **communication** and **negotiation** at run-time.

**Advantages:** Scalable, fault-tolerant, respects agent autonomy.
**Disadvantages:** Sub-optimality, coordination overhead, potential conflicts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Most exam questions test the *plan merging* and *coordination* flavors of decentralized planning.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of MAP

### Pillar 1 — Local Plan Generation
Each agent $a_i$ independently constructs a plan $\pi_i$ to satisfy its local goal $g_i$ using a classical planner (e.g., STRIPS, Graphplan, POP).

### Pillar 2 — Plan Coordination
Local plans are examined for:
- **Resource Conflicts** (two agents need the same exclusive resource at time $t$).
- **Precondition Conflicts** (agent A's action undoes agent B's precondition).
- **Temporal Conflicts** (ordering constraints violated).
- **Goal Interactions** (negative interactions between sub-goals).

### Pillar 3 — Plan Merging / Joint Plan Synthesis
A merged plan $\Pi_{joint} = \langle \pi_1 \oplus \pi_2 \oplus \cdots \oplus \pi_n, \prec \rangle$ is formed, where $\prec$ encodes the partial ordering that resolves all detected conflicts.

## 2.3 Cooperation Levels

KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes cooperation into **four increasing levels** (modeled after Durfee's Lesser framework):

| Level | Description | Communication Need |
|---|---|---|
| **L0 – Coincidental Cooperation** | Agents happen not to interfere. | None |
| **L1 – Simple Cooperation** | Agents recognize and avoid conflict. | Low |
| **L2 – Coordinated Cooperation** | Agents exchange enough info to align plans. | Medium |
| **L3 – Collaborative / Anticipatory Cooperation** | Agents reason about others' future actions. | High |

## 2.4 Coordination Mechanisms

### 2.4.1 Organizational Structure
- **Master–Slave:** One coordinator agent allocates tasks.
- **Peer-to-Peer:** Equal agents negotiate directly.
- **Hierarchical:** Tree of coordinators (Coalition, Federation).

### 2.4.2 Social Laws / Conventions
Pre-agreed rules that constrain individual behavior, reducing the need for run-time coordination. Example: "always drive on the left."

### 2.4.3 Negotiation Protocols
- **Contract Net Protocol (CNP):** Task announcement → bidding → awarding.
- **Auction-based:** Vickrey, English, Dutch auctions for task allocation.
- **Argumentation-based:** Agents exchange justifications, not just bids.

### 2.4.4 Multiagent Planning Algorithms
- **MA-STRIPS** (Boutilier & Brafman): extends STRIPS to multiple agents with separate action sets.
- **MA-PDDL:** Multiagent extension of PDDL used in IPC (International Planning Competition).
- **FMAP** (Borrajo): plan merging by detecting negative interactions.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Notation Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The symbols below are **mandatory** for answering KTU derivations and algorithm-based questions.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, \ldots, a_n\}$ | Set of agents | Defines the team |
| $\pi_i$ | Local plan of agent $a_i$ | Output of local planner |
| $\Pi = \bigoplus_i \pi_i$ | Merged joint plan | Cooperative synthesis |
| $g_i$ | Local goal of $a_i$ | Local objective |
| $G = \bigcup_i g_i$ | Joint global goal | Cooperation target |
| $Act_i$ | Action set of $a_i$ | Capability |
| $\prec$ | Partial-order relation on actions | Temporal/causal structure |
| $T(s, a)$ | Result of executing action $a$ in state $s$ | World dynamics |
| $V(C)$ | Coalition value function | Coalitional game theory |
| $\phi_i(v)$ | Shapley value of agent $i$ | Fair payoff division |
| $\mathcal{C}_i$ | Set of coordination constraints for $a_i$ | Conflicts to avoid |
| $M$ | Makespan of joint plan | Performance metric |

## 2.6 Shapley Value for Coalition Formation

When agents must split a joint reward, the **Shapley value** assigns each agent a fair payoff proportional to its marginal contribution:

$$
\phi_i(v) = \sum_{S \subseteq \mathcal{A} \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert! \cdot (\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert!} \bigl[ v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S) \bigr]
$$

Where:
- $v: 2^{\mathcal{A}} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the characteristic function of the coalitional game.
- $v(S)$ is the value the coalition $S$ can achieve on its own.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Shapley value is used in **smart-grid coalition pricing**, **federated learning reward division**, and **robot fleet task allocation**.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Warehouse Robotics** (Amazon Kiva, Ocado): hundreds of robots must coordinate paths.
- **Air Traffic Control:** each aircraft is an agent, no two may occupy the same airspace.
- **Smart Power Grids:** distributed energy resources negotiate supply schedules.
- **Disaster Response:** UAVs, UGVs, and human rescuers cooperate under time pressure.
- **Multi-Robot Assembly Lines:** synchronized workcell scheduling.
- **Satellite Constellation Coordination:** distributed mission planning in orbit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Detecting Coordination Conflicts Between Two Plans

We derive the **conflict detection condition** for two plans $\pi_1$ and $\pi_2$ acting on a shared resource $r$.

### Step 1 — Define each agent's plan as a time-indexed action sequence

A local plan for agent $a_i$ is an ordered sequence of grounded actions:

$$
\pi_i = \langle a_{i,1}, a_{i,2}, \ldots, a_{i,k_i} \rangle
$$

Each $a_{i,j}$ is grounded with concrete parameters from the domain.

### Step 2 — Define the preconditions and effects

For an action $a$, we have:
- $\text{pre}(a)$ – the set of facts that must be true to execute $a$.
- $\text{eff}(a) = \text{eff}^+(a) \cup \text{eff}^-(a)$ – positive (add) and negative (delete) effects.

### Step 3 — Identify the time window of each action

Assume each action occupies the time interval $[t_{start}, t_{end}]$.

For sequential planning, we can use a discrete time index: action $a_{i,j}$ is at time $\tau_{i,j} = j$ (start of $j$-th step).

### Step 4 — State the resource-conflict condition

Two actions $a_{1,p}$ and $a_{2,q}$ from different agents conflict on resource $r$ **iff**:

$$
\bigl[ r \in \text{eff}^-(a_{2,q}) \cap \text{pre}(a_{1,p}) \bigr] \;\wedge\; \bigl[ \tau_{1,p} \geq \tau_{2,q} \bigr]
$$

The first term: action $a_{2,q}$ deletes a fact that $a_{1,p}$ needs.
The second term: the order is such that the deletion happens before the need arises.

### Step 5 — Generalize to mutual exclusion

If both agents need $r$ exclusively, the condition becomes:

$$
\exists\, r \in R: \bigl[ r \in \text{pre}(a_{1,p}) \cap \text{pre}(a_{2,q}) \bigr] \;\wedge\; \bigl[ \tau_{1,p} = \tau_{2,q} \bigr]
$$

The two preconditions are simultaneous ⇒ a **mutual exclusion (mutex)** conflict.

### Step 6 — Compute the coordination constraint

To resolve the conflict, we add an ordering constraint to the merged plan:

$$
\prec_{joint} \supseteq \prec_{\pi_1} \cup \prec_{\pi_2} \cup \{ a_{2,q} \prec a_{1,p} \}
$$

(Or the reverse, whichever preserves feasibility.)

### Step 7 — Iterative conflict resolution

Repeat Steps 4–6 until no new conflict is detected. Termination is guaranteed because the number of action pairs is finite:

$$
\mid \pi_1 \mid \times \mid \pi_2 \mid = k_1 \cdot k_2 < \infty
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result is a **Plan Merging Algorithm** (a la Fikes et al., 1981; extended by Borrajo). KTU frequently asks for this in **Part B 14-mark questions**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cooperative MAP vs Competitive MAP

| Dimension | Cooperative MAP | Competitive / Adversarial MAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal Structure** | Shared global $G$ | Private, conflicting $g_i$ |
| **Coordination Need** | High — must align plans | Lower — but must anticipate opponent |
| **Solution Concept** | Joint optimal plan $\Pi^*$ | Nash equilibrium, dominant strategy |
| **Information Sharing** | Full transparency encouraged | Privacy-preserving; information is power |
| **Algorithm Class** | Plan merging, MA-STRIPS | Game-theoretic planning, RMDPs |
| **Example** | Search-and-rescue UAVs | Automated trading, soccer robots |
| **Evaluation Metric** | Joint utility $\sum_i U_i(\Pi)$ | Individual utility $U_i$ |
| **Failure Mode** | Coordination deadlock | Defection / no equilibrium |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Plan Merging in a Logistics Domain

**Scenario:**
- Agent $A$ (truck): must deliver package P1 from Warehouse to Customer-1.
- Agent $B$ (truck): must deliver package P2 from Warehouse to Customer-2.
- Shared resource: the only **forklift** at the warehouse.

**Local plan of A:**

$$
\pi_A = \langle \text{loadForklift}, \text{loadP1}, \text{drive(C1)}, \text{unloadP1} \rangle
$$

**Local plan of B:**

$$
\pi_B = \langle \text{loadForklift}, \text{loadP2}, \text{drive(C2)}, \text{unloadP2} \rangle
$$

**Step 1: Identify conflict.**
Both agents want $\text{loadForklift}$ as step 1 — a **mutex conflict** on the resource *forklift*.

**Step 2: Resolution by ordering.**
Add the coordination constraint:

$$
a_{B,1} \prec a_{A,1}
$$

**Step 3: Merged plan.**

$$
\Pi_{joint} = \langle \text{loadForklift}_B, \text{loadP2}, \text{loadForklift}_A, \text{loadP1}, \text{drive}(C2), \text{unloadP2}, \text{drive}(C1), \text{unloadP1} \rangle
$$

Wait — this still conflicts because the forklift is needed by A only after B finishes. Actually, since each agent's *drive* action does not need the forklift, the constraint $\text{loadForklift}_B \prec \text{loadForklift}_A$ suffices. The merged plan is valid.

## 3.4 Derivation: Optimal Coalition Structure

Given a set of agents $\mathcal{A}$, the **coalition structure** $\mathcal{CS}$ is a partition of $\mathcal{A}$ into disjoint coalitions. The optimal coalition structure maximizes the social welfare:

$$
\mathcal{CS}^* = \arg\max_{\mathcal{CS}} \sum_{C \in \mathcal{CS}} v(C)
$$

The number of possible partitions is the **Bell number** $B_n$, which grows super-exponentially. The search is therefore NP-hard, and heuristic methods (e.g., greedy, DP-based bottom-up) are used.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked "why is coalition formation difficult?", cite the **Bell number growth** and the **NP-hardness** of the optimal partition problem.

## 3.5 Algorithm: Generic Plan-Merge Pseudocode (For Examiner Reference)

> [!WARNING]
> The pseudocode below is *mathematical/algorithmic notation*, NOT programming code — included because PCCAT601 is a CS-related course and KTU 2024 expects algorithm analysis for this topic.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Input: } \pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n, \text{ domain } D \\
&\textbf{Output: } \Pi_{joint} \text{ or FAIL} \\
&1.\;\; \Pi_{joint} \leftarrow \bigcup_i \pi_i \quad \text{(partial order union)} \\
&2.\;\; \text{conflictSet} \leftarrow \emptyset \\
&3.\;\; \text{repeat} \\
&4.\;\; \quad \text{conflictSet} \leftarrow \text{DetectConflicts}(\Pi_{joint}, D) \\
&5.\;\; \quad \textbf{if } \text{conflictSet} = \emptyset \textbf{ then break} \\
&6.\;\; \quad c \leftarrow \text{SelectConflict}(\text{conflictSet}) \\
&7.\;\; \quad \Pi_{joint} \leftarrow \text{ResolveConflict}(\Pi_{joint}, c) \\
&8.\;\; \quad \textbf{if } \Pi_{joint} \text{ becomes inconsistent} \textbf{ then return FAIL} \\
&9.\;\; \text{until false} \\
&10.\; \text{return } \Pi_{joint}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Multiagent Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GoalLayer["Global Goal G"]
    AgentA["Agent A1 with Local Goal g1"]
    AgentB["Agent A2 with Local Goal g2"]
    AgentC["Agent An with Local Goal gn"]
    PlannerA["Local Planner for A1 STRIPS Module"]
    PlannerB["Local Planner for A2 STRIPS Module"]
    PlannerC["Local Planner for An STRIPS Module"]
    PlanA["Local Plan pi1"]
    PlanB["Local Plan pi2"]
    PlanC["Local Plan pin"]
    Coord["Coordination Module Conflict Detection and Resolution"]
    Merged["Joint Plan Pi_joint Partial Order"]
    Exec["Execution Monitor Shared Environment"]
    SharedKB["Shared Knowledge Base Communication BlackBoard"]

    GoalLayer --> AgentA
    GoalLayer --> AgentB
    GoalLayer --> AgentC
    AgentA --> PlannerA
    AgentB --> PlannerB
    AgentC --> PlannerC
    PlannerA --> PlanA
    PlannerB --> PlanB
    PlannerC --> PlanC
    PlanA --> Coord
    PlanB --> Coord
    PlanC --> Coord
    SharedKB --> Coord
    Coord --> Merged
    Merged --> Exec
    Exec --> SharedKB
```

## 4.2 Plan Merging Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start(["Input Local Plans pi1 to pin"])
    Detect["Detect Coordination Conflicts Resource Precondition Temporal"]
    Decision{"Conflicts Found?"}
    Resolve["Resolve by Adding Ordering Constraints"]
    Update["Update Joint Partial Order Pi_joint"]
    Verify["Verify Consistency of Pi_joint"]
    Fail(["Return FAIL"])
    Done(["Return Joint Plan Pi_joint"])

    Start --> Detect
    Detect --> Decision
    Decision -- Yes --> Resolve
    Resolve --> Update
    Update --> Verify
    Verify -- Consistent --> Detect
    Verify -- Inconsistent --> Fail
    Decision -- No --> Done
```

    L0["L0 Coincidental No Awareness"]
    L1["L1 Simple Conflict Avoidance"]
    L2["L2 Coordinated Information Exchange"]

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3
```

    end

    subgraph Decentralized["Decentralized Planning Approach"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Multiagent Planning (MAP). Differentiate between cooperative and competitive multiagent planning with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Multiagent Planning is the process by which multiple autonomous agents, each with their own knowledge and goals, generate and coordinate plans to achieve individual or collective objectives in a shared environment.

**Cooperative MAP (1 mark):**
In cooperative MAP, all agents share a common global goal $G$ and aim to produce a joint plan $\Pi_{joint}$ that maximizes collective utility. Example: Multiple UAVs collaboratively mapping a disaster zone.

**Competitive MAP (1 mark):**
In competitive MAP, agents have private, possibly conflicting goals, and the solution is typically a **Nash equilibrium** of the resulting game. Example: Two companies bidding in a sealed-bid auction.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Model]`
**What are social laws in Multiagent Systems? How do they reduce coordination overhead?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Social laws (Shoham & Tennenholtz, 1992) are pre-agreed restrictions on the actions agents may perform, designed to constrain the joint behavior of the system so that individual actions do not lead to harmful interactions.

**Mechanism of overhead reduction (2 marks):**
- They **eliminate the need for run-time negotiation** by forbidding conflict-prone action combinations a priori.
- They reduce the *branching factor* of each agent's decision space, allowing local planning without global communication.
- Example: The "drive-on-the-left" rule removes half of all potential collision conflicts in a multi-car traffic system.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **plan merging approach** to cooperative multiagent planning. Describe how coordination conflicts (resource, precondition, and temporal) are detected and resolved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Contract Net Protocol (CNP)** as a negotiation mechanism for task allocation in multiagent planning. Illustrate with a procurement scenario. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer to Question A

#### Part (a) — Plan Merging Approach (7 marks)

**1. Overview (2 marks):**
Plan merging is a decentralized MAP technique where each agent first generates a local plan $\pi_i$ for its individual goal $g_i$ using a classical planner. These local plans are then *merged* into a global joint plan $\Pi_{joint}$ that respects all coordination constraints.

**2. Algorithm (3 marks):**
The standard plan-merging algorithm proceeds as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Each agent } a_i \text{ generates } \pi_i \text{ independently.} \\
&\text{Step 2: Form the partial order union } \Pi_{joint} = \bigcup_i \pi_i. \\
&\text{Step 3: Detect conflicts of three types:} \\
&\quad \bullet \text{Resource: } r \in \text{pre}(a_p) \cap \text{pre}(a_q) \text{ for actions scheduled at time } \tau. \\
&\quad \bullet \text{Precondition: } f \in \text{eff}^-(a_q) \cap \text{pre}(a_p) \text{ where } \tau_q < \tau_p. \\
&\quad \bullet \text{Temporal: ordering constraints in local plans cannot be jointly satisfied.} \\
&\text{Step 4: Resolve each conflict by adding ordering constraints } a_q \prec a_p. \\
&\text{Step 5: Verify global consistency; repeat until no conflict remains.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Worked Illustration (1 mark):**
Consider two robots A and B that must each move through a narrow corridor — a mutual exclusion on the corridor segment. Local plans contain conflicting $\text{enterCorridor}$ actions. The plan merger adds $\text{enterCorridor}_A \prec \text{enterCorridor}_B$ to serialize access.

**4. Limitations (1 mark):**
Plan merging is **greedy** and may produce a sub-optimal joint plan. It also cannot recover from a *deeply unsolvable* local plan combination — the algorithm returns FAIL even if a totally different plan structure would succeed.

---

#### Part (b) — Contract Net Protocol (7 marks)

**1. Protocol Definition (2 marks):**
The **Contract Net Protocol** (Smith, 1980) is a high-level coordination mechanism for **task allocation** in distributed problem-solving. It uses an *announce–bid–award* cycle.

**2. Phases (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i) Task Announcement: Manager broadcasts a task with specifications.} \\
&\text{(ii) Bidding: Eligible contractors evaluate the task and submit bids.} \\
&\text{(iii) Awarding: Manager evaluates bids and awards the contract.} \\
&\text{(iv) Execution and Reporting: Contractor executes and reports back.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Procurement Scenario (2 marks):**
A construction firm (Manager) needs 100 tons of cement. It announces the requirement to potential suppliers. Three suppliers (Contractors) submit bids: Supplier-1 offers ₹4500/ton, Supplier-2 offers ₹4300/ton, Supplier-3 offers ₹4600/ton with faster delivery. The Manager evaluates bids on (price, delivery time, quality) and awards the contract to Supplier-2 (lowest price, acceptable delivery).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **three coordination mechanisms**: organizational structure, social laws, and negotiation. Compare their communication overhead and applicability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **coalition formation** in multiagent systems? Derive the **Shapley value** formula and explain its significance in fair payoff division with an example of three agents forming a coalition. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer to Question B

#### Part (a) — Coordination Mechanisms (7 marks)

**1. Organizational Structure (2 marks):**
Defines *who can talk to whom* and *who decides what*. Three sub-types: **Master–Slave** (one coordinator), **Peer-to-Peer** (equal agents), **Hierarchical** (tree of authority). High control, moderate communication.

**2. Social Laws (2 marks):**
Pre-agreed constraints on allowed actions. They convert dynamic coordination into static compliance. **Lowest communication overhead** — agents plan locally knowing everyone obeys the laws. Example: traffic rules.

**3. Negotiation (2 marks):**
Run-time exchange of proposals, bids, and counter-offers to reach agreements. **Highest communication overhead** but most flexible. Examples: Contract Net, Vickrey auctions, argumentation.

**4. Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Mechanism | Comm. Overhead | Flexibility | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Organizational | Medium | Low–Medium | Stable teams, clear hierarchy |
| Social Laws | Very Low | Low | Open, large-scale systems |
| Negotiation | High | Very High | One-off, ad-hoc cooperation |

---

#### Part (b) — Coalition Formation and Shapley Value (7 marks)

**1. Coalition Formation (2 marks):**
Agents form **coalitions** $C \subseteq \mathcal{A}$ to combine capabilities and achieve goals unattainable individually. A **coalition structure** is a partition of $\mathcal{A}$ into disjoint coalitions:

$$
\mathcal{CS} = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}, \quad \text{where } \bigcup_j C_j = \mathcal{A} \text{ and } C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset
$$

The challenge is to find the **optimal partition** that maximizes social welfare $\sum_j v(C_j)$.

**2. Shapley Value Definition (2 marks):**
The Shapley value $\phi_i(v)$ assigns each agent $i$ a payoff equal to its **average marginal contribution** across all permutations of the grand coalition.

**3. Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\phi_i(v) = \sum_{S \subseteq \mathcal{A} \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert! \, (\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert!} \bigl[ v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S) \bigr]
$$

For three agents $\{1, 2, 3\}$ with $v(\{1\})=10$, $v(\{2\})=20$, $v(\{3\})=30$, $v(\{1,2\})=40$, $v(\{1,3\})=50$, $v(\{2,3\})=60$, $v(\{1,2,3\})=100$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_1(v) &= \frac{0! \cdot 2!}{3!}\bigl[v(1)-v(\emptyset)\bigr] + \frac{1! \cdot 1!}{3!}\bigl[v(1,2)-v(2)\bigr] + \frac{1! \cdot 1!}{3!}\bigl[v(1,3)-v(3)\bigr] + \frac{2! \cdot 0!}{3!}\bigl[v(1,2,3)-v(2,3)\bigr] \\
&= \tfrac{1}{3}(10) + \tfrac{1}{6}(20) + \tfrac{1}{6}(20) + \tfrac{1}{3}(40) \\
&= 3.33 + 3.33 + 3.33 + 13.33 = 23.33
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry of similar calculation, $\phi_2 = 30$, $\phi_3 = 46.67$. Total = 100, which matches the grand coalition value.

**4. Significance (1 mark):**
Shapley value provides a **unique, fair, and axiomatically justified** payoff division (efficiency, symmetry, dummy-player, additivity) — used in cooperative game theory, federated learning, and smart-grid energy markets.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing "Multiagent" with "Multi-objective"** — MAP involves *multiple decision-makers*, not multiple objectives for a single agent. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Omitting conflict types** — When asked about plan merging, you MUST mention resource, precondition, AND temporal conflicts separately. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the joint-plan symbol $\Pi_{joint}$** — Always denote the merged plan with a *joint* symbol; using $\pi$ alone loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the cooperative levels (L0–L3)** — KTU frequently asks for the cooperation spectrum. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Shapley value mistakes** — Forgetting the factorial weight $\frac{|S|! (n-|S|-1)!}{n!}$ loses 2 marks in derivation.
> 6. **Centralized vs Decentralized confusion** — Remember: centralized = one planner with full info; decentralized = local planning + coordination.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Multiagent Planning (MAP)** = local planning by each agent + coordination to produce a conflict-free joint plan.
- ✅ A planning problem is formally $\Pi = \langle \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{S}, s_0, G, Act, T, C \rangle$.
- ✅ **Cooperative MAP** uses shared $G$; **Competitive MAP** uses private $g_i$ and Nash equilibrium.
- ✅ **Four levels of cooperation** (Durfee): Coincidental → Simple → Coordinated → Collaborative.
- ✅ **Plan Merging** is the classic decentralized MAP technique: detect conflicts → add ordering constraints → verify.
- ✅ **Three conflict types** in plan merging: **Resource, Precondition, Temporal**.
- ✅ **Coordination mechanisms**: Organizational Structure, Social Laws, Negotiation, Multiagent Planning Algorithms (MA-STRIPS, MA-PDDL, FMAP).
- ✅ **Contract Net Protocol** = Announce → Bid → Award → Execute; widely used in distributed task allocation.
- ✅ **Coalition formation** partitions $\mathcal{A}$; the optimal partition maximizes $\sum_j v(C_j)$ and is NP-hard (Bell number growth).
- ✅ **Shapley value** $\phi_i(v)$ is the canonical fair-payoff rule in coalitional games.
- ✅ **Performance metrics**: Makespan, Plan Length, Coordination Overhead, Plan Quality.
- ✅ **Centralized planning** = optimal but not scalable; **Decentralized** = scalable but sub-optimal.
- ✅ **MA-PDDL** is the standardized multiagent planning language used in the International Planning Competition.
- ✅ KTU often asks for **plan-merging algorithms** in 14-mark questions — memorize the detect–resolve–verify loop.
- ✅ Applications: warehouse robotics, ATC, smart grids, disaster response, satellite coordination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acting Under Uncertainty
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Acting Under Uncertainty

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Acting Under Uncertainty** refers to the design and operation of rational agents that must make decisions and take actions when the true state of the environment is not completely known and outcomes cannot be deterministically predicted. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCCAT601, this topic encompasses the probabilistic framework that enables an intelligent agent to reason about beliefs, update them in light of new evidence, and select actions that maximize expected utility under conditions of partial observability, nondeterminism, and stochastic environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The module mandates coverage of (a) uncertainty and probability axioms, (b) Bayesian reasoning and inference, (c) Bayesian networks (representation, construction, exact/approximate inference), (d) relational and temporal probability models, (e) acting under uncertainty (decision networks, utility theory, Maximum Expected Utility), and (f) introduction to Markov Decision Processes (MDPs).

Mathematically, an agent acting under uncertainty must compute:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \sum_{s \in S} P(\text{Result}(a) = s \mid e) \cdot U(s)$$

where $a^*$ is the optimal action, $P(\text{Result}(a)=s \mid e)$ is the posterior probability of state $s$ given evidence $e$, and $U(s)$ is the utility of that state.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are an autonomous vehicle approaching an intersection at night with a malfunctioning traffic signal. You cannot *see* with certainty whether a pedestrian is about to step onto the road, and your sensors give only probabilistic readings. **Acting under uncertainty** means you don't simply refuse to move (which would be paralyzed) and you don't gamble recklessly. Instead, you combine your prior knowledge ("pedestrians *usually* appear after dark"), your sensor evidence ("thermal reading suggests 65% chance of a body"), and your utility preferences ("hitting a pedestrian is catastrophic, slowing down mildly inconvenient") to choose the action — slow down — that yields the highest *expected* desirability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** An agent acting under uncertainty weighs *what it believes* (probability distribution over world states), *what it observes* (sensor evidence), and *what it values* (utility) to pick the best possible action when perfect knowledge is unavailable. It is decision-making under incomplete information, guided by the mathematics of probability and the philosophy of rational choice.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

Although this is a probabilistic/AI topic, the following standard mathematical conventions are universally invoked and must be remembered:

- **Probability range:** $0 \le P(\omega) \le 1$ for every atomic outcome $\omega$
- **Total probability axiom:** $\sum_{\omega \in \Omega} P(\omega) = 1$
- **Logarithmic utility convention:** Preferences are often expressed via the **nats** base $e$ rather than bits base $2$ for continuous priors.
- **Information content unit:** A probability of $0.5$ yields an information content of **1 bit**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Rational Agent Under Uncertainty — Operational Breakdown

A KTU-2024-aligned rational agent in an uncertain world executes the following logical loop:

1. **Perceive** the current percept $e_t$ from the environment through sensors.
2. **Update Belief State:** Maintain a probability distribution $P(S_t \mid e_{1:t})$ over all possible world states given the entire history of percepts.
3. **Compute Posterior:** Use Bayes' theorem to update beliefs when new evidence arrives.
4. **Evaluate Actions:** For each candidate action $a$, compute the **expected utility (EU)** by averaging the utility of each outcome, weighted by the probability of that outcome.
5. **Execute the MEU Action:** Choose $a^* = \arg\max_a EU(a)$ — this is the **Maximum Expected Utility (MEU)** principle.
6. **Transition:** Move to the new state $S_{t+1}$ determined by the action's stochastic outcome and repeat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MEU?** It is the unique decision criterion that satisfies the axioms of utility theory (ordering, transitivity, continuity, substitutability, monotonicity, decomposability). Any rational agent whose preferences obey these axioms *must* maximize expected utility (von Neumann–Morgenstern, 1944).

### 2.2 The Five Sources of Uncertainty in Agent Systems

A KTU student must be able to identify the **five canonical sources of uncertainty** (per AIMA/Russell & Norvig):

| # | Source | Description | Example |
|---|--------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Partial Observability** | Sensors cannot access the complete world state | Poker game — opponent's cards hidden |
| 2 | **Nondeterminism** | Actions may have multiple possible outcomes | Robot arm may drop a part |
| 3 | **Adversarial Opponents** | Other agents act strategically | Competitive games like Chess |
| 4 | **Stochastic Environments** | World dynamics are inherently random | Weather forecasting |
| 5 | **Model Ignorance** | The agent lacks a complete causal model of the world | Medical diagnosis with unknown diseases |

### 2.3 Foundations: Probability Axioms (Kolmogorov, 1933)

Probability theory rests on three axioms that form the bedrock of KTU exam questions:

| # | Axiom | Mathematical Form | Meaning |
|---|-------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Non-negativity** | $0 \le P(\omega) \le 1$ for any event $\omega$ | Probabilities cannot be negative or exceed 1 |
| 2 | **Normalization** | $P(\Omega) = 1$ | The probability of the entire sample space is 1 |
| 3 | **Additivity (Countable)** | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ for disjoint $A, B$ | Probabilities of mutually exclusive events add |

From these axioms, the following **derived identities** follow (frequently tested):

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \quad \text{(inclusion–exclusion)}$$

$$P(\neg A) = 1 - P(A) \quad \text{(complement rule)}$$

$$P(A) = P(A \cap B) + P(A \cap \neg B) \quad \text{(marginalization)}$$

### 2.4 Conditional Probability and Bayes' Theorem

Conditional probability expresses how an agent updates its belief after observing evidence:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{(provided } P(B) > 0\text{)}$$

The **Product Rule** states:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Bayes' Theorem** — the cornerstone of rational belief updating:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

In agent decision contexts, the canonical form is:

$$P(\text{Hypothesis} \mid \text{Evidence}) = \frac{P(\text{Evidence} \mid \text{Hypothesis}) \cdot P(\text{Hypothesis})}{P(\text{Evidence})}$$

| Term | Name | Intuitive Meaning |
|------|------|-------------------|
| $P(\text{Hypothesis})$ | **Prior** | Belief *before* seeing evidence |
| $P(\text{Evidence} \mid \text{Hypothesis})$ | **Likelihood** | Probability of observing evidence if hypothesis is true |
| $P(\text{Hypothesis} \mid \text{Evidence})$ | **Posterior** | Updated belief *after* seeing evidence |
| $P(\text{Evidence})$ | **Marginal likelihood** | Normalizing constant ensuring posterior sums to 1 |

### 2.5 Bayesian Networks — Graphical Models of Uncertainty

A **Bayesian Network (BN)** is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) where:

- **Nodes** represent random variables.
- **Edges** represent direct probabilistic dependencies (causal influences).
- Each node has a **Conditional Probability Table (CPT)** $P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$.

The **Joint Probability Distribution (JPD)** factorizes as:

$$P(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

**Example — Classic "Burglary/Earthquake/Alarm" Network:**

$$P(J, M, A, B, E) = P(B) \cdot P(E) \cdot P(A \mid B, E) \cdot P(J \mid A) \cdot P(M \mid A)$$

### 2.6 Conditional Independence and D-Separation

Two variables $X$ and $Y$ are **conditionally independent** given $Z$ if:

$$P(X \mid Y, Z) = P(X \mid Z)$$

Equivalently:

$$P(X, Y \mid Z) = P(X \mid Z) \cdot P(Y \mid Z)$$

**D-Separation Test:** A path is *blocked* by observed evidence $Z$ if it contains:
1. A chain $X \to W \to Y$ or fork $X \leftarrow W \to Y$ with $W \in Z$.
2. A collider $X \to W \leftarrow Y$ with $W \notin Z$ (and no descendant of $W$ in $Z$).

If all paths between $X$ and $Y$ are blocked by $Z$, then $X$ and $Y$ are d-separated by $Z$, hence conditionally independent.

### 2.7 Inference in Bayesian Networks

**Exact Inference Algorithms:**

| Algorithm | Complexity | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **Enumeration** | Exponential in network size | Direct enumeration of joint entries |
| **Variable Elimination** | Exponential in treewidth, linear in treewidth if bounded | Dynamic programming on factors |
| **Clique Tree (Junction Tree)** | Exponential in clique size | Most efficient exact method for general BNs |

**Approximate Inference Algorithms (used when exact inference is intractable):**

| Algorithm | Type | Notes |
|-----------|------|-------|
| **Direct Sampling** | Monte Carlo | Generates samples from prior; error $\propto 1/\sqrt{N}$ |
| **Rejection Sampling** | Monte Carlo | Rejects samples inconsistent with evidence |
| **Likelihood Weighting** | Importance sampling | Fixes evidence variables; avoids rejection |
| **Gibbs Sampling** | MCMC | Cycles through variables; samples from posterior |

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula / Rule | LaTeX Form | When to Use |
|---|----------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Belief updating with new evidence |
| 2 | BN Factorization | $P(\mathbf{X}) = \prod_i P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$ | Computing joint probability from a BN |
| 3 | Marginalization | $P(X) = \sum_y P(X, y)$ | Eliminating variables from a distribution |
| 4 | Conditioning | $P(X) = \sum_y P(X \mid y)\,P(y)$ | Computing prior from conditional + marginal |
| 5 | Conditional Independence | $P(X,Y \mid Z) = P(X \mid Z)\,P(Y \mid Z)$ | Simplifying large joint distributions |
| 6 | Chain Rule | $P(X_1,\dots,X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1,\dots,X_{i-1})$ | Any decomposition of joint probability |
| 7 | MEU Principle | $a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_s P(s \mid a,e)\,U(s)$ | Selecting optimal action under uncertainty |
| 8 | Expected Utility | $EU(a \mid e) = \sum_s P(\text{Result}(a)=s \mid e)\,U(s)$ | Computing value of an action |
| 9 | Information Gain | $IG(X) = H(P) - \sum_v P(v)\,H(P \mid X=v)$ | Value of perfect information |
| 10 | Shannon Entropy | $H(P) = -\sum_x P(x)\,\log_2 P(x)$ | Measuring uncertainty of a distribution |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework of acting under uncertainty is the foundation of:
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems** (QMR-DT, INTERNIST, modern clinical decision support)
- **Autonomous Vehicles** (probabilistic localization with Kalman/Particle filters)
- **Spam Filtering** (Naive Bayes classifiers in production email systems)
- **Speech Recognition** (HMMs and Bayesian language models)
- **Robotics SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping under sensor noise)
- **Financial Risk Modeling** (Bayesian risk assessment, fraud detection)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Joint Distribution to Conditional Independence

**Problem Setup.** We are given a joint distribution $P(A, B, C)$ defined over three boolean random variables. A student must derive $P(A \mid C)$ and verify whether $A$ and $B$ are conditionally independent given $C$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the joint distribution expression.**

By the chain rule of probability, the joint distribution over $\{A, B, C\}$ can be factored in multiple ways. The most common factorization used in BNs is:

$$P(A, B, C) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A, B)$$

This expresses the causal ordering: $A$ influences $B$ and $C$, and $B$ influences $C$.

**Step 2 — Compute the marginal $P(C)$ by summing out $A$ and $B$.**

$$P(C) = \sum_{a \in \{0,1\}} \sum_{b \in \{0,1\}} P(a, b, C)$$

Substituting the factorization:

$$P(C) = \sum_{a} \sum_{b} P(a) \cdot P(b \mid a) \cdot P(C \mid a, b)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the joint $P(A, C)$ by marginalizing out $B$.**

$$P(A, C) = \sum_{b \in \{0,1\}} P(A, b, C) = \sum_{b} P(A) \cdot P(b \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A, b)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the conditional $P(A \mid C)$ using the definition.**

By definition of conditional probability:

$$P(A \mid C) = \frac{P(A, C)}{P(C)}$$

Substituting the expressions derived in Steps 2 and 3:

$$P(A \mid C) = \frac{\sum_{b} P(A)\,P(b \mid A)\,P(C \mid A, b)}{\sum_{a} \sum_{b} P(a)\,P(b \mid a)\,P(C \mid a, b)}$$

Since $P(A)$ does not depend on $b$, it can be factored out of the numerator:

$$P(A \mid C) = \frac{P(A) \cdot \sum_{b} P(b \mid A)\,P(C \mid A, b)}{\sum_{a} \sum_{b} P(a)\,P(b \mid a)\,P(C \mid a, b)}$$

**Step 5 — Test for conditional independence of $A$ and $B$ given $C$.**

For $A \perp\!\!\!\perp B \mid C$, we require:

$$P(A, B \mid C) = P(A \mid C) \cdot P(B \mid C)$$

First compute $P(A, B \mid C)$:

$$P(A, B \mid C) = \frac{P(A, B, C)}{P(C)} = \frac{P(A)\,P(B \mid A)\,P(C \mid A, B)}{\sum_{a} \sum_{b} P(a)\,P(b \mid a)\,P(C \mid a, b)}$$

Now compute $P(B \mid C)$ via marginalization of $P(A, B \mid C)$ over $A$:

$$P(B \mid C) = \sum_{a} P(a, B \mid C) = \frac{\sum_{a} P(a)\,P(B \mid a)\,P(C \mid a, B)}{\sum_{a'} \sum_{b'} P(a')\,P(b' \mid a')\,P(C \mid a', b')}$$

Then check whether the equality holds. In general, for this factorization, the equality **does not** hold unless the CPT $P(C \mid A, B)$ factorizes further — specifically, unless $P(C \mid A, B) = P(C \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid B)$ or similar independence conditions apply.

This is exactly why **Bayesian Networks require structural analysis (d-separation)** to determine conditional independence rather than algebraic manipulation alone.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Variable Elimination on a Small BN

**Network Structure:**

```
   A
   |
   v
   B --> C
```

with conditional probability tables:
- $P(A) = \langle 0.7, 0.3 \rangle$ for $a^1, a^2$
- $P(B \mid A)$: $P(b^1 \mid a^1)=0.9$, $P(b^1 \mid a^2)=0.5$
- $P(C \mid B)$: $P(c^1 \mid b^1)=0.8$, $P(c^1 \mid b^2)=0.2$

**Query:** Compute $P(A \mid c^1)$ — the posterior probability of $A$ given that $C = c^1$.

**Step 1 — State the Bayes' theorem form.**

$$P(A \mid c^1) = \frac{P(c^1 \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(c^1)} = \alpha \cdot P(c^1 \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

where $\alpha = 1/P(c^1)$ is the normalization constant.

**Step 2 — Compute $P(c^1 \mid A)$ by marginalizing out $B$.**

$$P(c^1 \mid A) = \sum_{b \in \{b^1, b^2\}} P(c^1 \mid b) \cdot P(b \mid A)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate for $A = a^1$:**

$$P(c^1 \mid a^1) = P(c^1 \mid b^1) \cdot P(b^1 \mid a^1) + P(c^1 \mid b^2) \cdot P(b^2 \mid a^1)$$

$$= (0.8 \cdot 0.9) + (0.2 \cdot 0.1)$$

$$= 0.72 + 0.02 = 0.74$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for $A = a^2$:**

$$P(c^1 \mid a^2) = (0.8 \cdot 0.5) + (0.2 \cdot 0.5)$$

$$= 0.40 + 0.10 = 0.50$$

**Step 5 — Compute the unnormalized posteriors.**

For $A = a^1$: $P(c^1 \mid a^1) \cdot P(a^1) = 0.74 \times 0.7 = 0.518$

For $A = a^2$: $P(c^1 \mid a^2) \cdot P(a^2) = 0.50 \times 0.3 = 0.150$

**Step 6 — Normalize.**

Sum of unnormalized values: $0.518 + 0.150 = 0.668$

$$P(a^1 \mid c^1) = \frac{0.518}{0.668} \approx 0.775$$

$$P(a^2 \mid c^1) = \frac{0.150}{0.668} \approx 0.225$$

**Verification:** $0.775 + 0.225 = 1.000$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Observing $C = c^1$ increases our belief in $A = a^1$ from the prior $0.7$ to the posterior $0.775$. This is the agent *acting under uncertainty* by rationally updating its belief.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Under Uncertainty Methods

| Criterion | Formula | Use Case | Risk Attitude |
|-----------|---------|----------|---------------|
| **Maximum Expected Utility (MEU)** | $a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_s P(s \mid a) U(s)$ | Rational choice with known probabilities | Risk-neutral (utility = value) |
| **Maximin** | $a^* = \arg\max_a \min_s U(s, a)$ | Adversarial / worst-case scenarios | Pessimistic (risk-averse) |
| **Maximax** | $a^* = \arg\max_a \max_s U(s, a)$ | Optimistic best-case scenarios | Risk-seeking |
| **Minimax Regret** | $a^* = \arg\min_a \max_s \left[ U^*(s) - U(s, a) \right]$ | Balanced comparison | Regret-averse |
| **Hurwicz (weighted optimism)** | $a^* = \arg\max_a [\alpha \max_s U + (1-\alpha) \min_s U]$ | Configurable optimism | Hybrid |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Proof — Bayes' Theorem from the Product Rule

**Given:** Definitions of conditional probability:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

**Step 1 — Express the joint probability $P(A \cap B)$ in two ways using the definitions above.**

From the first definition:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

From the second definition:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions.**

$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $P(B)$ (assuming $P(B) > 0$).**

$$\frac{P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)}{P(B)} = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

This is Bayes' theorem, **derived rigorously from first principles** of conditional probability.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: The Rational Agent Loop Under Uncertainty

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PERCEPT MODULE"]:::io --> nodeB["BELIEF UPDATE MODULE"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["INFERENCE ENGINE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["UTILITY EVALUATION"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["ACTION SELECTOR MEU"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ACTUATION MODULE"]:::io
    nodeF --> nodeG["STOCHASTIC ENVIRONMENT"]:::env
    nodeG --> nodeA
    nodeC -.uses.-> nodeH["BAYESIAN NETWORK"]:::kb
    nodeD -.uses.-> nodeI["UTILITY TABLE"]:::kb
    classDef io fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef env fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef kb fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Diagram: Bayesian Network — Burglary Alarm Canonical Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeB["BURGLARY B"]:::root --> nodeA["ALARM A"]
    nodeE["EARTHQUAKE E"]:::root --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeJ["JOHN CALLS J"]
    nodeA --> nodeM["MARY CALLS M"]
    classDef root fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**CPT Summary (used in KTU problem sets):**

| Node | Parents | CPT |
|------|---------|-----|
| B | none | P(B)=0.001 |
| E | none | P(E)=0.002 |
| A | B, E | P(A=t mid B=t,E=t)=0.95; P(A=t mid B=t,E=f)=0.94; P(A=t mid B=f,E=t)=0.29; P(A=t mid B=f,E=f)=0.001 |
| J | A | P(J=t mid A=t)=0.90; P(J=t mid A=f)=0.05 |
| M | A | P(M=t mid A=t)=0.70; P(M=t mid A=f)=0.01 |

### 4.3 Diagram: Decision Network (Influence Diagram) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeC["CHANCE NODE RAIN"]:::chance --> nodeU["UTILITY NODE TRAVEL_TIME"]
    nodeD["DECISION NODE UMBRELLA"]:::decision --> nodeU
    nodeF["FORECAST NODE"]:::chance --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeF
    classDef chance fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Legend:**
- **Chance Node** (oval/green): Random variable with probability distribution
- **Decision Node** (rectangle/purple): Action chosen by agent
- **Utility Node** (diamond): Value function mapping outcomes to utilities

### 4.4 Diagram: Information Flow in Bayesian Inference (Inference Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["PRIOR P H"] --> nodeE["EVIDENCE E"]
    nodeE --> nodeL["LIKELIHOOD P E GIVEN H"]
    nodeL --> nodeB["BAYES RULE"]
    nodeP --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeM["MARGINAL P E"]
    nodeM --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeR["POSTERIOR P H GIVEN E"]
    classDef io fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Diagram: D-Separation Patterns — Chain, Fork, and Collider

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHAIN["CHAIN  X to W to Y"]
        X1["X"] --> W1["W"] --> Y1["Y"]
    end
    subgraph FORK["FORK  X from W to Y"]
        X2["X"] <-.-> W2["W"]
        W2 <-.-> Y2["Y"]
    end
    subgraph COLL["COLLIDER  X to W from Y"]
        X3["X"] --> W3["W"]
        Y3["Y"] --> W3
    end
```

**D-Separation Decision Table:**

| Structure | Path Active When W is NOT in Z | Path Blocked When W is in Z |
|-----------|-------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Chain $X \to W \to Y$ | YES | YES (blocked by conditioning) |
| Fork $X \leftarrow W \to Y$ | YES | YES (blocked by conditioning) |
| Collider $X \to W \leftarrow Y$ | NO (blocked unless descendant observed) | YES (active when conditioned) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "rational agent acting under uncertainty" and state the Maximum Expected Utility (MEU) principle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A rational agent acting under uncertainty is one that chooses actions to maximize its expected performance measure based on its percept history and built-in knowledge, when the outcomes of actions are not deterministically known. The Maximum Expected Utility (MEU) principle states that a rational agent should choose the action $a$ that maximizes the expected utility, given the available evidence:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[U(\text{Result}(a)) \mid e] = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s} P(\text{Result}(a)=s \mid e)\,U(s)$$

The MEU principle is justified by the axioms of utility theory (ordering, transitivity, continuity, substitutability, monotonicity, decomposability).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List and briefly explain the five sources of uncertainty faced by intelligent agents.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Partial Observability** — Sensors cannot capture the full world state (e.g., hidden cards in poker).
2. **Nondeterminism** — Same action may yield different outcomes (e.g., robot arm grasping failure).
3. **Adversarial Opponents** — Other agents act strategically against the agent (e.g., chess).
4. **Stochastic Environments** — World dynamics are inherently random (e.g., weather).
5. **Model Ignorance / Laziness** — The agent lacks complete causal knowledge of the world.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the axioms of probability and derive Bayes' theorem from the definition of conditional probability. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the "Burglary–Earthquake–Alarm" Bayesian network. Given:
- $P(B) = 0.001$, $P(E) = 0.002$
- $P(A \mid B,E) = 0.95$, $P(A \mid B,\neg E) = 0.94$, $P(A \mid \neg B, E) = 0.29$, $P(A \mid \neg B,\neg E) = 0.001$
- $P(J \mid A) = 0.90$, $P(J \mid \neg A) = 0.05$

Compute $P(B \mid J=t, M=t)$ where both John and Mary call. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *[Refer to Section 3.4 for the rigorous derivation.]* The three Kolmogorov axioms are: non-negativity ($0 \le P(\omega) \le 1$), normalization ($P(\Omega)=1$), and countable additivity ($P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ for disjoint $A,B$). From the two equivalent forms of the product rule $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) P(B) = P(B \mid A) P(A)$, division by $P(B)$ yields Bayes' theorem.

**(b)** Use variable elimination:

$$P(B \mid j, m) = \alpha \cdot P(B) \cdot P(j \mid B) \cdot P(m \mid B)$$

where

$$P(j \mid B) = \sum_{a, e} P(j \mid a) \cdot P(a \mid B, e) \cdot P(e)$$

For $B = t$:

$$P(j \mid B=t) = \sum_e P(e) \sum_a P(j \mid a) P(a \mid B=t, e)$$

$$= P(e) [P(j \mid a) P(a \mid B=t, e) + P(j \mid \neg a) P(\neg a \mid B=t, e)] + P(\neg e)[\dots]$$

$$= 0.002 [0.90 \cdot 0.95 + 0.05 \cdot 0.05] + 0.998 [0.90 \cdot 0.94 + 0.05 \cdot 0.06]$$

$$= 0.002 [0.855 + 0.0025] + 0.998 [0.846 + 0.003]$$

$$= 0.002 \times 0.8575 + 0.998 \times 0.849$$

$$= 0.001715 + 0.847202 = 0.848917$$

Similarly for $B = f$:

$$P(j \mid B=f) = 0.002 [0.90 \cdot 0.29 + 0.05 \cdot 0.71] + 0.998 [0.90 \cdot 0.001 + 0.05 \cdot 0.999]$$

$$= 0.002 [0.261 + 0.0355] + 0.998 [0.0009 + 0.04995]$$

$$= 0.000593 + 0.050710 = 0.051303$$

Assuming symmetry $P(m \mid B) \approx P(j \mid B)$, and combining both pieces of evidence:

$$P(B=t \mid j, m) \approx \frac{0.001 \times 0.849 \times 0.849}{0.001 \times 0.849^2 + 0.999 \times 0.0513^2}$$

$$\approx \frac{0.000721}{0.000721 + 0.002626} = \frac{0.000721}{0.003347} \approx 0.2154$$

So $P(B=t \mid j=t, m=t) \approx 0.215$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes on Question A(b):**
> 1. **Forgetting to marginalize over hidden variables** (Alarm $A$ and Earthquake $E$) — failing to sum out intermediate nodes drops 3 marks.
> 2. **Computational arithmetic errors** in CPT multiplications — show every multiplication step.
> 3. **Skipping the normalization step** — final answer must be a valid probability summing with its complement to 1.
> 4. **Confusing prior and posterior** — explicitly state which term is prior ($P(B)$) and which is updated ($P(B \mid j,m)$).

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** What is a Bayesian Network? Explain its representation using a directed acyclic graph (DAG) and conditional probability tables (CPTs). Discuss the conditional independence semantics as captured by d-separation with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Construct a Bayesian network for the following medical diagnosis scenario. A patient may have **Fever (F)** with prior $P(F)=0.05$. Fever may be caused by **Flu (Fl)** or **Cold (C)** which are independent with $P(Fl)=0.03$ and $P(C)=0.10$. The patient is also tested for **Body Pain (BP)** which depends on Flu ($P(BP \mid Fl)=0.9$, $P(BP \mid \neg Fl)=0.2$) and on Cold ($P(BP \mid C)=0.7$, $P(BP \mid \neg C)=0.2$). Compute $P(F \mid BP=t)$ using Bayes' theorem. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A **Bayesian Network** is a compact graphical representation of a joint probability distribution over a set of random variables, encoded as a DAG where nodes are random variables and edges represent direct probabilistic dependencies. Each node $X_i$ is associated with a CPT specifying $P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$. The full JPD factorizes as:

$$P(X_1, \dots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$$

**Conditional Independence via d-separation:** Two sets of nodes $X$ and $Y$ are conditionally independent given a third set $Z$ if and only if every path between any node in $X$ and any node in $Y$ is *blocked* by $Z$. A path is blocked if it contains (i) a chain or fork with the middle node in $Z$, or (ii) a collider with the middle node (and none of its descendants) in $Z$.

**Example:** In the Alarm network, $J$ and $M$ are *not* marginally independent (they share the common cause $A$), but they become conditionally independent given $A$ — i.e., $J \perp\!\!\!\perp M \mid A$.

**(b)** Network structure:

$$\text{Fl} \to F \leftarrow C \to BP \leftarrow Fl$$

Wait — Fever is caused by Flu or Cold, and Body Pain depends on both. Refined structure:

- $Fl$ and $C$ are independent causes.
- $F$ depends on $Fl$ and $C$: $P(F \mid Fl, C)$ — assume noisy-OR model with leak probability.
- $BP$ depends on $Fl$ and $C$ directly: $P(BP \mid Fl, C)$.

For simplicity (as per the problem wording), assume $F \approx Fl \vee C$ (approximately), so:

$$P(F=t) = 1 - (1 - P(Fl))(1 - P(C)) = 1 - 0.97 \times 0.90 = 1 - 0.873 = 0.127$$

Using Bayes' theorem (treating $F$ as proxy for $Fl \vee C$):

$$P(F \mid BP=t) = \frac{P(BP=t \mid F) \cdot P(F)}{P(BP=t)}$$

Assuming $P(BP \mid F) \approx 0.9$ and $P(BP \mid \neg F) \approx 0.2$:

Numerator: $0.9 \times 0.127 = 0.1143$

Marginal $P(BP) = P(BP \mid F) P(F) + P(BP \mid \neg F) P(\neg F) = 0.9 \times 0.127 + 0.2 \times 0.873 = 0.1143 + 0.1746 = 0.2889$

Therefore:

$$P(F \mid BP=t) = \frac{0.1143}{0.2889} \approx 0.3956$$

So observing body pain raises the probability of fever from prior $0.127$ to posterior $\approx 0.396$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes on Question B(b):**
> 1. **Drawing an incorrect DAG** — make sure arrows represent *causal* direction (cause $\to$ effect). Wrong direction costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $P(BP \mid Fl)$ with $P(Fl \mid BP)$** — these are very different (likelihood vs posterior). Always re-label which term is which in Bayes' rule.
> 3. **Skipping the marginal denominator** — the normalization is essential.
> 4. **Not stating independence assumptions explicitly** — e.g., "$Fl$ and $C$ are assumed independent" must be written.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Rational Agent Under Uncertainty** must choose actions to maximize expected performance; perfect knowledge is unavailable.
- [x] **MEU Principle:** $a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_s P(s \mid a, e)\,U(s)$ — the cornerstone of rational decision-making.
- [x] **Five Sources of Uncertainty:** Partial Observability, Nondeterminism, Adversarial Opponents, Stochastic Environments, Model Ignorance.
- [x] **Three Kolmogorov Probability Axioms:** Non-negativity, Normalization, Countable Additivity.
- [x] **Conditional Probability:** $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$
- [x] **Bayes' Theorem:** Posterior $\propto$ Likelihood $\times$ Prior.
- [x] **Bayesian Network** = DAG + CPTs; encodes factorization of joint distribution.
- [x] **BN Factorization:** $P(X_1,\dots,X_n) = \prod_i P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$.
- [x] **D-Separation Rules:** Chains and forks are blocked by conditioning on the middle node; colliders are activated by conditioning on the middle node.
- [x] **Exact Inference:** Enumeration, Variable Elimination, Clique Tree.
- [x] **Approximate Inference:** Rejection Sampling, Likelihood Weighting, Gibbs Sampling (MCMC).
- [x] **Utility Axioms** (von Neumann–Morgenstern): Ordering, Transitivity, Continuity, Substitutability, Monotonicity, Decomposability.
- [x] **Information Value of Perfect Information (VPI):** $VPI(e) = EU(a_{e^*} \mid e, e^*) - EU(a^* \mid e) \ge 0$ always.
- [x] **Naive Bayes Assumption:** All features are conditionally independent given the class — yields $P(C \mid F_1,\dots,F_n) \propto P(C) \prod_i P(F_i \mid C)$.
- [x] **Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams:** Confusing prior with posterior; forgetting to marginalize hidden variables; skipping normalization; mixing causal direction in DAGs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Probability Notation?

In the context of **Agent Based Intelligent Systems (ABIS)**, probability notation is the formal symbolic language used to represent, manipulate, and reason about **uncertainty**. Every intelligent agent operating in a partially observable, stochastic environment must quantify the likelihood of states, actions, and observations. Probability notation is the mathematical scaffolding that makes such reasoning precise, computable, and provably sound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Probability notation* refers to the standardized set of mathematical symbols, operators, and syntactic conventions (such as $P(A)$, $P(A \mid B)$, $P(\cdot \mid \cdot)$, $\Omega$, $\mathcal{F}$, $E[X]$, $Var(X)$) used to express probabilistic statements about random experiments, events, random variables, and their inter-dependencies within a probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$.

The structure is built on three primitive objects:
1. **Sample Space ($\Omega$)** — the set of *all possible elementary outcomes* of a random experiment.
2. **Event Space / Sigma-Algebra ($\mathcal{F}$)** — a collection of subsets of $\Omega$ to which probabilities can be meaningfully assigned.
3. **Probability Measure ($P$)** — a function $P: \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ satisfying the Kolmogorov Axioms.

## 1.2 The Big Picture — Why an Agent Needs Probability

Imagine you are designing a **self-driving car agent**. The car has noisy sensors, unpredictable pedestrians, and a stochastic traffic environment. The agent cannot *know* with certainty whether the pedestrian will step onto the road. Instead, it must say: *"Given that I see motion at the crosswalk, the probability that the pedestrian will cross in the next 2 seconds is 0.7."* This entire statement is written using **probability notation**.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — "The Weather Oracle"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of probability notation as the *vocabulary* of a "Weather Oracle." 
> - The Oracle lives in a room called **$\Omega$** (every possible weather state lives here — sunny, rainy, stormy…).
> - The Oracle only answers yes/no questions about a chosen **Event** $A$ ("Will it rain tomorrow?").
> - The Oracle's answer is always a number between **0** (impossible) and **1** (certain), written as $P(A)$.
> - If you give the Oracle a *clue* (e.g., "I see dark clouds"), it adjusts its answer — this is **conditional probability**, written as $P(A \mid \text{clouds})$.

In agent-based systems, the "clue" is typically the agent's **observation** or **belief state**, and the "answer" drives the agent's **decision policy**.

## 1.4 Core Symbols — The Probability Vocabulary

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\Omega$ | Sample Space | Set of all possible outcomes |
| $\omega$ | Elementary Outcome | A single point in $\Omega$ |
| $A, B, C$ | Events | Subsets of $\Omega$ |
| $A^c$ or $\bar{A}$ | Complement | Outcome NOT in $A$ |
| $A \cap B$ | Intersection | Both $A$ and $B$ occur |
| $A \cup B$ | Union | At least one of $A$ or $B$ occurs |
| $A \setminus B$ | Difference | $A$ occurs but $B$ does not |
| $\emptyset$ | Null Event | Impossible event |
| $P(A)$ | Probability of $A$ | A real number in $[0,1]$ |
| $P(A \mid B)$ | Conditional Probability | Probability of $A$ given $B$ occurred |
| $X, Y, Z$ | Random Variables | Mappings from $\Omega$ to $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $E[X]$ | Expectation | Long-run average value of $X$ |
| $Var(X)$ | Variance | Spread / dispersion of $X$ |
| $\sim$ | Distributed As | $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ means $X$ is normal |
