- ✅ **Reactive vs Deliberative:** Reactive = fast, no model; Deliberative = slow, symbolic, optimal.
- ✅ **MAS architectures** often use BDI for individual agents and Blackboard for collaboration.
- ✅ Real-world examples: **Mars rovers** (hybrid), **Waymo** (hybrid), **AlphaGo** (learning), **HEARSAY-II** (blackboard).
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers: always include *at least one diagram* and a *comparison table*.
- ✅ KTU expects familiarity with **Rao & Georgeff (1995)** as the canonical BDI reference.
- ✅ **Standard formula:** $I_{t+1} = \text{filter}(B_{t+1}, \text{options}(B_{t+1}, I_t), I_t)$ — memorize verbatim.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time and Uncertainty
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time and Uncertainty

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Time" in Agent-Based Intelligent Systems?

In the context of intelligent agents, **Time** refers to the formal representation, reasoning, and inference about temporal relationships between events, actions, and states in a dynamic environment. It is the framework that allows an agent to answer questions like *"What happened before what?"*, *"How long will an action take?"*, and *"Will event A still be true at time t?"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Formal):** Time in agent systems is the modeling of temporal phenomena — events, intervals, durations, and constraints — using logical, probabilistic, or algebraic formalisms, enabling an agent to perform **temporal reasoning** about the world.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Schedule Diary" Intuition
Imagine you are a personal assistant managing a CEO's calendar. You don't just know *what* meetings are scheduled; you know the **order** (meeting A before lunch), the **duration** (1 hour), the **gaps** (15 min travel), and the **overlaps** (two meetings clash). That mental model of "when" is exactly what temporal reasoning in agents achieves. The agent's knowledge base is like a diary, and temporal logic gives it the rules to update that diary as new information arrives.

---

### 1.2 What is "Uncertainty" in Agent-Based Intelligent Systems?

**Uncertainty** is the inability of an agent to know the *exact* true state of the world, the *exact* outcome of an action, or the *exact* truth value of a fact. The real world is noisy, sensors are imperfect, information is incomplete, and the future is unpredictable. Intelligent agents must therefore reason with **degrees of belief** rather than absolute truths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Formal):** Uncertainty is the lack of complete knowledge or deterministic predictability about the state of the environment, the outcomes of actions, or the truth of observations, requiring the agent to employ probabilistic, evidential, or fuzzy formalisms to act rationally under incomplete information.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Forecast" Intuition
When your phone says *"70% chance of rain"*, it is not lying and it is not certain — it is expressing **uncertainty**. You still take an umbrella because the system has quantified its belief. An intelligent agent does the same: instead of `rain = true` or `rain = false`, it says `P(rain) = 0.7`. The agent then makes decisions (umbrella or no umbrella) that maximize expected utility.

---

### 1.3 Why Both Concepts Co-exist in Module 3

Time and uncertainty are studied together because:
- **Predictions about the future** (a temporal concept) are inherently **uncertain**.
- **Sensor data arrives over time**, and each new reading updates belief (learning under temporal uncertainty).
- **Plans must be scheduled** under uncertain durations (e.g., *"drive time could be 30–50 minutes"*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCCAT601 Module 3):** The module unifies these two ideas under the umbrella of **reasoning in dynamic, partially observable environments** — the hardest case for any intelligent agent.

---

### 1.4 Sources of Uncertainty (KTU Classification)

| Source | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Partial Observability** | Sensors cannot see the full state | A vacuum robot not knowing where the cat is |
| **Stochastic Environments** | Action outcomes are probabilistic | Dice roll in a game agent |
| **Adversarial Opponents** | Other agents act unpredictably | Chess opponent's strategy |
| **Sensor Noise** | Readings are imprecise | A camera reporting 51°C when it is 50°C |
| **Model Incompleteness** | The world model omits some variables | Unknown chemical reaction pathway |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A 3-mark question often asks to *"List the sources of uncertainty"* — memorize the five above using the mnemonic **P-S-S-A-M** (Partial, Stochastic, Sensor, Adversarial, Model).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Temporal Representation — Allen's Interval Algebra

The dominant formalism for representing time in AI agents is **Allen's Interval Algebra (1983)**, which defines **13 mutually exclusive temporal relations** between any two time intervals.

Let the agent know two intervals: interval `X` and interval `Y`.

| Relation | Symbol | Meaning | Inverse |
|---|---|---|---|
| X before Y | `<` | X ends before Y starts | Y after X |
| X meets Y | `m` | X ends exactly when Y starts | Y met-by X |
| X overlaps Y | `o` | X and Y share some time, X starts first | Y overlapped-by X |
| X starts Y | `s` | X and Y start together, X ends first | Y started-by X |
| X during Y | `d` | X is fully contained in Y | Y contains X |
| X finishes Y | `f` | X and Y end together, X starts later | Y finished-by X |
| X equals Y | `=` | X and Y are identical | Y equals X |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Allen's algebra supports **constraint propagation**. If the agent knows `A before B` and `B before C`, it can infer `A before C` — this is *temporal transitivity* used in planning and scheduling agents.

---

### 2.2 Temporal Logic (LTL Basics)

**Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)** extends propositional logic with temporal operators used by agent planners.

| Operator | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Next | $\bigcirc \phi$ | $\phi$ holds in the next time step |
| Eventually | $\Diamond \phi$ | $\phi$ holds at *some* future time step |
| Always (Globally) | $\Box \phi$ | $\phi$ holds at *all* future time steps |
| Until | $\phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi$ | $\phi$ holds until $\psi$ becomes true |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Express 'the door will eventually be closed and stay closed' in LTL."* → Answer: $\Diamond \Box \, \text{closed}$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Uncertainty Quantification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for uncertainty formulas in the KTU PCCAT601 syllabus. Every formula here is **fair game** for the End Semester Exam (ESE).

| Concept | Formula | Notation Key | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prior Probability** | $P(A)$ | Belief in $A$ before evidence | $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| **Joint Probability** | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Only if $A,B$ independent | Joint events |
| **Conditional Probability** | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Probability of $A$ given $B$ | Updates with evidence |
| **Total Probability** | $P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i)\,P(A_i)$ | Law of total probability | Marginalization |
| **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Hypothesis update from evidence | Core of Bayesian agents |
| **Posterior Odds** | $\dfrac{P(H \mid E)}{P(\neg H \mid E)} = \dfrac{P(E \mid H)}{P(E \mid \neg H)} \cdot \dfrac{P(H)}{P(\neg H)}$ | Bayes factor $\times$ prior odds | Decision threshold |
| **Certainty Factor (CF)** | $CF(h,e) = MB(h,e) - MD(h,e)$ | Measure of belief $\in [-1,1]$ | MYCIN-style expert systems |
| **Dempster's Combination** | $m_{1} \oplus m_{2}(C) = K^{-1} \sum_{A \cap B = C} m_{1}(A)\,m_{2}(B)$ | $K = 1 - \sum_{A \cap B = \emptyset} m_{1}(A)\,m_{2}(B)$ | Evidence fusion |
| **Fuzzy Membership** | $\mu_{\text{hot}}(T) \in [0,1]$ | Degree of "hotness" | Linguistic variables |
| **Fuzzy AND (t-norm)** | $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$ | Conjunction | Fuzzy logic |
| **Fuzzy OR (s-norm)** | $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$ | Disjunction | Fuzzy logic |
| **Expected Utility** | $EU(a) = \sum_{s} P(s) \cdot U(s,a)$ | Sum over world states $s$ | Rational decision |
| **Entropy (Information)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Bits of uncertainty | Information gain |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for the KTU Exam:** When writing Bayes' theorem in your answer sheet, always use **proper fraction notation**. The vertical bar `|` in $P(A \mid B)$ is the **conditional symbol** — never confuse it with division by a parenthesized quantity.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Combine temporal logic (lane change within 3 seconds) with Bayesian filtering (track pedestrian position under sensor noise).
- **Medical Diagnosis Agents (MYCIN, IBM Watson Health):** Use certainty factors to combine multiple weak pieces of evidence.
- **Smart Grid / IoT Agents:** Use fuzzy logic to handle linguistic rules like *"if temperature is HIGH and humidity is MEDIUM, fan speed is FAST"*.
- **Financial Trading Agents:** Use Bayesian networks to update stock-price beliefs as new market data arrives over time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

We derive Bayes' theorem rigorously, since this is a **high-yield 14-mark derivation question** in KTU exams.

**Given:** Two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of conditional probability for $A$ given $B$:**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to express the joint probability $P(A \cap B)$:**

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

**Step 3 — Write the symmetric expression by swapping $A$ and $B$:**

$$P(B \cap A) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 4 — Observe that joint probability is commutative, so $P(A \cap B) = P(B \cap A)$:**

$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $P(B)$ to isolate $P(A \mid B)$ on the left:**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 6 — Use the law of total probability to expand $P(B)$ when $A$ partitions the sample space:**

$$P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \neg A) \cdot P(\neg A)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the expanded $P(B)$ into the Bayes' equation to obtain the canonical form:**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \neg A) \cdot P(\neg A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology mapping for KTU exams:**
> - $P(A)$ = **Prior** (belief before seeing evidence $B$)
> - $P(A \mid B)$ = **Posterior** (belief after seeing evidence $B$)
> - $P(B \mid A)$ = **Likelihood** (how probable is the evidence under the hypothesis)
> - $P(B)$ = **Evidence** (normalizing constant)

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Bayesian Diagnostic Agent (Full Calculation)

**Problem (KTU Sample Style):** A medical diagnostic agent knows the following:
- **1%** of the population has Disease $D$: $P(D) = 0.01$
- **99%** do not have it: $P(\neg D) = 0.99$
- The test for $D$ is **90% sensitive**: $P(+ \mid D) = 0.90$ (positive test given disease)
- The test is **95% specific**: $P(- \mid \neg D) = 0.95$, so $P(+ \mid \neg D) = 0.05$ (false positive rate)

A patient tests **positive**. What is the probability that they actually have the disease, $P(D \mid +)$?

**Step 1 — Identify the unknowns and knowns.**
We need $P(D \mid +)$. We know the prior, the likelihoods, and the negation. This is a direct Bayes' theorem application.

**Step 2 — Apply the Bayes' formula with two-partition evidence:**

$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{P(+ \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(+ \mid D) \cdot P(D) + P(+ \mid \neg D) \cdot P(\neg D)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{(0.90) \cdot (0.01)}{(0.90) \cdot (0.01) + (0.05) \cdot (0.99)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$0.90 \times 0.01 = 0.009$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator's first term (true positives):**

$$0.90 \times 0.01 = 0.009$$

**Step 6 — Compute the denominator's second term (false positives):**

$$0.05 \times 0.99 = 0.0495$$

**Step 7 — Sum the two terms to get the total evidence probability $P(+)$:**

$$P(+) = 0.009 + 0.0495 = 0.0585$$

**Step 8 — Divide numerator by denominator to obtain the posterior:**

$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{0.009}{0.0585} = 0.1538$$

**Step 9 — Convert to percentage for the final answer:**

$$P(D \mid +) \approx 15.38\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive KTU insight:** Even with a test that is 90% accurate, a positive result only means a 15% chance of disease! This is the famous **"base-rate fallacy"** — the agent must weight by the prior $P(D)$. KTU examiners love this as a "discussion" part of a 14-mark question.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Uncertainty Method

| Situation in the Agent's World | Best Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor noise with known statistical model | **Bayes' Theorem / Bayesian Networks** | Mathematically rigorous, optimal under known priors |
| Multiple independent experts giving weak evidence | **Certainty Factors (CF)** | Combines belief and disbelief additively (MYCIN-style) |
| Evidence may come from **conflicting sources** | **Dempster-Shafer Theory** | Explicitly models ignorance, fuses evidence via Dempster's rule |
| Linguistic rules from human experts ("hot", "high") | **Fuzzy Logic** | Handles graded membership without sharp thresholds |
| Time-evolving hidden state (e.g., robot location) | **Hidden Markov Models (HMM)** | Temporal + uncertainty combined |
| Time-evolving continuous state (e.g., drone position) | **Kalman Filter** | Optimal Bayesian update for Gaussian linear systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Dempster-Shafer** with **Bayesian**. Remember: in Bayesian theory, you must commit to a prior; in Dempster-Shafer, you can express *ignorance* through the belief mass of the *frame of discernment* $\Theta$.

---

### 3.4 Temporal Reasoning — Worked Example with Allen's Relations

**Problem (KTU Sample Style):** An agent knows three events: $A, B, C$. Given $A$ overlaps $C$ and $C$ meets $B$, infer the relation between $A$ and $B$.

**Step 1 — Translate the first relation into interval language:**
$A$ overlaps $C$ means $A$ starts before $C$, but $A$ ends after $C$ starts. So: $\text{start}(A) < \text{start}(C) < \text{end}(A)$.

**Step 2 — Translate the second relation:**
$C$ meets $B$ means $C$ ends exactly when $B$ starts: $\text{end}(C) = \text{start}(B)$.

**Step 3 — Chain the start times:**
From step 1: $\text{start}(A) < \text{start}(C)$.
From step 2: $\text{end}(C) = \text{start}(B)$, so $\text{start}(B)$ comes *after* $\text{start}(C)$ (since $C$ has non-zero duration in an overlap).

**Step 4 — Combine:**
$\text{start}(A) < \text{start}(C) < \text{start}(B)$, and $A$ ends after $C$ starts but $B$ starts when $C$ ends.

**Step 5 — Conclude:** $A$ ends *before* $B$ starts (since $C$ lies entirely in the gap). Therefore: $A$ **before** $B$ (relation symbol `<`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's tip:** Always draw a small timeline sketch on the answer sheet. It earns 1–2 free "presentation" marks and leaves no room for the examiner to misinterpret your reasoning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Bayesian Reasoning Cycle in an Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startAgent[Agent Receives New Observation] --> priorStage[Stage 1: Retrieve Prior P-H from KB]
    priorStage --> likelihoodStage[Stage 2: Compute Likelihood P-E-given-H from Sensor Model]
    likelihoodStage --> evidenceStage[Stage 3: Compute Evidence P-E via Total Probability]
    evidenceStage --> combineStage[Stage 4: Combine via Bayes Rule to get Posterior P-H-given-E]
    combineStage --> decideStage[Stage 5: Select Action Maximising Expected Utility EU]
    decideStage --> actStage[Agent Executes Action in Environment]
    actStage --> observeStage[New Observation Arrives]
    observeStage --> startAgent
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** This loop is the heart of a **Bayesian agent** (a type of intelligent agent in the KTU syllabus). Each cycle represents one *perception–cognition–action* round. The agent's belief state is updated by Bayes' rule at every step.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Taxonomy of Time & Uncertainty

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootTU[Time and Uncertainty in Agents]
    rootTU --> timeBranch[Time]
    rootTU --> uncertaintyBranch[Uncertainty]

    timeBranch --> intervals[Intervals and Points]
    timeBranch --> relations[Relations: Allens 13]
    timeBranch --> logic[Temporal Logic: LTL]
    timeBranch --> hmm[HMM and Kalman Filter]

    uncertaintyBranch --> prob[Probabilistic]
    uncertaintyBranch --> evi[Evidential]
    uncertaintyBranch --> fuzzy[Fuzzy]

    prob --> bayes[Bayesian Networks]
    prob --> msm[Markov Models]
    evi --> cf[Certainty Factors]
    evi --> ds[Dempster-Shafer]
    fuzzy --> fl[Fuzzy Logic Systems]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The left subtree handles *temporal* concerns (when, in what order, for how long). The right subtree handles *epistemic* concerns (how sure, with what evidence, with what belief). Real agents almost always combine a branch from each subtree.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Dempster-Shafer Evidence Fusion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ev1[Evidence Source 1] --> mass1[Mass Function m1 over Theta]
    ev2[Evidence Source 2] --> mass2[Mass Function m2 over Theta]
    mass1 --> combineFunc[Dempsters Combination Rule]
    mass2 --> combineFunc
    combineFunc --> conflict{Conflict K equals zero?}
    conflict -- Yes --> resultA[Combined Belief Bel A and Plausibility Pl A]
    conflict -- No --> normalize[Apply Normalization 1 over 1-K]
    normalize --> resultA
    resultA --> decision[Agent Decision with Combined Belief]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Dempster's rule is the **fusion operator** of Dempster-Shafer theory. $K$ is the conflict mass; if two sources strongly contradict each other, $K \to 1$ and the rule becomes unstable — a known weakness that KTU examiners may ask you to critique.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Certainty Factor Combination (MYCIN-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rule1[Rule 1 fires with CF1] --> c1[Combine CF1 and CF2]
    rule2[Rule 2 fires with CF2] --> c1
    c1 --> formulaStep[CF combined = CF1 + CF2 times 1 minus CF1 if both positive]
    c1 --> altCase[CF combined = CF1 + CF1 times CF2 if both negative]
    c1 --> mixCase[CF combined = CF1 plus CF2 divided by 1 minus min of absolute values if signs differ]
    formulaStep --> finalCF[Final Certainty Factor in range -1 to 1]
    altCase --> finalCF
    mixCase --> finalCF
    finalCF --> threshold{CF greater than threshold 0.2?}
    threshold -- Yes --> concludeH[Hypothesis Accepted]
    threshold -- No --> concludeR[Hypothesis Rejected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The three branches correspond to the three sign-cases of certainty factor combination in the MYCIN expert system. The 0.2 threshold is a representative value — KTU questions often use 0.2 or 0.5 as a cutoff.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**  
**Define uncertainty in intelligent agents. List any four sources of uncertainty.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Uncertainty is the lack of complete or deterministic knowledge about the state of the environment, the outcomes of actions, or the truth of observations, which prevents an agent from reasoning with absolute Boolean certainty.

**Four Sources of Uncertainty:**
1. **Partial Observability** — the agent's sensors cannot perceive the complete world state.
2. **Stochasticity** — actions or the environment have probabilistic outcomes.
3. **Sensor Noise** — percept readings are imprecise or contain errors.
4. **Adversarial Agents** — other agents may act in ways that are unpredictable or hostile.

> [!NOTE]
> A fifth source often accepted is **Model Incompleteness** (the agent's internal world model is missing variables).

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for sources. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**  
**Explain the role of temporal reasoning in an intelligent agent. State Allen's seven primitive temporal relations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Temporal Reasoning:** Temporal reasoning allows an agent to represent, order, and infer about events, actions, and states that occur over time. It enables the agent to answer queries such as "what happened before what?", "how long will this take?", and "will the goal be satisfied at the deadline?". Without temporal reasoning, the agent cannot plan, schedule, or react intelligently in dynamic environments.

**Allen's Seven Primitive Relations** (any seven of the thirteen are acceptable, the most common seven are):

| # | Relation | Symbol | Inverse |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Before | `<` | After |
| 2 | Meets | `m` | Met-by |
| 3 | Overlaps | `o` | Overlapped-by |
| 4 | Starts | `s` | Started-by |
| 5 | During | `d` | Contains |
| 6 | Finishes | `f` | Finished-by |
| 7 | Equals | `=` | Equals |

*(Examiner's note: 1.5 marks for role, 1.5 marks for relations with brief descriptions.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Bayesian reasoning cycle** in an intelligent agent. Define the terms *prior*, *posterior*, *likelihood*, and *evidence* in the context of Bayes' theorem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A spam-filtering agent knows the following statistics for the word *"offer"* in emails:
- $P(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) = 0.80$ (likelihood)
- $P(\text{offer} \mid \neg \text{spam}) = 0.10$ (false positive rate)
- $P(\text{spam}) = 0.40$ (base rate of spam in inbox)

An incoming email contains the word *"offer"*. Using **Bayes' theorem**, calculate $P(\text{spam} \mid \text{offer})$ and decide whether the agent should classify it as spam. Use a decision threshold of $0.5$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Bayesian Reasoning Cycle**

**Definition of terms:**
- **Prior $P(H)$:** the agent's initial belief in hypothesis $H$ before observing evidence.
- **Likelihood $P(E \mid H)$:** the probability of observing evidence $E$ if hypothesis $H$ is true.
- **Evidence $P(E)$:** the total probability of observing $E$ under all hypotheses (acts as normalizer).
- **Posterior $P(H \mid E)$:** the updated belief in $H$ after observing $E$.

**Bayes' formula:**

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

**Cycle Explanation (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):**

1. **Percept:** The agent observes new evidence $E$ from the environment.
2. **Prior retrieval:** It loads the current prior belief $P(H)$ from its knowledge base.
3. **Likelihood computation:** The agent's sensor model gives $P(E \mid H)$.
4. **Evidence computation:** It computes $P(E)$ by marginalizing over all possible hypotheses.
5. **Posterior update:** It applies Bayes' rule to update the belief.
6. **Decision:** It chooses the action $a$ that maximises expected utility $EU(a) = \sum_{s} P(s) \cdot U(s, a)$.
7. **Act:** The chosen action is executed, and the cycle repeats as new percepts arrive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is a valid agent loop:** The posterior from one cycle becomes the prior of the next cycle, making the agent a **continuous Bayesian learner**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation**

**Step 1 — Identify the knowns and the unknown:**
- $P(\text{spam}) = 0.40$, so $P(\neg \text{spam}) = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$
- $P(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) = 0.80$
- $P(\text{offer} \mid \neg \text{spam}) = 0.10$
- Find: $P(\text{spam} \mid \text{offer})$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem with the two-hypothesis form:**

$$P(\text{spam} \mid \text{offer}) = \frac{P(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{spam})}{P(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{spam}) + P(\text{offer} \mid \neg \text{spam}) \cdot P(\neg \text{spam})}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$P(\text{spam} \mid \text{offer}) = \frac{(0.80)(0.40)}{(0.80)(0.40) + (0.10)(0.60)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$0.80 \times 0.40 = 0.32$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator's first term:**

$$0.80 \times 0.40 = 0.32$$

**Step 6 — Compute the denominator's second term:**

$$0.10 \times 0.60 = 0.06$$

**Step 7 — Sum the two terms for total evidence probability:**

$$P(\text{offer}) = 0.32 + 0.06 = 0.38$$

**Step 8 — Divide numerator by denominator:**

$$P(\text{spam} \mid \text{offer}) = \frac{0.32}{0.38} = 0.8421$$

**Step 9 — Compare with decision threshold:**

$$0.8421 > 0.5 \implies \text{Classify as SPAM}$$

**Conclusion:** The agent should classify the email as spam, because the posterior probability of spam given the word *"offer"* is approximately **84.21%**, which exceeds the 0.5 threshold.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **Certainty Factor (CF) model** used in the MYCIN expert system. Show how certainty factors of two rules supporting the same hypothesis are combined in the three different sign cases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A monitoring agent for a chemical plant uses **Dempster-Shafer theory** with a frame of discernment $\Theta = \{F, \neg F\}$ where $F$ means "fire detected". Two sensors report the following mass functions:

- **Sensor 1:** $m_1(\{F\}) = 0.7$, $m_1(\{\neg F\}) = 0.2$, $m_1(\Theta) = 0.1$
- **Sensor 2:** $m_2(\{F\}) = 0.6$, $m_2(\{\neg F\}) = 0.3$, $m_2(\Theta) = 0.1$

Apply **Dempster's rule of combination** to find the combined mass for $\{F\}$, $\{\neg F\}$, and $\Theta$. State the final belief and plausibility of fire. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Certainty Factor Model**

The **Certainty Factor (CF)** model was introduced in the **MYCIN** medical diagnosis expert system (Shortliffe, 1970s) to handle uncertain rule-based reasoning without requiring full Bayesian priors (which were hard to obtain from physicians).

**Definition:**

$$CF(h, e) = MB(h, e) - MD(h, e)$$

where:
- $MB(h, e)$ = **Measure of Belief** — degree to which evidence $e$ supports hypothesis $h$.
- $MD(h, e)$ = **Measure of Disbelief** — degree to which evidence $e$ refutes hypothesis $h$.
- $CF \in [-1, 1]$: $CF = +1$ means certainly true, $CF = -1$ means certainly false, $CF = 0$ means no evidence.

**Combining Two Certainty Factors $CF_1$ and $CF_2$ — Three Sign Cases:**

**Case 1 — Both $CF_1$ and $CF_2$ are positive (both support the hypothesis):**

$$CF_{\text{combined}} = CF_1 + CF_2 \cdot (1 - CF_1)$$

**Case 2 — Both $CF_1$ and $CF_2$ are negative (both refute the hypothesis):**

$$CF_{\text{combined}} = CF_1 + CF_2 \cdot (1 + CF_1)$$

**Case 3 — One positive and one negative (conflicting evidence):**

$$CF_{\text{combined}} = \frac{CF_1 + CF_2}{1 - \min(\vert CF_1 \vert, \vert CF_2 \vert)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Note the use of `\vert` for absolute value. Always use proper absolute value notation in KTU answer sheets; writing `|x|` is acceptable on paper but in **typed submissions** use `\vert x \vert`.

**Threshold for action:** A typical MYCIN threshold is **$CF \ge 0.2$** to conclude the hypothesis.

---

**Part (b) — Dempster-Shafer Combination**

**Step 1 — Set up the combination table.** We must compute the combined mass for every non-empty intersection of subsets in the focal sets. The focal sets are $\{F\}$, $\{\neg F\}$, and $\Theta$.

**Step 2 — Build the combination matrix:**

| $m_1 \downarrow$ \ $m_2 \rightarrow$ | $\{F\}$ (0.6) | $\{\neg F\}$ (0.3) | $\Theta$ (0.1) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$\{F\}$ (0.7)** | $\{F\}$: 0.42 | $\emptyset$: 0.21 | $\{F\}$: 0.07 |
| **$\{\neg F\}$ (0.2)** | $\emptyset$: 0.12 | $\{\neg F\}$: 0.06 | $\{\neg F\}$: 0.02 |
| **$\Theta$ (0.1)** | $\{F\}$: 0.06 | $\{\neg F\}$: 0.03 | $\Theta$: 0.01 |

**Step 3 — Identify the conflict mass $K$** (sum of all $\emptyset$ intersections):

$$K = 0.21 + 0.12 = 0.33$$

**Step 4 — Compute the normalization factor:**

$$1 - K = 1 - 0.33 = 0.67$$

**Step 5 — Compute the combined mass for $\{F\}$:**

$$m_{1 \oplus 2}(\{F\}) = \frac{0.42 + 0.07 + 0.06}{0.67} = \frac{0.55}{0.67} \approx 0.8209$$

**Step 6 — Compute the combined mass for $\{\neg F\}$:**

$$m_{1 \oplus 2}(\{\neg F\}) = \frac{0.06 + 0.02 + 0.03}{0.67} = \frac{0.11}{0.67} \approx 0.1642$$

**Step 7 — Compute the combined mass for $\Theta$ (ignorance):**

$$m_{1 \oplus 2}(\Theta) = \frac{0.01}{0.67} \approx 0.0149$$

**Step 8 — Verify the sum equals 1:**

$$0.8209 + 0.1642 + 0.0149 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

**Step 9 — Compute Belief $Bel(\{F\})$** = sum of masses of all non-empty subsets of $\{F\}$:

$$Bel(\{F\}) = m(\{F\}) = 0.8209$$

**Step 10 — Compute Plausibility $Pl(\{F\})$** = 1 - Belief of complement:

$$Pl(\{F\}) = 1 - Bel(\{\neg F\}) = 1 - 0.1642 = 0.8358$$

**Step 11 — Interpretation for the agent:**

The agent believes the probability of fire lies in the interval $[0.8209, \, 0.8358]$ — a narrow interval, indicating high confidence. The agent can conclude **"fire is highly likely"** and trigger the alarm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1 (2 marks lost):** Forgetting to subtract the conflict mass $K$ from 1 before normalizing. Without normalization, the masses will not sum to 1.
> - **Common mistake 2 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting the entries where the intersection is $\Theta$ (i.e., the third column × first row and first column × third row). These contribute positively to the result and must not be omitted.
> - **Common mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** Reporting the *belief* as the final probability. In DS theory, you must report the **interval** $[Bel, Pl]$.
> - **Common mistake 4 (2 marks lost):** Confusing Dempster-Shafer's $m(\Theta)$ (ignorance) with Bayesian's $1 - P(F)$. They are conceptually different.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PCCAT601 Module 3 — Time and Uncertainty**

**Conceptual Pillars:**
- ⏱ **Time** = events + intervals + relations + reasoning over order and duration.
- 🎲 **Uncertainty** = belief + evidence + probability/degrees-of-truth.
- 🤝 **Together** = reasoning in *dynamic, partially observable* worlds (the hardest AI setting).

**Allen's 13 Interval Relations (must memorize):** before, after, meets, met-by, overlaps, overlapped-by, starts, started-by, during, contains, finishes, finished-by, equals.

**LTL Operators (must memorize):** $\bigcirc$ (next), $\Diamond$ (eventually), $\Box$ (always), $\mathcal{U}$ (until).

**The Five Sources of Uncertainty (mnemonic P-S-S-A-M):**
- **P**artial observability
- **S**tochastic actions
- **S**ensor noise
- **A**dversarial agents
- **M**odel incompleteness

**The Four Major Uncertainty Formalisms (must distinguish):**
- **Bayesian** → needs prior, gives posterior, uses $P$.
- **Certainty Factor (CF)** → MYCIN-style, range $[-1,1]$, three sign cases for combination.
- **Dempster-Shafer** → explicit ignorance via $m(\Theta)$, Dempster's rule, $[Bel, Pl]$ interval.
- **Fuzzy Logic** → linguistic variables, $\mu \in [0,1]$, t-norm and s-norm.

**Key Numerical Results to Remember:**
- Entropy $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ in **bits**.
- Expected utility $EU(a) = \sum_s P(s) \cdot U(s,a)$.
- Bayes' theorem has **four** names: prior, likelihood, evidence, posterior.
- MYCIN threshold for action: $CF \ge 0.2$.

**Formula Hot-Spots for the Exam:**
- Bayes' theorem → always the highest-yield formula.
- Dempster's rule with normalization $1 - K$.
- Fuzzy AND = min, Fuzzy OR = max.
- CF combination in all three sign cases.

**Diagram Hot-Spots for the Exam:**
- Draw a **timeline sketch** for Allen's relations (1–2 free marks).
- Draw the **Bayesian update loop** (Percept → Update → Decide → Act).
- Draw a **combination table** for Dempster's rule.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always write the *interpretation* of the numerical answer, not just the number. The examiner awards 1–2 marks for a meaningful sentence like *"The agent should classify the email as spam because the posterior 0.84 exceeds the threshold 0.5."* Skipping the interpretation is the single most common reason students lose the final 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temporal Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temporal Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Temporal Models

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temporal Models** in intelligent agent systems are formal mathematical frameworks that enable an agent to represent, reason about, and predict changes in a dynamic environment over time. They provide the agent with the ability to encode **states**, **events**, **actions**, and **temporal relationships** (precedence, overlap, causality) so that the agent can plan, schedule, and make decisions based on *when* things happen — not just *what* happens.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for **Agent Based Intelligent Systems**, temporal models fall under the **knowledge representation and reasoning** layer of an agent architecture. They extend classical propositional and first-order logic with explicit temporal operators.

### 1.2 Three Pillars of Temporal Reasoning

| Pillar | What it Tracks | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **States** | Conditions that hold over an interval | "The robot is moving" |
| **Events** | Discrete occurrences at a time point | "Door opens" |
| **Processes** | Continuous activities | "Pump is running" |

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU exam definition: *Temporal logic is an extension of classical logic with modal operators (□, ◇, ○, U) that qualify the truth of a proposition with respect to time.*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Train Timetable

Imagine you are a **station-master agent** controlling train signals. A temporal model is your **timetable + rules engine** combined:

- **Time points** = scheduled arrivals/departures.
- **Intervals** = the time window a platform is occupied by a train.
- **Allen's relations** = "Train A *finishes before* Train B enters."
- **Event calculus axioms** = "If the signal turns green AND no train is on the track, THEN the train starts moving."

Without a temporal model, the agent can only react to the *current* sensor reading. With one, the agent can **predict**, **plan**, and **explain** sequences like: *Because Train A was scheduled to depart at 10:00, Train B cannot enter Platform 3 until 10:05.*

### 1.4 Why Temporal Models Matter for Agents

1. **Planning under deadlines** — Agents must reason about "I must finish task T before deadline D."
2. **Concurrent action monitoring** — Multiple sub-agents act in parallel.
3. **Causality** — Distinguishing *cause* from *coincidence* (event A happened *before* event B, therefore A may have caused B).
4. **Persistence** — Knowing that "the door is still open" is true *over* an interval, not just at one instant.
5. **Diagnosis** — Reasoning about *why* a system entered a faulty state by tracing the history of events.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently frame temporal-model questions around a **robotic vacuum cleaner**, **autonomous vehicle**, or **smart-home agent** scenario. Memorize the mapping from English sentences to temporal logic formulas — this is the single most exam-relevant skill.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Major Families of Temporal Logic

#### A. Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)

LTL assumes a single, linear timeline — at every instant there is exactly **one** possible future.

**Core Operators:**

| Symbol | Operator | Meaning | Formula | English |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\bigcirc \phi$ | **Next** | $\phi$ is true at the next time step | $\bigcirc P$ | "P will be true tomorrow" |
| $\Diamond \phi$ | **Finally** (Eventually) | $\phi$ is true at *some* future moment | $\Diamond P$ | "P will eventually hold" |
| $\Box \phi$ | **Globally** (Always) | $\phi$ is true at *all* future moments | $\Box P$ | "P is always true" |
| $\phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi$ | **Until** | $\phi$ holds continuously until $\psi$ becomes true | $P \, \mathcal{U} \, Q$ | "P until Q" |
| $\phi \, \mathcal{W} \, \psi$ | **Weak Until** | Like Until, but $\psi$ need not occur | $P \, \mathcal{W} \, Q$ | "P unless Q" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duality identities** (must memorize):
> $$\Diamond \phi \;\equiv\; \neg \Box \neg \phi$$
> $$\Box \phi \;\equiv\; \neg \Diamond \neg \phi$$
> $$\bigcirc (\phi \wedge \psi) \;\equiv\; \bigcirc \phi \wedge \bigcirc \psi$$

#### B. Computation Tree Logic (CTL) / CTL\*

CTL models time as a **branching tree** of possible futures — useful when an agent's actions have multiple outcomes.

**Path quantifiers** $\mathbf{A}$ (all paths) and $\mathbf{E}$ (exists a path) combined with temporal operators.

| Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\mathbf{A}\Box \phi$ | On *every* possible future path, $\phi$ is always true |
| $\mathbf{E}\Diamond \phi$ | There *exists* a path where $\phi$ eventually becomes true |
| $\mathbf{A}\bigcirc \phi$ | In *all* next states, $\phi$ holds |
| $\mathbf{A}(\phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi)$ | On every path, $\phi$ until $\psi$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** LTL and CTL are *not interchangeable*. LTL = linear, no path quantifier. CTL = branching, every temporal operator **must** be paired with a path quantifier. An expression like $\Box \phi$ alone is **invalid CTL**.

### 2.2 Allen's Interval Algebra (1983)

James Allen defined **13 mutually exclusive qualitative relations** between two time intervals. This is a high-frequency KTU question.

| # | Relation | Symbol | Inverse | Diagram |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | X *before* Y | $X < Y$ | $Y > X$ | `[XX] [YY]` |
| 2 | X *meets* Y | $X \, m \, Y$ | $Y \, mi \, X$ | `[XX][YY]` |
| 3 | X *overlaps* Y | $X \, o \, Y$ | $Y \, oi \, X$ | `[XX` |
|   |   |   |   | ` YY]` |
| 4 | X *starts* Y | $X \, s \, Y$ | $Y \, si \, X$ | `[XX` |
|   |   |   |   | `  YY]` |
| 5 | X *during* Y | $X \, d \, Y$ | $Y \, di \, X$ | ` [XX]` |
|   |   |   |   | `[YYYYY]` |
| 6 | X *finishes* Y | $X \, f \, Y$ | $Y \, fi \, X$ | `  [XX]` |
|   |   |   |   | `[YYYYY]` |
| 7 | X *equal* Y | $X = Y$ | $X = Y$ | `[XXXX]` |

> [!NOTE]
> Allen's algebra supports **constraint propagation** via a transitivity table. For example: if $X < Y$ and $Y < Z$, then $X < Z$ (composition).

### 2.3 Event Calculus (Kowalski & Sergot)

Event calculus represents a dynamic world using **fluents** (time-varying properties) and **events**.

**Core Predicates:**

| Predicate | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\text{HoldsAt}(f, t)$ | Fluent $f$ is true at time $t$ |
| $\text{Initiates}(e, f, t)$ | Event $e$ at time $t$ starts fluent $f$ |
| $\text{Terminates}(e, f, t)$ | Event $e$ at time $t$ ends fluent $f$ |
| $\text{Happens}(e, t)$ | Event $e$ occurs at time $t$ |
| $\text{InitiatedAt}(f, t)$ | $f$ starts at $t$ (derived) |
| $\text{TerminatedAt}(f, t)$ | $f$ ends at $t$ (derived) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Box \phi \equiv \phi \wedge \bigcirc \Box \phi$ | Expansion law of Always | 2 |
| 2 | $\Diamond \phi \equiv \phi \vee \bigcirc \Diamond \phi$ | Expansion law of Eventually | 2 |
| 3 | $\phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi \equiv \psi \vee (\phi \wedge \bigcirc (\phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi))$ | Until expansion | 2 |
| 4 | $\Box \phi \rightarrow \phi$ | Validity: "If always $\phi$, then now $\phi$" | 1 |
| 5 | $\Box(\phi \rightarrow \psi) \rightarrow (\Box \phi \rightarrow \Box \psi)$ | Distribution of $\Box$ over $\rightarrow$ | 1 |
| 6 | $\text{HoldsAt}(f, t_2) \leftarrow \text{HoldsAt}(f, t_1) \wedge \neg \text{Clipped}(t_1, f, t_2)$ | Event calculus persistence | 3 |
| 7 | $\text{Clipped}(t_1, f, t_2) \equiv \exists e,t\, [t_1 < t < t_2 \wedge \text{Terminates}(e, f, t)]$ | A terminating event in $(t_1,t_2)$ | 3 |
| 8 | $\text{Reified approach}$: $\text{TrueAt}(\phi, t)$ | Embed time as an argument | 2 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** LTL specifications for motion planning (e.g., $\Box(\text{red\_light} \rightarrow \bigcirc \text{stop})$).
- **Industrial Robotics:** Allen's relations for task scheduling on a shop floor.
- **Smart Grid:** Event calculus for fault diagnosis in power systems.
- **Healthcare Monitoring:** Temporal abstraction of vital signs to detect deterioration.
- **Web Services / IoT:** CTL model checking of business process workflows.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time Representation Models

### A. Point-Based Time
Time is modelled as a totally ordered set of **instants**: $T = \{t_0, t_1, t_2, \ldots\}$ with ordering $<$ (the *precedes* relation).

- Discrete time: $T = \mathbb{N}$ (used in LTL model checking)
- Dense time: $T = \mathbb{Q}$ or $\mathbb{R}$ (used in continuous physics)
- Linear vs. branching

### B. Interval-Based Time
Time is modelled as **durations** $[t_{\text{start}}, t_{\text{end}}]$ with $t_{\text{start}} \le t_{\text{end}}$.

- Used in Allen's algebra
- Better for actions that *persist* (e.g., "the agent is computing for 3 seconds")

### C. State-Based vs. Event-Based

- **State-based (situational):** $\text{at}(\text{agent}, \text{location}, s)$ — the world is a sequence of *snapshots*.
- **Event-based (event calculus):** changes are *atomic events* that initiate or terminate fluents.

## 2.2 LTL Semantics — Formal Walk-Through

A **Kripke structure** $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ is used as the semantic model where:
- $S$ = set of states
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ = initial states
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ = transition relation
- $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ = labeling function (atomic propositions true in each state)

For a path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$:

$$\pi \models \phi \;\Leftrightarrow\; s_0 \models \phi$$

$$\pi \models \bigcirc \phi \;\Leftrightarrow\; \pi^{1} \models \phi \quad \text{(suffix from } s_1\text{)}$$

$$\pi \models \Box \phi \;\Leftrightarrow\; \forall i \ge 0, \; \pi^{i} \models \phi$$

$$\pi \models \Diamond \phi \;\Leftrightarrow\; \exists i \ge 0, \; \pi^{i} \models \phi$$

$$\pi \models \phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi \;\Leftrightarrow\; \exists i \ge 0, \; \pi^{i} \models \psi \wedge \forall 0 \le j < i, \; \pi^{j} \models \phi$$

## 2.3 Allen's Algebra Transitivity Table (Excerpt)

If $X \, r_1 \, Y$ and $Y \, r_2 \, Z$, what is $X \, ? \, Z$? The full table has $13 \times 13 = 169$ entries. Key ones:

| $r_1$ | $r_2$ | Composition $r_1 \circ r_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| before | before | before |
| before | meets | before |
| before | overlaps | before, overlaps, meets |
| meets | before | before |
| meets | meets | before |
| overlaps | overlaps | before, overlaps, meets, during, starts, equals |
| starts | finishes | during |
| during | during | during |
| finishes | finishes | finishes, equal, before? (varies) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU Part B questions, you only need to demonstrate the composition rule on a **small example** (e.g., compose `before` with `meets`). Don't try to memorize the entire $13 \times 13$ table.

## 2.4 Event Calculus — Formal Axiom Set

The **circular** version of event calculus uses these clauses (EC, after Shanahan 1997):

$$\text{Happens}(a, t) \rightarrow \text{InitiatedAt}(f, t) \quad \text{if Initiates}(a, f, t) \text{ in domain}$$

$$\text{Happens}(a, t) \rightarrow \text{TerminatedAt}(f, t) \quad \text{if Terminates}(a, f, t) \text{ in domain}$$

$$\text{TerminatedAt}(f, t) \rightarrow \neg \text{HoldsAt}(f, t)$$

$$\text{InitiatedAt}(f, t) \rightarrow \text{HoldsAt}(f, t)$$

$$\text{HoldsAt}(f, t) \wedge \neg \text{Clipped}(t, f, t') \wedge t < t' \rightarrow \text{HoldsAt}(f, t')$$

## 2.5 Choosing the Right Model — Decision Table

| Scenario | Best Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Robot must obey "always stop at red light" | LTL ($\Box$ operator) | Safety property over linear time |
| "There exists a plan to reach goal" | CTL ($\mathbf{E}\Diamond$ operator) | Branching, existential |
| "Boil egg for 5 minutes" | Allen's interval algebra | Duration reasoning |
| "Pressing button X turns light on" | Event calculus | Causal effect of discrete event |
| Medical record: "patient had fever on Mon & Wed" | Interval logic | Multi-interval persistence |

## 2.6 KTU Examiner Tip — The "3-Operator Trick"

> [!WARNING]
> Whenever a KTU question asks *"Express the following requirement in LTL"*, use exactly **three operators** in this order:
> 1. $\Box$ (always — for invariants)
> 2. $\Diamond$ (eventually — for goals)
> 3. $\bigcirc$ (next — for immediate response)
>
> Example: *"The agent must always eventually respond."* → $\Box \Diamond \text{respond}$

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expansion Law of $\Diamond \phi$

**Goal:** Prove that $\Diamond \phi \equiv \phi \vee \bigcirc \Diamond \phi$.

### Step 1 — Recall semantic definition

By definition, $\pi \models \Diamond \phi$ iff there exists some index $i \ge 0$ such that $\pi^{i} \models \phi$.

### Step 2 — Split into two cases

We consider two disjoint sub-cases for the witness index $i$:

**Case A:** $i = 0$. Then $\pi^{0} = s_0 \models \phi$, which means $\pi \models \phi$.

**Case B:** $i \ge 1$. Then there exists $i' = i - 1 \ge 0$ such that starting from the *suffix* $\pi^{1}$, the proposition $\phi$ holds at step $i'$. This is exactly the semantic condition for $\pi^{1} \models \Diamond \phi$, i.e., $\pi \models \bigcirc \Diamond \phi$.

### Step 3 — Combine cases

Since Case A and Case B are exhaustive and mutually exclusive:

$$\pi \models \Diamond \phi \;\Leftrightarrow\; \pi \models \phi \;\text{ or }\; \pi \models \bigcirc \Diamond \phi$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

$$\boxed{\Diamond \phi \;\equiv\; \phi \vee \bigcirc \Diamond \phi}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The expansion law says "$\phi$ holds *now* OR (after one step, $\phi$ eventually holds)." It is the logical foundation for the **eventual-progress fairness** used in agent planning.

## 3.2 Derivation: Distribution of $\Box$ over Implication

**Claim:** $\Box(\phi \rightarrow \psi) \rightarrow (\Box \phi \rightarrow \Box \psi)$ is a **valid** LTL formula.

### Step 1 — Assume the antecedent

Suppose $\pi \models \Box(\phi \rightarrow \psi)$. Then for every $i \ge 0$, $\pi^{i} \models \phi \rightarrow \psi$.

### Step 2 — Assume the second antecedent

Suppose additionally $\pi \models \Box \phi$. Then for every $i \ge 0$, $\pi^{i} \models \phi$.

### Step 3 — Combine per index

For any fixed $i \ge 0$, we have both $\pi^{i} \models \phi$ and $\pi^{i} \models \phi \rightarrow \psi$. By **modus ponens**, $\pi^{i} \models \psi$.

### Step 4 — Universally quantify

Since $i \ge 0$ was arbitrary, $\forall i \ge 0, \; \pi^{i} \models \psi$, which is exactly $\pi \models \Box \psi$.

### Step 5 — Conclusion

$$\Box(\phi \rightarrow \psi) \rightarrow (\Box \phi \rightarrow \Box \psi) \quad \text{is valid} \;\blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Allen's Relation Transitivity Example

**Given:**
- Event A *before* Event B: $\,A < B$
- Event B *meets* Event C: $\,B \, m \, C$

**Find:** Possible relations between A and C.

### Step 1 — Decode "before"

A $<$ B means $\text{end}(A) < \text{start}(B)$.

### Step 2 — Decode "meets"

B $m$ C means $\text{end}(B) = \text{start}(C)$.

### Step 3 — Chain the inequalities

$$\text{end}(A) < \text{start}(B) = \text{end}(B) = \text{start}(C)$$

Therefore $\text{end}(A) < \text{start}(C)$.

### Step 4 — Classify

Strict inequality with positive gap → A is **before** C.

$$\boxed{A < C}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Event Calculus Persistence

**Axiom to derive:** If a fluent $f$ holds at $t_1$ and is not *clipped* (i.e., no terminating event occurs) between $t_1$ and $t_2$, then $f$ still holds at $t_2$.

### Step 1 — Hypothesis

$\text{HoldsAt}(f, t_1)$ and $\neg \text{Clipped}(t_1, f, t_2)$ and $t_1 < t_2$.

### Step 2 — Define Clipped

$\text{Clipped}(t_1, f, t_2) \equiv \exists e, t' \, [t_1 < t' < t_2 \wedge \text{Terminates}(e, f, t') \wedge \text{Happens}(e, t')]$.

So $\neg \text{Clipped}$ means: **no** event $e$ terminates $f$ strictly between $t_1$ and $t_2$.

### Step 3 — Apply effect-of-event

If no terminating event occurs, then no axiom $\text{TerminatedAt}(f, t')$ is derivable for any $t' \in (t_1, t_2)$.

### Step 4 — Apply fluent-effect clauses

Therefore for every $t' \in (t_1, t_2)$, $\text{HoldsAt}(f, t')$ continues to be true by induction on time steps (discrete case) or by continuity (dense case).

### Step 5 — Conclusion

$$\text{HoldsAt}(f, t_1) \wedge \neg \text{Clipped}(t_1, f, t_2) \wedge t_1 < t_2 \;\rightarrow\; \text{HoldsAt}(f, t_2) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example

**Scenario (from KTU sample paper style):**

> A cleaning robot must satisfy: *(i) If it is in a room, then the next moment it will be either still in that room OR moving to an adjacent room. (ii) It must eventually visit every room. (iii) It must never enter a forbidden zone.*

**Solution in LTL:**

| Sub-requirement | LTL Formula | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| (i) State continuity | $\Box \forall r\,[\text{in}(r) \rightarrow \bigcirc (\text{in}(r) \vee \text{adjacent}(r))]$ | "Next-step neighbourhood constraint" |
| (ii) Coverage | $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Diamond \text{in}(r_i)$ | "Every room eventually visited" |
| (iii) Safety | $\Box \neg \text{in}(\text{forbidden})$ | "Forbidden zone is always avoided" |
| Full spec | $\Box \neg \text{in}(\text{forbidden}) \;\wedge\; \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Diamond \text{in}(r_i) \;\wedge\; \Box \forall r\,[\text{in}(r) \rightarrow \bigcirc (\text{in}(r) \vee \text{adjacent}(r))]$ | Conjunction of all three |

    B -->|Initiates| C[Fluent f becomes True at t]

    C --> E[Persistence Check]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Clipped by another event?}

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define temporal logic. List any four temporal operators used in LTL with their symbols.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Temporal logic** is an extension of classical propositional/first-order logic that introduces operators to qualify the truth value of a proposition with respect to time.

**Four LTL operators:**

| Symbol | Name | Informal meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\bigcirc$ | Next | True at the next time instant |
| $\Box$ | Globally (Always) | True at every future time instant |
| $\Diamond$ | Finally (Eventually) | True at some future time instant |
| $\mathcal{U}$ | Until | $\phi$ true continuously until $\psi$ becomes true |

**(3 marks)** — 1 for definition, 2 for correct table.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between LTL and CTL with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LTL | CTL |
|---|---|---|
| Time structure | Linear (one path) | Branching (tree) |
| Path quantifier | None | $\mathbf{A}$ (all), $\mathbf{E}$ (exists) |
| Syntax | $\bigcirc \phi$, $\Box \phi$, $\Diamond \phi$, $\phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi$ | $\mathbf{A}\bigcirc \phi$, $\mathbf{E}\Box \phi$, $\mathbf{A}(\phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi)$ |
| Typical use | Hardware verification, robot safety | Model checking of reactive systems |

**Example:**
- LTL: $\Box(\text{request} \rightarrow \Diamond \text{response})$ — "Every request is eventually followed by a response."
- CTL: $\mathbf{A}\Box \neg \text{error}$ — "On every possible path, error never occurs."

**(3 marks)** — 1.5 for table, 1.5 for example.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3 Internal Choice]`

> An autonomous vacuum-cleaner agent operates in a 3-room house (Kitchen K, Living L, Bedroom B). Formulate an LTL specification that captures the following requirements:
> (a) **(7 marks)** The agent must never enter the Kitchen while it is *wet* (water spilled), and must eventually visit both the Living and Bedroom.
> (b) **(7 marks)** Explain Allen's interval algebra. Using the seven basic relations, determine the possible relation between events A and C given: A *overlaps* B, and B *during* C. Show all reasoning steps.

#### Model Solution

**(a) LTL Formulation (7 marks)**

Define atomic propositions:
- $\text{wet}(\text{Kitchen})$
- $\text{in}(r)$ for $r \in \{\text{Kitchen}, \text{Living}, \text{Bedroom}\}$

**Step 1 — Safety (no entry when wet):**

$$\Box \big[\text{wet}(\text{Kitchen}) \rightarrow \neg \text{in}(\text{Kitchen})\big]$$

> "Globally, if the Kitchen is wet, the agent is not in it."

**Step 2 — Liveness (must eventually visit):**

$$\Diamond \text{in}(\text{Living}) \;\wedge\; \Diamond \text{in}(\text{Bedroom})$$

> "The agent will eventually visit both Living and Bedroom."

**Step 3 — Combine (3 marks for combination + structure):**

$$\boxed{\Box \big[\text{wet}(\text{Kitchen}) \rightarrow \neg \text{in}(\text{Kitchen})\big] \;\wedge\; \Diamond \text{in}(\text{Living}) \;\wedge\; \Diamond \text{in}(\text{Bedroom})}$$

**Examiner's note:** Award 2 marks for Step 1, 2 marks for Step 2, 2 marks for combination, 1 mark for variable definitions and clarity.

---

**(b) Allen's Algebra + Transitivity (7 marks)**

**Definition (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Allen's interval algebra** is a qualitative temporal reasoning framework that defines 13 mutually exclusive relations between two time intervals, capturing how one interval is positioned relative to another along a timeline.

The **seven basic relations** (the other six are their inverses):

1. **Before** ($<$)
2. **Meets** ($m$)
3. **Overlaps** ($o$)
4. **Starts** ($s$)
5. **During** ($d$)
6. **Finishes** ($f$)
7. **Equal** ($=$)

**Transitivity Computation (4 marks):**

**Given:** A *overlaps* B, and B *during* C.
**Find:** Possible relation(s) between A and C.

**Step 1 — Decode the relations.**

A $o$ B means:
$$\text{start}(A) < \text{start}(B) < \text{end}(A) < \text{end}(B)$$

B $d$ C means:
$$\text{start}(C) < \text{start}(B) < \text{end}(B) < \text{end}(C)$$

**Step 2 — Combine constraints.**

Combining inequalities:
$$\text{start}(A) < \text{start}(B)$$
$$\text{start}(B) < \text{end}(A)$$
$$\text{end}(A) < \text{end}(B)$$
$$\text{end}(B) < \text{end}(C)$$
$$\text{start}(C) < \text{start}(B)$$

Therefore we know $\text{start}(A) < \text{start}(B) < \text{end}(B) < \text{end}(C)$, so $\text{end}(A) < \text{end}(C)$.

But the relative position of $\text{start}(A)$ to $\text{start}(C)$ is **not determined** — $\text{start}(A)$ could be less than, equal to, or greater than $\text{start}(C)$.

**Step 3 — Enumerate outcomes.**

The possible Allen relations between A and C are: **{overlaps, during, finished-by, equal, starts, finishes, before}** — but the *most informative* answer is that **A overlaps C or A is during C** depending on whether $\text{start}(A) < \text{start}(C)$ or not.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{A \, o \, C \;\;\vee\;\; A \, d \, C \;\;\vee\;\; A = C}$$

(For full credit, list the *complete* disjunction of all consistent relations from the Allen transitivity table.)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3 Internal Choice]`

> Consider a smart-home agent that controls lighting and heating.
> (a) **(7 marks)** Express the following in Event Calculus axioms:
>     - Event `switchOn(L1)` initiates fluent `lightOn(L1)`.
>     - Event `switchOff(L1)` terminates fluent `lightOn(L1)`.
>     - Persistence: a fluent remains true until an event terminates it.
> (b) **(7 marks)** With a neat Mermaid/flowchart, explain the block-level architecture of a temporal-reasoning agent. Show how perception, the time clock, the temporal database, and the planner interact.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Event Calculus Formulation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define predicates used:**

- $\text{Happens}(e, t)$ — event $e$ occurs at time $t$
- $\text{Initiates}(e, f, t)$ — event $e$ at time $t$ makes fluent $f$ true
- $\text{Terminates}(e, f, t)$ — event $e$ at time $t$ makes fluent $f$ false
- $\text{HoldsAt}(f, t)$ — fluent $f$ is true at time $t$
- $\text{Clipped}(t_1, f, t_2)$ — some event between $t_1$ and $t_2$ terminates $f$

**Step 2 — Domain axioms (3 marks):**

$$\text{Initiates}(\text{switchOn}(L1), \text{lightOn}(L1), t)$$

$$\text{Terminates}(\text{switchOff}(L1), \text{lightOn}(L1), t)$$

**Step 3 — Effect clauses (2 marks):**

$$\text{Happens}(e, t) \wedge \text{Initiates}(e, f, t) \rightarrow \text{HoldsAt}(f, t)$$

$$\text{Happens}(e, t) \wedge \text{Terminates}(e, f, t) \rightarrow \neg \text{HoldsAt}(f, t)$$

**Step 4 — Persistence axiom (2 marks):**

$$\text{HoldsAt}(f, t_1) \wedge \neg \text{Clipped}(t_1, f, t_2) \wedge t_1 < t_2 \rightarrow \text{HoldsAt}(f, t_2)$$

**Step 5 — Clipped definition (1 mark, in final answer):**

$$\text{Clipped}(t_1, f, t_2) \equiv \exists e, t'\, [t_1 < t' < t_2 \wedge \text{Terminates}(e, f, t') \wedge \text{Happens}(e, t')]$$

---

**(b) Block Architecture (7 marks)**

**Mermaid Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Perception Sensors] --> ED[Event Detector]
    TC[Global Time Clock] --> TDB[Temporal Database]
    ED --> TDB
    TDB --> AR[Allens Reasoner]
    TDB --> EC[Event Calculus Engine]
    TDB --> LTL[LTL Model Checker]
    AR --> KB[Knowledge Base]
    EC --> KB
    LTL --> KB
    KB --> PL[Planner]
    PL --> AS[Action Scheduler]
    AS --> AC[Actuator Interface]
    KB --> EX[Explanation Module]
    style P fill:#bbdefb
    style TC fill:#fff9c4
    style TDB fill:#c8e6c9
    style PL fill:#ffccbc
    style AS fill:#f8bbd0
    style AC fill:#d1c4e9
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**

1. **Perception Sensors → Event Detector:** Raw sensor data is parsed into discrete events (door opens, motion detected).
2. **Global Time Clock → Temporal Database:** Each event is timestamped and stored.
3. **Three Reasoners (Allen's, Event Calculus, LTL)** run in parallel, each consulting the temporal database.
4. **Knowledge Base** aggregates their results and maintains the current world state.
5. **Planner** queries the knowledge base to decide the next action.
6. **Action Scheduler** dispatches the action to the **Actuator Interface** (motors, lights, etc.).
7. **Explanation Module** generates human-readable traces using the temporal database.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\Box$ and $\Diamond$** — Most common. Remember: $\Box$ is "always" (universal), $\Diamond$ is "eventually" (existential). *Mnemonic:* $\Box$ has 4 sides (everywhere), $\Diamond$ is a single point (somewhere).
> 2. **Forgetting the safety/liveness distinction** — A safety property uses $\Box$ ("never"); a liveness property uses $\Diamond$ ("eventually"). KTU Part B questions almost always combine both.
> 3. **Mixing LTL and CTL operators** — Writing $\Box \mathbf{A} \phi$ or $\mathbf{A}\Diamond$ without proper nesting. CTL requires *every* temporal operator to be *immediately* prefixed by a path quantifier.
> 4. **Allen's inverse confusion** — If X *before* Y, then Y is *after* X, not *before* X. The inverse of "meets" is "met-by", etc.
> 5. **Event Calculus — omitting $\text{Happens}$** — You cannot conclude $\text{HoldsAt}(f, t)$ from $\text{Initiates}(e, f, t)$ alone; you must also assert $\text{Happens}(e, t)$.
> 6. **No domain model** — Always state which atomic propositions you are defining before writing LTL formulas. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for undefined symbols.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Temporal Models (Module 3)**
>
> - [ ] **Temporal logic** = classical logic + temporal operators ($\bigcirc, \Box, \Diamond, \mathcal{U}$).
> - [ ] **LTL = linear**, single timeline; **CTL = branching**, uses path quantifiers $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{E}$.
> - [ ] **Duality laws:** $\Box \phi \equiv \neg \Diamond \neg \phi$ and $\Diamond \phi \equiv \neg \Box \neg \phi$.
> - [ ] **Expansion laws:** $\Box \phi \equiv \phi \wedge \bigcirc \Box \phi$ and $\Diamond \phi \equiv \phi \vee \bigcirc \Diamond \phi$.
> - [ ] **Until expansion:** $\phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi \equiv \psi \vee (\phi \wedge \bigcirc (\phi \, \mathcal{U} \, \psi))$.
> - [ ] **Safety = $\Box$**, **Liveness = $\Diamond$**, **Response = $\Box(\phi \rightarrow \Diamond \psi)$**.
> - [ ] **Allen's 13 relations:** 7 basic + 6 inverses + 1 equality.
> - [ ] **Allen's transitivity** is computed by chaining start/end inequalities.
> - [ ] **Event Calculus core predicates:** $\text{Happens}$, $\text{HoldsAt}$, $\text{Initiates}$, $\text{Terminates}$, $\text{Clipped}$.
> - [ ] **Persistence axiom:** $\text{HoldsAt}(f,t_1) \wedge \neg \text{Clipped} \wedge t_1 < t_2 \rightarrow \text{HoldsAt}(f,t_2)$.
> - [ ] **Three time representations:** point-based, interval-based, event-based.
> - [ ] **State vs. event-based reasoning:** Situational Calculus (state) vs. Event Calculus (event).
> - [ ] **Common KTU question patterns:** "Express in LTL", "Use Allen's algebra to compute", "Write event calculus axioms".
> - [ ] **Always define atomic propositions** before writing any formula.
> - [ ] **Memorize the duality, expansion, and persistence formulas** — they appear in 80% of exam answers.
>
> **One-line memory hook:** *"Always (□) box, sometimes (◇) diamond, one step (○) is just a Next-step."*
