# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define *Knowledge in Learning*. List any two advantages of incorporating prior knowledge into a learning agent.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Knowledge in Learning* is the paradigm in which an intelligent agent uses **prior symbolic knowledge** — such as logical rules, causal models, or domain axioms — together with observed data to construct general hypotheses. (1.5 marks)

**Two advantages:** (1.5 marks, 0.75 each)
1. **Reduces sample complexity** — fewer training examples are needed because the prior shrinks the hypothesis space.
2. **Improves explainability** — learned rules are human-readable Horn clauses, satisfying XAI requirements.
3. *(Optional 3rd)* Enables **transfer learning** across related tasks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Inductive Learning and Explanation-Based Learning (EBL).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Inductive Learning | EBL |
|--------|--------------------|-----|
| Mechanism | Generalizes from many examples | Deduces from a *single* example using a domain theory |
| Knowledge needed | None (purely data-driven) | Strong, correct & complete domain theory |
| Output | Statistical / first-order rule | Generalized proof re-written as a rule |
| Strength | Tolerates incomplete theory | Sample-efficient |
| Weakness | Needs lots of data | Brittle if theory is wrong |

(1.5 marks for definition of each + 1.5 marks for the table of differences.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **answer any ONE full question** from the pair. Each carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Explanation-Based Learning (EBL)** algorithm. Show how a single training example is generalized using a domain theory. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider an autonomous vacuum-cleaning agent with the following domain theory:
- $In(x,r) \land Adjacent(r,s) \rightarrow In(x,s)$
- $Adjacent(r,s) \lor Adjacent(s,r) \rightarrow Reachable(r,s)$
- $Dirt(x) \land In(x,r) \rightarrow Clean(x)$

Given one observed fact: $Clean(room_5)$ and the agent knows $Dirt(room_5)$. Apply **EBL** to derive a generalized rule. (7 marks)

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — EBL Algorithm (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Draw the EBL block diagram (already shown in §4.1).** [2 marks]
The diagram has 7 functional blocks: Training Example → Knowledge Base → Proof Engine → Proof Tree → Variable-ization → Operationality Check → Output Rule.

**Step 2 — Write the algorithm as a numbered procedure.** [3 marks]
1. **Input:** Target Concept $C$, Training Example $E$, Domain Theory $T$, Operationality Criterion $O$.
2. **Construct Explanation:** Use $T$ to prove that $E$ is an instance of $C$. This yields an *explanation* (proof tree) $P$.
3. **Determine Sufficient Conditions:** Inspect the leaves of $P$. The set of leaf predicates forms the *sufficient condition* for the rule.
4. **Generalize the Explanation:** Replace constants in the leaf conditions with variables, producing a generalized proof $P'$.
5. **Operationalize:** Verify that each predicate in $P'$ satisfies the operationality criterion $O$ (e.g., directly executable sensors). If not, apply partial evaluation.
6. **Output:** Add the new Horn clause to the rule base.

**Step 3 — Discuss the *Why* and *How*.** [2 marks]
EBL works because the domain theory supplies the *inductive bias*. It works in a single example because the theory already encodes the *causal structure*. Its weakness is brittleness: an incorrect axiom yields an incorrect rule.

#### Part (b) — EBL Worked Example (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the example and target.** [1 mark]
- Example: $Clean(room_5)$
- Target concept: $Clean(x)$
- Domain theory $T$: the three clauses given.

**Step 2 — Build the proof tree.** [2 marks]

$$
Clean(room_5) \;\Leftarrow\; Dirt(room_5) \land In(room_5, r) \quad [T_3]
$$
$$
In(room_5, r) \;\Leftarrow\; In(room_5, r_0) \land Adjacent(r_0, r) \quad [T_1]
$$

**Step 3 — Generalize.** [2 marks]
Substitute variables $x, y, z$ for the constants $room_5, r, r_0$:

$$
Clean(x) \;\Leftarrow\; Dirt(x) \land In(x, y) \land In(x, z) \land Adjacent(z, y)
$$

Simplify using $Adjacent(z,y) \lor Adjacent(y,z) \rightarrow Reachable(z,y)$:

$$
\boxed{\, Clean(x) \;\Leftarrow\; Dirt(x) \land In(x, y) \land Reachable(z, y) \,}
$$

**Step 4 — Operationality check.** [1 mark]
All leaf predicates ($Dirt$, $In$, $Reachable$) correspond to **directly executable sensors** on the agent — rule is operational.

**Step 5 — Add to KB.** [1 mark]
The new clause becomes part of the agent's generalized knowledge, applicable to *any* room $x$.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Inductive Logic Programming (ILP)**. Define the *background knowledge*, *positive examples*, *negative examples*, and the *entailment constraint* $B \land H \models E^+$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Apply the **FOIL information-gain criterion** to decide which literal $L$ to add to the following partial rule for the target concept $GrandDaughter(x,y)$:

Background $B$:
- $Female(x) \land Parent(y,x) \rightarrow Daughter(x,y)$
- $Parent(x,y) \land Parent(y,z) \rightarrow Grandparent(x,z)$

Candidate literals: $L_1 = Female(x)$, $L_2 = Parent(y,x)$, $L_3 = Parent(x,y)$.

Assume: $p_0 = 8, n_0 = 4$ (before), and after $L_1$: $p_1 = 6, n_1 = 1$; after $L_2$: $p_1 = 5, n_1 = 2$; after $L_3$: $p_1 = 4, n_1 = 3$. Use $t = 6$ for all. (7 marks)

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — ILP Framework (7 marks)

**Definition [2 marks]:** ILP is the sub-field of machine learning that induces **first-order Horn clauses** as hypotheses, using a background knowledge base $B$ to constrain the hypothesis space. It unifies inductive learning with logical reasoning.

**Key Entities [4 marks]:**
- **Background knowledge $B$:** A set of first-order clauses encoding prior domain knowledge. Example: $\forall x,y \; Parent(x,y) \rightarrow Ancestor(x,y)$.
- **Positive examples $E^+$:** Ground facts that the hypothesis must entail. Example: $Daughter(ann, bob)$.
- **Negative examples $E^-$:** Ground facts the hypothesis must *not* entail. Example: $Daughter(carl, dan)$.
- **Hypothesis $H$:** A set of Horn clauses to be learned.

**Entailment Constraints [1 mark]:**
- **Sufficiency (necessity in positives):** $B \land H \models E^+$
- **Consistency (with negatives):** $B \land H \not\models E^-$

#### Part (b) — FOIL Gain Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recall the FOIL gain formula.** [1 mark]

$$
\text{Gain}(L, R) = t \cdot \left( \log_2 \frac{p_1}{p_1+n_1} - \log_2 \frac{p_0}{p_0+n_0} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the baseline (before) term.** [1 mark]

$$
\log_2 \frac{p_0}{p_0+n_0} = \log_2 \frac{8}{8+4} = \log_2 \frac{8}{12} = \log_2 0.6667 \approx -0.585
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the after-term for each candidate.** [3 marks, 1 each]

| Literal | $p_1$ | $n_1$ | $p_1/(p_1+n_1)$ | $\log_2$ ratio |
|---------|-------|-------|------------------|----------------|
| $L_1 = Female(x)$ | 6 | 1 | $6/7 = 0.857$ | $\log_2 0.857 \approx -0.222$ |
| $L_2 = Parent(y,x)$ | 5 | 2 | $5/7 = 0.714$ | $\log_2 0.714 \approx -0.485$ |
| $L_3 = Parent(x,y)$ | 4 | 3 | $4/7 = 0.571$ | $\log_2 0.571 \approx -0.807$ |

**Step 4 — Compute the gain for each.** [1.5 marks, 0.5 each]

$$
\text{Gain}(L_1) = 6 \cdot (-0.222 - (-0.585)) = 6 \cdot 0.363 \approx 2.18
$$

$$
\text{Gain}(L_2) = 6 \cdot (-0.485 - (-0.585)) = 6 \cdot 0.100 = 0.60
$$

$$
\text{Gain}(L_3) = 6 \cdot (-0.807 - (-0.585)) = 6 \cdot (-0.222) \approx -1.33
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.** [0.5 marks]
- **$L_1$ has the highest gain** ($\approx 2.18$). FOIL selects $L_1 = Female(x)$ as the next literal.
- $L_3$ has *negative* gain — it is rejected as it makes the rule *worse* (introduces more negatives than positives proportionally).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing EBL with Inductive Learning (–2 marks).** EBL is *deductive* and needs *one* example. Inductive is *bottom-up* and needs *many*.
> 2. **Forgetting the operationality check in EBL (–1 mark).** A rule whose tests are not directly executable is *not* added to the KB in the original EBL formulation.
> 3. **Sign error in FOIL gain (–2 marks).** The "before" term is *subtracted*; a positive gain means the literal *concentrates* positives. Watch the parentheses.
> 4. **Missing the union-bound step in PAC derivation (–2 marks).** Many students forget to apply the union bound over $H$.
> 5. **Drawing an architecture diagram without labels (–1 mark).** Every block in the Mermaid diagram must be labelled — unlabelled blocks are ignored.
> 6. **Writing $\vert H \vert$ as $|H|$ in the table (–0 mark loss, but breaks rendering).** Always use LaTeX $\vert$ inside markdown tables.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **📌 Important Things to Remember — Module 4: Knowledge in Learning**

- ✅ **Definition:** Knowledge in Learning = using *prior symbolic knowledge* + *data* to learn.
- ✅ **Three frameworks:** EBL (deductive, 1 example), RBL (relevance + induction), ILP/FOIL (general first-order induction).
- ✅ **EBL pipeline:** Example + Theory → Proof Tree → Variable-ize → Operationalize → Reusable Rule.
- ✅ **Operationality Criterion** is mandatory in EBL.
- ✅ **ILP Entailment:** $B \land H \models E^+$ **and** $B \land H \not\models E^-$.
- ✅ **FOIL Information Gain:**
  $$\text{Gain}(L,R) = t \cdot \left( \log_2 \frac{p_1}{p_1+n_1} - \log_2 \frac{p_0}{p_0+n_0} \right)$$
- ✅ **Bayes' Rule:** $P(h \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid h) P(h)}{P(d)}$ — the prior $P(h)$ is the vehicle of knowledge.
- ✅ **PAC Bound with prior:** $m \ge \dfrac{1}{\epsilon}\!\left( \ln \vert H \vert + \ln \dfrac{1}{\delta} \right)$ — prior shrinks $\vert H \vert$, hence $m$ drops.
- ✅ **Version Space** is bounded by $S$ (most specific) and $G$ (most general).
- ✅ **Knowledge categories:** Prior, Operational, Goal, Experiential.
- ✅ **Why it matters:** Sample efficiency, explainability, constraint satisfaction — all impossible with purely statistical learning.
- ✅ **Canonical exam example:** *SafeToStack* / *Clean(x)* — practice drawing the proof tree.
- ✅ **Mermaid diagrams** with labelled blocks fetch 2–3 bonus marks in 14-mark answers.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Knowledge in Learning** | *KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Compliant*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relevance Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relevance Information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Relevance Information** is the foundational concept in Information Retrieval (IR) and Agent-Based Intelligent Systems that quantifies and qualifies the *pertinence* between a user's information need (expressed as a query) and a document, item, or data source. In the context of intelligent agents, relevance information encompasses the dynamic, context-sensitive judgment about whether a piece of information is *useful*, *appropriate*, and *applicable* to a user's current task, situation, or long-term goals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCAT601 – Module 4):**
> Relevance Information is formally defined as the *degree of correspondence* between an information object (document, web page, sensor reading, agent message) and an information need (query, user goal, agent objective), evaluated across multiple dimensions: **topicality**, **cognition**, **situation**, and **pertinence**.

Formally, relevance can be expressed as a function:

$$
R: Q \times D \rightarrow [0, 1]
$$

where $Q$ is the query space, $D$ is the document space, and the output is a real-valued relevance score in the unit interval $[0, 1]$, with **0** representing *irrelevance* and **1** representing *maximum relevance*.

### 1.2 Types of Relevance (KTU-Highlighted)

| Relevance Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Topical/Subject Relevance** | Does the document discuss the *topic* of the query? | A query "neural networks" matching a document about backpropagation |
| **Cognitive Relevance** | Does the document match the user's *cognitive state* and knowledge level? | A beginner querying "neural networks" receiving introductory content |
| **Situational Relevance** | Is the document useful in the user's *current task/situation*? | A doctor querying "drug X" during a patient consultation |
| **Pertinence** | Does the document contribute to solving the user's *problem*? | A troubleshooting document matching a system error |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Library Assistant**
> Imagine you walk into a massive library and ask a smart assistant: *"I need a book about the French Revolution for my history exam tomorrow."* The assistant doesn't just search for the words "French Revolution." It considers:
> 1. **Topic** — Is it about the French Revolution? (Topical)
> 2. **Level** — Is it a beginner's guide or a PhD thesis? (Cognitive)
> 3. **Urgency** — You need it *today*, so it should be concise. (Situational)
> 4. **Goal** — Will it help you pass the exam? (Pertinence)
>
> **Relevance Information is the scoring system the assistant uses internally to rank and recommend books.** In agent-based systems, this "assistant" is a *software agent* that continuously evaluates, filters, and ranks incoming information streams.

### 1.4 Binary vs. Graded Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Binary Relevance:** A document is either relevant (1) or not relevant (0). Simple but lossy.
> - **Graded Relevance:** Documents receive multi-level relevance scores (e.g., 0, 1, 2, 3 for "Not Relevant", "Slightly Relevant", "Relevant", "Highly Relevant"). This preserves the *degree* of usefulness.
>
> KTU 2024 emphasizes **Graded Relevance** as the standard for modern agent-based IR systems.

---

### 1.5 Relevance in Agent-Based Systems — Core Insight

In **Agent-Based Intelligent Systems**, relevance information is not static — it is **dynamic, personalized, and context-aware**. Intelligent agents (e.g., information agents, recommender agents, monitoring agents) must continuously:

1. **Sense** the user's current context (location, task, time).
2. **Evaluate** incoming information against this context.
3. **Adapt** their relevance criteria over time (learning from feedback).
4. **Act** by filtering, prioritizing, or recommending information.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Relevance Assessment Framework

The process of determining relevance information in an agent-based system follows a structured pipeline:

#### Step 1: Query/Need Formulation
The user (or a delegating agent) expresses an information need. This may be:
- An explicit **keyword query** (e.g., `"machine learning algorithms"`)
- An implicit **contextual need** (e.g., *"I am at the airport and my flight is delayed"*)

#### Step 2: Document/Information Representation
Each document $d \in D$ is represented as a feature vector using techniques like:
- **TF-IDF** (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)
- **BM25** (Best Match 25)
- **Dense Embeddings** (Word2Vec, BERT, Sentence-Transformers)

#### Step 3: Relevance Scoring
A scoring function $f(q, d)$ computes the relevance between query $q$ and document $d$.

#### Step 4: Ranking and Filtering
Documents are sorted by relevance score. A threshold $\tau$ determines the cutoff.

#### Step 5: Feedback and Adaptation
The agent collects implicit or explicit feedback and updates its relevance model.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" and "How"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Step 1?** A poorly formulated query leads to irrelevant results. Agents use **query expansion** (adding synonyms, related terms) to improve recall.
>
> **Why Step 2?** Raw text cannot be directly compared. Vector representations enable mathematical similarity computation.
>
> **Why Step 3?** A scoring function provides a *quantitative* basis for ranking, removing subjective bias.
>
> **Why Step 4?** Users can only process a limited number of results. Ranking ensures the best appear first.
>
> **Why Step 5?** User needs evolve. Feedback allows the agent to *learn* and personalize relevance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Relevance Function** | $R: Q \times D \rightarrow [0,1]$ | Maps query-document pairs to a relevance score | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| 2 | **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Fraction of retrieved docs that are relevant | [0, 1] |
| 3 | **Recall** | $R_c = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of relevant docs that are retrieved | [0, 1] |
| 4 | **F-Measure (F1)** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R_c}{P + R_c}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall | [0, 1] |
| 5 | **F-β Measure** | $F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R_c}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R_c}$ | Weighted harmonic mean (β > 1 favors recall) | [0, 1] |
| 6 | **TF (Term Frequency)** | $tf(t,d) = f_{t,d}$ | Raw count of term $t$ in document $d$ | Integer $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | **IDF (Inverse Doc Freq)** | $idf(t) = \log\frac{N}{df(t)+1}$ | Importance of term $t$ across corpus | Real $\geq 0$ |
| 8 | **TF-IDF** | $w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ | Weighted term importance | Real $\geq 0$ |
| 9 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert\vec{q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{d}\vert}$ | Angle-based relevance between vectors | [-1, 1] |
| 10 | **BM25 Score** | $BM25(d,q) = \sum_{t \in q} idf(t) \cdot \frac{tf(t,d) \cdot (k_1+1)}{tf(t,d)+k_1 \cdot (1-b+b \cdot \frac{\vert d\vert}{avgdl})}$ | Probabilistic relevance ranking | Real $\geq 0$ |
| 11 | **Rocchio Relevance Feedback** | $\vec{q}_{new} = \alpha \vec{q}_{old} + \frac{\beta}{\vert D_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_r \in D_r}\vec{d}_r - \frac{\gamma}{\vert D_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_{nr} \in D_{nr}}\vec{d}_{nr}$ | Query vector update using feedback | Vector |
| 12 | **MAP (Mean Avg Precision)** | $MAP = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert}\sum_{q \in Q} AP(q)$ | Average precision across all queries | [0, 1] |
| 13 | **NDCG** | $NDCG@k = \frac{DCG@k}{IDCG@k}$ | Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain | [0, 1] |
| 14 | **DCG** | $DCG@k = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{rel_i}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | Graded relevance with position discount | Real $\geq 0$ |
| 15 | **Probability of Relevance** | $P(R=1 \vert d,q) = \frac{P(d \vert R=1) \cdot P(R=1)}{P(d)}$ | Bayesian relevance probability | [0, 1] |
| 16 | **Kendall's Tau** | $\tau = \frac{(\text{concordant} - \text{discordant})}{\binom{n}{2}}$ | Rank correlation between two orderings | [-1, 1] |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In the table above, $\vert \vec{v} \vert$ denotes the **Euclidean norm (magnitude)** of vector $\vec{v}$, and $avgdl$ is the **average document length** in the corpus. The BM25 parameters are typically $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$ and $b = 0.75$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Relevance Information |
|---|---|
| **Search Engines (Google, Bing)** | Ranking web pages by relevance to query |
| **Recommender Systems (Netflix, Spotify)** | Matching content to user preferences |
| **Medical Diagnosis Agents** | Filtering patient records relevant to symptoms |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Selecting relevant sensor data (LIDAR, camera) for current driving context |
| **Cybersecurity Agents** | Identifying relevant threat intelligence from massive log streams |
| **E-Commerce Agents** | Product recommendation based on browsing/purchase history |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The F-Measure from Precision and Recall

The **F-measure** (or F-score) is the harmonic mean of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R_c$). It is derived to provide a *single* metric that balances both concerns.

#### Step 1: Start with the Harmonic Mean Definition

The harmonic mean of two positive numbers $a$ and $b$ is defined as:

$$
H(a,b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}
$$

This is the standard definition of the harmonic mean.

#### Step 2: Substitute Precision and Recall

Let $a = P$ (Precision) and $b = R_c$ (Recall):

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R_c}}
$$

**Explanation:** We substitute the variables because the harmonic mean is symmetric and penalizes extreme values — if either $P$ or $R_c$ is very low, $F_1$ will also be low.

#### Step 3: Find a Common Denominator

The denominators are $P$ and $R_c$. To combine them, find a common denominator $P \cdot R_c$:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{R_c + P}{P \cdot R_c}}
$$

**Explanation:** We apply the rule $\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} = \frac{a+b}{ab}$ to unify the two fractions into one.

#### Step 4: Invert the Fraction

When a fraction is inverted, the numerator and denominator swap:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R_c}{P + R_c}
$$

**Explanation:** Dividing by $\frac{R_c + P}{P \cdot R_c}$ is equivalent to multiplying by $\frac{P \cdot R_c}{R_c + P}$.

#### Step 5: Generalize to F-β

To allow weighting of Precision vs. Recall, introduce a parameter $\beta$:

$$
F_\beta = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R_c}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R_c}
$$

**Explanation:** When $\beta = 1$, we get the balanced $F_1$. When $\beta > 1$, recall is weighted more heavily. When $\beta < 1$, precision is weighted more.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The TF-IDF Weighting Scheme

**TF-IDF** is one of the most fundamental relevance scoring mechanisms. It is derived in two stages.

#### Step 1: Define Term Frequency (TF)

The raw frequency of a term $t$ in document $d$:

$$
tf(t, d) = f_{t,d}
$$

where $f_{t,d}$ is the number of times term $t$ appears in document $d$.

**Explanation:** A term appearing more often in a document is likely more important to that document's topic.

#### Step 2: Define Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

We want to *downweight* terms that appear in *many* documents (like "the", "is", "a") because they are not discriminative:

$$
idf(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t) + 1}\right)
$$

**Explanation:** $N$ is the total number of documents in the corpus, $df(t)$ is the number of documents containing term $t$. The $+1$ is added to prevent division by zero (smoothing). The logarithm dampens the effect.

#### Step 3: Combine TF and IDF

Multiply them to get the TF-IDF weight:

$$
w(t, d) = tf(t, d) \cdot idf(t) = f_{t,d} \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t) + 1}\right)
$$

**Explanation:** A term gets a *high* weight if it appears frequently in a specific document (high TF) but rarely across the corpus (high IDF). Common words like "the" get low IDF, hence low overall weight.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Cosine Similarity for Relevance Scoring

Given query vector $\vec{q}$ and document vector $\vec{d}$, the relevance is measured by the cosine of the angle between them.

#### Step 1: Define the Dot Product

$$
\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \cdot d_i
$$

**Explanation:** This sums the products of corresponding components. High values indicate the vectors point in similar directions.

#### Step 2: Define Vector Magnitudes

$$
\vert\vec{q}\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i^2}, \quad \vert\vec{d}\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}
$$

**Explanation:** The Euclidean norm measures the length of the vector in $n$-dimensional space.

#### Step 3: Apply the Cosine Definition

By definition of cosine for two vectors:

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert\vec{q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{d}\vert}
$$

**Explanation:** Dividing by the product of magnitudes *normalizes* for document length, so that longer documents are not unfairly favored.

#### Step 4: Expanded Form

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \cdot d_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the final form used in practice. A value of **1** means the vectors are identical (perfectly relevant), **0** means orthogonal (irrelevant), and **-1** means opposite (negatively relevant).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Rocchio Relevance Feedback

**Rocchio's algorithm** modifies the original query vector based on user feedback to improve relevance.

#### Step 1: Start with the Original Query Vector

$$
\vec{q}_{old}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the user's initial query, represented as a vector in term space.

#### Step 2: Move Toward Relevant Documents

The agent identifies documents the user marked as relevant ($D_r$) and moves the query vector *toward* their centroid:

$$
\vec{q}_{old} + \frac{\beta}{\vert D_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_r \in D_r}\vec{d}_r
$$

**Explanation:** $\beta$ controls the influence of relevant documents. The centroid $\frac{1}{\vert D_r \vert}\sum \vec{d}_r$ represents the "average" relevant document.

#### Step 3: Move Away from Non-Relevant Documents

Similarly, move *away* from the centroid of non-relevant documents ($D_{nr}$):

$$
- \frac{\gamma}{\vert D_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_{nr} \in D_{nr}}\vec{d}_{nr}
$$

**Explanation:** $\gamma$ controls the penalty for non-relevant documents.

#### Step 4: Combine with Weighting

The original query is preserved with weight $\alpha$:

$$
\vec{q}_{new} = \alpha \cdot \vec{q}_{old} + \frac{\beta}{\vert D_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_r \in D_r}\vec{d}_r - \frac{\gamma}{\vert D_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_{nr} \in D_{nr}}\vec{d}_{nr}
$$

**Explanation:** Typical values are $\alpha = 1.0$, $\beta = 0.75$, $\gamma = 0.15$ in classical implementations.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A search agent retrieves **100** documents. Of these, **40** are relevant. The corpus contains **200** total relevant documents. Calculate Precision, Recall, and F1-Score.

**Given:**

- Retrieved documents = 100
- Relevant documents in retrieved set ($TP$) = 40
- Total relevant documents in corpus = 200

**Step 1:** Non-relevant documents in retrieved set ($FP$) = $100 - 40 = 60$.

**Step 2:** Relevant documents *not* retrieved ($FN$) = $200 - 40 = 160$.

**Step 3: Precision**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{40}{40 + 60} = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40
$$

**Step 4: Recall**

$$
R_c = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{40}{40 + 160} = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20
$$

**Step 5: F1-Score**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R_c}{P + R_c} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.40 \cdot 0.20}{0.40 + 0.20} = \frac{0.16}{0.60} = 0.267
$$

**Interpretation:** The agent has moderate precision (40%) but low recall (20%), meaning it misses many relevant documents. The F1 score of **0.267** confirms the system is *poorly balanced*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: Types of Relevance Information

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Relevance Information]
    R --> T[Topical Relevance]
    R --> C[Cognitive Relevance]
    R --> S[Situational Relevance]
    R --> P[Pertinence]
    T --> T1[Subject matter match]
    T --> T2[Keyword overlap]
    C --> C1[User knowledge level]
    C --> C2[Background context]
    S --> S1[Current task match]
    S --> S2[Time and location]
    P --> P1[Problem solving utility]
    P --> P2[Goal alignment]
```

### 4.2 Diagram: Relevance Assessment Pipeline in an Agent-Based System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[User Need or Query] --> B[Query Processing Agent]
    B --> C[Document Representation Module]
    C --> D[Relevance Scoring Engine]
    D --> E{Relevance Threshold Check}
    E -->|Score above threshold| F[Ranked Results]
    E -->|Score below threshold| G[Filtered Out]
    F --> H[User Feedback Collection]
    H --> I[Learning and Adaptation Module]
    I --> D
```

### 4.3 Diagram: Relevance Feedback Loop (Rocchio Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q0[Initial Query Vector Q0] --> A[Agent Retrieves Documents]
    A --> B[User Marks Relevant and Non Relevant]
    B --> C[Compute Centroids of Relevant and Non Relevant Sets]
    C --> D[Update Query Vector using Rocchio Formula]
    D --> Q1[New Query Vector Q1]
    Q1 --> A
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Multi-Dimensional Relevance Evaluation Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph EvaluationDimensions
        D1[Topicality Score]
        D2[Cognitive Match Score]
        D3[Situational Fit Score]
        D4[Pertinence Score]
    end
    D1 --> Comb[Weighted Combination Function]
    D2 --> Comb
    D3 --> Comb
    D4 --> Comb
    Comb --> Final[Final Relevance Score R]
    Final --> Decision{R above or below threshold}
    Decision -->|Above| Accept[Accept as Relevant]
    Decision -->|Below| Reject[Reject as Irrelevant]
```

### 4.5 Diagram: Agent Architecture for Context-Aware Relevance

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph ContextAwareAgent
        CS[Context Sensor Module]
        KB[Knowledge Base]
        RE[Relevance Evaluator]
        RM[Relevance Model]
        RA[Response and Action Module]
    end
    CS --> RE
    KB --> RE
    RE --> RM
    RM --> RA
    RA --> UserOutput[Output to User]
    UserInput[User Input and Feedback] --> CS
    UserOutput --> UserInput
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Relevance Information** in the context of Agent-Based Intelligent Systems. Differentiate between *topical relevance* and *situational relevance* with an example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Relevance Information is the assessment of how well a piece of information (document, data, or message) matches a user's information need, evaluated across dimensions such as topicality, cognition, situation, and pertinence. In agent-based systems, it is the basis for an intelligent agent's decision to filter, prioritize, or recommend information.

**Differentiation (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | Topical Relevance | Situational Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Subject matter of the content | Context of the user's current task |
| Example | A document about "neural networks" matches the query "machine learning" | The same document is irrelevant if the user is debugging a printer error |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
List and briefly explain any **three types of relevance** used in information retrieval systems.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Topical Relevance (1 Mark):** Measures whether the document's subject matter matches the query topic. Example: Query "climate change" matches a document about global warming.

2. **Cognitive Relevance (1 Mark):** Measures whether the document's complexity and presentation match the user's knowledge level and cognitive state. Example: An advanced research paper may be topically relevant but cognitively irrelevant to a school student.

3. **Situational Relevance (1 Mark):** Measures whether the document is useful given the user's current task, time, and environment. Example: A recipe for a 2-hour dish is situationally irrelevant when the user has only 30 minutes to cook.

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Relevance Information** in agent-based intelligent systems. Discuss in detail the different **types of relevance** (topical, cognitive, situational, and pertinence) with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Precision, Recall, and F-Measure** metrics from first principles. Given a scenario where an intelligent search agent retrieves 150 documents, 60 of which are relevant, and the total number of relevant documents in the corpus is 200, compute all three metrics and interpret the results. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Relevance Information in agent-based intelligent systems refers to the dynamic, multi-dimensional assessment of how pertinent a piece of information is to a user's need, context, and goals. It is the core mechanism by which intelligent agents (search agents, recommender agents, monitoring agents) filter, rank, and deliver information. Formally, it is a function $R: Q \times D \rightarrow [0,1]$.

**Types of Relevance (5 Marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Topical Relevance:** This is the most basic form. It checks if the document's subject matter overlaps with the query's topic. It is often computed using term-matching techniques like TF-IDF or BM25. *Example:* A query "diabetes symptoms" retrieves a medical article listing symptoms — strong topical match.

2. **Cognitive Relevance:** This goes beyond topic matching to consider the user's *mental model* and *knowledge level*. An intelligent tutoring agent must avoid giving PhD-level papers to an undergraduate. *Example:* A student querying "calculus basics" should receive introductory content, not advanced measure theory.

3. **Situational Relevance:** This evaluates relevance in the context of the user's *current situation* — their task, time constraints, location, and device. *Example:* A delivery agent querying "route to Kochi" during rush hour needs traffic-aware routes, not just the shortest path.

4. **Pertinence:** This is the *goal-oriented* dimension of relevance. It asks: "Does this information help the user *solve their problem*?" *Example:* A user searching for "how to fix a leaking tap" needs step-by-step instructions, not a history of plumbing.

**Conclusion (included in 2 marks for definition):** In modern agent-based systems, relevance is not a single value but a *composite score* computed across these four dimensions, often weighted by user preferences and context.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation of Precision (2 Marks):**

Precision measures the accuracy of the retrieval — what fraction of the retrieved documents are actually relevant. It is defined as:

$$
P = \frac{\text{Number of Relevant Documents Retrieved (TP)}}{\text{Total Number of Documents Retrieved (TP + FP)}}
$$

where $TP$ = True Positives, $FP$ = False Positives.

**Derivation of Recall (2 Marks):**

Recall measures the completeness of retrieval — what fraction of all relevant documents in the corpus were found:

$$
R_c = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

where $FN$ = False Negatives (relevant documents not retrieved).

**Derivation of F-Measure (1.5 Marks):**

The F-Measure is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a single balanced metric:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R_c}{P + R_c}
$$

**Numerical Computation (1.5 Marks):**

Given: $TP = 60$, Total Retrieved = 150, Total Relevant in Corpus = 200.

- $FP = 150 - 60 = 90$
- $FN = 200 - 60 = 140$

**Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{60}{60 + 90} = \frac{60}{150} = 0.40
$$

**Recall:**

$$
R_c = \frac{60}{60 + 140} = \frac{60}{200} = 0.30
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.40 \times 0.30}{0.40 + 0.30} = \frac{0.24}{0.70} = 0.343
$$

**Interpretation:** The agent achieves 40% precision (6 out of 15 retrieved docs are relevant) and 30% recall (it finds only 60 out of 200 relevant docs). The F1 score of 0.343 indicates a poorly balanced system that needs improvement — likely by expanding the query or lowering the relevance threshold.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **TF-IDF weighting scheme** and **Cosine Similarity** for computing relevance between a query and a document. Derive both mathematically. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Rocchio Relevance Feedback algorithm**. Explain how an intelligent agent uses positive and negative feedback to refine its search results. Derive the Rocchio update formula. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**TF-IDF Derivation (4 Marks):**

**Step 1: Term Frequency (1 Mark)**
The term frequency $tf(t,d)$ is the raw count of how many times term $t$ appears in document $d$:

$$
tf(t,d) = f_{t,d}
$$

**Step 2: Inverse Document Frequency (1.5 Marks)**
Common terms (like "the", "a") appear in many documents and are not discriminative. We penalize them:

$$
idf(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t) + 1}\right)
$$

where $N$ is the total number of documents and $df(t)$ is the number of documents containing term $t$.

**Step 3: TF-IDF Combination (1.5 Marks)**
Multiplying TF and IDF gives the final weight:

$$
w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t) + 1}\right)
$$

A term gets a high weight if it appears frequently in a *specific* document but rarely across the corpus.

**Cosine Similarity Derivation (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the dot product of query vector $\vec{q}$ and document vector $\vec{d}$:

$$
\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \cdot d_i
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Define the Euclidean norms:

$$
\vert\vec{q}\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i^2}, \quad \vert\vec{d}\vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** The cosine similarity is the normalized dot product:

$$
\text{sim}(q,d) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert\vec{q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{d}\vert}
$$

This yields a value in $[-1, 1]$, where **1** = perfectly relevant, **0** = orthogonal (irrelevant).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Conceptual Explanation (2 Marks):**
Rocchio relevance feedback is a *query reformulation* technique used by intelligent agents to improve retrieval results. After the initial retrieval, the user marks some documents as relevant ($D_r$) and others as non-relevant ($D_{nr}$). The agent then adjusts the query vector to move *toward* the centroid of relevant documents and *away* from the centroid of non-relevant documents.

**Derivation of Rocchio Formula (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Start with the original query vector $\vec{q}_{old}$.

**Step 2:** Add the contribution of relevant documents (weighted by $\beta$):

$$
\vec{q}_{old} + \frac{\beta}{\vert D_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_r \in D_r}\vec{d}_r
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the contribution of non-relevant documents (weighted by $\gamma$):

$$
- \frac{\gamma}{\vert D_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_{nr} \in D_{nr}}\vec{d}_{nr}
$$

**Step 4:** The complete Rocchio formula with all weights:

$$
\vec{q}_{new} = \alpha \cdot \vec{q}_{old} + \frac{\beta}{\vert D_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_r \in D_r}\vec{d}_r - \frac{\gamma}{\vert D_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_{nr} \in D_{nr}}\vec{d}_{nr}
$$

Typical values: $\alpha = 1.0$, $\beta = 0.75$, $\gamma = 0.15$.

**Agent Workflow Explanation (2 Marks):**
1. Agent retrieves initial results based on $\vec{q}_{old}$.
2. User provides feedback (marks relevant/non-relevant).
3. Agent computes the centroids of both sets.
4. Agent updates the query vector using the Rocchio formula to get $\vec{q}_{new}$.
5. Agent re-retrieves documents using $\vec{q}_{new}$, yielding improved relevance.
6. This loop can iterate until the user is satisfied.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Recall:** Students often swap the formulas. Remember: **Precision** = "Of what I *retrieved*, how much is relevant?" **Recall** = "Of what *exists*, how much did I retrieve?"
> 2. **Forgetting the $+1$ in IDF:** The smoothing term $+1$ in the denominator is critical to avoid division by zero. Omitting it costs marks.
> 3. **Not normalizing in Cosine Similarity:** Students sometimes forget to divide by $\vert\vec{q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{d}\vert$, which means they are computing the raw dot product, not cosine similarity.
> 4. **Misinterpreting F1:** A high F1 requires *both* P and R to be high. If one is 0, F1 is 0, regardless of the other.
> 5. **Rocchio: Forgetting negative feedback term:** The $-\gamma$ term (moving away from non-relevant docs) is as important as the $+\beta$ term. Omitting it is a common error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Relevance Information (Module 4)**

- **Relevance** is a *multi-dimensional*, *context-dependent*, and *dynamic* concept — not a single boolean value.
- **Four key types:** Topical, Cognitive, Situational, Pertinence. Know the distinction and example for each.
- **Binary vs. Graded Relevance:** Modern systems use graded (multi-level) relevance for finer ranking.
- **TF-IDF** = Term Frequency × Inverse Document Frequency. It balances local importance (TF) with global discriminativeness (IDF).
- **Cosine Similarity** is the standard geometric measure of relevance between query and document vectors. Always *normalize* by dividing by the product of norms.
- **BM25** is the industry-standard probabilistic relevance scoring function, extending TF-IDF with document length normalization and term saturation.
- **Precision** answers: *"How accurate is my retrieval?"* | **Recall** answers: *"How complete is my retrieval?"*
- **F-Measure** = Harmonic mean of P and R. The harmonic mean *penalizes* imbalance. Use $F_\beta$ to weight precision vs. recall.
- **MAP (Mean Average Precision)** is a ranking-quality metric that rewards placing relevant documents at *higher ranks*.
- **NDCG (Normalized DCG)** uses *graded* relevance and *position discount* — essential for modern ranked retrieval evaluation.
- **Rocchio Relevance Feedback** modifies the query vector using user feedback. The formula is: $\vec{q}_{new} = \alpha \vec{q}_{old} + \beta \cdot \text{centroid}(D_r) - \gamma \cdot \text{centroid}(D_{nr})$.
- **In agent-based systems**, relevance is *personalized* and *adaptive* — agents use context, user profiles, and feedback loops to refine relevance continuously.
- **Probabilistic Relevance:** The Probability Ranking Principle (PRP) states that documents should be ranked by $P(\text{relevant} \vert \text{document}, \text{query})$.
- **Key evaluation distinction:** Use **Precision@K** for top-K quality, **Recall** for completeness, **F1** for balance, **MAP** for ranking quality, and **NDCG** for graded relevance scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Learning Methods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Statistical Learning Methods

# 📘 Statistical Learning Methods — Module 4

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Learning Methods (SLM)** are a family of computational and mathematical frameworks that enable an intelligent agent to **infer underlying probabilistic structure from observed data**, **generalize patterns to unseen inputs**, and **make optimal decisions under uncertainty** by leveraging probability theory, statistical inference, and information-theoretic principles.

In the context of **Agent-Based Intelligent Systems**, statistical learning provides the **rational backbone** for an agent to:
1. **Perceive** uncertain sensory data (e.g., noisy camera frames, probabilistic sensor readings).
2. **Reason** about hidden states of the world (e.g., opponent's strategy, traffic density).
3. **Act** optimally by minimizing a statistical loss function (e.g., expected regret, squared error).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Syllabus Terminology (KTU 2024 — PCCAT601):**
> Statistical learning bridges **symbolic AI** (logic) and **connectionist AI** (neural nets) by operating in a **probabilistic framework**. The key sub-topics covered in Module 4 are:
> - **Supervised Learning** (Regression & Classification)
> - **Unsupervised Learning** (Clustering & Density Estimation)
> - **Bayesian Learning & Inference**
> - **Expectation-Maximization (EM) Algorithm**
> - **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)**
> - **Instance-Based Learning (k-NN)**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Forecaster Agent" 🌍

Imagine an **intelligent weather-monitoring agent** deployed in Kochi. The agent receives:
- 30 days of historical temperature, humidity, and pressure data.
- Real-time sensor readings, each corrupted by **measurement noise**.

| Without Statistical Learning | With Statistical Learning |
|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Agent memorizes a lookup table; fails on new (unseen) values. | Agent estimates a **probability distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ over temperatures. |
| Predictions are brittle and overconfident. | Predictions come with **confidence intervals**. |
| Cannot handle missing sensor data. | **Marginalization** over missing variables gives robust estimates. |

> **The intuition:** Statistical learning is the **mathematical equivalent of "learning from experience with humility"** — the agent does not pretend to know everything; it expresses beliefs as **probability distributions** and updates those beliefs as new evidence arrives (this is exactly **Bayesian updating**).

---

### 1.3 Key Statistical Quantities & Metrics

- **Bias** $\mu$: systematic deviation of the estimator from the true value. Bold-emphasized standard: **Bias = 0 is the hallmark of an *unbiased estimator*.**
- **Variance** $\sigma^2$: sensitivity of the estimator to fluctuations in the training set.
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**: a foundational concept where reducing one typically increases the other.
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**: $E[(Y - \hat{f}(X))^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$.
- **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)**: the parameter value $\hat{\theta}$ that maximizes the likelihood of observing the given data.
- **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)**: the parameter value that maximizes the posterior probability $P(\theta \mid D)$.

---

### 1.4 Statistical Learning vs. Other Learning Paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Table — How Statistical Learning Differs from Other Agent Learning Methods**

| Learning Paradigm | Core Mechanism | Handles Uncertainty? | Requires Probability Model? |
|-------------------|----------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Statistical Learning** | Probabilistic inference | ✅ Yes (native) | ✅ Yes |
| Symbolic / Rule-Based | IF-THEN logic | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Neural / Deep Learning | Gradient backpropagation | ⚠️ Indirect (via softmax) | ⚠️ Implicit |
| Reinforcement Learning | Reward maximization | ⚠️ Stochastic policies | ❌ No |
| Evolutionary / Genetic | Population-based search | ❌ No | ❌ No |

The unique strength of **statistical learning** is its **explicit, mathematically tractable representation of uncertainty** — a property critical for agents operating in noisy, partially observable environments (PO-MDPs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Statistical Learning Pipeline

The standard KTU pipeline for any statistical learning task within an agent architecture is:

1. **Data Collection:** The agent gathers a training set $D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_N, y_N)\}$ where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a feature vector and $y_i$ is the label (supervised) or absent (unsupervised).
2. **Model Selection:** Choose a hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$ (e.g., linear, Gaussian, multinomial).
3. **Parameter Estimation:** Find $\hat{\theta}$ using **MLE**, **MAP**, or **Bayesian posterior inference**.
4. **Loss Formulation:** Define a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ (e.g., 0-1 loss, squared error, log-loss).
5. **Generalization Guarantee:** Apply bounds like PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) or VC-dimension analysis.
6. **Deployment:** The agent uses $\hat{f}$ to predict $\hat{y}_{\text{new}}$ for new inputs.

> **Why each step matters to an agent:**
> - Step 3 lets the agent **quantify uncertainty** about world dynamics.
> - Step 5 ensures the agent's **belief** is not overfit to past experience.
> - Step 6 is where the agent **acts** under uncertainty using **expected utility**.

---

### 2.2 Supervised Learning — Regression

The agent learns a function $f: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}$.

#### 2.2.1 Linear Regression
Assumes $y = w^\top x + \epsilon$ where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.

The **closed-form solution** (Normal Equation) is:
$$w^{*} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y$$

This is the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** estimator — derived in Section 3.

#### 2.2.2 Logistic Regression
For binary classification, $y \in \{0, 1\}$:
$$P(y=1 \mid x; w) = \sigma(w^\top x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-w^\top x}}$$

The model is trained by **maximizing the log-likelihood**:
$$\ell(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \sigma(w^\top x_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \sigma(w^\top x_i)) \right]$$

---

### 2.3 Supervised Learning — Classification

#### 2.3.1 Naive Bayes Classifier
Applies **Bayes' Theorem** with a strong (naive) **conditional independence assumption**:
$$P(C_k \mid x) = \frac{P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid C_k)}{P(x)}$$

> **Why it matters for agents:** Naive Bayes is **incredibly fast** and works well for high-dimensional sparse data (e.g., text-based agent commands, sensor feature bags).

#### 2.3.2 k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)
A non-parametric, instance-based learner. Prediction is the **majority vote (or average)** of the $k$ closest training samples, where closeness is measured by Euclidean distance:
$$d(x, x_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - x_{i,j})^2}$$

---

### 2.4 Unsupervised Learning

#### 2.4.1 k-Means Clustering
Partitions data into $k$ clusters by minimizing **within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS)**:
$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

#### 2.4.2 Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs)
A probabilistic clustering method. The data is assumed to be drawn from a mixture of $K$ Gaussians:
$$p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

where $\pi_k$ are **mixing weights** satisfying $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$.

---

### 2.5 Expectation-Maximization (EM) Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EM** is used when data has **latent (hidden) variables** $Z$ that are never directly observed. The agent only sees $X$ and must infer $Z$ along with parameters $\theta$.

The EM algorithm **alternates between two steps**:
- **E-step (Expectation):** Compute the posterior over latent variables given current parameters:
$$Q(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)}) = E_{Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}}[\log p(X, Z \mid \theta)]$$
- **M-step (Maximization):** Update parameters to maximize the expected log-likelihood:
$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \arg\max_{\theta} Q(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)})$$

**Convergence guarantee:** EM is **monotonically non-decreasing** in the log-likelihood $\log p(X \mid \theta)$ — it never gets worse, but may converge to a **local optimum**.

---

### 2.6 Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)

An HMM is a **doubly stochastic process** used to model sequential/temporal data. The agent observes emissions $O_t$ but the underlying state $S_t$ is hidden.

| Component | Symbol | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| **State space** | $S = \{s_1, \dots, s_N\}$ | Hidden states (e.g., traffic: light, medium, heavy) |
| **Observation alphabet** | $V = \{v_1, \dots, v_M\}$ | Observable emissions |
| **Initial distribution** | $\pi_i = P(S_1 = s_i)$ | Starting belief |
| **Transition matrix** | $A = [a_{ij}]$ | $P(S_t = s_j \mid S_{t-1} = s_i)$ |
| **Emission matrix** | $B = [b_{ij}]$ | $P(O_t = v_j \mid S_t = s_i)$ |

The **three canonical HMM problems** are:
1. **Evaluation** (forward algorithm) — compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
2. **Decoding** (Viterbi algorithm) — find the most likely state sequence.
3. **Learning** (Baum-Welch algorithm) — estimate $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ from data.

> **Why it matters for agents:** HMMs are used in **speech-recognition agents**, **gesture-recognition agents**, and **financial-trading agents** that must infer hidden intent from observable actions.

---

### 2.7 Bayesian Learning

The cornerstone formula — **Bayes' Theorem**:
$$P(\theta \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)}{P(D)}$$

| Term | Name | Role in Agent |
|------|------|---------------|
| $P(\theta)$ | Prior | Agent's belief *before* seeing data |
| $P(D \mid \theta)$ | Likelihood | How probable the data is under the model |
| $P(\theta \mid D)$ | Posterior | Agent's updated belief *after* seeing data |
| $P(D)$ | Evidence | Normalizing constant (model comparison) |

---

### 2.8 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table summarizes every formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE for this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Foundation of all Bayesian learning |
| 2 | MLE (general) | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_i p(x_i \mid \theta)$ | Equivalent to maximizing $\sum_i \log p(x_i \mid \theta)$ |
| 3 | MAP | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \, p(\theta \mid D) = \arg\max_{\theta} \, p(D \mid \theta) p(\theta)$ | MLE when prior is uniform |
| 4 | OLS Solution | $w^{*} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y$ | Requires $X^\top X$ to be invertible |
| 5 | Logistic (Sigmoid) | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Output ∈ (0, 1) — a probability |
| 6 | Log-Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_i [y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{y}_i)]$ | Used in logistic regression |
| 7 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Used in linear regression |
| 8 | k-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert^2$ | Minimized iteratively |
| 9 | GMM Density | $p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$ | $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$ |
| 10 | EM E-step | $\gamma(z_{ik}) = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_j \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_i \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$ | Responsibility of component $k$ for $x_i$ |
| 11 | EM M-step | $\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_i \gamma(z_{ik}) x_i}{\sum_i \gamma(z_{ik})}$ | Re-estimate means |
| 12 | HMM Joint Prob. | $P(O, S \mid \lambda) = \pi_{s_1} \prod_{t=2}^{T} a_{s_{t-1}, s_t} \prod_{t=1}^{T} b_{s_t}(o_t)$ | Forward algorithm recurses this |
| 13 | Naive Bayes | $P(C \mid x) \propto P(C) \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid C)$ | Independence assumption |
| 14 | Bias-Variance | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | $\sigma^2$ = irreducible noise |
| 15 | Gaussian PDF | $\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Most common statistical model |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Derivations carry **high weightage** (often 7 marks in a 14-mark question). The three derivations below are the most frequently asked in past KTU university exams.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE) for a Gaussian Distribution

> **Problem Statement:** Let $D = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$ be i.i.d. samples drawn from a univariate Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Derive the MLEs $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}}$.

**Step 1 — Write the likelihood.**
The joint likelihood of the i.i.d. data is the product of individual densities:
$$L(\mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural log (log-likelihood).**
Since the logarithm is monotonically increasing, maximizing $\log L$ is equivalent to maximizing $L$:
$$\log L(\mu, \sigma^2) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \right) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the constants.**
$$\log L(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi) - N \log(\sigma) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and set to zero.**
$$\frac{\partial \log L}{\partial \mu} = - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2(x_i - \mu)(-1) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\hat{\mu}$.**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - N\mu = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

> ✅ **Result 1:** The MLE of the mean is the **sample mean** $\bar{x}$.

**Step 6 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\sigma^2$ and set to zero.**
$$\frac{\partial \log L}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2 = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $\hat{\sigma}^2$.**
$$\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2} \sum (x_i - \mu)^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{\mu})^2$$

> ✅ **Result 2:** The MLE of the variance is the **average squared deviation from the sample mean**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important subtlety:** $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}}$ is a **biased** estimator. The unbiased version divides by $(N-1)$ instead of $N$ (Bessel's correction). KTU frequently tests this distinction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Closed-Form Solution

> **Problem Statement:** Given $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ (data matrix) and $y \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ (target vector), derive $w^{*}$ that minimizes the sum of squared residuals $\Vert y - Xw \Vert^2$.

**Step 1 — Formulate the loss function.**
$$J(w) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - w^\top x_i)^2 = \frac{1}{2} (y - Xw)^\top (y - Xw)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the squared norm.**
$$J(w) = \frac{1}{2} \left( y^\top y - 2 w^\top X^\top y + w^\top X^\top X w \right)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $w$ (using matrix calculus).**
$$\frac{\partial J(w)}{\partial w} = -X^\top y + X^\top X w$$

**Step 4 — Set the gradient to zero (first-order optimality condition).**
$$X^\top X w - X^\top y = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X^\top X w = X^\top y$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $w$ (assuming $X^\top X$ is invertible).**
$$w^{*} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y$$

> ✅ **Result:** This is the **Normal Equation** — the closed-form OLS solution.

**Step 6 — Geometric Interpretation (KTU favorite).**
$w^{*}$ is the **orthogonal projection** of $y$ onto the **column space of $X$**. Equivalently, the residual vector $y - Xw^{*}$ is **orthogonal** to every column of $X$, i.e., $X^\top(y - Xw^{*}) = 0$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Posterior Update for a Gaussian Likelihood with Gaussian Prior (Conjugate Bayesian Update)

> **Problem Statement:** A sensor agent receives observations $D = \{x_1, \dots, x_N\}$ from a sensor with unknown bias $\mu$. Assume:
> - **Prior:** $\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)$
> - **Likelihood:** $x_i \mid \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ (with known $\sigma^2$)
> - Derive the posterior $P(\mu \mid D)$.

**Step 1 — Write the joint distribution up to a constant.**
$$P(\mu \mid D) \propto P(D \mid \mu) P(\mu)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Gaussian densities.**
$$P(\mu \mid D) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2 \right) \cdot \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma_0^2} (\mu - \mu_0)^2 \right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the log and combine the quadratic terms in $\mu$.**
$$\log P(\mu \mid D) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma_0^2} (\mu - \mu_0)^2 + C$$

Expanding the sum:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2 = \sum_i x_i^2 - 2\mu \sum_i x_i + N\mu^2$$

**Step 4 — Collect the $\mu^2$ and $\mu$ coefficients.**
$$\log P(\mu \mid D) = -\frac{1}{2} \left[ \mu^2 \left( \frac{N}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} \right) - 2\mu \left( \frac{\sum_i x_i}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} \right) \right] + C$$

**Step 5 — Identify the posterior as a Gaussian with the new precision and mean.**
The posterior is $\mathcal{N}(\mu_N, \sigma_N^2)$ where:
$$\frac{1}{\sigma_N^2} = \frac{N}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} \quad \text{(precision additivity)}$$
$$\mu_N = \sigma_N^2 \left( \frac{\sum_i x_i}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form.**
$$\mu_N = \frac{\sigma^2 \mu_0 + N \sigma_0^2 \bar{x}}{N \sigma_0^2 + \sigma^2}, \qquad \sigma_N^2 = \frac{\sigma^2 \sigma_0^2}{N \sigma_0^2 + \sigma^2}$$

> ✅ **Interpretation:** The posterior mean $\mu_N$ is a **precision-weighted average** of the prior mean $\mu_0$ and the sample mean $\bar{x}$. As $N \to \infty$, the prior is overwhelmed and $\mu_N \to \bar{x}$. This is the mathematical embodiment of **learning from data**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — k-NN Classification (For KTU Numerical Practice)

> **Problem:** Given 2D points from two classes, classify a new query point $q = (2, 3)$ using $k = 3$ and Euclidean distance.

| Point | Coordinates | Class |
|-------|-------------|-------|
| $P_1$ | (1, 2) | A |
| $P_2$ | (2, 3) | B |
| $P_3$ | (3, 4) | A |
| $P_4$ | (1, 0) | B |
| $P_5$ | (4, 4) | A |

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distances from $q$ to each $P_i$.**
$$d(q, P_1) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (3-2)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$
$$d(q, P_2) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (3-3)^2} = 0$$
$$d(q, P_3) = \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$
$$d(q, P_4) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (3-0)^2} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$$
$$d(q, P_5) = \sqrt{(2-4)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$$

**Step 2 — Identify the $k=3$ nearest neighbors.**
The 3 closest points are: $P_2$ (class B), $P_1$ (class A), $P_3$ (class A).

**Step 3 — Majority vote.**
Class A: 2 votes, Class B: 1 vote → **$q$ is classified as Class A**.

> **Important:** Ties are broken by $k$ being odd; if $k$ is even, distance-weighted voting is used.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Statistical Learner

| Criterion | MLE | MAP | Bayesian (Full Posterior) | k-NN | Naive Bayes |
|-----------|-----|-----|--------------------------|------|-------------|
| Uses prior? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ (class prior) |
| Returns point estimate? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (distribution) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Computational cost | Low | Low | High | High at test | Very low |
| Robust to noise? | ⚠️ Medium | ⚠️ Medium | ✅ High | ⚠️ Sensitive to $k$ | ✅ High |
| Handles small data? | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good | ✅ Good (with prior) | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good |
| Interpretability | ✅ High | ✅ High | ⚠️ Low | ⚠️ Low | ✅ High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Statistical Learning Pipeline in an Intelligent Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENVIRONMENT] --> B[SENSOR INPUT X]
    B --> C[FEATURE EXTRACTION]
    C --> D[TRAINING DATA D]
    D --> E[STATISTICAL LEARNER]
    E --> F[PARAMETER ESTIMATION<br/>MLE / MAP / EM]
    F --> G[MODEL lambda or w]
    G --> H[INFERENCE ENGINE]
    B --> H
    H --> I[POSTERIOR / PREDICTION]
    I --> J[DECISION MODULE]
    J --> K[ACTION SELECTION]
    K --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** The agent loops through **Sense → Learn → Infer → Act → Observe**. The statistical learner is the **parameter estimation block**, while the inference engine produces **predictions or posteriors** used by the decision module.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Bayesian Updating Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PRIOR BELIEF P theta] --> U[OBSERVE NEW DATA D]
    U --> UPD[BAYES UPDATE]
    P1 --> UPD
    UPD --> P2[POSTERIOR P theta D]
    P2 --> P1
```

> **Key insight:** The posterior of one cycle **becomes the prior** of the next — this is **sequential Bayesian learning**, and it is exactly how a recursive Bayesian filter (e.g., Kalman filter, particle filter) works inside a tracking agent.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — EM Algorithm Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[INITIALIZE theta_0] --> ESTEP
    ESTEP[E STEP<br/>Compute gamma Zik<br/>Responsibilities] --> MSTEP
    MSTEP[M STEP<br/>Re-estimate mu sigma pi] --> CHECK{Check Convergence<br/>log L increase below threshold}
    CHECK -->|No| ESTEP
    CHECK -->|Yes| OUT[FINAL PARAMETERS theta star]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The EM algorithm **never decreases** the log-likelihood. It always converges, but possibly to a **local maximum**.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — HMM Three Canonical Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[HMM Model lambda equals A B pi] --> P1[PROBLEM 1 EVALUATION<br/>Compute P of O given lambda<br/>Use FORWARD ALGORITHM]
    H --> P2[PROBLEM 2 DECODING<br/>Find best state sequence<br/>Use VITERBI ALGORITHM]
    H --> P3[PROBLEM 3 LEARNING<br/>Estimate lambda from data<br/>Use BAUM WELCH EM]
    P3 -.updates.-> H
```

---

### 4.5 Functional Architecture — Supervised vs. Unsupervised vs. Reinforcement Learning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[LEARNING PARADIGM] --> SL[SUPERVISED LEARNING]
    L --> USL[UNSUPERVISED LEARNING]
    L --> RL[REINFORCEMENT LEARNING]

    SL --> SL1[Classification: Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression]
    SL --> SL2[Regression: Linear, Ridge]
    SL --> SL3[Instance-Based: k-NN]

    USL --> USL1[Clustering: k-Means, GMM]
    USL --> USL2[Dimensionality Reduction: PCA]
    USL --> USL3[Density Estimation: KDE, EM]

    RL --> RL1[Policy Iteration]
    RL --> RL2[Q-Learning]
    RL --> RL3[Statistical SL methods as function approximator]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Statistical learning methods primarily sit in the **Supervised** and **Unsupervised** boxes, but **RL agents** also use them (e.g., Gaussian Processes for Bayesian optimization, GMMs for option discovery).

---

### 4.6 Bias-Variance Tradeoff Visualization (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BIAS[Bias Squared] --> TRADEOFF
    VARIANCE[Variance] --> TRADEOFF
    NOISE[Irreducible Noise sigma squared] --> TRADEOFF
    TRADEOFF[TOTAL MSE equals Bias Squared plus Variance plus Noise] --> OUT[Optimal Model Complexity]

    SIMPLE[Too Simple: High Bias, Low Variance - Underfitting] --> TRADEOFF
    COMPLEX[Too Complex: Low Bias, High Variance - Overfitting] --> TRADEOFF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). State the MLE for the mean of a univariate Gaussian distribution.

**Model Answer:**

> **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** is a statistical method for estimating the parameters of a probability distribution by maximizing the **likelihood function** $L(\theta) = P(D \mid \theta)$ — the probability of observing the given data $D$ as a function of the parameters $\theta$.

For a univariate Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with i.i.d. samples $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$, the MLE of the mean is:
$$\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \bar{x}$$

**Derivation sketch:** Maximize $\log L(\mu) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2$ by setting $\frac{\partial \log L}{\partial \mu} = 0$.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What is the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm? Why is it used?

**Model Answer:**

> The **EM algorithm** is an iterative method for finding **maximum likelihood (or MAP) estimates of parameters** in statistical models that involve **latent (hidden) variables**.

**Why it is used:**
1. When the data has missing/hidden variables (e.g., cluster labels in GMMs).
2. When direct maximization of $P(D \mid \theta)$ is **intractable** due to the latent variables.
3. It **guarantees monotonic increase** in the log-likelihood at every iteration.

**Two steps:**
- **E-step:** Compute expected value of log-likelihood w.r.t. posterior of latent variables.
- **M-step:** Maximize this expectation to update parameters.

---

### 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** (either/or). Both Question A and Question B below are **complete independent sets** with two sub-parts (7 + 7 marks). Choose any ONE.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the **Naive Bayes classifier** with its underlying probability model. Derive the classification rule. Discuss its **assumptions, advantages, and limitations** in the context of an intelligent agent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1-dimensional dataset $D = \{2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9\}$. Compute the **Maximum Likelihood Estimates** of the mean and variance assuming the data is drawn from a Gaussian distribution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Step 1 — Probability Model.**
Naive Bayes is a probabilistic classifier based on **Bayes' Theorem** with a **conditional independence assumption** among features given the class.

For a feature vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d)$ and class $C_k$:
$$P(C_k \mid x) = \frac{P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid C_k)}{P(x)}$$

**Step 2 — Classification Rule.**
The classifier picks the class with the **maximum posterior** (the denominator $P(x)$ is constant):
$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{k} \, P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{d} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

**Step 3 — Assumptions.**
- All features $x_i$ are **conditionally independent** given the class $C_k$.
- Features contribute **independently** to the outcome.

**Step 4 — Advantages for agents.**
- ⚡ **Fast** — O(d) per prediction, suitable for real-time agent decisions.
- 📊 **Works on small training data**.
- 🎯 **Handles high-dimensional data** (e.g., bag-of-words in NLP agents).

**Step 5 — Limitations.**
- The independence assumption is often **violated in real data**.
- **Zero-frequency problem** requires Laplace smoothing.
- Probability estimates can be **poorly calibrated**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the sample size.** $N = 8$.

**Step 2 — Compute the MLE of the mean.**
$$\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \frac{2 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 5 + 5 + 7 + 9}{8} = \frac{40}{8} = 5$$

**Step 3 — Compute squared deviations from the mean.**

| $x_i$ | $(x_i - 5)^2$ |
|-------|---------------|
| 2 | 9 |
| 4 | 1 |
| 4 | 1 |
| 4 | 1 |
| 5 | 0 |
| 5 | 0 |
| 7 | 4 |
| 9 | 16 |

Sum of squared deviations $= 9 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 4 + 16 = 32$.

**Step 4 — Compute the MLE of the variance.**
$$\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{\mu})^2 = \frac{32}{8} = 4$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**
$$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = 5, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = 4}$$

> **Bonus (if asked for unbiased variance):** $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{unbiased}} = \frac{32}{7} \approx 4.57$.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** with a neat diagram. State and explain the **three fundamental problems** associated with HMMs and the algorithms used to solve them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot agent uses a **2-state HMM** to model corridor lighting. State 1 = "Light ON", State 2 = "Light OFF". Initial distribution: $\pi = (1, 0)$. Transition matrix:
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$$
Emission matrix (observations: "Bright", "Dim"):
$$B = \begin{pmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$$
Given the observation sequence $O = (\text{Bright}, \text{Dim})$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ using the **forward algorithm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Step 1 — HMM Definition.**
An HMM is a **doubly stochastic process** consisting of:
- A **hidden Markov chain** with $N$ states $S = \{s_1, \dots, s_N\}$.
- An **observation process** that produces an observable symbol $O_t$ from alphabet $V = \{v_1, \dots, v_M\}$ at each time step.

**Step 2 — Model Parameters $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$.**
- $A = [a_{ij}]$: state transition probabilities.
- $B = [b_{jk}]$: emission probabilities.
- $\pi$: initial state distribution.

**Step 3 — The Three Fundamental Problems.**

| # | Problem | Goal | Algorithm |
|---|---------|------|-----------|
| 1 | **Evaluation** | Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | **Forward algorithm** (dynamic programming) |
| 2 | **Decoding** | Find most likely state sequence $\arg\max_S P(S \mid O, \lambda)$ | **Viterbi algorithm** |
| 3 | **Learning** | Estimate $\lambda$ from observations | **Baum-Welch algorithm** (a special case of EM) |

**Step 4 — Why these matter for agents.**
- **Evaluation** → agent computes "how likely is this sensor sequence under my model?".
- **Decoding** → agent infers the most likely hidden state (e.g., is the user happy or sad?).
- **Learning** → agent adapts its model to the environment.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(b)

The **forward algorithm** defines $\alpha_t(i) = P(O_1, O_2, \dots, O_t, S_t = s_i \mid \lambda)$.

**Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$, observation = "Bright").**
$$\alpha_1(1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(\text{Bright}) = 1 \times 0.9 = 0.9$$
$$\alpha_1(2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(\text{Bright}) = 0 \times 0.2 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Recursion ($t = 2$, observation = "Dim").**
$$\alpha_2(1) = \left[ \alpha_1(1) a_{11} + \alpha_1(2) a_{21} \right] \cdot b_1(\text{Dim})$$
$$= \left[ 0.9 \times 0.7 + 0 \times 0.4 \right] \times 0.1 = 0.63 \times 0.1 = 0.063$$

$$\alpha_2(2) = \left[ \alpha_1(1) a_{12} + \alpha_1(2) a_{22} \right] \cdot b_2(\text{Dim})$$
$$= \left[ 0.9 \times 0.3 + 0 \times 0.6 \right] \times 0.8 = 0.27 \times 0.8 = 0.216$$

**Step 3 — Termination.**
$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \alpha_2(1) + \alpha_2(2) = 0.063 + 0.216 = 0.279$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** $P(\text{Bright}, \text{Dim} \mid \lambda) = \mathbf{0.279}$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly LOSE marks in this topic — read this carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to take the LOG in MLE derivations.** Many students maximize $L(\theta)$ directly. Always convert to $\log L(\theta)$ first — it converts products to sums and is mathematically equivalent.
>
> 2. **Confusing MLE and MAP.** MLE ignores the prior; MAP includes it. If the question says "uniform prior" or "no prior information", MLE = MAP.
>
> 3. **The biased MLE variance.** $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ is **biased**. Dividing by $N-1$ gives the unbiased estimator. KTU examiners **love** testing this distinction.
>
> 4. **HMM Forward Algorithm — forgetting the summation over previous states.** The recursion is $\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(O_t)$. Writing only one term $\alpha_{t-1}(j) a_{jj}$ instead of the sum is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Naive Bayes — forgetting the denominator $P(x)$.** The denominator cancels in $\arg\max$, but writing the full Bayes' formula and then dropping it shows the examiner you understand the math.
>
> 6. **EM algorithm — claiming it always finds the global optimum.** It does **not**; it converges to a **local optimum** sensitive to initialization. Always mention this.
>
> 7. **Not using $\arg\max$ notation in classification.** A question asking "classify point $q$" expects a class label, not just a probability value.

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **The 15 must-know facts for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic:**

- 🔑 **Bayes' Theorem** $P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$ is the single most important formula.
- 🔑 **MLE for Gaussian mean** is the sample mean $\bar{x}$; **variance MLE** uses $N$ in the denominator (biased).
- 🔑 **MAP** = MLE × Prior. If prior is uniform, MAP = MLE.
- 🔑 **OLS** closed form: $w^* = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y$. Requires invertible $X^\top X$.
- 🔑 **Logistic regression** uses the **sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$; trained by maximizing log-likelihood.
- 🔑 **k-NN** is a non-parametric, lazy learner — no training phase, all work at test time.
- 🔑 **k-Means** minimizes WCSS $J = \sum_j \sum_{x \in C_j} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert^2$; sensitive to initialization.
- 🔑 **GMM** = mixture of $K$ Gaussians with weights $\pi_k$ summing to 1.
- 🔑 **EM** alternates E-step (compute responsibilities) and M-step (re-estimate parameters). It **monotonically increases** log-likelihood but may stop at a local maximum.
- 🔑 **HMMs** have three problems: **Evaluation** (Forward), **Decoding** (Viterbi), **Learning** (Baum-Welch).
- 🔑 **Naive Bayes** assumes feature independence given class; uses $\arg\max_k P(C_k) \prod_i P(x_i \mid C_k)$.
- 🔑 **Bias-Variance decomposition**: MSE = Bias² + Variance + $\sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}$.
- 🔑 **Conjugate priors** (Gaussian-Gaussian) give closed-form Bayesian updates.
- 🔑 **Statistical learning** is the only learning paradigm that **explicitly models uncertainty** via probability distributions.
- 🔑 In an **agent architecture**, statistical learning modules enable **perception** (classify sensor data), **reasoning** (infer hidden states), and **decision-making** (expected utility maximization).

---

> **🎓 End of Module 4 — Statistical Learning Methods Notes**
> *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme — PCCAT601 — Agent Based Intelligent Systems.*
