topics.title: Reinforcement Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reinforcement Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a machine learning paradigm in which an **autonomous agent** learns an optimal **policy** $\pi$ by continuously interacting with a **stochastic environment** in discrete time steps, observing **states**, executing **actions**, and receiving **scalar rewards**, with the explicit objective of maximizing the **expected cumulative discounted reward** over time.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PCCAT601 — Agent Based Intelligent Systems), RL is treated as the *third pillar* of machine learning, sitting between **Supervised Learning** (labelled data) and **Unsupervised Learning** (no labels). It is uniquely suited for *sequential decision-making* under uncertainty — a foundational requirement for any truly **intelligent agent**.

### 1.2 Canonical Components of an RL System

| Symbol | Component | Role in the System |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | **State** | Complete description of the environment at time $t$ |
| $A$ | **Action** | Set of moves available to the agent |
| $P$ | **Transition Model** | $P(s'\vert s,a)$ — probability of reaching $s'$ from $s$ via action $a$ |
| $R$ | **Reward Function** | $R(s,a,s')$ — scalar feedback signal |
| $\pi$ | **Policy** | Mapping from states to action probabilities, $\pi(a \vert s)$ |
| $V$ | **Value Function** | Expected long-term return starting from state $s$ |
| $Q$ | **Action-Value Function** | Expected return of taking action $a$ in state $s$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Training a Pet

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Teaching a Dog New Tricks:**
> Imagine training a Labrador puppy. You cannot show it a dataset of "correct dog behaviour" (no supervised labels exist). Instead, every time it sits when you say *sit*, you hand it a biscuit (**positive reward**); when it chews your slippers, you say *no* firmly (**negative reward**). Over hundreds of trials, the puppy builds an internal *mental map* — *"in this situation, that action gave me biscuits before."* That mental map is, mathematically, the **policy $\pi$**.
>
> The puppy is the **Agent**. Your house is the **Environment**. Its current posture (sitting, standing, barking) is the **State**. The actions (sit, stay, bark) are the **Action set**. The biscuit is the **Reward**. The puppy's eventual habit of obeying commands is the **learned optimal policy**.

### 1.4 Why Reinforcement Learning Matters for Intelligent Agents

RL is the *only* learning paradigm where the agent learns **purely from interaction consequences**, not from pre-labelled examples. This makes it the cornerstone of:
- **Game-playing agents** (AlphaGo, Stockfish, OpenAI Five)
- **Robotic locomotion and manipulation**
- **Autonomous navigation** (self-driving cars, drones)
- **Dynamic resource allocation** in networks and cloud computing
- **Multi-agent negotiation systems** — directly relevant to PCCAT601

The two **canonical discount-related constants** every KTU student must memorise are:
- **Discount Factor** $\gamma \in [0,1]$ — typically $\gamma = 0.9$ or $\gamma = 0.99$
- **Learning Rate** $\alpha \in (0,1]$ — typically $\alpha = 0.1$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Reinforcement Learning Loop — Operational Breakdown

The RL interaction unfolds as a strict, repeating sequence. Every iteration is one **time step** $t$.

1. **Observation** — The agent perceives the current state $s_t \in S$ from the environment.
2. **Decision** — Using its current policy $\pi$, the agent samples an action $a_t \in A$ such that $a_t \sim \pi(\cdot \vert s_t)$.
3. **Execution** — The environment receives $a_t$ and transitions to a new state $s_{t+1}$ with probability $P(s_{t+1} \vert s_t, a_t)$.
4. **Reward** — The environment emits a scalar reward $r_{t+1} = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$.
5. **Learning** — The agent updates its internal value estimates using $s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1}$.
6. **Loop** — Increment $t \leftarrow t+1$ and return to step 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Loop:** Unlike supervised learning (where the error signal is *given*), in RL the error signal *must be constructed* by the agent itself via temporal-difference reasoning. This is the deepest conceptual point in KTU Module 4.

### 2.2 Markov Decision Process (MDP) — The Mathematical Skeleton

Every formal RL problem is modelled as an **MDP**, defined by the 5-tuple $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Markov Property** states that the future depends on the past *only* through the present: $P(s_{t+1} \vert s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \ldots) = P(s_{t+1} \vert s_t, a_t)$. This is the assumption that lets RL scale — the agent never needs to remember history, only the current state.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Note:** Master every equation in this table. Questions worth 7 to 14 marks routinely demand direct application of the Bellman equation or the Q-Learning update rule.

| # | Equation / Concept | LaTeX Form | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cumulative Discounted Return** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$ | Total reward from time $t$ onward, future-weighted |
| 2 | **State-Value Function** | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert s_t = s]$ | Expected return when starting in $s$ and following $\pi$ |
| 3 | **Action-Value Function** | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert s_t=s, a_t=a]$ | Expected return from $(s,a)$ under $\pi$ |
| 4 | **Bellman Expectation for $V$** | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | Recursive decomposition of $V$ |
| 5 | **Bellman Expectation for $Q$** | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert s') Q^{\pi}(s',a')]$ | Recursive decomposition of $Q$ |
| 6 | **Bellman Optimality for $V^*$** | $V^{*}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$ | Defines the *best possible* value of $s$ |
| 7 | **Bellman Optimality for $Q^*$** | $Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')]$ | Defines the *best possible* $(s,a)$ value |
| 8 | **Q-Learning Update (Off-policy)** | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | Model-free learning update |
| 9 | **SARSA Update (On-policy)** | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | On-policy variant |
| 10 | **TD Error** | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | Core learning signal in TD methods |
| 11 | **$\epsilon$-Greedy Policy** | $a = \begin{cases} \arg\max_a Q(s,a), & \text{w.p. } 1-\epsilon \\ \text{random}, & \text{w.p. } \epsilon \end{cases}$ | Balances explore/exploit |
| 12 | **Optimal Policy Extraction** | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_a Q^{*}(s,a)$ | Derives the policy from $Q^*$ |

### 2.4 Exploration vs. Exploitation — The Central Trade-Off

- **Exploitation**: Pick the best-known action now to maximise immediate reward.
- **Exploration**: Try a seemingly-suboptimal action to gather information that may yield larger future rewards.
- **Solution**: The **$\epsilon$-greedy** strategy. With probability $1-\epsilon$ exploit; with probability $\epsilon$ explore.
- As training progresses, $\epsilon$ is typically **decayed** (e.g., $\epsilon_t = 0.1 \cdot 0.99^t$) to favour exploitation late.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

RL is the engine behind:
- **Warehouse robotics** (Amazon Kiva bots path planning)
- **Adaptive traffic signal control** (SUMO simulator)
- **Smart grid energy dispatch**
- **Portfolio management** in quantitative finance
- **Dialogue agents and recommender systems** at scale

### 2.6 Tabular Summary — Model-Based vs Model-Free RL

| Aspect | Model-Based RL | Model-Free RL |
|---|---|---|
| Knows $P(s' \vert s,a)$? | Yes | No |
| Sample Efficiency | High | Lower |
| Computation | Heavy (planning) | Light |
| Examples | AlphaZero planning, Dyna | Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN |
| KTU Typical Use | Policy Iteration, Value Iteration | Q-Learning derivations |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^{\pi}(s)$

**Goal:** Express $V^{\pi}(s)$ recursively in terms of itself and one-step rewards.

**Starting Point** — definition of state-value:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,G_t \mid s_t = s\,\right]$$

**Step 1:** Expand $G_t$ using the definition $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s\,\right]$$

**Step 2:** By linearity of expectation, separate the terms.

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,r_{t+1} \mid s_t = s\,\right] + \gamma\,\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s\,\right]$$

**Step 3:** Condition on the action $a_t = a$ chosen by the policy at $s$, then on the next state $s_{t+1} = s'$. Apply the **Law of Total Expectation**.

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in A}\pi(a \vert s)\sum_{s' \in S}P(s' \vert s,a)\!\left[\,R(s,a,s') + \gamma\,\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1} = s'\,\right]\,\right]$$

**Step 4:** Recognise that the final inner expectation is precisely $V^{\pi}(s')$.

$$\boxed{\;V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in A}\pi(a \vert s)\sum_{s' \in S}P(s' \vert s,a)\!\left[\,R(s,a,s') + \gamma\,V^{\pi}(s')\,\right]\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The value of state $s$ equals the *expected* one-step reward plus the *discounted* expected value of the next state. This single recursive identity is the foundation of every RL algorithm studied in KTU Module 4.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*(s)$

**Goal:** Find the value of the *best possible* policy.

**Starting Point** — define $V^*(s)$ as the maximum achievable value.

$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the Bellman expectation equation and pull the $\max$ inside the action sum.

$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a}\sum_{s' \in S}P(s' \vert s,a)\!\left[\,R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{*}(s')\,\right]$$

**Step 2:** Justification — the optimal policy must select the action $a$ that maximises the resulting expected value. The $\max$ over policies reduces to a $\max$ over actions because for any policy, we can always pick the best first action and then follow the optimal policy thereafter.

**Step 3 (Final Boxed Result):**

$$\boxed{\;V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in A}\sum_{s' \in S}P(s' \vert s,a)\!\left[\,R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{*}(s')\,\right]\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Q-Learning Update Rule

**Goal:** Derive the model-free update that adjusts $Q(s,a)$ toward the Bellman optimality target.

**Step 1 — Sample the Transition:** Execute $a_t$ in $s_t$. The environment produces:
- Reward $r_{t+1}$
- Next state $s_{t+1}$

**Step 2 — Compute the TD Target:** If $Q^*$ were known, the optimal value of taking action $a_t$ would be the Bellman optimality target.

$$\text{Target} = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_{t+1}, a')$$

**Step 3 — Approximate Using Current Estimate:** Since $Q^*$ is unknown, use the current $Q$-table as the estimator.

$$\widehat{\text{Target}} = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a')$$

**Step 4 — Define the TD Error** $\delta_t$:

$$\delta_t = \widehat{\text{Target}} - Q(s_t, a_t) = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Stochastic Gradient Descent:** Take a step of size $\alpha$ in the direction of the TD error.

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha\,\delta_t$$

**Step 6 — Final Boxed Result:**

$$\boxed{\;Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\,\right]\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Theorem (Watkins, 1989):** If every state-action pair is visited infinitely often, the learning rate $\alpha$ satisfies the Robbins-Monro conditions $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$, and rewards are bounded, then $Q(s,a) \rightarrow Q^*(s,a)$ with probability 1. **KTU Examiners frequently award bonus marks for stating this theorem.**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Feature | Q-Learning | SARSA | Value Iteration | Policy Iteration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Off-policy, Model-free | On-policy, Model-free | Model-based | Model-based |
| Update Target | $r + \gamma \max Q(s',a')$ | $r + \gamma Q(s',a')$ | $\max_a [R + \gamma V(s')]$ | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max Q$ |
| Convergence Speed | Moderate | Moderate | Fast | Fast |
| Handles Stochasticity | Yes | Yes (safer) | Yes | Yes |
| KTU Mark Weightage | **Very High** | High | High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

This is the **canonical diagram** every KTU examiner expects in Module 4 answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    agentA[Agent]
    envB[Environment]
    stateAct[State s_t]
    actionAct[Action a_t]
    rewardAct[Reward r_t+1]
    nextState[Next State s_t+1]
    policyAct[Policy pi]
    valueAct[Value Function V or Q]

    stateAct --> agentA
    agentA -->|selects| actionAct
    actionAct --> envB
    envB --> rewardAct
    envB --> nextState
    nextState --> stateAct
    rewardAct --> agentA
    agentA -.updates.-> policyAct
    policyAct -.drives.-> agentA
    agentA -.updates.-> valueAct
    valueAct -.informs.-> policyAct
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The solid arrows show the *physical* data flow each time step. The dashed arrows show the *learning* flow — the policy and value function are updated *after* observing the reward, but they influence *future* action selection.

---

### 4.2 Q-Learning Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Initialise Q s a = 0 for all s a]
    initEps[Set epsilon epsilon and learning rate alpha]
    loopStart[FOR each episode]
    obsS[Observe initial state s]
    stepLoop[FOR each step of episode]
    actSelect{With probability epsilon}
    randAct[Pick random action a]
    greedyAct[Pick a = argmax Q s a]
    exeAct[Execute action a in environment]
    getR[Observe reward r and next state s prime]
    updateQ[Update Q s a]
    qForm[Q s a = Q s a + alpha r + gamma max Q s prime a prime minus Q s a]
    setS[Set s = s prime]
    termCheck{Is s terminal?}
    epEnd[Episode Ends]
    cond[Until s is terminal]
    allEnd[All Episodes Complete: Output Q star]

    startA --> initEps
    initEps --> loopStart
    loopStart --> obsS
    obsS --> stepLoop
    stepLoop --> actSelect
    actSelect -->|epsilon| randAct
    actSelect -->|1 minus epsilon| greedyAct
    randAct --> exeAct
    greedyAct --> exeAct
    exeAct --> getR
    getR --> updateQ
    updateQ --> qForm
    qForm --> setS
    setS --> cond
    cond -->|No| stepLoop
    cond -->|Yes| epEnd
    epEnd --> loopStart
    loopStart -->|Done| allEnd
```

---

### 4.3 MDP State Transition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stCurr[State s]
    actTake[Action a]
    stNext[State s prime]
    rewEmit[Reward R]

    stCurr -->|input| actTake
    actTake -->|P s prime vs a| stNext
    actTake -->|emits| rewEmit
    stCurr -.->|initial state value| rewEmit
    stNext -.->|future discounted value| rewEmit
```

---

### 4.4 Exploration vs. Exploitation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    atState[At State s]
    draw[Draw random number u in 0 1]
    checkEps{u less than epsilon}
    doExplore[Explore: Random Action]
    doExploit[Exploit: Best Known Action a = argmax Q]
    envResp[Environment Returns Reward and Next State]

    atState --> draw
    draw --> checkEps
    checkEps -->|Yes| doExplore
    checkEps -->|No| doExploit
    doExplore --> envResp
    doExploit --> envResp
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Real production systems often replace $\epsilon$-greedy with **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB)** or **Thompson Sampling**, which give principled, theoretically-justified exploration. KTU questions sometimes mention these as a "compare and contrast" follow-up.

---

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Full RL System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    senseModule[Perception Module]
    stateEncoder[State Encoder]
    policyNet[Policy Network pi a vs s]
    valueNet[Value Network V s or Q s a]
    actionOut[Action Output]
    envInterface[Environment Interface]
    rewardProc[Reward Processor]
    memoryBuf[Experience Replay Buffer]
    updater[Gradient Updater]

    senseModule --> stateEncoder
    stateEncoder --> policyNet
    stateEncoder --> valueNet
    policyNet --> actionOut
    actionOut --> envInterface
    envInterface --> rewardProc
    envInterface --> stateEncoder
    rewardProc --> memoryBuf
    stateEncoder --> memoryBuf
    memoryBuf --> updater
    updater --> policyNet
    updater --> valueNet
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define Reinforcement Learning. List and briefly explain its four primary elements.*

**Model Answer:**

Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a goal-directed machine learning paradigm in which an agent learns optimal behaviour through trial-and-error interactions with a dynamic environment, guided by scalar reward signals, with the objective of maximising cumulative discounted return.

The four primary elements are:

1. **Policy** $\pi$ — The agent's behavioural strategy mapping states to actions.
2. **Reward Signal** $R$ — The immediate scalar feedback defining the goal.
3. **Value Function** $V^{\pi}(s)$ — The expected long-term return, indicating *how good* a state is.
4. **Model of the Environment** (optional) — The agent's internal representation of state-transition dynamics $P(s' \vert s,a)$.

A complete answer should additionally mention the agent–environment boundary as the fifth conceptual element. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the Exploration–Exploitation dilemma in Reinforcement Learning? How does the $\epsilon$-greedy strategy resolve it?*

**Model Answer:**

The Exploration–Exploitation dilemma is the central trade-off in RL: the agent must decide whether to **exploit** its current best-known action to maximise immediate reward, or **explore** seemingly-inferior actions to discover potentially superior long-term strategies.

The **$\epsilon$-greedy** strategy resolves this by randomising the choice: with probability $1-\epsilon$ the agent selects the action with the highest current $Q$-value (exploitation), and with probability $\epsilon$ it picks a uniformly random action (exploration). The parameter $\epsilon$ is often **annealed** (decayed) over training — starting near 1 and settling near 0.01 — so exploration dominates early and exploitation dominates late. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students routinely lose **2 to 3 marks** by writing the Bellman equation without defining its variables. *Always* define $V^{\pi}, P, R, \gamma$ inline.
> - In Q-Learning derivations, students forget to mention the **convergence assumptions** (visiting every $(s,a)$ infinitely often, Robbins–Monro conditions on $\alpha$). Examiners dock 2 marks for this omission.
> - Confusion between **on-policy (SARSA)** and **off-policy (Q-Learning)** is the #1 reason for 14-mark answer failures in KTU Module 4. Memorise the difference.

---

#### QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample QP aligned to 2024 Scheme Module 4]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework as the formal foundation of Reinforcement Learning. Discuss all five components of an MDP and clearly state the Markov property. Also derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare and contrast Q-Learning and SARSA algorithms. For each algorithm, write the complete update rule, classify it as on-policy or off-policy with justification, and describe a scenario where one would be preferred over the other.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

An MDP is the canonical mathematical framework for sequential decision-making under stochasticity. It is formally defined by the 5-tuple $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$.

- **$S$**: Finite (or continuous) set of *states*.
- **$A$**: Set of *actions* available to the agent.
- **$P(s' \vert s, a)$**: *Transition probability* — probability of landing in $s'$ after taking action $a$ in $s$.
- **$R(s, a, s')$**: *Reward function* — expected immediate reward.
- **$\gamma \in [0,1]$**: *Discount factor* controlling the present value of future rewards.

**The Markov Property** asserts that the transition probability depends *only* on the current state $s_t$, not on the entire history:

$$P(s_{t+1} \vert s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \ldots, s_0, a_0) = P(s_{t+1} \vert s_t, a_t)$$

**Derivation of Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^{\pi}(s)$:** (Write the full derivation from Section 3.1 — 4 to 5 lines of algebra, concluding with the boxed equation.)

$$\boxed{\;V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in A}\pi(a \vert s)\sum_{s' \in S}P(s' \vert s,a)\!\left[\,R(s,a,s') + \gamma\,V^{\pi}(s')\,\right]\;}$$

**Interpretation:** The value of a state equals the *expected one-step reward* plus the *discounted expected value* of the successor state, weighted by how often the agent takes each action. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Feature | Q-Learning | SARSA |
|---|---|---|
| Update Rule | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ |
| Policy Class | **Off-policy** (learns optimal policy regardless of action taken) | **On-policy** (learns value of the policy actually followed) |
| Update Target | Uses $\max_{a'}$ over next actions | Uses the *actual* next action $a'$ sampled by current policy |
| Convergence | $Q \rightarrow Q^*$ under standard assumptions | $Q \rightarrow Q^{\pi}$ for the current behaviour policy |

**Why Q-Learning is Off-Policy:** The $\max$ operator assumes the next action is *greedy*, but the agent may actually have taken an exploratory action. The update thus learns the optimal policy *as if* the agent behaved greedily.

**Why SARSA is On-Policy:** All five elements $s, a, r, s', a'$ come from the agent's actual trajectory — hence the acronym **S-A-R-S-A**.

**Preferred Scenario:**
- **Q-Learning** is preferred in **offline learning from logged data**, in environments where risky exploration must be avoided, and when the optimal policy is the goal irrespective of the data-collection policy. Example: medical treatment recommendation from historical patient records.
- **SARSA** is preferred in **safety-critical online control**, where the agent must learn the value of the *exploring* policy it actually follows. Example: cliff-walking robot where an exploratory step off the cliff must be penalised in the learned value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** *Define the action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$. Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*(s,a)$ and explain its role in model-free reinforcement learning.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Describe the Q-Learning algorithm in detail. Write its update equation, list the convergence assumptions, and explain how the $\epsilon$-greedy strategy is used during training. Discuss the limitations of tabular Q-Learning.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **action-value function** $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ is the expected return when starting in state $s$, taking action $a$, and thereafter following policy $\pi$:

$$Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\,\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1} \,\Big\vert\, s_t=s, a_t=a\,\right]$$

**Derivation of Bellman Optimality for $Q^*$:**

**Step 1:** Write the Bellman expectation equation for $Q^{\pi}$ under the optimal policy $\pi^*$.

$$Q^{*}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,r_{t+1} + \gamma\,\mathbb{E}_{\pi^{*}}[\,G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1}\,] \,\Big\vert\, s_t=s, a_t=a\,\right]$$

**Step 2:** The inner expectation under $\pi^*$ is the *maximum* achievable action-value from $s'$.

$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi^{*}}[\,G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1}=s'\,] = \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and apply the law of total expectation over next states.

$$\boxed{\;Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s' \in S}P(s' \vert s,a)\!\left[\,R(s,a,s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')\,\right]\;}$$

**Role in Model-Free RL:** This equation expresses $Q^*$ purely in terms of one-step rewards and the recursive maximum of next-state $Q$-values. Crucially, it requires no value of $\pi$ and no transition model — only samples $(s, a, r, s')$. This is what makes it the foundation of **model-free** RL algorithms like Q-Learning. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Q-Learning Algorithm Description:**
1. Initialise $Q(s,a) = 0$ for all $(s,a) \in S \times A$.
2. Set hyperparameters $\alpha$ (learning rate), $\gamma$ (discount factor), $\epsilon$ (exploration rate).
3. **For each episode**:
   - Observe initial state $s$.
   - **For each step**:
     - With probability $\epsilon$, select a random action (exploration); otherwise select $a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a')$ (exploitation).
     - Execute $a$, observe reward $r$ and next state $s'$.
     - **Update** using the Q-Learning rule:

$$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\,\right]$$

     - Set $s \leftarrow s'$.

**Convergence Assumptions (Watkins, 1989):**
1. Every state-action pair $(s, a)$ is visited infinitely often.
2. The learning rate $\alpha_t(s,a)$ satisfies Robbins-Monro conditions: $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
3. Rewards are bounded: $\vert r \vert \leq R_{\max} < \infty$.
4. The Markov property holds for state transitions.

Under these conditions, $Q(s,a) \rightarrow Q^*(s,a)$ with probability 1.

**Role of $\epsilon$-greedy:**
The $\epsilon$-greedy policy provides *continued exploration* during training. Without it, the agent would lock into the first reasonable action and never discover better alternatives. Typical schedule: $\epsilon$ starts near 1.0 and decays exponentially to 0.01.

**Limitations of Tabular Q-Learning:**
1. **Curse of Dimensionality:** The $Q$-table grows as $\vert S \vert \times \vert A \vert$, infeasible for large/continuous state spaces.
2. **No Generalisation:** Each $(s,a)$ is updated independently — similar states share no information.
3. **Slow Convergence:** Realistic problems require millions of episodes.
4. **Solution:** Function approximation (Deep Q-Networks / DQN) replaces the table with a neural network $Q(s,a;\theta)$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Reinforcement Learning:**

- RL is **learning from interaction consequences**, not from labelled data.
- Core tuple: **$(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$** — the MDP 5-tuple. Know each component by heart.
- The **Markov Property** is the assumption that the future is independent of the past given the present.
- **Return** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k}r_{t+k+1}$. Memorise this exact summation form.
- **State-value** $V^{\pi}(s)$ vs **action-value** $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ — both are expectations of $G_t$ under different conditionings.
- **Bellman Expectation Equations** = policy-specific recursion. **Bellman Optimality Equations** = policy-independent recursion with a $\max$.
- The **Q-Learning update** is **off-policy**, model-free, and converges to $Q^*$ under standard assumptions.
- **SARSA** is **on-policy** — its update uses the actually-executed next action.
- **$\epsilon$-greedy** is the workhorse exploration strategy; remember to *decay* $\epsilon$ over training.
- **TD Error** $\delta_t = r + \gamma V(s') - V(s)$ is the core learning signal in temporal-difference methods.
- Tabular methods fail at scale — this motivates **Deep RL / DQN**, a popular follow-up topic in advanced courses.
- **Common pitfalls**: confusing on-policy/off-policy, omitting convergence assumptions, forgetting to define $\gamma$ and $\alpha$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Communication in Multi-Agent Systems (MAS)** refers to the structured exchange of information, intentions, requests, and commitments between autonomous software agents using a well-defined **Agent Communication Language (ACL)**, grounded in formal semantics (typically **Speech Act Theory**) and executed through standardized **Interaction Protocols**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Agent Based Intelligent Systems*, communication is the mechanism that enables agents to coordinate, cooperate, negotiate, and resolve conflicts without sharing a common memory or execution context.

$$ Communication = \langle Sender, Receiver, Message, Ontology, Protocol, Semantics \rangle $$

Where each element has a precise formal role:

| Element | Formal Role | Mandatory? |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| Sender | Agent initiating the communicative act | **Yes** |
| Receiver | Target agent(s) interpreting the act | **Yes** |
| Message | The content + performative structure | **Yes** |
| Ontology | Shared vocabulary to resolve term meaning | **Yes** |
| Protocol | Governed sequence of allowable message exchanges | **Yes** |
| Semantics | Formal mapping from message to mental/social state change | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A communication act is NOT merely data transfer. It is a **performative** action — by sending a message, the agent actually *does something* (e.g., requests, promises, informs), and this alters the mental state of both sender and receiver.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two diplomats from different countries negotiating a treaty.

- They do not share a native language → they need a **common language with formal grammar** (this is the ACL).
- They need a **shared dictionary** so that the word "border" means exactly the same thing to both → this is the **Ontology**.
- They cannot just blurt out anything; they must follow a ceremony (greeting → proposal → counter-proposal → signing) → this is the **Interaction Protocol**.
- When the ambassador says *"I promise to reduce tariffs"*, this is not just information — it is an **act of commitment** → this is the **Speech Act / Performative**.

In software agents, this diplomatic precision is necessary because agents are autonomous and cannot peek into each other's memory. Every meaningful intention must be **explicitly uttered**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Agent Communication (must remember):**
> 1. **Speech Act Theory** — gives meaning to utterances.
> 2. **Agent Communication Language** — gives the syntactic vehicle (KQML / FIPA ACL).
> 3. **Ontology & Interaction Protocol** — gives the shared vocabulary and orderly flow.

---

### 1.3 Why Communication Is a Hard Problem

- Agents are **heterogeneous** (different platforms, languages, vendors).
- There is **no global state** — coordination must be message-driven.
- Communication must be **semantically unambiguous** — otherwise the system collapses under misinterpretation.
- Standardization is required for **interoperability** — hence FIPA / IEEE FIPA standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard body for agent communication: **FIPA (Foundation for Intelligent Physical Agents)**, now part of IEEE, defines the **FIPA ACL** — the de-facto industry standard referenced in the KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Speech Act Theory — The Philosophical Backbone

**Speech Act Theory**, formulated by **J.L. Austin (1962)** and extended by **John Searle (1969)**, classifies utterances not as mere statements of fact, but as **performative actions**.

Austin identified three layered aspects of a single utterance:

| Layer | Name | Meaning | Agent System Equivalent |
|-------|------|---------|------------------------|
| **Locutionary** | What was said | The literal syntactic form | The raw message string |
| **Illocutionary** | What was meant / intended | The performative force (request, promise) | The **performative** field |
| **Perlocutionary** | What effect it produced | The effect on the listener | Updated mental state of receiver |

**Searle's Taxonomy of Illocutionary Acts** (foundational for ACL design):

1. **Representatives** — assert a proposition (e.g., *inform*, *assert*).
2. **Directives** — attempt to get the hearer to do something (e.g., *request*, *query*).
3. **Commissives** — commit the speaker to a future action (e.g., *promise*, *commit*).
4. **Expressives** — express a psychological state (e.g., *apologize*, *thank*).
5. **Declaratives** — change the world via the utterance itself (e.g., *declare war*, *pronounce*).

> [!NOTE]
> Most agent communication focuses on **Representatives, Directives, and Commissives**, since these map directly to coordination, delegation, and contracting.

---

### 2.2 Agent Communication Languages (ACLs)

#### 2.2.1 KQML (Knowledge Query and Manipulation Language)

Developed under the **DARPA Knowledge Sharing Effort (1990s)**. KQML separates the **communication layer** from the **content layer**.

**KQML Message Structure:**

$$ KQML\_Message = (Performative \;:\; \{ key_1 : value_1, \; key_2 : value_2, \ldots \} \;:\; Content) $$

Example:
```
(ask-one
    :sender  AgentA
    :receiver AgentB
    :language Prolog
    :ontology  Travel-Ontology
    :content   (price (?flight KTM-BLR))
    :reply-with q1
    :in-reply-to q0)
```

**KQML Reserved Performatives (high-yield for KTU):**

| Category | Performatives |
|----------|---------------|
| **Basic Query** | ask-if, ask-one, ask-all |
| **Multi-response** | stream-about, stream-all |
| **Response** | reply, sorry |
| **Generative** | tell, achieve, advertise, recommend |
| **Capability** | advertise, subscribe |
| **Networking** | register, unregister, forward, broadcast |

#### 2.2.2 FIPA ACL

The **FIPA ACL** improves upon KQML with:
- A formal **semantic framework** based on **Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI)** logic.
- A standardized **message structure** with mandatory parameters.
- Well-defined **interaction protocols** (FIPA Request, FIPA Contract-Net, FIPA Subscribe, etc.).

**FIPA ACL Message Structure:**

$$
\text{FIPA-ACL Message} =
\begin{cases}
\text{performative:} & \text{communicative act type} \\
\text{sender:} & A_s \\
\text{receiver:} & A_r \\
\text{content:} & \phi \\
\text{language:} & L \\
\text{ontology:} & O \\
\text{protocol:} & \pi \\
\text{conversation-id:} & cid \\
\text{in-reply-to:} & rid \\
\text{reply-by:} & t_{max}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **FIPA ACL**, only `performative`, `sender`, `receiver`, and `content` are **mandatory**. All other fields are optional but strongly recommended.

**FIPA Communicative Acts (selected, exam-relevant):**

| FIPA Performative | Searle Category | Meaning |
|-------------------|-----------------|---------|
| `inform` | Representative | Sender informs receiver that $\phi$ is true |
| `request` | Directive | Sender requests receiver to perform action |
| `query-if` | Directive | Asks whether $\phi$ holds |
| `query-ref` | Directive | Asks for the value of a referential expression |
| `cfp` (call for proposal) | Directive | Initiates a contract-net negotiation |
| `propose` | Commissive | Sender proposes to do action under conditions |
| `accept-proposal` | Commissive | Sender accepts a proposal |
| `reject-proposal` | Commissive | Sender rejects a proposal |
| `agree` | Commissive | Sender agrees to perform an action |
| `refuse` | Commissive | Sender refuses the request |
| `subscribe` | Directive | Long-running query (notification on change) |
| `confirm` / `disconfirm` | Representative | Confirms/denies a proposition's truth |

---

### 2.3 Formal Semantics of FIPA ACL

FIPA uses **SL (Semantic Language)** — a quantified modal logic with BDI operators: $B_i \phi$ (agent $i$ believes $\phi$), $D_i \phi$ (agent $i$ desires $\phi$), $Intent_i \phi$ (agent $i$ intends $\phi$), and $U_i \phi$ (agent $i$ uncertain about $\phi$).

**Pre-condition (FP) and Rational Effect (RE)** of `inform`:

$$
\underbrace{\langle i, \; \text{inform}(j, \phi) \rangle}_{\text{communicative act}}
\;\Rightarrow\;
\begin{cases}
\text{FP: } & B_i \phi \;\wedge\; \neg B_i (B_j \; \phi \vee \neg B_j \; \phi) \\
\text{RE: } & B_j \phi
\end{cases}
$$

**Translation:**
- **Feasibility Pre-condition:** Agent $i$ believes $\phi$ AND does not already believe that $j$ either believes or disbelieves $\phi$.
- **Rational Effect:** Agent $j$ comes to believe $\phi$.

This formal semantics is what makes FIPA ACL **mathematically verifiable** — a key difference from KQML's more informal semantics.

---

### 2.4 Ontologies

An **ontology** is a formal, explicit specification of a shared vocabulary — a set of concepts, relations, and axioms within a domain.

For two agents to communicate meaningfully, both must:
1. Reference the **same ontology** in their message header.
2. Use terms whose meaning is grounded in that ontology.

Examples: **DAML+OIL, OWL, RDF Schema, FIPA Ontology Service**.

$$ M_1 \leftrightarrow M_2 \;\;\iff\;\; \exists O : \text{terms}(M_1) \subseteq \text{vocab}(O) \;\wedge\; \text{terms}(M_2) \subseteq \text{vocab}(O) $$

---

### 2.5 Interaction Protocols

An **Interaction Protocol (IP)** is a finite-state specification of the legal message exchanges between agents in a given conversation.

**Major FIPA Protocols (high-yield for KTU):**

| Protocol | Purpose | Key Performatives |
|----------|---------|-------------------|
| **FIPA Request** | Simple action request | request → agree/refuse → inform-done |
| **FIPA Query** | One-shot information query | query-if / query-ref → inform |
| **FIPA Contract Net** | Task allocation via bidding | cfp → propose → accept/reject-proposal |
| **FIPA Subscribe** | Persistent notification | subscribe → inform (repeated) |
| **FIPA Iterated Contract Net** | Repeated rounds of negotiation | cfp ↔ propose → accept |
| **FIPA English Auction** | Open-cry ascending auction | call → bid ↔ bid → accept |
| **FIPA Dutch Auction** | Descending price auction | announce → accept / drop |

> [!NOTE]
> A protocol is **not** the message content. It is the **allowed sequence**. The same performative (e.g., `inform`) can appear in many protocols.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Symbol | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Communication act tuple | $\langle S, R, M, O, P, Sem \rangle$ | Designing MAS middleware |
| 2 | Speech act layers | Locution + Illocution + Perlocution | ACL message design |
| 3 | Searle categories | 5 types (Rep, Dir, Com, Exp, Dec) | Performative selection |
| 4 | FIPA ACL parameters | 12+ fields, 4 mandatory | Standardized inter-agent messaging |
| 5 | `inform` feasibility | $B_i \phi \wedge \neg B_i(B_j \phi \vee \neg B_j \phi)$ | Trust / belief reasoning |
| 6 | `inform` rational effect | $B_j \phi$ | Receiver belief update |
| 7 | `request` FP | $FP_{i} \Rightarrow \text{action}_j$ is feasible | Action delegation |
| 8 | `agree` RE | $Intent_j \; action$ | Commitment formation |
| 9 | Ontology grounding | Common $O$ referenced by both agents | Semantic interoperability |
| 10 | Protocol compliance | Conversation $C \subseteq L(P)$ where $L(P)$ is legal language | Conformance testing |

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- Agents **cannot** communicate without an **agreed ontology** → silent failure.
- **Sender must believe the proposition** for `inform` to be rational.
- **Receiver is not obliged** to accept a belief or perform an action unless it issues `agree` / `accept-proposal`.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Use of Agent Communication |
|--------|----------------------------|
| **Supply Chain Management** | Agents representing factories, warehouses, retailers negotiate stock orders via Contract-Net. |
| **Smart Grid** | Distributed energy agents `inform` price changes, `propose` load-shedding plans. |
| **e-Healthcare** | Patient-monitoring agents `subscribe` to sensor data streams and `request` clinician action. |
| **Logistics / Routing** | Vehicle agents coordinate via `request`/`agree` to yield traffic priority. |
| **Robotics Swarms** | Robots exchange `inform` on obstacle positions and `cfp` for task assignment. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Formal Semantics of `inform` in FIPA ACL

**Goal:** Derive the pre-condition and rational effect of the FIPA `inform` communicative act using SL (Semantic Language).

**Step 1 — Define the participants.**
Let:
- $i$ = sender agent (informant)
- $j$ = receiver agent (informee)
- $\phi$ = proposition being communicated

**Step 2 — Define the BDI primitives.**

$$
B_i \phi \quad \text{(i believes } \phi\text{)}
$$
$$
D_i \phi \quad \text{(i desires } \phi\text{)}
$$
$$
Intent_i \phi \quad \text{(i intends to bring about } \phi\text{)}
$$
$$
U_i \phi \quad \text{(i is uncertain about } \phi\text{)}
$$

**Step 3 — State the communicative act.**

$$
\langle i, \; \text{inform}(j, \phi) \rangle
$$

This denotes agent $i$ performing the communicative act `inform` toward agent $j$ with content $\phi$.

**Step 4 — Derive the Feasibility Pre-condition (FP).**

The agent can only sincerely inform if it actually believes $\phi$ AND does not already have a definite belief about $j$'s belief state (otherwise the inform would be redundant or presumptuous).

$$
FP \;:\;\; B_i \phi \;\wedge\; \neg B_i (B_j \phi \vee \neg B_j \phi)
$$

Reading: "Agent $i$ believes $\phi$ is true, and agent $i$ does not have a settled belief about whether $j$ already believes or disbelieves $\phi$."

**Step 5 — Derive the Rational Effect (RE).**

The point of informing is to update $j$'s belief state. Therefore:

$$
RE \;:\;\; B_j \phi
$$

Reading: "After the act, $j$ comes to believe $\phi$."

**Step 6 — Combine into the full formal semantics.**

$$
\boxed{\;
\langle i, \; \text{inform}(j, \phi) \rangle \;\Rightarrow\; \bigl[ B_i \phi \;\wedge\; \neg B_i (B_j \phi \vee \neg B_j \phi) \bigr] \;\triangleright\; B_j \phi
\;}
$$

Where $\triangleright$ separates feasibility pre-condition from rational effect.

**Step 7 — Verify logical soundness.**
- If $i$ does not believe $\phi$, then $i$ cannot rationally inform $j$ of $\phi$. ✓
- If $i$ knows that $j$ already believes $\phi$, informing is redundant. ✓
- After informing, $j$'s belief state is updated. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Formal Semantics of `request`

**Step 1 — Define the act.**

$$
\langle i, \; \text{request}(j, a) \rangle
$$

Where $a$ is an **action** (not a proposition) that $i$ wants $j$ to perform.

**Step 2 — Feasibility Pre-condition.**

Agent $i$ must itself not be the one to perform $a$, and must believe $a$ is feasible for $j$:

$$
FP \;:\;\; \neg B_i \; (I_i \; a) \;\wedge\; B_i \; (\text{feasible}(j, a))
$$

Where $I_i a$ means "agent $i$ intends action $a$."

**Step 3 — Rational Effect.**

Agent $j$ should come to intend $a$:

$$
RE \;:\;\; Intent_j \; a
$$

**Step 4 — Full formal expression.**

$$
\boxed{\;
\langle i, \; \text{request}(j, a) \rangle \;\Rightarrow\; \bigl[ \neg B_i(I_i a) \;\wedge\; B_i \; \text{feasible}(j, a) \bigr] \;\triangleright\; Intent_j \; a
\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Note the asymmetry.** `request` only attempts to influence; it does **not force** $j$. This is why the follow-up `agree` or `refuse` performative is needed to close the loop.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performative vs. Allowed Follow-Up

This matrix shows which performatives can legally follow which in a *FIPA Request* protocol conversation.

| Current Act ↓ / Reply → | `agree` | `refuse` | `inform-done` | `failure` |
|-------------------------|---------|----------|---------------|-----------|
| `request` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| `agree` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| `refuse` (terminal) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |

Reading: After a `request`, $j$ may either `agree` or `refuse`. After `agree`, $j$ eventually sends `inform-done` or `failure`. `refuse` terminates the conversation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix embodies the **conversation policy** of the protocol. KTU questions often ask students to identify whether a given message sequence is **protocol-compliant**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: FIPA Contract Net Protocol Flow

$$
\text{Task Announcement Phase} \rightarrow \text{Bidding Phase} \rightarrow \text{Award Phase} \rightarrow \text{Execution Phase}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initiator} &: \text{send cfp} \\
\text{Participants} &: \text{respond with propose} \;\lor\; \text{refuse} \\
\text{Initiator} &: \text{evaluate proposals} \\
\text{Initiator} &: \text{send accept-proposal} \;\lor\; \text{reject-proposal} \\
\text{Winner} &: \text{perform task} \\
\text{Winner} &: \text{inform-done} \;\lor\; \text{failure}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mathematical condition for a valid award:**

$$
\text{award}(p^*) \;\;\text{where}\;\; p^* = \arg\max_{p \in P} \; \text{utility}(p, \text{Initiator})
$$

If $P = \emptyset$ (no bids), initiator issues `cancel` and the protocol terminates.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Agent Communication

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef agentStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffcc00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef msgStyle fill:#2d5a27,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ontStyle fill:#5c2d72,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef protStyle fill:#7a4a1f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    A1[Agent A Sender]:::agentStyle
    A2[Agent B Receiver]:::agentStyle
    A3[Agent C Third Party]:::agentStyle

    M1[ACL Message Formatter]:::msgStyle
    M2[Speech Act Parser]:::msgStyle
    M3[BDI Mental State Updater]:::msgStyle

    O1[Shared Ontology Service]:::ontStyle
    O2[Content Language SL or Prolog]:::ontStyle

    P1[Interaction Protocol Engine]:::protStyle
    P2[Conversation Policy Manager]:::protStyle
    P3[Message Transport Layer]:::protStyle

    A1 --> M1
    M1 --> P3
    P3 --> P1
    P1 --> A2
    A2 --> M2
    M2 --> O1
    O1 --> M3
    M3 --> P2
    P2 --> A3

    O2 -.shared vocabulary.-> A1
    O2 -.shared vocabulary.-> A2
    P2 -.governs sequence.-> P1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Agent A formats a message through the **ACL Message Formatter**.
- The **Interaction Protocol Engine** validates the message against the allowed conversation flow.
- Agent B parses the message via **Speech Act Parser**.
- The **Shared Ontology Service** resolves term meanings.
- The **BDI Mental State Updater** adjusts the agent's beliefs/desires/intentions.
- The **Conversation Policy Manager** governs future allowable replies.

---

### 4.2 FIPA Contract Net Protocol Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator Agent
    participant P1 as Participant 1
    participant P2 as Participant 2
    participant P3 as Participant 3

    I->>P1: cfp task T with constraints
    I->>P2: cfp task T with constraints
    I->>P3: cfp task T with constraints

    P1->>I: propose bid B1
    P2->>I: refuse
    P3->>I: propose bid B3

    I->>P3: accept-proposal B3
    I->>P1: reject-proposal B1
    I->>P2: reject-proposal implicit

    P3->>I: inform task done result R
```

**Flow interpretation:**
1. **Call For Proposal (cfp):** Initiator announces the task to all participants.
2. **Bidding:** Participants respond with `propose` or `refuse`.
3. **Award:** Initiator sends `accept-proposal` to winner and `reject-proposal` to losers.
4. **Execution:** Winner executes and reports back with `inform-done`.

---

### 4.3 Three-Layer Communication Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef l1 fill:#1a3a52,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef l2 fill:#2e5a3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef l3 fill:#5a2a3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    L3[Application Layer Domain Task and Content]:::l3
    L2[Communication Layer ACL KQML FIPA ACL Performatives]:::l2
    L1[Transport Layer TCP IP HTTP IIOP Agent Platform]:::l1

    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
```

**Reading:** Domain-specific content travels through the ACL layer (which provides performative semantics) and over the transport layer. The reverse path reconstitutes the message at the receiver. Each layer is logically independent — a key design principle of KQML and FIPA ACL.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain Speech Act Theory and its relevance to Agent Communication Languages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Speech Act Theory, given by J.L. Austin and J.L. Searle, treats utterances as **performative actions** rather than mere truth-valued statements.

**Three layers (1 mark):**
- *Locutionary* — what is literally said.
- *Illocutionary* — the intended force (request, inform, promise).
- *Perlocutionary* — the effect produced on the hearer.

**Relevance to ACL (1 mark):** ACLs such as **KQML** and **FIPA ACL** use the *illocutionary* layer (the performative field) as the central organizing element. Every FIPA communicative act is named after a speech act (e.g., `inform`, `request`, `propose`), giving the ACL a rigorous philosophical foundation for what each message **means** in terms of agent mental-state transitions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between KQML and FIPA ACL.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | KQML | FIPA ACL |
|-----------|------|----------|
| Origin | DARPA KSE (1990s) | FIPA / IEEE (2000s) |
| Semantics | Informal, ad-hoc | Formal SL-based semantics |
| Message structure | S-expression (Lisp-like) | Slotted structure (field-value) |
| Protocol support | Implicit, ad-hoc | Explicitly standardized protocols |
| BDI grounding | Absent | Strong (Belief-Desire-Intention) |
| Industrial adoption | Mostly academic | Standardized, widely adopted |

**Conclusion (implicit):** FIPA ACL is the **modern, formal successor** of KQML.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** describe performatives without giving an example — you will lose at least 2 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT** skip the *feasibility pre-condition* when writing FIPA semantics; examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse *protocol* with *message content*. A protocol governs the **order**, not the payload.
> 4. **Do NOT** forget to specify which **ontology** is referenced — without an ontology, the message is semantically ungrounded.
> 5. Always end answers on a small **diagram or table** when possible — visual synthesis attracts bonus marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper aligned to Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three layers of Speech Act Theory** and show how they map to a **FIPA ACL message**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **FIPA Request Interaction Protocol** with a sequence diagram and discuss the role of `agree` and `refuse` performatives in commitment formation. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to A(a) — 7 marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Speech Act Theory, rooted in the philosophy of language (Austin 1962, Searle 1969), classifies every utterance as a performative action.

**Three Layers (3 marks):**

| Layer | Definition | FIPA ACL Mapping |
|-------|------------|------------------|
| Locutionary | Literal textual content | The `:content` field (e.g., `(price (?flight KTM-BLR))`) |
| Illocutionary | The intent / force | The `performative` field (e.g., `query-ref`, `inform`) |
| Perlocutionary | Effect on hearer's mental state | Updated belief $B_j \phi$ after `inform` |

**Mapping to FIPA ACL (2 marks):**
A FIPA ACL message is precisely a structured encoding of a speech act:

$$
\text{FIPA-Msg} = \langle \text{performative}, \text{sender}, \text{receiver}, \text{content}, \text{ontology}, \text{language}, \ldots \rangle
$$

- The **performative** carries the **illocutionary force**.
- The **content** carries the **locution**.
- The **rational effect** (e.g., $B_j \phi$) is the **perlocutionary consequence**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Without Speech Act Theory, ACL messages would be uninterpretable — the performative field gives them meaning.

---

**Solution to A(b) — 7 marks**

**FIPA Request Protocol Steps (3 marks):**

```
Initiator (I) → Participant (P) : request   (I asks P to perform action a)
P → I : agree                              (P commits to perform a)
                                     OR
P → I : refuse                             (P declines)
[After action completion]
P → I : inform-done                        (P reports success)
                                     OR
P → I : failure                            (P reports failure)
```

**Sequence Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator
    participant P as Participant

    I->>P: request action a
    alt P agrees
        P->>I: agree
        P->>I: inform-done result r
    else P refuses
        P->>I: refuse with reason
    end
```

**Role of `agree` and `refuse` in Commitment (2 marks):**

- `agree` is a **commissive** act — by uttering it, the participant **commits** itself to the BDI logical state $Intent_j a$.
- `refuse` is a **terminal** act — it ends the conversation with no commitment.
- The asymmetry: `request` only *attempts* to influence; `agree` is what actually *creates* the commitment. This is essential in systems where agents may fail or lie without it.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper aligned to June 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **KQML architecture** with its three layers (content, message, communication). List any **eight reserved performatives** with their categories. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat sequence diagram, explain the **FIPA Contract Net Protocol**. Show how the initiator decides the winning bid. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to B(a) — 7 marks**

**KQML Architecture (3 marks):**
KQML follows a **three-layer separation** to maximize interoperability:

| Layer | Purpose | Example |
|-------|---------|---------|
| **Content Layer** | Carries the actual proposition or action in a domain language | Prolog, KIF, Lisp |
| **Message Layer** | Encodes the performative and core message fields | `(ask-one :sender A :receiver B ...)` |
| **Communication Layer** | Handles transport, addressing, and low-level delivery | TCP/IP, HTTP, SMTP |

This separation lets KQML carry *any* content language while keeping message semantics independent.

**Eight Reserved Performatives (4 marks — 0.5 each):**

| # | Performative | Category |
|---|--------------|----------|
| 1 | `ask-one` | Query |
| 2 | `ask-all` | Query |
| 3 | `tell` | Generative / Assertive |
| 4 | `achieve` | Directive |
| 5 | `advertise` | Capability |
| 6 | `subscribe` | Long-running query |
| 7 | `reply` | Response |
| 8 | `sorry` | Negative response |

**Conclusion:** KQML's flexibility comes from its layered design and rich performative set.

---

**Solution to B(b) — 7 marks**

**Contract Net Protocol Overview (2 marks):**
A **task-allocation protocol** in which one agent (Initiator) announces a task and multiple agents (Participants) bid. The initiator awards the task to the best bidder.

**Sequence Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator
    participant B1 as Bidder 1
    participant B2 as Bidder 2
    participant B3 as Bidder 3

    I->>B1: cfp task T deadline d
    I->>B2: cfp task T deadline d
    I->>B3: cfp task T deadline d

    B1->>I: propose price 100 duration 2h
    B2->>I: refuse unavailable
    B3->>I: propose price 80 duration 3h

    Note over I: Select best proposal based on utility function

    I->>B3: accept-proposal
    I->>B1: reject-proposal

    B3->>I: inform task completed result R
```

**Winner Selection Logic (2 marks):**

Let $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$ be the set of proposals. The initiator chooses the winner $p^*$ that maximizes its **utility function**:

$$
p^* = \arg\max_{p_k \in P} \; U(p_k)
$$

A typical utility function might combine price and time:

$$
U(p_k) = w_1 \cdot \text{quality}(p_k) - w_2 \cdot \text{price}(p_k) - w_3 \cdot \text{duration}(p_k)
$$

Where $w_1, w_2, w_3 \geq 0$ are weighting coefficients. If $P = \emptyset$, the initiator issues `cancel` and the protocol terminates with no winner.

**Conclusion:** Contract Net is the **canonical task allocation mechanism** in MAS, foundational for grid computing, logistics, and auction systems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Communication in MAS**

- **Communication ≠ data transfer.** It is a *performative act* that alters mental states.
- **Speech Act Theory** has **three layers**: Locutionary, Illocutionary, Perlocutionary.
- **Searle's 5 illocutionary types**: Representatives, Directives, Commissives, Expressives, Declaratives.
- **KQML** = older, layered, Lisp-like, informal semantics.
- **FIPA ACL** = modern, slotted structure, formal SL/BDI semantics.
- **FIPA ACL mandatory fields**: performative, sender, receiver, content.
- **FIPA `inform`**: FP = $B_i \phi \wedge \neg B_i(B_j \phi \vee \neg B_j \phi)$, RE = $B_j \phi$.
- **FIPA `request`**: RE = $Intent_j a$ (attempt, not obligation).
- **Ontology** = shared vocabulary, mandatory for semantic grounding.
- **Protocol** = allowed message sequence; not the message content.
- **FIPA Request Protocol**: `request` → `agree/refuse` → `inform-done/failure`.
- **FIPA Contract Net**: `cfp` → `propose/refuse` → `accept/reject-proposal` → `inform-done`.
- **Winner selection**: $p^* = \arg\max_{p \in P} U(p)$.
- **Three FIPA message layers**: Content → ACL → Transport (independent & stackable).
- **Standard body**: IEEE FIPA (formerly Foundation for Intelligent Physical Agents).
- **Common pitfall**: confusing `request` (attempt) with `agree` (commitment).
- **Common pitfall**: assuming agents can communicate without a shared ontology.
- **Common pitfall**: describing a protocol without naming the performatives used.
- **Common pitfall**: skipping pre-conditions in FIPA formal semantics.
- **Real-world uses**: smart grid, supply chain, healthcare monitoring, robotics swarms.

> **End of Notes — PCCAT601, Module 4: Communication**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formal Grammar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAT601 - AGENT BASED INTELLIGENT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Formal Grammar # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Formal Grammar** is a precise mathematical framework used to describe the syntax and structure of a language. In the context of Agent-Based Intelligent Systems, formal grammar provides the foundational mechanism by which intelligent agents parse, interpret, and generate structured information (such as natural language, communication protocols, knowledge representation languages, and agent communication languages). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** A **Formal Grammar** $G$ is formally defined as a 4-tuple (quadruple) structure: > $$G = (V, T, P, S)$$ > where: > - $V$ = Finite set of **Non-Terminal symbols** (Variables / Syntactic Categories) > - $T$ = Finite set of **Terminal symbols** (Alphabet / Lexical tokens) > - $P$ = Finite set of **Production Rules** (Rewrite rules of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$) > - $S$ = Designated **Start Symbol** ($S \in V$) > - With the constraint that $V \cap T = \emptyset$ (disjoint sets). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Recipe Book" of Language Think of formal grammar as a **strict recipe book** for a chef (the agent): - **Non-Terminals ($V$)** = The *placeholder names* in the recipe, like ``, ``, ``. They are abstract categories that need to be expanded. - **Terminals ($T$)** = The *actual items* on the kitchen shelf — turmeric, salt, onion. These cannot be broken down further; they are the final output. - **Productions ($P$)** = The *cooking instructions* written in the recipe, e.g., ` → turmeric \vert chili \vert pepper`. They tell us how to replace a placeholder with concrete items. - **Start Symbol ($S$)** = The *title of the dish* (e.g., ``). Every derivation starts from this symbol. Just as a chef follows the recipe card from `` down to actual ingredients, a parser starts from $S$ and applies productions until only terminals remain. The final sequence of terminals is called a **sentence** in the language generated by $G$, denoted $L(G)$. > [!NOTE] > **Language Generated by Grammar $G$:** > $$L(G) = \{ w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w \}$$ > where $\Rightarrow^*$ denotes *zero or more derivation steps*. ## 1.3 Why Formal Grammar Matters in Intelligent Agents In multi-agent systems (a KTU 2024 Module 4 focus), agents must: 1. **Communicate** using standardized languages (e.g., FIPA ACL — Agent Communication Language, KQML). 2. **Parse** incoming messages to extract meaning (syntactic + semantic analysis). 3. **Generate** valid responses and plans. 4. **Share ontologies** with consistent, unambiguous representations. All of these tasks are mathematically grounded in formal grammar theory. **Without a formal grammar, an agent cannot reliably determine whether a received message is valid, well-formed, or meaningful.** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Components — Detailed Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Non-Terminal Symbols ($V$) - Capital letters used by convention: $A, B, C, S$. - Sometimes denoted $N$ (Non-terminals) in alternate notation. - Represent *syntactic categories* or *phrasal types* (e.g., ``, ``). - Can be recursively expanded using production rules. ### 2.1.2 Terminal Symbols ($T$) - Lowercase letters by convention: $a, b, c, 0, 1$. - Form the **alphabet** $\Sigma$ of the language. - A **sentence** (or *string*) is a finite sequence $w = a_1 a_2 \dots a_n$ where each $a_i \in T$. - The empty string is denoted $\varepsilon$ (epsilon). ### 2.1.3 Production Rules ($P$) Each production has the form: $$\alpha \rightarrow \beta$$ where $\alpha$ is the *left-hand side (LHS)* and $\beta$ is the *right-hand side (RHS)*. The classification of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ determines the **grammar type** in the Chomsky Hierarchy. ### 2.1.4 Start Symbol ($S$) - A distinguished non-terminal $S \in V$ from which **every** derivation begins. - Every string in $L(G)$ must be derivable from $S$. ## 2.2 The Chomsky Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield Topic) Noam Chomsky classified formal grammars into **four types** based on restrictions on production rules. This is **frequently tested** in KTU exams. | Type | Name | Production Form | Recognizer | Language Power | |------|------|-----------------|------------|----------------| | **Type 0** | Unrestricted (Recursively Enumerable) | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ with $\alpha$ containing at least one non-terminal | Turing Machine | Most powerful | | **Type 1** | Context-Sensitive (CSG) | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ where $\gamma \neq \varepsilon$ | Linear Bounded Automaton | Context-dependent | | **Type 2** | Context-Free (CFG) | $A \rightarrow \gamma$ where $A \in V$ and $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^*$ | Pushdown Automaton | Most-used in AI/NLP | | **Type 3** | Regular Grammar (RG) | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (Right-linear) | Finite Automaton | Least powerful | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mnemonic — Increasing Restriction (Top → Bottom):** *Unrestricted → Context-Sensitive → Context-Free → Regular*. The set of languages becomes **smaller** as we go down, but the grammars become **easier to parse**. ## 2.3 Backus-Naur Form (BNF) Notation BNF is the **standard notation** used by KTU to write grammar rules. It is metalanguage for describing context-free grammars. **BNF Symbols:** | Symbol | Meaning | |--------|---------| | $::=$ | "is defined as" (replaces $\rightarrow$) | | $\vert$ | "or" (alternation) | | $< >$ | Encloses non-terminals | | $\{ \}$ | Zero or more repetitions | | $[ \, ]$ | Optional element | **Example BNF Grammar (Arithmetic Expressions):** $$ \begin{aligned} \langle \text{Expression} \rangle &::= \langle \text{Expression} \rangle \; \text{"+"} \; \langle \text{Term} \rangle \;\vert\; \langle \text{Term} \rangle \\ \langle \text{Term} \rangle &::= \langle \text{Term} \rangle \; \text{"*"} \; \langle \text{Factor} \rangle \;\vert\; \langle \text{Factor} \rangle \\ \langle \text{Factor} \rangle &::= \text{"("} \; \langle \text{Expression} \rangle \; \text{")"} \;\vert\; \text{"id"} \end{aligned} $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Mathematical Form | KTU Exam Use | |---|---------|-------------------|--------------| | 1 | Grammar Tuple | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | Definition question | | 2 | Language of $G$ | $L(G) = \{ w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w \}$ | Derivation problems | | 3 | Direct Derivation | $x A y \Rightarrow x \alpha y$ (using $A \rightarrow \alpha$) | Derivation steps | | 4 | Closure | $\Rightarrow^*$ = zero or more steps | KTU frequently asked | | 5 | Positive Closure | $\Rightarrow^+$ = one or more steps | Derivation counts | | 6 | CFG Production | $A \rightarrow \alpha,\; A \in V$ | CFG identification | | 7 | Regular (Right) | $A \rightarrow aB \;\vert\; a$ | RG identification | | 8 | Regular (Left) | $A \rightarrow Ba \;\vert\; a$ | RG identification | | 9 | Context-Sensitive | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta,\; \vert \gamma \vert \geq 1$ | CSG identification | | 10 | Empty String Rule | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ allowed only if $S$ not on RHS | Ambiguity check | > [!NOTE] > **Empty String Constraint:** In context-sensitive grammars, the rule $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is **only allowed** if $A = S$ (start symbol) AND $S$ does not appear on the RHS of any production. This is a classic KTU trick question. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Grammar Type Used | Example | |--------------------|-------------------|---------| | Programming Language Compilers | Type 2 (CFG) | Java, C++, Python syntax | | Agent Communication (FIPA ACL) | Type 2 (CFG in BNF) | KQML message structure | | DNA / Protein Sequence Analysis | Type 0 / Type 1 | Bioinformatics | | Pattern Matching in IDS | Type 3 (Regular) | Network packet signatures | | Natural Language Processing (NLP) | Type 2 with augmentations | Parsing English sentences | | Robot Planning Languages | Type 2 (CFG) | PDDL (Planning Domain Definition Language) | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Generating a String Using a CFG **Given Grammar $G_1$:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow aSb \;\vert\; \varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ **Task:** Show that the string $w = aabb$ is in $L(G_1)$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: We start from the start symbol $S$ and apply productions to reach $w = aabb$. $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow aSb && \text{(Step 1: Apply } S \rightarrow aSb) \\ &\Rightarrow aaSbb && \text{(Step 2: Apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ again)} \\ &\Rightarrow aa\,\varepsilon\,bb && \text{(Step 3: Apply } S \rightarrow \varepsilon) \\ &\Rightarrow aabb && \text{(Step 4: Concatenate terminals)} \end{aligned} $$ Since we reached $w = aabb$ in a finite number of steps, we conclude $aabb \in L(G_1)$. **General Language Generated:** $$L(G_1) = \{ a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0 \}$$ This is the classic non-regular language — a favorite KTU question. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Leftmost vs. Rightmost Derivation **Given Grammar $G_2$:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow AB \\ A &\rightarrow aA \;\vert\; a \\ B &\rightarrow bB \;\vert\; b \end{aligned} $$ **Task:** Derive $w = aab$ using **leftmost** and **rightmost** derivations. ### Leftmost Derivation (always expand the leftmost non-terminal first): $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow AB && \text{(expand } S) \\ &\Rightarrow aAB && \text{(expand } A \text{ — leftmost)} \\ &\Rightarrow aaB && \text{(expand } A \text{ — leftmost)} \\ &\Rightarrow aabB && \text{(expand } B \text{ — leftmost)} \\ &\Rightarrow aab && \text{(expand } B \text{ to } b) \end{aligned} $$ ### Rightmost Derivation (always expand the rightmost non-terminal first): $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow AB && \text{(expand } S) \\ &\Rightarrow AbB && \text{(expand } B \text{ — rightmost)} \\ &\Rightarrow Ab && \text{(expand } B \text{ to } b) \\ &\Rightarrow aAb && \text{(expand } A \text{ — rightmost)} \\ &\Rightarrow aab && \text{(expand } A \text{ to } a) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Both derivations produce the same string $aab$, but the intermediate sentential forms differ. If two different parse trees exist for the same string, the grammar is **ambiguous** — a heavily tested concept in KTU Module 4. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Identifying Grammar Type **Given Productions:** $$ \begin{aligned} (1) \quad & S \rightarrow aSBC \;\vert\; aBC \\ (2) \quad & CB \rightarrow BC \\ (3) \quad & aB \rightarrow ab \\ (4) \quad & bB \rightarrow bb \\ (5) \quad & cB \rightarrow bc \\ (6) \quad & cC \rightarrow cc \end{aligned} $$ **Classification Procedure:** | Rule | LHS Pattern | Type Match? | |------|-------------|-------------| | (1) | $S \rightarrow aSBC$ | Single non-terminal on LHS → **Type 2 (CFG)** | | (2) | $CB \rightarrow BC$ | Two non-terminals on LHS, context matters → **Type 1 (CSG)** | | (3)-(6) | $aB \rightarrow ab$ | Mixed terminal/non-terminal on LHS → **Type 1 (CSG)** | **Conclusion:** Since the *most permissive* rule restricts us to Type 1, the **grammar as a whole is Context-Sensitive (Type 1)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule of Thumb:** A grammar's type is determined by its **most restrictive** production. The type of the grammar equals the **strongest restriction satisfied by all rules**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Building a Parse Tree **Grammar:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow NP \; VP \\ NP &\rightarrow Det \; N \\ VP &\rightarrow V \; NP \\ Det &\rightarrow \text{the} \;\vert\; \text{a} \\ N &\rightarrow \text{cat} \;\vert\; \text{dog} \\ V &\rightarrow \text{sees} \;\vert\; \text{chases} \end{aligned} $$ **Parse Tree for sentence: "the cat sees a dog"** The tree root is $S$, and each production rule becomes a node with its children being the RHS symbols. This tree is the standard way to demonstrate **syntactic validity** in NLP-based agent systems. > [!NOTE] > **Yield of a parse tree:** The concatenation of leaf nodes (left to right) gives the derived string. For our example, yield = "the cat sees a dog". KTU often asks students to verify yield or count tree depth. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Grammar Comparison Summary | Feature | Type 0 | Type 1 (CSG) | Type 2 (CFG) | Type 3 (RG) | |---------|--------|--------------|--------------|-------------| | LHS Restriction | $\alpha \geq 1$ non-terminal | $\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$ | Single non-terminal | Single non-terminal | | RHS Restriction | Any string | $\vert \beta \vert \geq \vert \alpha \vert$ | Any string | $aB$ or $a$ | | Allows $\varepsilon$? | Yes (if $S$ unused on RHS) | No (except $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$) | Yes | No (except $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$) | | Recognizer | Turing Machine | LBA | Pushdown Automaton | DFA / NFA | | Used in Agents? | Rarely | Sometimes (FIPA semantics) | **Yes (most common)** | Tokenizers, pattern matchers | | Decision Problem | Undecidable | Decidable but PSPACE | Polynomial (CYK algo) | Linear time | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Chomsky Hierarchy — Set Inclusion Diagram The following Mermaid block illustrates the **strict containment relationship** between language families in the Chomsky hierarchy. Each inner set is a strict subset of the outer one. ```mermaid graph TD nodeS["Language L0
Recursively Enumerable
Type 0 Grammar"] nodeA["Language L1
Context Sensitive
Type 1 Grammar"] nodeB["Language L2
Context Free
Type 2 Grammar"] nodeC["Language L3
Regular
Type 3 Grammar"] nodeS --> nodeA nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD["Finite Languages
Trivial Case"] nodeE["Recognizer:
Turing Machine"] nodeF["Recognizer:
Linear Bounded Automaton"] nodeG["Recognizer:
Pushdown Automaton"] nodeH["Recognizer:
Finite Automaton"] nodeS -.-> nodeE nodeA -.-> nodeF nodeB -.-> nodeG nodeC -.-> nodeH ``` **Reading the diagram:** Every regular language is also context-free; every context-free language is also context-sensitive; and so on. The recognizer becomes *less powerful but more efficient* as we descend. --- ## 4.2 Derivation Process — Block-Level Flow The following diagram shows the **functional architecture** of how an agent's parser (e.g., CYK or Earley algorithm) processes a grammar to produce a parse tree. ```mermaid flowchart TD inputA["Input: Grammar G = (V, T, P, S)"] inputB["Input: Target String w in T*"] startStep["Step 1: Initialize
Current sentential form = S"] lookupStep["Step 2: Identify leftmost non-terminal A
in current sentential form"] ruleStep["Step 3: Look up all productions
A to alpha in P"] selectStep["Step 4: Select production rule
and replace A with alpha"] checkStep["Step 5: Is sentential form now
all terminals equal to w?"] doneStep["Step 6: YES — Accept w
Build parse tree"] failStep["Step 7: NO — Backtrack or
declare derivation failed"] outputStep["Output: Parse Tree or Rejection Notice"] inputA --> startStep inputB --> startStep startStep --> lookupStep lookupStep --> ruleStep ruleStep --> selectStep selectStep --> checkStep checkStep -->|Yes| doneStep checkStep -->|No| lookupStep doneStep --> outputStep selectStep -->|No rule applies| failStep failStep --> outputStep ``` > [!NOTE] > **Agent System Context:** This block diagram is precisely how a *syntactic analysis module* inside an intelligent agent works. The agent receives a message (string $w$), references its grammar knowledge base ($G$), and outputs either a structured parse tree (success) or rejection (failure). --- ## 4.3 Production Rule Application — Leftmost Derivation Trace For the string $aab$ from Grammar $G_2$ of Section 3.2: ```mermaid graph TD stepRoot["S
Start Symbol"] nodeA1["a A B
Apply S to AB"] nodeA2["a a A B
Apply A to aA"] nodeA3["a a B
Apply A to a"] nodeA4["a a b B
Apply B to bB"] nodeA5["a a b
Apply B to b
FINAL STRING"] stepRoot -->|Step 1| nodeA1 nodeA1 -->|Step 2| nodeA2 nodeA2 -->|Step 3| nodeA3 nodeA3 -->|Step 4| nodeA4 nodeA4 -->|Step 5| nodeA5 ``` **Reading trace:** Each node represents a sentential form. Each edge represents one production application. The yield of the rightmost path is the derived string. --- ## 4.4 Grammar Classification Decision Diagram Use this flowchart to determine the type of any given grammar in KTU exam problems: ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode["Start: Given Grammar G with productions P"] q1{"Does any rule have
multiple non-terminals
on LHS?"} q2{"Is the LHS a
single non-terminal A?"} q3{"Is the RHS a single
terminal or terminal-then-non-terminal?
e.g., aB or a"} q4{"Does the rule satisfy
length non-decreasing:
|alpha| less than or equal to |beta|?"} type0["Type 0 — Unrestricted"] type1["Type 1 — Context-Sensitive"] type2["Type 2 — Context-Free"] type3["Type 3 — Regular"] startNode --> q1 q1 -->|Yes| q4 q4 -->|Yes| type1 q4 -->|No| type0 q1 -->|No| q2 q2 -->|Yes| q3 q3 -->|Yes| type3 q3 -->|No| type2 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define a formal grammar. State and explain its components with a suitable example.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **formal grammar** $G$ is a 4-tuple defined as $G = (V, T, P, S)$, where: - $V$ is a finite set of **non-terminal symbols** (variables used as placeholders). - $T$ is a finite set of **terminal symbols** (final alphabet), with $V \cap T = \emptyset$. - $P$ is a finite set of **production rules** of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$. - $S \in V$ is the distinguished **start symbol**. **Example:** $G = (\{S, A\}, \{a, b\}, \{S \rightarrow aA,\; A \rightarrow bA \vert \varepsilon\}, S)$ generates the language $L(G) = \{a b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Differentiate between Context-Free Grammar (CFG) and Regular Grammar (RG) with production rule examples.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Context-Free Grammar (CFG) | Regular Grammar (RG) | |---------|---------------------------|----------------------| | Production Form | $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$ | $A \rightarrow aB \;\vert\; a$ (right-linear) | | Power | More powerful (can describe nested structures) | Less powerful (linear only) | | Example | $S \rightarrow aSb \;\vert\; \varepsilon$ | $S \rightarrow aS \;\vert\; b$ | | Recognizer | Pushdown Automaton | Finite Automaton | | Language | $\{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ | $\{a^m b \mid m \geq 0\}$ | The CFG can express the non-regular language $a^n b^n$, whereas the regular grammar cannot. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format:** Answer **ONE** of the following: --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]* **(a)** Define the **Chomsky Hierarchy** of formal grammars.
